title: Platforms at Twilio: Unlocking Developer Effectiveness
description: A very interesting case study about how to create and evolve an internal platform and increase the efficiency of all the engineering organization.
notes:
Justin Kitagawa, platform leader at Twilio responsible for platform foundation (five teams spanning edge infrastructure and cloud orchestration), argues that internal platforms are a lever that lets every engineering team "stand on the shoulders of giants" and unlock developer effectiveness. His core thesis is that a platform rebuilt around four principles—API-first, self-service, declarative over imperative, and built with empathy—transformed Twilio from a company that couldn't reliably ship into one deploying around 100 times a day. He frames platform engineering as "Twilio for Twilio," the intersection of DevOps and software.

* **Organizational context shapes the platform**: Twilio is ~100 "two-pizza box" teams (a model inherited from Amazon, where CEO Jeff Lawson was an early AWS PM), each a mini-startup running its own polyglot microservices (Java, Scala, Python, PHP), with 1000+ server roles across tens of thousands of VMs in nine points of presence.
* **DevOps is the culture, not a role**: "You build it, you run it." There is no centralized QA, build, or release team. The guiding saying is "job number one: operations and security; job number two: everything else."
* **The broken "before" state**: Five years prior the platform was imperative infrastructure-as-code requiring engineers to know Chef, with a Turing-complete DSL checked into Git as the control plane. The core deployment system hit only "two nines" reliability against a five-nines aspiration—an existential threat where "the company that ships software couldn't ship."
* **Anti-patterns named explicitly**: an era of gatekeepers (the dreaded "I-request" Jira tickets manually routed and executed by a platform engineer), lack of empathy (platform teams not using their own tooling), and vendor sprawl (every team needing its own Jenkins administrator).

### The four principles
* **API-first**: The REST/web API—not Git, a CLI, or a UI—is the platform's control plane. Twilio wraps everything, including third-party vendors, behind APIs to ease migrations and avoid lock-in. If you don't publish an interface, engineers will find one (their service discovery once had five hacked entry points). Thin language wrappers call platform sidecar REST services so all polyglot languages benefit equally.
* **Self-service over gatekeepers**: Cited as the most important principle, drawing on Bezos's 2011 shareholder letter that even well-meaning gatekeepers slow innovation. He warns against the "automation fallacy": if something only runs on someone's laptop or a special Jenkins server, it is scripted, not automated, because it is not self-service.
* **Declarative over imperative (the what over the how)**: Prefer data models over code; users declare desired state and the "how" is codified into the platform, reducing cognitive load. Embedding the how means engineers automatically inherit evolving best practices. This emerged in parallel with Twilio's own declarative product APIs (Proxy and Notify).

### Declarative platform in practice
* **Starship and API definitions**: Starship is the edge gateway that routes, transforms, filters, authenticates, authorizes, and applies concurrency limits on all Twilio API traffic. The entire REST API is declared as per-resource API definitions; code compiles them into gateway code and loads it into Starship, replacing the old consulting model where cycle time ran weeks to months.
* **What the definitions unlock**: schema-aware filtering of internal attributes to prevent leaking sensitive data; shadow deployments (mirror production traffic to v1 and v2 and diff responses); gradual feature-flag rollouts; auto-generated helper libraries via a transpiler called Yoyodyne that turns JSON definitions into SDK code across six languages, refreshed weekly, ending manual SDK drift; and auto-generated docs including GDPR PII annotations.
* **Declarative deployments via Admiral**: Deployments decompose into software (a manifest of versioned RPM artifacts), hardware (a formation, e.g. 40 nodes of given RAM/disk/CPU), and externalized config per Heroku's 12-factor—all exposed as APIs and via the Admiral console. Twilio runs disposable infrastructure (cattle not pets) with zero-downtime red-black deployments via canary and load-balancer cutover, auto-spreading across three AZs.
* **Container migration without mental-model shift**: Kubernetes is the eventual orchestrator, but "having engineers know the intricacies of Kubernetes is a failure state." Teams specify a container manifest instead of RPMs; today these deploy as a pod onto a single VM and will later transpose onto Kubernetes pods with no migration pain.
* **Zero-downtime MySQL deployments**: Schema migrations always run on a replica, never the primary, with S3 backup, replica promotion, and an SSH tunnel forwarding outstanding writes. Previously semi-manual scripts were a constant drain across the ~30% of roles using MySQL; now Admiral handles it declaratively, drawing emotional Slack reactions.
* **Service mesh (moving to Envoy)**: Because red-black deployments constantly change IPs, a sidecar daemon on every host acts as the mesh control plane (WebSockets for push, REST for pull); services reach each other through a local-host HAProxy virtual IP acting as a client-side load balancer, removing remote-call latency and scaling linearly. The mesh is being upgraded from HAProxy+NGINX to Envoy via a declarative service catalog where engineers just name downstream dependencies.

### Build with empathy
* **Drink your own champagne**: Platform builders must use the platform so they feel the same pain and "file down the sharp burrs." Value feedback over opinion—"an opinion is a hypothesis, feedback is data."
* **Hospitality over service**: Citing restaurateur Danny Meyer, treat engineers as paying customers; internal-tool UX matters, and platform outages deserve the same scrutiny as product outages.
* **Measuring with NPS**: A quarterly survey to all engineers; Twilio's internal-platform NPS rose from -59 four years ago to +20 last quarter, targeting 50+. A per-product Slackbot also gathers feedback (one request for a deploy progress bar was committed to).
* **Results and closing**: ~100 production deployments per day (~30,000+/year) and two days between customer-facing features landing. No single technology is a magic bullet, but adopting some or all of the four principles makes engineers more effective.

title: What is this cloud native thing anyway?
description: A good summary of the topic.

title: The Root Cause of Product Failure
description: Marty Cagan explores why traditional product roadmaps lead to failure and how organizations can achieve consistent innovation by empowering **"missionary" teams** to focus on **business outcomes** through continuous product discovery.
notes:
To achieve consistent innovation, organizations must move away from fixed feature roadmaps and "mercenary" work cultures, instead empowering "missionary" teams to solve prioritized business problems through rapid, continuous product discovery. This approach prioritizes validating a solution's value and feasibility through experimentation before committing to high-fidelity development, shifting the focus from mere output to meaningful business results.

*   Innovation failure is primarily caused by **how companies work**, rather than a lack of talent or engineering skill.
*   The traditional **product roadmap** is a fundamental root cause of failure because it functions as a prioritized list of features (output) rather than a commitment to business results (outcomes).
*   **The Two Inconvenient Truths of Product:** At least **50% of ideas on a roadmap will fail** to meet objectives, and those that do succeed typically require **3 to 5 iterations** to deliver actual value.
*   **Business cases are fallacious** in the early stages because it is impossible to accurately predict the return on investment or the cost of a solution before the solution has actually been discovered and defined.
*   **Engineers are the single best source of innovation** because they are closest to the technology and understand what is now possible; they must be included in the discovery process from the beginning.
*   Organizations must shift from **teams of mercenaries** (who simply implement features dictated by others) to **teams of missionaries** (who are empowered to solve problems and believe in the product vision).
*   **Product Discovery** is a continuous process meant to separate good ideas from bad ones in hours or days, rather than the months required by a traditional waterfall-style launch.
*   **MVPs should be treated as experiments**, not products. A strong team should run **15 to 20 experiments per week** to validate value, usability, feasibility, and business viability.
*   Replace feature-based roadmaps with **Outcome-based Performance Management (like OKRs)**, where leadership provides prioritized business problems to solve rather than a list of features to build.
*   Successful product leadership requires being **stubborn on the vision but flexible on the details**, allowing teams the autonomy to find the right solution to reach the goal.
*   Modern product teams focus on **Time to Money (or Value)** rather than just Time to Market, recognizing that the first version of a feature is rarely the one that achieves the business objective.

title: Engineering Your Organization: Services, Platforms, and Communities
description: Great summary about the different ways high-performing engineering organizations gain leverage by specialization and sharing.
notes:
Randy Shoup, at the time VP of Engineering and Chief Architect at eBay (and previously a senior engineering leader at Google, Stitch Fix, and WeWork), argues for deliberately "engineering your organization" the way you'd engineer software. His core thesis is that "all organizations are wrong, but some are useful": an org chart is a single-dimensional slice through the multidimensional reality of how people interact, so the goal is to pick the structure that is least wrong. He builds the talk around four layers that make an engineering organization sustainable: domain-aligned services, shared platforms, cross-cutting communities, and the leadership characteristics that hold it all together. The recurring principle is internal market discipline — teams choose what to consume, and that choice forces providers to be genuinely better than the alternatives.

* **"All organizations are wrong, but some are useful":** Drawing on 30 years in the industry, Shoup frames any org as a flawed projection of multidimensional human interaction; the aim is to choose a structure better than the alternatives, not a perfect one. A fully full-stack-per-team model (each team building down to chips and data centers) is rejected as inefficient and as denying teams the chance to specialize where they have most impact.

* **Domain-aligned, full-stack service teams (Conway's Law):** Following domain-driven design, each team is aligned to a business problem and given "a clear set of golden metrics" that matter to its customers (external or internal). eBay examples include teams for shipping, selling, and search. Teams colocate idea generation, software development, QA, and operations so they can move with fast cycle time and independently design, develop, deploy, and operate their services "cradle to grave."

* **Internal vendor–customer relationship:** A service provider is an "internal vendor" and consumers are "internal customers," exactly analogous to a third-party vendor relationship. "A service is only useful to the extent that it actively provides value to our customer teams." In mature organizations teams get to choose whether to use an internal service, which "forces me as a service provider to be strictly better than any of the alternatives" — building it themselves, buying from a third party, borrowing via open source, or using another team's.

* **Services evolve like cells; nothing is static:** New services appear to solve new problems and are deprecated once no longer used; teams grow and split "just like cellular mitosis." Shoup quotes the Google quip that "every service at Google is either deprecated or not ready yet."

* **What a common platform contains:** Shared infrastructure (often third-party cloud: compute, storage, databases, an event system), a common developer platform (ideally one source-control system, dev/test environments, CD pipelines), common capabilities (authentication, secrets management, observability, alerting), and standard frameworks — including a "service chassis" to spin up new services, plus standardized communication protocols and data formats. Rationale: rolling your own of these is not an efficient use of an application team's time.

* **The platform provider's job is the "paved road":** The goal is to "reduce the cognitive load" on customer teams by offering a consistent, integrated set of capabilities that is the path of least resistance (cited examples: Netflix, Google, eBay). Crucially, teams may still "bushwhack through the jungle" if they believe there's a better way, but the paved road makes the standard choice come "for free." Providers should be fully self-service (automatic provisioning, monitoring out of the box, examples and documentation) and even supply basic mocks so consumer teams can test.

* **As a consumer, embrace what you adopt — don't fear lock-in:** Shoup advises against building abstraction layers to guard against switching cloud providers or internal services, because abstraction yields a "least common denominator" and you forfeit the full suite of capabilities. In his experience replacing such platforms is rare, so optimizing for that edge case is the wrong trade-off. "To the extent that you feel locked in, that is exactly another way of saying that that third-party capability is providing active value."

* **Charge internal customers to drive efficiency:** Free internal usage gives "no incentive, economic or otherwise" to control scarce resources; charging motivates both provider and consumer to optimize. His Google App Engine story (a PaaS serving ~3 million external and ~15,000 internal applications) describes an egregious internal customer — "the predecessor game to Pokémon GO" — consuming about 25% of a scarce, expensive resource. Bugs, emails, and meetings failed; handing them a bill for "a not insignificant number of millions of dollars" got the usage cut 10x within weeks, and it made their app faster.

* **Communities cut across the org chart:** Orthogonal to services and platforms, communities form around language/framework ecosystems, roles (security, UX), users of a given service or platform, and communities of practice. They collaborate via dedicated Slack channels, mailing lists/groups, periodic meetings, and internal conferences — sharing knowledge and expertise without routing through the formal hierarchy.

* **Internal open source — "pull requests are accepted":** Provider teams get more feature requests than they have time for, so they accept contributions internally just like external open source: PRs come with tests, the provider reviews and iterates with the contributor, then merges. He references GitHub's open source guide and its practices — document your processes, "learn to say no" (not every request fits the platform), leverage the community for ideas, and embrace automation.

* **Lead like a parent; be a "technological maestro":** Shoup refactors org structure "like an engineer" but leads people "as a parent." Citing Ron Westrum (via Gene Kim's Idealcast) and the generative-vs-pathological culture work, he wants leaders who are competent technologists — high energy, asking the right questions, holding high quality standards, and "good on the details" without micromanaging. He quotes Jacob Rabinow: "If the man at the top is a dope or ignorant, everyone under him will soon be a dope or ignorant, because he sets the tone," refining the adage to "A players hire and retain A players, whereas B players hire and retain C players."

* **Theory X vs Theory Y and psychological safety:** Referencing Douglas McGregor's 1960 The Human Side of Enterprise, he contrasts Theory X (people are lazy, avoid responsibility, need extrinsic motivation → micromanagement) with Theory Y (people are intrinsically motivated and want ownership → empowerment), noting Accelerate/State of DevOps research favors Theory Y. He adds to Hanlon's razor: "never attribute to incompetence what can be adequately explained by perverse incentives." Google's Project Aristotle found the top predictor of high-performing teams was being "safe for interpersonal risk-taking," letting people bring 100% of their ideas. He cites a 2016 study where fully diverse teams (geography, age, gender) made better decisions 87% of the time, twice as fast with half the meetings, and delivered 60% better results, closing with the proverb quoted by Bob Taylor: "None of us is as smart as all of us."

* **Going off the paved road — "you must be this high":** In Q&A, Shoup argues exceptions (e.g., a new language like Haskell or Scala) should be judged on outcomes, not banned: the choice must integrate with deployment tooling, monitoring, and security/vulnerability requirements, and the team implicitly signs up to maintain those integrations. The justification must be order-of-magnitude, not a 20% saving, and the team inherits the cost — a real risk when reorgs later hand the codebase to people who don't know the language. At small companies ("three more months of Series A funding") he'd lean toward saying no; at eBay or Google scale he'd say "let's talk about it" with eyes open to the trade-offs. Sidecars now soften the once-binary choice, and he notes Google ran four supported language environments (Java, C++, Python, Go). On charging at small companies, below the Dunbar number informal conversation suffices; beyond it, start with "showback" (or budgets) before sending real bills, which is far easier in 2021 because cloud providers already meter and itemize. He closes with Martin Fowler: "If you can't change your organization, you can change your organization."

title: Wardley Maps for concious strategy definition
description: Share ideas on how to align strategy and create situational awareness using Wardley Maps, DDD, and Team Topologies among others tools and frameworks.
notes:
Wardley Mapping provides a visual framework for understanding your product's landscape and evolution, allowing you to move beyond intuition to make conscious, data-driven strategic decisions. By integrating this situational awareness with principles like Team Topologies and Domain-Driven Design, organizations can effectively align their internal structures and practices with market forces to drive innovation and competitive advantage.

* **The Strategic Cycle:** A five-step iterative process consisting of Purpose (the "why"), Landscape (the environment), Climate (external forces), Doctrine (universal principles), and Leadership (strategic decisions).
* **Wardley Map Components:**
    * **Vertical Axis (Value Chain):** Represents visibility to the user, with users and their needs at the top acting as the "anchor."
    * **Horizontal Axis (Evolution):** Tracks elements through four stages: Genesis (experimental), Custom Built, Product (+ rental), and Commodity/Utility.
* **Evolutionary Characteristics:** 
    * **Genesis:** High failure tolerance, undefined market, focused on future potential.
    * **Commodity:** Low failure tolerance, mature market, focused on high-volume efficiency.
* **Climate Patterns:** External rules that impact the landscape, such as "efficiency enables innovation" (standardized components allow for new higher-order features) and "success breeds inertia" (resistance to changing what worked in the past).
* **Doctrines:** Universal best practices applied regardless of context, including using a common language, focusing on user needs, and "thinking small" by breaking systems and teams into smaller, manageable components.
* **Methodology Alignment:** There is no "one size fits all" approach; Agile/XP are best suited for Genesis/Custom stages, while Lean and Six Sigma are more appropriate for Product/Commodity stages.
* **Integration with Team Topologies:** Maps help identify "streams of change" to organize teams (Stream-aligned, Platform, Enabling, or Complicated Subsystem) to reduce cognitive load and foster autonomy.
* **Integration with Domain-Driven Design (DDD):** Strategic design and context mapping help identify which parts of the map are "Core" (competitive advantage), "Supporting," or "Generic."
* **Leadership Gameplays:** Strategic moves based on map awareness, such as "Talent Raids" (securing scarce skills), "Open Approaches" (using open source to accelerate a component toward commodity), and "Alliances."
* **Situational Awareness:** The primary goal of mapping is to move away from intuition-based "guessing" toward data-driven discussions and conscious strategy definition.

title: CTO Craft 2021: Cross the River by Feeling the Stones
description: In this session, Simon examines the issue of situational awareness. Using examples from government and the commercial world, Simon explores how we can map our environment, identify opportunities to exploit and learn to play the game.
notes:
Effective strategy requires genuine situational awareness gained through mapping the landscape of user needs and component evolution, allowing leaders to visualize the "rules of the game" and challenge assumptions rather than relying on buzzword-heavy stories. By understanding how components predictably evolve from custom-built to commodities, organizations can apply the right management methods to the right parts of their business and make informed tactical decisions.

* **Sun Tzu's Five Factors of Competition**: Effective strategy requires understanding five elements: **Purpose** (the moral imperative), **Landscape** (the environment), **Climatic Patterns** (external rules of the game), **Doctrine** (universal principles of organization), and **Leadership** (context-specific gameplay).
* **The OODA Loop Integration**: Strategy is a cycle where you **Observe** the landscape and climatic patterns, **Orient** yourself using doctrine and principles, **Decide** where to act, and then **Act** through leadership and gameplay.
* **Maps vs. Graphs**: A true map requires an **anchor** (e.g., the user), **relative position** of components, and **consistency of movement**; most business diagrams are merely graphs because moving a component does not change its fundamental meaning.
* **Wardley Mapping Axes**: A business map is created by plotting a **Value Chain** (vertical axis of visibility to the user) against **Evolution** (horizontal axis: Genesis, Custom-built, Product/Rental, and Commodity/Utility).
* **Evolutionary Flow vs. Process Flow**: A common organizational failure is **optimizing process flow** (such as automating a task) for a component that is custom-built but should be treated as a **commodity**.
* **Method Selection by Evolution**: Management methods must match the stage of evolution: **Agile/XP** is best for the high-change Genesis/Custom side, **Lean/Scrum** for the transitional Product stage, and **Six Sigma/Outsourcing** for the stable Commodity stage.
* **Climatic Patterns and Inertia**: Everything evolves toward utility due to competition, but **past success creates inertia**, making organizations resistant to shifting from old practices to new, more efficient ones.
* **Efficiency Enables Innovation**: As components evolve into commodities and utilities, they provide the **efficiency** that allows for the creation of new, **higher-order systems** and sources of value.
* **Universal Doctrine**: Organizations should follow roughly 40 principles, such as **focusing on user needs**, understanding the details of the supply chain, and using a common language to describe the environment.
* **Context-Specific Gameplay**: Strategic plays, such as **open sourcing** to accelerate industrialization or using "fear, uncertainty, and doubt" (FUD), are moves that must be chosen based on the current state of the map.
* **Safe Challenge of Assumptions**: Mapping allows for **collaborative strategy** by enabling team members to challenge the "map" rather than the "storyteller," reducing personal conflict and improving situational awareness.
* **Iterative Strategy**: Strategy is not a one-time plan but an iterative process of **"crossing the river by feeling the stones,"** taking small steps and adjusting based on the changing landscape.
* **Utility of Imperfect Maps**: Because a perfect map would need to be 1:1 scale with the territory, all maps are **imperfect and technically "wrong,"** yet they remain essential for making informed decisions in competitive environments.

title: Keynote: Creating a Holistic Developer Experience
description: Great talk to understand what is development experience.
notes:
Organizations should prioritize a holistic and people-centered developer experience by treating internal tools as products and focusing on the entire development workflow rather than solving technical problems in isolation. This approach ensures that improvements in discoverability, usability, capabilities, and stability directly enhance the daily lives and productivity of engineers.

* **Holistic Approach**: Focus on the **entire development workflow** and its impact on people rather than solving isolated, small issues in a "whack-a-mole" fashion.
* **Developer UX**: Treat internal tools as products where **UX includes command lines, configurations, and documentation**, not just graphical interfaces.
* **The Four Pillars (DUCS)**:
    * **Discoverability**: Reduce the toil of finding information by **single-sourcing guidance and templates** through developer hubs. Track metrics such as **onboarding time** and customer satisfaction.
    * **Usability**: Ensure tasks can be completed with effectiveness and efficiency using **"Golden Paths," automation, and linters**. Measure **success rates** and time-based efficiency through usability testing.
    * **Capabilities**: Provide tools tailored to **specific developer personas** (e.g., machine learning) by using **journey mapping** to identify gaps in offerings.
    * **Stability**: Build developer confidence by prioritizing **intentional post-mortems** and tracking **Mean Time Between Outages** and uptime.
* **Human-Centered Strategy**: Prioritize the **"who" before the "how"** by using empathy to understand developer challenges.
* **Collaboration**: Avoid solving problems in silos; instead, **connect and collaborate continuously** across teams to create a seamless workflow.

title: Building Evolutionary Architectures: Principles and Practices
description: This talk presents some principles of evolutionary architecture that allow systems to respond to change without needing to predict the future. We then briefly describe approaches that realize these principles and discuss how these approaches support the adaptability of systems in an evolutionary way.
notes:
Successful long-term system health requires building **evolutionary architectures** that support guided, incremental change through automated **fitness functions** and a culture of experimentation. This approach allows organizations to adapt to shifting business needs and technological advancements without sacrificing structural integrity or losing enterprise value.

**Evolutionary Architecture Definition**: An evolutionary architecture supports guided, incremental change across multiple dimensions.

**Fitness Functions**: These are objective, often automated, functions used to define and protect "what is good" for specific architectural characteristics (e.g., uptime, security, cyclomatic complexity). They act as guardrails to prevent architectural "bit rot."

**Guided Change**: The "guided" aspect of evolutionary architecture is achieved through these fitness functions, which should be re-evaluated and evolved as business goals, regulations, or technology landscapes change.

**Incremental Change**: Change must be addressed at both the application level (how new features are added) and the operational level (how changes are deployed and monitored in running systems).

**Decision Timing**: Architectural decisions should be made at the "last responsible moment" to maximize the information available. If a high-impact decision must be made early, it should be designed for reversibility.

**Domain Boundaries**: Align technical modules and service boundaries with the mental models of business stakeholders. This reduces cognitive load and ensures that architectural changes correspond to business logic changes.

**Postel’s Law**: Be forgiving in what you receive and conservative in what you send. Avoid validating data you don't use to prevent unnecessary breaking changes when other teams update their services.

**Testability as a Design Tool**: Designing for testability naturally leads to better architectural boundaries and prevents the logic from leaking into hard-to-test middleware.

**Conway’s Law**: Team structures must reflect the desired system architecture. If the current organization does not match the target architecture, teams should be reorganized first.

**Continuous Delivery**: CD is a prerequisite for evolutionary architecture. It provides the necessary visibility into system configuration and code, making radical changes less risky.

**Culture of Experimentation**: Organizations must separate "bad ideas" from "failed experiments." A safe environment for hypothesis-driven experimentation is required to respond to shrinking business model lifetimes.

**Legacy Management**: When dealing with "big ball of mud" architectures, identify the most concerning characteristic, start measuring it, and focus on improving the trend rather than achieving an absolute number. Don't waste effort cleaning up code that is not actively changing.

title: Measuring Continuous Delivery
description: This talk provides a practical framework for measuring Continuous Delivery adoption by using stability and throughput indicators to guide iterative improvements and organizational success across large-scale engineering teams.
notes:
Successfully navigating the complexities of continuous delivery requires organizations to use ongoing measurements of stability and throughput as indicators to identify where to focus improvement efforts and uncover the unique stories behind team performance. By treating these metrics as a guide rather than a goal, teams can lift the "fog" of adoption and make better decisions through iterative, data-driven progress.

*   **Definition of Continuous Delivery**: An organization is in a state of continuous delivery when the **speed and stability of IT delivery satisfy business demand**. "Discontinuous delivery" occurs when IT is too slow or not stable enough to match that demand.
*   **The Improvement Kata**: This framework is used to facilitate incremental and iterative changes toward continuous delivery through four steps: 
    1.  **Creating a direction** (defining the required stability and speed).
    2.  **Analyzing the current condition** (understanding the distance traveled).
    3.  **Establishing a target condition** (setting an improvement milestone).
    4.  **Running experiments** (using the Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle).
*   **Measurement to Reduce Uncertainty**: It is essential to measure the **stability and throughput** of the release process to make better decisions. Stability and throughput are linked in a **virtuous circle**; improving one often improves the other.
*   **Deployment Stability Indicators**: These consist of the **deployment failure rate** (percentage of changes that go wrong) and **deployment failure recovery time** (how long it takes to remediate a failure).
*   **Deployment Throughput Indicators**: These include **deployment lead time** (the time between creating an artifact and it being in production) and **deployment interval** (the frequency of production deployments).
*   **Build Indicators**: Continuous Integration is a predictor of continuous delivery. It is measured via **build stability** (failure rate and recovery time) and **build throughput** (lead time from commit to artifact and build interval).
*   **Trunk-Based Development Indicators**: Committing to master frequently is a predictor of CI success. Key metrics include **mainline commit lead time** (time between branch creation and merging to master) and **mainline commit interval**.
*   **Data Application**: Metrics should be used to **show teams their own progress** and help them improve themselves rather than to compare one team against another. 
*   **Finding the "Stories"**: Measurement does not tell you what decisions to make; instead, it identifies **where to find the high-value stories** and problems that need attention.
*   **Enabling Techniques**: Practical improvements found through measurement include adopting **automated monitoring dashboards** and using **feature toggles** to replace long-lived branching strategies.
*   **Complexity**: Organizations are **complex adaptive systems** where cause and effect can often only be established in retrospect, making continuous measurement vital for seeing the real impact of organizational or technological changes.

title: The Logic of Flow: Some Indispensable Concepts
description: This talk explores key concepts and mathematical principles behind achieving flow in processes, like product development, drawing parallels to flow dynamics in traffic and internet systems. Don Reinertsen explains the economics of queuing, batch size reduction, and fast feedback loops, highlighting their impact on cycle time and overall process efficiency.
notes:
**Efficient flow in product development is achieved by using economic tradeoffs to manage invisible information queues and reducing batch sizes through lower transaction costs, rather than attempting to eliminate the variability essential for innovation.** You should remember to **monitor queue sizes as a leading indicator** of performance and avoid high capacity utilization, which triggers "congestion collapse" and causes cycle times to grow nonlinearly in stochastic systems.

* **Nonlinear Impact of Utilization:** In stochastic systems like product development, cycle time increases nonlinearly as capacity utilization approaches 100%. Queues typically double every time the remaining distance to full capacity is halved.
* **Invisibility of Development Queues:** Unlike manufacturing queues, which are physical and visible on balance sheets, product development queues consist of information. This makes them both physically and financially invisible, leading to chronic over-utilization.
* **Economic Trade-offs:** Managing flow requires bringing capacity utilization and queue size into the same unit of measure—money. This is done by quantifying the Cost of Delay to find the "flat-bottomed U-curve" of optimal total cost.
* **Mathematical Leverage:** Reducing capacity utilization provides more leverage for reducing queues than reducing variation does.
* **Arrival Variation:** Flow through a bottleneck can be improved by reducing the arrival time variation from the preceding process, not just by increasing the capacity of the bottleneck itself.
* **Batch Size and Transaction Costs:** Batch size is an economic trade-off between transaction costs and holding costs. Reducing transaction costs is the key to enabling smaller batch sizes, which in turn facilitates faster feedback.
* **Leading vs. Lagging Indicators:** Queue size is a leading indicator of future cycle time problems, whereas cycle time is a lagging indicator. Processes should be managed by monitoring and reacting to queue size.
* **Asymmetric Queue Growth:** Queues build much faster than they can be cleared. Rapidly responding to emergent queues prevents "passenger minutes" of delay from accumulating without adding to the long-term cost of capacity.
* **The "Knee of the Curve":** Peak throughput does not occur at maximum occupancy or maximum speed. Operating at the "knee" of the throughput curve provides the best balance of flow and stability.
* **Congestion Collapse:** When occupancy exceeds optimal levels, feedback loops become unstable and regenerative, causing speed to drop and density to increase until the system collapses.
* **Strategic Capacity Margins:** Extra capacity should be added specifically in zones of high variation (e.g., "steep hills" in traffic) rather than distributed evenly across the system.
* **Managing vs. Eliminating Variation:** Innovation inherently requires introducing variability. Systems should be designed to function in the presence of variability (using models like the internet or air traffic control) rather than trying to eliminate it through manufacturing-style standardization.
* **Decentralized Control:** Fast and effective adjustments are best achieved by combining decentralized actors with valid, centralized, real-time information (e.g., "time to next exit" signs).
* **Beyond Manufacturing Models:** Product development involves non-homogeneous, unpredictable flows. Practitioners should look toward operating system design and internet protocols for advanced flow control strategies rather than relying solely on repetitive manufacturing techniques.

title: LKUK13: Cycle Time Analytics - Fast #NoEstimate Forecasting & Decision Making
description: Good talk about hot to use probabilistic forecasting to obtain better results than using estimates and without all of the problems derived.
notes:
Utilizing historical cycle time data and statistical simulations allows teams to move beyond wasteful manual estimation and achieve more predictable project forecasting by accounting for the inherent uncertainty of work. By understanding the distribution patterns of cycle time, organizations can make better-informed decisions about delivery dates, costs, and staffing needs.

* **Forecasting Delivery Dates and Costs**: Uses cycle time data to predict project completion and costs more reliably than traditional methods.
* **Eliminating Story Point Estimation**: Positions story point estimation as potential waste and offers a data-driven alternative through cycle time analytics.
* **Cycle Time Distribution Patterns**: Leverages the fact that cycle time data follows known statistical distribution patterns for more accurate modeling.
* **Monte Carlo Simulation**: Employs Monte Carlo techniques to simulate software development projects (Kanban or Scrum) to account for variability.
* **Compounding Uncertainty**: Addresses how multiple uncertain factors in a project compound and the importance of understanding these risks for predictability.
* **Managing Limited Historical Data**: Provides strategies for forecasting even when historical data is minimal or completely unavailable through inference.
* **Staff Skill Balancing**: Uses simulation data to determine the optimal balance of roles, such as the ratio of developers to QA staff.
* **Metric Comparison**: Highlights the advantages of cycle time analytics over traditional planning tools like velocity, burn-down charts, and cumulative flow diagrams.
* **Low-Weight Metrics**: Emphasizes that capturing cycle time is a low-overhead "Lean" process compared to intensive estimation rituals.
* **Decision-Induced Uncertainty**: Explores how specific decisions and model depth impact the level of uncertainty in project forecasts.

title: This Mental Model Changed How I Design Software FOREVER
description: Applying the mental model of Test-Driven Development (TDD) as an empirical engineering approach to master software design and manage complexity alongside Agentic AI tools.
notes:
Test-Driven Development (TDD) functions as an empirical engineering approach and a vital mental model for software design, enabling engineers to manage complexity and break down problems efficiently, which is essential when working with Agentic AI tools. Mastering TDD allows humans to bring necessary engineering skills to the process, ensuring good designs are achieved faster, whereas a lack of a good design mental model means Agentic AI will likely help make a mess faster.

- **TDD as the Essential Mental Model for Software Design:** Test-Driven Development (TDD) provides a crucial mental model for approaching software design, enabling engineers to achieve better results and reduce cognitive load.
- **Human Engineering Skills Remain Paramount with Agentic AI:** In the age of Generative AI coding agents, what humans contribute is no longer typing but engineering skills, making a good design mental model like TDD more important than ever.
- **TDD as an Empirical Engineering Approach:** TDD functions as an empirical engineering approach to software design, focusing on iteration and continuous learning about the problem and the medium.
- **Managing Complexity is the Core Job:** A fundamental job of a software engineer is to manage complexity in software by finding good abstractions, modularizing code, and separating concerns. TDD facilitates this by forcing focus and breaking down problems into smaller, manageable chunks.
- **The Risk of Agentic AI Without Good Design Skills:** Lacking a good mental model for software design means Agentic AI tools will likely only help create a complex mess faster, leading to difficulty managing complexity and failure to produce modular, loosely coupled code.
- **The Iterative Process with Agentic AI:** The recommended process involves starting with user needs, breaking the problem into chunks, specifying behavior and coding standards for one chunk, having the AI write the code, improving the design, and iterating on the next chunk.
- **Importance of Acceptance Tests and Specifications:** Working with AI tools is enhanced by using automated acceptance tests to specify required functionality in detail, providing clear constraints and guards against regression.
- **Guiding AI with Principles:** It is helpful to include clear code principles in prompts, such as preferring simple, clean, and maintainable solutions, prioritizing readability, using small functions, and following the Single Responsibility Principle, to ensure the AI generates reasonably good designs.
- **AI for Design Improvement vs. Refactoring:** If the AI's initial design needs improvement, engineers can ask the Generative AI tool to try again and create a new design that fits the constraints, but this is distinct from deterministic refactoring, which often still requires a dedicated tool or manual work in an IDE.
- **Context Window Optimization:** A new consideration when working with Agentic AI is optimizing the tool's context window as a first-order concern, though this is closely related to the TDD process of managing complexity and focusing on one aspect of design at a time.

title: Towards an Evolutionary Architecture
description: Good advise about how to delay decision and maintain the options open. Don't resolve problems that you don't have and of course make decision based on knowledge.
notes:
Architecture is the set of high-level, strategic, hard-to-change decisions — and we traditionally make them at the very start, when we know least about the system. Evolutionary architecture defers and sequences those decisions so the architecture serves the product, instead of the team defending an architecture built in ignorance.

- The paradox: our knowledge grows over a project, yet we're told to fix the architecture up front when we have the least clue; the architect then defends that architecture (to avoid being fired) even as reality diverges from it.
- Evolving late has its own risk — a feature that doesn't fit can blow up the architecture. Mitigate it with adaptive planning: score each feature by business value AND architectural significance, and do the high-value/high-impact ones first. Velocity looks slower early but it's real, and speeds up as later features fit what's built.
- Architecture is like salt: necessary, but it serves the meal — the goal is a valuable, maintainable product, not the architecture itself (Fowler's "Who Needs an Architect?", Ralph Johnson on architecture as the decisions that are hard to change).
- Keep it simple by removing problems that don't need solving: a client had 60% of its code devoted to concurrency it didn't actually need; another wrapped DB→XML→XML→DB for imagined extensibility. Solve the real problem, not the hypothetical one.
- Refactor willfully: "make it work, then make it better real soon." Brooks's "throw one away" isn't practical wholesale, but writing small pieces and continuously refactoring (with TDD and prototyping) achieves the same.
- Make reversibility explicit: keep a lightweight document of reversible vs irreversible decisions, their cost and consequences, so irreversibility (e.g. being married to a DB via stored procedures) is a conscious choice, not an accident.
- Defer to the last responsible moment (not procrastination — that's past it) and apply YAGNI; decide based on economic impact (you're smarter tomorrow; cost-now vs cost-later vs certainty-of-need). The last responsible moment for language was day one; for the database it was deployment time.
- Extensibility needs domain + software knowledge (pair the domain expert with the dev): a product is "extensible" only until you ask it to extend in a way nobody anticipated. Prefer triangulation (Kent Beck) — implement two cases, then extract the commonality — and earn reuse through use (Booch: "make it usable first"), not up-front frameworks. Beware resume-driven development and a pile of libraries you must maintain.

title: DOES14 On the Care and Feeding of Feedback Cycles
description: This talk examines the many forms of feedback, the questions each can answer, and the risks each can mitigate. Agile practices involve testing early and often. However feedback comes in many forms, only some of which are traditionally considered testing. Continuous integration, acceptance testing with users, even cohort analysis to validate business hypotheses are all examples of feedback cycles.
notes:
Focusing on the **care and feeding of rapid feedback cycles** allows organizations to replace risky speculation with empirical evidence, ultimately transforming them into highly competitive learning organizations. By **automating processes** and driving testing to the lowest possible level, teams drastically reduce feedback latency and ensure their implementations truly align with actual user needs.

* **Feedback cycles** consist of doing a thing, observing the result, and adjusting (steering) accordingly based on what was learned.
* There are **three essential feedback loops** in development: checking if intentions match needs, if implementation matches intentions, and if the final implementation actually meets the need.
* **Feedback latency**—the time it takes to go from action to observation—directly determines project risk; reducing this time is the most effective way to manage uncertainty.
* **Empirical evidence trumps speculation** every time; requirements and design documents are merely forms of speculation until the software is running in production.
* The **"risk curve"** represents the accumulation of speculation over time; long cycles without validation create massive, hidden risks that lead to budget and schedule overruns.
* **"Fragile" agile** occurs when teams utilize "stabilization sprints," which act as mini-waterfalls that prevent software from being truly "done-done" and delay critical feedback.
* **Automation of "all the things"** is required to shorten cycles and free engineers from the manual, repetitive tasks that hinder exploration and rapid delivery.
* **Quality is a collective responsibility**; having a separate, siloed QA team creates weight states and communication gaps that break feedback loops.
* A healthy ecosystem requires **layered feedback**: local unit tests (first line of defense), automated CI, continuous story acceptance, exploratory testing, and production monitoring.
* **Product Managers should own story acceptance** and perform it as soon as a story is ready, rather than waiting for a sprint demo.
* **Exploratory testing** is vital for discovering unknown risks and ensuring the team is not "fooling themselves" with passing automated tests.
* The **"Tetris Principle of Testing"** involves driving tests to the lowest possible level (unit tests) to maximize execution speed and efficiency.
* **Visibility of feedback** is crucial; teams should be surrounded by real-time data via dashboards and monitors showing build health and production metrics.
* High-functioning feedback loops transform a company into a **"learning organization,"** which is the ultimate competitive advantage in a rapidly changing world.

title: Talking Serverless #27 - Gojko Adzic Partner at Neuri Consulting
What Does Tech Excellence Look Like?
description: Martin explores how culture, structures, talent, and technology choices help to build organisations that have technical excellence ingrained in them.
notes:
Achieving superior business performance requires prioritizing technical excellence in strategic areas through short delivery cycles and autonomous, business-aligned teams. Maintaining high software quality is essential to this success because it acts as the primary driver of speed and flexibility rather than a trade-off.

* **Technical excellence is a business imperative** because IT performance directly correlates with overall organizational performance and return on assets.
* **Cycle Time**—the period from idea conception to production—is the most critical measure, as fast cycles provide early feedback and allow for rapid experimentation.
* **Continuous Delivery** ensures software is always in a deployable state, turning the release of new features into a business decision rather than a technical milestone.
* **Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR)** is often more important than preventing failures; the ability to detect and fix issues rapidly is a hallmark of high-performing organizations.
* Teams should be **small ("Two Pizza Teams"), stable, and autonomous**, possessing all the skills necessary to deliver software from the user interface to the backend.
* **Business Orientation** is vital; teams should be organized around business functions rather than technical silos, and developers should have direct contact with their users.
* **Psychological safety** and trust are the most significant factors in team success, allowing members to share problems and knowledge without fear.
* Organizations must distinguish between **Strategic software** (which provides competitive differentiation) and **Utility software** (which should focus on cost-reduction).
* **Technical excellence must be prioritized in Strategic areas**, where value and speed should overwhelm concerns about cost.
* **Bimodal IT is a flawed concept** because strategic changes often require full-stack modifications, and separating "fast" frontends from "slow" backends creates bottlenecks.
* **High quality is a prerequisite for speed**, not a trade-off; technical practices like automated testing and modular design actually reduce bug counts and increase delivery velocity.

title: Testing and Refactoring Legacy Code
description: A very interesting refactoring session  conducted by Sandro. Lot of tips and tricks, some for Java and other for a lot of contexts and languages.
notes:
To successfully manage legacy code, developers should systematically build a safety net by testing the simplest execution paths first and then **refactoring from the most complex logic outward in small, incremental steps**. This disciplined approach reduces the risk of breaking functionality while transforming rigid, coupled systems into clean, maintainable code through a craftsmanship-focused mindset.

* **Definition of Legacy Code**: Code that is without tests or code that developers are afraid to touch because they do not understand the potential side effects of making changes.
* **Testing Strategy**: Approach legacy code by focusing on the **shallowest branches** first. Write tests for the simplest execution paths to build knowledge and a safety net incrementally before tackling the "deepest" logic.
* **Refactoring Strategy**: Once a safety net is established, refactor from the **inside out**, starting with the **deepest branch**. This often causes the surrounding code to simplify or "collapse" naturally.
* **The Seam Technique**: To test code with hardwired dependencies (like Singletons or static methods), create a "seam" by wrapping the dependency call in a protected method. You can then override this method in a test-specific subclass to inject mocks or stubs.
* **Golden Rule of Legacy Code**: Avoid changing production code manually until it is covered by tests. The only exception is using automated refactoring tools to create the seams necessary for testing.
* **Behavior-Driven Testing**: Name test methods based on the expected business behavior (e.g., `shouldReturnTripsWhenUsersAreFriends`) rather than the technical implementation. This makes tests act as documentation.
* **Builder Pattern for Tests**: Use the Builder pattern to manage complex object creation in tests. This keeps test setup readable and focuses the test on the specific data relevant to the scenario.
* **Variable Inlining**: To simplify complex methods, bring variables closer to where they are used and inline them. This removes "clutter" and helps break the method into distinct, manageable blocks of logic.
* **Addressing Feature Envy**: Identify logic that performs operations on another object's data and move that behavior into the object that actually owns the data.
* **Levels of Abstraction**: Maintain a "balanced" method where every line of code is at the same level of abstraction. High-level business rules should not be mixed with low-level implementation details like list initializations.
* **Small Increments**: Work in cycles of just a few minutes, running tests constantly and committing to version control frequently. This ensures you are never more than one "Undo" away from a green, working state.
* **Dependency Injection**: Use refactoring to transition from hidden global dependencies to explicit parameters. This improves design and makes the code naturally easier to test.
* **Craftsmanship Mindset**: Software craftsmanship is an attitude toward excellence and a commitment to using professional practices like TDD, simple design, and continuous integration to manage technical debt.

title: Chris Matts on BDD, Real Options, Risk Management and the Impact of Culture for Effective Outcomes
description: Chris Matts on real options as a risk-management technique, and using a four-point risk/governance framework to drive Agile cultural change in regulated enterprises.
notes:
Chris Matts (co-creator of the given/when/then format with Dan North, and of feature injection) frames Agile decision-making and scaling through real options — a risk-management technique — and argues you change an enterprise's culture by changing its governance framework, not by ignoring its need for one.

- Real options has three rules — options have value, options expire, never commit early unless you know why — plus a fourth: the value of options increases with uncertainty. Commit deliberately (Dan North), don't mistake making a decision for making progress.
- It's not mere procrastination: options expire, so manage decisions by their deadline. Prefer the last responsible moment, which is usually a bit before the "last irresponsible moment" (catch the last-but-one bus, in case the last one breaks down) — and the point of arriving early is having choices.
- A lightweight risk/governance framework (built with Tony Grout) that auditors can accept, with just four requirements: deliver value (executives see a metric graphed against the investment over time, and must justify continued spend), reduce lead time (to a couple of months), sustainable quality (demonstrably reducing technical debt), and manage risk (above all, transparency — present information so people can consume it).
- Use the culture to change the culture: forcing teams to deliver value (e.g. "increase customers") instead of features/widgets shifts them, in Cynefin terms, from complicated (controllable) to complex (uncertain) — deliberately injecting the uncertainty that high-uncertainty-avoidance, high-power-distance cultures (Hofstede) hate.
- Agile works fine at team level; the failure is "water-scrum-fall" — the up-front commitment above the team. So measure lead time across the whole value stream, not one team, and have teams collaborate to get value to production fast.
- The community has drifted to a "community of solutions"; scaling Agile needs the "community of needs" again — test ideas in new contexts, learn from failures where they break. A practice is Agile only if it honours the Manifesto (especially the first line: uncovering better ways by doing it) — don't sell things as Agile just because they sell.

title: Should Computers Run the World?
description: Hannah Fry takes us on a tour of the good, the bad and the downright ugly of the algorithms that surround us. She lifts the lid on their inner workings, to demonstrate their power, expose their limitations, and examine whether they really are an improvement on the humans they are replacing.
notes:
True progress comes from creating a partnership between humans and machines that exploits their respective strengths while remaining skeptical of their shared fallibilities. Instead of choosing between human judgment and algorithmic precision, we must learn to scrutinize technology as a fallible tool that requires constant human oversight and contextual understanding.

* Humans are **not uniquely talented** in complex tasks like medical diagnosis or creative composition; machines (and even animals) can be trained to match or exceed human performance in specific areas.
* Algorithms are essentially **logical processes** that take an input to produce an output, often relying on probability and patterns rather than true understanding.
* The critical challenge is not the technical design of algorithms, but the **human relationship** with them and how they are integrated into society.
* **Blind faith** in technology, often called automation bias, leads humans to follow machine instructions even when they contradict common sense or physical reality.
* While human decision-makers are **inconsistent and biased** by external factors (like hunger or mood), algorithms are also prone to **deep-hidden biases** and logical flaws.
* Algorithms lack **context and nuance**; they do not understand the world and can make calamitous mistakes when faced with data outside their training or simple changes in environment.
* Tools labeled as "AI" or "algorithms" often take on an **unearned air of authority**, making them difficult for people to question or scrutinize, even when they are fundamentally flawed.
* Machines excel at **sensitivity**, meaning they are highly effective at detecting tiny, obscure patterns across massive datasets that humans would miss.
* Humans excel at **specificity**, possessing a "superpower" for correctly identifying what is normal and avoiding the false positives that can plague automated systems.
* The ideal future is a **human-machine partnership** rather than a competition, where algorithms trawl data for concerns and humans provide the final, nuanced judgment.
* Technology should be treated like any other **source of power**, requiring constant questioning, accountability, and the acknowledgement of its inherent flaws.

title: Be Quick But Don't Hurry
description: Inspirational. These two minutes summarize very well why it is important to be fast but maintain control in order to be truly agile.
notes:
Mastering the balance between **quickness and haste** is essential for true agility, as moving quickly allows for speed and ease while maintaining control, whereas hurrying leads to being out of control and making mistakes. You should remember to operate on that **laser-thin edge** where you move as fast as possible without losing the grace and control necessary to avoid waste.

* **"Be Quick But Don't Hurry" Mantra**: A fundamental principle for success in both sports and business, emphasizing speed without losing composure.
* **Quickness vs. Haste**: Quickness is moving with ease, grace, and control. Haste (or hurrying) is being out of control, which leads to waste, errors, and potential harm.
* **Defining Agile**: In an agile context, being "agile" means being quick, easy, and graceful in execution.
* **The "Laser Thin Edge"**: There is a very fine line between moving at maximum speed and falling into haste. Finding this balance is a critical skill.
* **Maintaining Control**: Speed is valuable as long as you remain in control, even when pushed to the edge of your abilities. Once control is lost, quickness turns into waste-producing haste.
* **Practice**: Mastering the ability to be quick without hurrying is a difficult discipline that requires consistent practice.

title: How to Get People to Follow You
description: Inside Quest

title: No Vibes Allowed: Solving Hard Problems in Complex Codebases – Dex Horthy, HumanLayer
description: Dex Horthy explores advanced context engineering and the "Research, Plan, Implement" (RPI) workflow to effectively solve complex problems in brownfield codebases while minimizing AI-generated "slop" and maintaining team alignment.
notes:
Successfully solving complex problems in brownfield codebases requires **intentional context engineering** through a "Research, Plan, Implement" (RPI) workflow to keep AI agents in their high-performance "smart zone" and prevent the accumulation of low-quality "slop". This methodology emphasizes that humans must **not outsource their thinking**, but instead use compacted research and detailed plans to maintain mental alignment across the team and ensure high-leverage, reliable code execution.

- **Advanced context engineering** is essential for solving complex problems in brownfield codebases and avoiding the production of low-quality "slop".
- LLM performance drops significantly in the **"dumb zone,"** which begins when the context window reaches roughly 40% capacity; maintaining a small context window keeps the model in the "smart zone".
- **Intentional compaction** involves regularly compressing a conversation or codebase state into markdown files to give agents a clear, succinct starting point.
- The **Research, Plan, Implement (RPI) workflow** is a systematic framework designed to maintain objective truth, explicit intent, and low-context execution.
- **Sub-agents** should be utilized specifically for **context management**—such as searching a large codebase to return a single relevant file—rather than for anthropomorphizing team roles.
- **Planning provides leverage** when it includes explicit steps and **code snippets**, ensuring high confidence that the model will execute the intended changes correctly.
- A primary goal of using AI in teams is maintaining **mental alignment**; technical leaders should review AI-generated plans to ensure the entire team understands how the system is evolving.
- AI cannot replace thinking; it only **amplifies human intent**, meaning engineers must remain "in the loop" to validate research and plans before implementation.
- The future challenge for technical leadership is **adapting the SDLC and team culture** to a world where 99% of code may be AI-generated, preventing a rift between senior engineers and those using AI to fill skill gaps.

title: The Agile Revolution Podcast: Episode 180: Extreme Programming & 3X Explained with Kent Beck
description: The Agile Revolution podcast with Kent Beck on the birth of Extreme Programming and his 3X model (Explore, Expand, Extract) — why the rules of the game, even the value of courage, change with a product's phase.
notes:
On The Agile Revolution, Kent Beck — father of Extreme Programming — revisits XP's origins and lays out his later 3X model, arguing that good practice depends on which phase of value a product is in.

- XP was born (from his Tektronix Labs days, ~1984) by comparing what genuinely effective developers actually did against what the books on "proper, grown-up software engineering" prescribed; today people do XP practices (JUnit, TDD) without realizing it's XP, and Scrum simply "marketed it better."
- The 3X model — Explore, Expand, Extract — says the rules of the game change by phase: Explore looks for a new source of value, Expand is when you've found one growing faster than you can keep up, and Extract is steady growth that funds the next round of exploration.
- Even XP's own values are phase-dependent: courage is awesome when exploring (it finds value others chicken out of), but the last thing you want when extracting from a successful business with growth left — there, testing, refactoring and automation pay off because you'll live with the code a long time.
- He'd rather his work be known than his name: the measure of impact is that the practices live on — and the nostalgia people feel for their one great XP team says something about what those practices made possible.

title: AWS re:Invent 2023 - Do modern cloud applications lock you in?
description: This talk reframes modern cloud application lock-in as switching costs, explaining how to manage them through architecture trade-offs, increasing developer velocity, and focusing on design intent rather than just service selection
notes:
The most important lesson conveyed is that perceived cloud "lock-in" should be reframed as manageable **switching costs**, which are most effectively reduced by focusing on core engineering disciplines like increasing organizational **velocity** and maintaining clear **software design intent** and meaningful **architecture decisions**, rather than solely relying on simple service mapping or abstraction layers. The ultimate takeaway is that having development discipline, expressing design intent, high velocity, and good automation mean switching costs won't be your biggest problem; capturing market opportunity will be.

Here are the most important points of the presentation:
- Modern cloud applications are defined by their effective utilization of cloud capabilities, being fine-grained, loosely coupled, often event-driven, and offering benefits like resilience, scalability, transparency, agility, and observability.
- The perceived "lock-in" of modern cloud applications should be reframed not as being literally locked up, but as **manageable switching costs** – a financial consideration involving effort, time, and money to move from one solution to another.
- Lock-in/switching costs are **multidimensional**, including vendor lock-in, but also switching costs related to product versions, skillsets, and even **mental lock-in**. Architecture is about seeing these multiple dimensions and making **trade-offs**, not a simple good vs. evil scenario.
- A powerful architectural maneuver is to break down problems into more dimensions (like using a two-dimensional matrix comparing **utility vs. switching cost**) and broadening the solution space.
- **Solving problems where they originate** is crucial; trying to solve application development problems like portability solely at the infrastructure level after the fact is ineffective.
- Simple approaches to managing switching costs often **don't work well**:
    - **Service mapping** across clouds fails because clouds are not carbon copies and their services have fundamental, non-matching details and integrations.
    - Basic **abstraction layers** (the "grim wrapper") are also ineffective for fine-grained distributed systems because logical layers cannot abstract **physical properties** such as pricing models, quotas, scalability limits, error handling, and service availability across different platforms.
- The **option to move** your workload is mathematically valuable but comes at a **cost**, including effort, complexity (which is very hard to undo), and crucially, **opportunity cost** (what else your developers could have been building).
- Wanting all options all the time ("Gregor's law") leads to drowning in complexity. The goal is not to eliminate switching costs entirely (like an option with a zero strike price or zero-deductible insurance) but to **minimize the _total_ cost** (investment now + future liability).
- Organizations aiming for agility should make decisions assuming today's dollars (investment now) are more expensive than future dollars (potential switching cost liability), prioritizing current investment in productivity and market opportunity.
- **Productivity comes before portability**. If you're not productive, the question of portability likely becomes irrelevant because your business may not succeed. Open source is great but often undersells itself by leading with portability rather than community, openness, and productivity benefits.
- There are effective approaches to manage switching costs:
    - Using **managed open source services**, which offer utility while keeping switching costs in check.
    - Significantly increasing **your own organizational velocity**. High velocity through automation, reduced friction, and smaller iterations dramatically reduces switching costs, potentially by orders of magnitude, regardless of the technology used. Velocity is a powerful dial within your control.
    - Maintaining clear **software design intent** and focusing on architecture as the sequence of meaningful decisions, rather than just service selection. Expressing intent using patterns and decisions (like in the Scatter-Gather example) prevents **mental lock-in** to one platform's way of thinking and provides a better starting point if you need to switch.
- Ultimately, if you have **development discipline**, express design intent with patterns, have high velocity, and good automation, the switching cost isn't going to be your biggest problem; **capturing market opportunity and maintaining velocity** will be the critical focus.

title: Complex Adaptive Systems
description: Inspiring talk about Complex Adaptive Systems and what are the most effective approaches to deal with them.
notes:
To navigate complex adaptive systems effectively, you must shift from rigid, linear modeling toward a **discovery architecture** that utilizes small, safe-to-fail experiments to interact with the system and uncover emergent, beneficial opportunities [1-3]. This approach prioritizes **exaptation**—the radical repurposing of existing capabilities—and distributed interpretation over centralized, engineering-style control to better manage inherent uncertainty and foster innovation [4-6].

* **Language and Perception:** Language determines how we see the world. Humans suffer from inattentional bias and generally only see what they expect to see; they often miss even large anomalies if they are not looking for them.
* **Typology vs. Taxonomy:** Taxonomies are often over-rigid structures where boundary conditions become dangerous. Typologies provide different perspectives to make sense of the world without rigid classification.
* **Models and Frameworks:** A model seeks to represent the world, but the only valid model of a human system is the system itself. Frameworks should be used as typologies to make distinctions and allow for different ways of acting.
* **Scaffolding:** Scaffolding is a means to an end, not the endpoint. In IT and design, there is a tendency to treat the scaffolding (methods and rituals) as the goal rather than removing it once the objective is met.
* **Chefs vs. Recipe Users:** "Recipe book users" follow instructions but lack the resilience to adjust when things go wrong. "Chefs" combine theoretical knowledge with practical apprenticeship, providing the resilience needed to adapt to changing conditions.
* **Exaptation:** Radical innovation often comes from exaptation—the repurposing of a trait or technology that evolved for one function for a completely different one (e.g., feathers for warmth becoming wings for flight, or radar tech becoming the microwave oven).
* **Complex Adaptive Systems (CAS):** These systems lack linear causality; they are dispositional rather than predictable. In a CAS, doing "X" will not always produce "Y."
* **Safe-to-Fail Experiments:** Because complex systems have unintended consequences, they should be managed through multiple, small, parallel "safe-to-fail" experiments to see what works before committing to a single path.
* **The Cynefin Framework:**
    * **Obvious:** Clear cause and effect; apply best practice.
    * **Complicated:** Cause and effect require expert analysis; apply good practice.
    * **Complex:** No linear causality; patterns emerge through probing and sensing.
    * **Chaotic:** No effective constraints; act immediately to establish order and find novelty.
* **Bureaucracy and Workarounds:** Over-constraining a system leads to "workarounds." If rules are too rigid for the context, people will find unofficial ways to bypass them to get the job done.
* **Discovery vs. Delivery Architecture:** Architecture should move beyond just delivering what users ask for (articulated needs) to a "discovery architecture" that surfaces unarticulated needs through high-abstraction metadata and observation of day-to-day frustrations.
* **Scaling via Decomposition:** Do not scale a complex system by aggregation or imitation. Instead, decompose the system into the lowest coherent objects, define protocols for their interaction, and allow novel patterns to emerge through recombination.
* **Fractal Engagement:** Effective change is achieved when individuals make decisions about what they can do tomorrow within their own sphere of competence, allowing the system to orient through multiple distributed actions.
* **Distributed Interpretation:** To understand a human system, the power of interpretation must be handed back to the people themselves. Avoid having experts or AI centrally interpret the narratives of others.

title: Microservices and Rules Engines – a blast from the past
description: Good insights about the real meaning of delete in different domains and about how to design solution to model complicated services as search service, price service, risk service, that require to process information from different subdomains.
notes:
**True architectural agility is achieved by treating microservices as logical boundaries focused on data encapsulation rather than units of deployment, allowing components from multiple services to be co-deployed into generic engines to solve complex business problems without creating tight coupling. Success requires moving beyond remote calls to embrace a model where services own their data entirely and plug specialized logic into shared infrastructure for tasks like search, pricing, and fraud detection.**

* **Microservices vs. SOA:** Microservices are essentially a rebranding of Service Oriented Architecture (SOA). The goal remains building complex, enterprise-grade software by focusing on logical decoupling rather than just technical implementation.
* **The Coupling Fallacy:** Moving code into separate processes, using Docker/Kubernetes, or calling services via JSON/HTTP does not eliminate coupling. It often just "spreads the mess around" without improving software quality.
* **Logical Coupling and Layers:** Traditional layered architectures (Database, Domain, API, UI) create tight vertical coupling. A change in one field requires updates across every layer, which is inherently unmaintainable.
* **Vertical Slices:** Instead of technical layers, systems should be split into vertical slices based on business logic (e.g., price, inventory, ratings). A single screen or process can be a composition of components from multiple services.
* **Complete Data Encapsulation:** Services must own their data entirely. They should not share databases or transmit volatile business data via API calls or events. Communication should be limited to simple identifiers.
* **Services as Logical Constructs:** A service is not a unit of deployment. A single service may have components deployed across multiple processes (e.g., a UI component, a backend component, and a search rule), and a single process may host components from multiple services.
* **The Rules Engine Pattern:** Complex business requirements that span multiple domains (like search, customer-specific pricing, and fraud detection) often force tight coupling. This can be solved by using a generic rules engine pattern.
* **Generic Infrastructure:** An engine (like a pricing or search engine) should be a generic piece of infrastructure that defines interfaces. Individual services then "plug in" their specific logic components to these interfaces.
* **Order Independence:** Effective rules engines use logic that is independent of execution order, such as multiplicative factors for pricing or additive scores for fraud detection, allowing services to remain autonomous.
* **Dynamic Composition:** At runtime, engines should scan directories for assemblies that implement specific interfaces. This allows developers to add or change business rules by simply dropping in new service components without re-architecting the core engine.

title: Snow White and the 777.777.777 Dwarfs
description: Great talks about how cloud is changing the risk profile of our application and the software quality practices.
notes:
As cloud ecosystems and third-party dependencies shift software risk from internal code units to external integrations, teams must evolve beyond the traditional testing pyramid toward **active production monitoring** and **visual, approval-based verification**. This transition acknowledges that in a world of commodity infrastructure and algorithmic users, the most critical risks often emerge only after a release is live.

*   **Shift in Risk Profiles:** The transition from controlled, "immortal" hardware to commodity cloud and serverless ecosystems means software must be designed to be "broken" all the time, as infrastructure is no longer under the developer's direct control.
*   **Algorithmic Users:** As APIs and marketplaces grow, software users are increasingly algorithms rather than humans. Algorithms can enter self-reinforcing loops—such as automated pricing wars or high-frequency trading—that human-centric testing heuristics cannot predict.
*   **Difficulty of Asserting Correctness:** With the rise of machine learning, image recognition, and voice interfaces, the traditional practice of asserting a single "correct" result is failing. Results are often probabilistic, making standard verification difficult.
*   **Integration vs. Unit Testing:** Risk has shifted away from individual code units toward the integration of third-party services. If a product is mostly a "mashup" of external APIs, testing the internal code unit provides little value compared to testing the integration points.
*   **Post-Deployment Risks:** Major risks now occur after release. Changes in third-party APIs or cloud environments (e.g., a Google Drive update) can break software without any changes being made to the internal codebase.
