It has an identity ("bottom") value, and operations called "inflations" (e.g., add to a set, increment/decrement a counter). The payoff is "strong eventual consistency": predictable semantics that never create conflicts under unreliable networks or multiple actors, without the latency/coordination cost of Paxos or Raft. He notes Basho's involvement in the European FP7 research project SyncFree, slogan "large-scale computation without synchronization."

* **Riak data modeling principles**: Structure data so a read returns everything needed in ideally one fetch of a single key; design around the key as the index; de-normalize (put more in a single key rather than spread across keys); and weigh data size against how tightly the data coheres. These apply to CRDTs too, with more flexibility.

* **Riak 2.0 data types**: Targeted for "this summer" (with betas available). Counters (increment/decrement, positive or negative; present since Riak 1.4); sets (add/remove string or blob elements, no duplicates); and maps (a dictionary from byte keys to associated convergent data types, nestable to any depth). Two types usable only inside maps: registers (a single cell of bytes, last-write-wins) and flags (on/off booleans, starting off). Maps support batching any number of nested updates/removes in a single round trip.

* **Observed-remove semantic and context**: Not all operations commute — set add and remove do not. The data types use an "observed-remove" semantic that prefers added items, so removing requires a logical token called the "context" (analogous to the KV-side vector clock). Removing something you have no context for is rejected: "Riak will say bye-bye, no, I can't remove that."

* **Counter use case (Roger, online advertising)**: Tracks impressions and click-throughs per ad campaign, partitioned by hour. Uses a three-segment namespace (bucket type → bucket → key); creates a bucket type with property data_type counter, activates it once every node has seen it. Counters need no context, so he creates a new counter and increments without a prior fetch. Roll-ups are read across a key range. His data showed people click through more during the lunch hour.

* **Set use case (Joan, PartyOn event app)**: Models RSVPs, friend connections, and likes using sets under one set-typed bucket type, separated by bucket name. Adds need no context; she uses return_body=True on store to get back the full set plus context, sending the context to the client so a user can undo an accidental RSVP via a remove tied to their own add — avoiding conflicts with concurrent adds by others.

* **Map use case (Dom and Betty, gaming network)**: Models user profiles (with flags for feature toggles, blanket-assigned registers for simple fields), per-game trophies (a set inside a map, retrievable in one fetch), and game state. The world-builder game state example — adding a structure to a set while decrementing gold, wood, and stone counters in one stored map — "is basically the example that we were given by one of our customers that inspired the development of the map about a year ago." Matchmaking uses Solr search, which integrates directly with data types so no schema is needed; an asteroids-game index lets a user find opponents with scores between their own and infinity.

* **Benefits and trade-offs**: The biggest benefit is writing mutations rather than new state — "here's what I want you to do on my behalf" — analogous to issuing SQL to a relational database, with no ad hoc merge function to write. Trade-offs: value size still matters (large values incur latency and clog network buffers); updates to data types are not idempotent (operations aren't uniquely tagged at the client, so running a function twice changes the result); and cross-key atomicity isn't possible yet. Both the idempotency and cross-key problems are part of the SyncFree research.

* **Q&A highlights**: No support yet for clients acting as offline replicas/caches — the server uses a state-based interface while clients send accrued operations; making clients replicas explodes garbage accumulation and raises hard unique-identifier problems (related to the idempotency issue). No practical upper bound on set size beyond the value-size caveat (linear relationship between items and storage). Data types are stored as specially tagged values on top of the existing key-value storage. Maps cannot go inside sets (a set element is its own identity, so the map couldn't be manipulated in place), but maps can go inside maps. Counters cannot be reset to zero directly; inside a map you can effectively reset by removing the field (a "reset-remove" semantic where seen increments drop but concurrent ones survive). On whether you need Paxos/Raft, Cribbs cites Peter Bailis and colleagues at UC Berkeley, noting more use cases tolerate loose coordination than require strict coordination, and that they scaled the TPC-C benchmark "basically without bound, like linearly" because only one operation (get the next ID) needed coordination. He credits the closing picture to Kyle Kingsbury.

title: Habits, Practices & Strategy For Building Viral Products
description: A discussion on the habits, practices and strategy for building products that are extremely viral and able to catch the attention of your target market, so you can grow your business faster.
notes:
Building viral growth is a strategic design choice rather than a gimmick, achieved by creating inherently social or team-oriented products that leverage network effects and deep customer obsession. Success depends on making a product "customer aware" so that its value increases naturally as users invite others into the experience through self-service and collaborative features.

* **Virality as a Core Strategy:** Virality is not a marketing gimmick or a secondary feature; it is a fundamental product strategy built into the user experience to drive rapid, organic growth.
* **Traction vs. Virality:** A clear distinction must be made between general traction (getting users) and true virality (users bringing in more users through the product's natural use).
* **Social and Team-Based Design:** Products should be designed to be inherently social or built for team collaboration (like Slack or Calendly) to trigger natural growth loops.
* **Network Effects:** The product should become more valuable to every user as the total number of users increases, creating a compounding benefit for the network.
* **Incentivized Loops:** Utilizing referral systems and reward-based invitations (as seen with Uber) to lower acquisition costs and turn users into advocates.
* **Product-Led Promotion:** Leveraging the product's output to advertise itself, such as the famous Hotmail viral signature that turned every sent email into a promotion.
* **Exclusivity and Scarcity:** Using invitation-only models (like OnePlus) to create demand and curiosity within specific market segments.
* **Self-Service Adoption:** Reducing friction by allowing users to sign up and experience the product's value immediately without needing manual onboarding or sales intervention.
* **Customer Obsession:** Maintaining a deep awareness of customer needs and social behaviors to ensure viral mechanics feel natural rather than forced.
* **Customer-Aware Features:** Building features that acknowledge the user's social or professional context to facilitate easy sharing and interaction with non-users.

title: Interview of Kevin Kelly, Co-Founder of WIRED, Polymath, Most Interesting Man In The World?
description: Esta es una entrevista recomendada por @Pasku que me ha resultado bastante enriquecedora. Entiendo que en su caso está bastante alineada con lo que pretende hacer como nómada digital.

title: Six Decades of Software Engineering
description:
Great talk with the evolution of our field from a lean perspective. Great insights about the engineering role, agile, the current painful division between business and development, how we can think about complex systems, the failure of having proxy roles as the product owner, etc... Great talk, lot of computer and development history, and great Q&A session.
Slides: <https://speakerdeck.com/devopslx/2020-dot-07-meetup-talk-may-poppendieck-six-decades-of-software-engineering>
notes:
Effective software engineering is defined by a **philosophy of responsibility**, where multi-disciplinary teams focus on achieving overall system outcomes and maintaining a rapid flow of value rather than adhering to rigid processes or relying on organizational proxies. Building for the future requires prioritizing **architectural resilience and adaptability**, ensuring that systems and teams are prepared to respond quickly and effectively to unpredictable "black swan" events.

*   **Architecture as a Foundation:** Breakthrough innovations in software engineering are almost always driven by novel architectural concepts that challenge established norms.
*   **Conway's Law:** Systems architecture and organizational architecture are mirrors of each other; if they do not align, the system will eventually force them into congruence.
*   **Principles of Serious Scale:** Reliability at scale requires three key elements: redundancy, isolation, and local responsibility. Centralized control is a vulnerability that leads to cascading failures.
*   **The Philosophy of Responsibility:** Engineering excellence is rooted in the "Responsible Engineer" model, where individuals and teams are accountable for their components working correctly and understanding how those components serve the mission of the entire system.
*   **Flow over Resource Efficiency:** Optimizing for the flow of value (small batches, rapid movement) is more effective than optimizing for resource efficiency. Large batches in software lead to "spoilage," similar to perishable goods.
*   **Dependency as a Bottleneck:** Dependencies are the greatest barrier to flow. Transitioning from centralized "systems of record" to distributed data stores and microservices is essential for independent deployment and speed.
*   **Cathedral vs. Bazaar:** The "Bazaar" model of development (independent actors, local responsibility, open source) has proven more resilient and scalable than the "Cathedral" model (centralized planning and standard processes).
*   **The Problem with Proxies:** Intermediaries like "Product Owners" or "Business Analysts" often create a gap between engineers and the actual problem. Engineers should have direct contact with customers to develop both technical and product solutions.
*   **Resilience and Adaptability:** Instead of trying to build systems that never break, organizations should focus on resilience—the ability to respond and rebuild rapidly when "Black Swan" events occur.
*   **Outcome-Oriented Teams:** Future success depends on chartering multi-disciplinary teams with specific outcomes and purposes rather than focusing on tools, technology, or standard processes.
*   **Implementing Change:** Successful organizational change occurs when management sets a clear vision and urgency, but allows frontline workers to design the detailed implementation of new systems.
*   **Abstraction and Complexity:** Complexity expands to fit the latest abstraction. Higher-level languages and tools do not eliminate the need for skilled programmers; they simply allow those programmers to manage higher levels of complexity.

title: Programer Anarchy
description: Interesting talk about self organization of a highly motivated technical teams, working together with the business people.
notes:
Software organizations can achieve exceptional success by replacing traditional management and rigid processes with a culture of "programmer anarchy," where self-organizing teams are trusted to drive development based on direct business metrics. By prioritizing rapid experimentation and continuous deployment over estimates and bureaucratic oversight, companies can eliminate the fear of failure and empower developers to innovate at an unprecedented pace.

* **Cynefin Framework Application**: The "Programmer Anarchy" model is specifically designed for the **Complex** domain, where cause and effect are not predictable and can only be understood in hindsight.
* **Elimination of Roles**: All specialized roles—including managers, project managers, business analysts, and testers—are removed. The only remaining role is **Developer**.
* **Self-Organization (Anarchy)**: True anarchy is defined as self-organization from within rather than organization imposed from the outside. Developers do not ask for permission and no one has the authority to tell them "no."
* **Resource Rumble**: Work is assigned through a process where business leaders communicate priorities, and the programmers themselves decide how to distribute their talent across projects based on who has the best skills for the specific task (e.g., databases, UI, performance).
* **Business-Centric Success**: Success is measured by **business metrics** (e.g., "did we make more money yesterday?") rather than technical metrics like lines of code, function points, or velocity.
* **Microservices Architecture**: The system is built using hundreds or thousands of **tiny services** (averaging 100 lines of code). This allows for extreme decoupling and technology flexibility.
* **Rewrite Over Refactor**: Because services are so small, developers **rewrite** code instead of refactoring it. If a service is no longer optimal or the developer wants to try a new language, they simply replace it.
* **Continuous Deployment**: High-frequency deployment where the majority of commits go live instantly (e.g., every four minutes) without manual approval or a traditional QA gate.
* **Abandoning "Crutches"**: Traditional Agile/XP practices like iterations, standups, story narratives, and estimates are discarded because they are often used to assign blame or manage fear rather than drive results.
* **Polyglot Programming**: Developers choose the language best suited for the problem (e.g., Ruby, Clojure, Node.js, C++), fostering high motivation and technical innovation.
* **Experimentation Culture**: Innovation is driven by constant experimentation where **failure is expected**. Two-thirds of developers may be working on experiments at any given time.
* **Conflict Resolution**: Disagreements are settled through **contests of execution**. If two developers disagree on an approach, both can write the code and let live business metrics determine which one performs better.
* **Hiring by Peers**: Only programmers hire programmers. The focus is on finding **self-learners** who have a bias toward delivery and technical curiosity.
* **Trust over Process**: The model requires a massive "leap of faith" and a high-trust relationship between the business/funding sources and the development team.

title: the deep synergy between testability and good design
description: Interesting talk about how to do good software design and the relationship between this design and the ease of testing.
notes:
**Difficulties encountered during testing serve as concrete evidence of underlying design flaws, highlighting that testability and good design are deeply synergistic.** Rather than viewing testing as an independent hurdle, developers should use testing pain as a diagnostic tool to identify and refactor poor architecture into a more modular and maintainable state.

* **Deep Synergy between Testability and Good Design:** If code is difficult to test, it is almost always due to an underlying design problem. A design that is truly "good" is inherently testable.
* **Testing Pain as a Diagnostic Tool:** Concrete difficulties encountered while writing tests (like difficult setup or global state issues) serve as immediate feedback that the design needs improvement.
* **The Problem of "Iceberg Classes":** Classes with a small public interface and massive private internals often violate the **Single Responsibility Principle**. Extracting these internal responsibilities into new classes makes them public and testable without polluting the original class's API.
* **Long Methods and Hidden State:** Methods exceeding 5–10 lines typically handle multiple responsibilities. Breaking them down into smaller, orthogonal pieces makes state more visible and testing simpler.
* **Coupling and Constructor Bloat:** When instantiating an object for a test requires bringing in half the application, the class has too much coupling. Factoring code into smaller classes with fewer dependencies resolves this.
* **Global Mutable State (Singletons):** Global state leads to state leaks across tests, causing inconsistent results. Testing makes the inherent danger of global variables and Singletons tangible.
* **Separation of Concerns:** Framework frustration often indicates that domain logic is too tightly coupled with the framework. Separating computation from delivery (GUI, Database, Framework) improves both design and testability.
* **Law of Demeter Violations:** "Chain mocking" (mocking an object to return another mock, etc.) indicates pervasive dependencies. Code should generally only operate on objects it directly owns.
* **Dependency Inversion Principle:** Difficulty substituting a component for a mock indicates a dependence on concrete classes rather than abstractions.
* **Open-Closed Principle and "Test Thrash":** When minor code changes cause widespread test failures, the design likely lacks locality of concerns. Adhering to the Open-Closed Principle creates a more stable and less brittle test suite.
* **Cognitive Alignment:** Good design follows cognitive principles. Smaller, focused pieces are easier for the human brain to reason about and easier for automated tests to verify.
* **TDD as a Natural Optimizer:** Projects using **Test-Driven Development** naturally trend toward lower cyclomatic complexity and smaller methods/classes, even without explicit design instructions.
* **The Pitfall of "Groping" Tools:** Tools that allow testing private methods act as a "get out of jail free card" that allows developers to ignore design flaws rather than fixing them through refactoring.
* **Core Conclusion:** Testing is not inherently hard; it is only hard in the presence of poor design. If you struggle to test something, you should **reconsider the design** rather than the test.

title: An introduction to Cynefin and related awesome thinking tools
description: Liz Keogh's introduction to Cynefin (Dave Snowden's sense-making framework) and the thinking tools around it: matching your approach to the domain, deliberate discovery and real options for acting under uncertainty.
notes:
Cynefin is a sense-making framework that classifies situations by the relationship between cause and effect, so you can match your approach to the domain instead of treating everything the same. Most knowledge work lives in the complex domain, where you must probe with safe-to-fail experiments rather than analyze — and human cognitive bias is what trips us up.

- Five domains (not quadrants — fuzzy borders, movement between them): obvious (cause/effect clear, sense-categorize-respond), complicated (needs expertise, analyzable, cause/effect correlated — the watchmaker/mechanic), complex (cause/effect only clear in hindsight, emergent), chaotic (act first), and disorder in the middle (not knowing which applies — which decays into chaos).
- Commoditized requirements vs differentiators: a camera phone must still make calls (commoditized, won't fail); features that are new are where discovery happens. Differentiators get "spoiled" and commoditized over time (Nokia copying cameras).
- Complex = emergent outcomes you couldn't predict: Flickr emerged from a game's screenshot-sharing site (Ludicorp), Sage from an astrology site's payment gateway. The response is probe → sense → respond with safe-to-fail experiments.
- Chaos = act-sense-respond, fast, not safe-to-fail (a burning house; Knight Capital's $440M deploy to 8 of 9 servers). You throw constraints around the problem to climb back into complexity. Beware the complacency cliff from obvious straight into chaos.
- A simple 1-5 scale (who has done this before? nobody in the world → everyone on the team) maps work onto the domains. Risk and value both concentrate in the 4s and 5s — so do those first, yet the whole industry pushes them to the end of projects, where the discoveries then bite (the dev weekend).
- Why complexity dominates software: we only do risky, novel projects (same tech/people/market again never happens). The hardest and best thing about software is the same — people seeing patterns: apophenia/pareidolia (the "face on Mars") and 150+ cognitive biases make us decide badly under uncertainty.
- Tools for acting under uncertainty: Dan North's deliberate discovery (assume second-order ignorance — unknown unknowns — and optimize for discovery), and Chris Matts's real options (options have value and expire; never commit early unless you know why; extend the last responsible moment; when in doubt pick the choice that's easy to change, e.g. a throwaway persistence layer; rollback turns a release into an experiment).
- The "shallow dive into chaos" generates experiments by gathering ideas from diverse people separately (silent work), because groups reach consensus badly (following the first or most dominant voice).

title: Applied Performance Theory
description: **This talk provides a practical guide to performance modeling, using queuing theory and scalability laws to analyze system behavior, optimize response times, and plan capacity for both single servers and clusters.**
notes:
Applying performance theory alongside empirical testing provides a rigorous framework to predict system behavior, identify scaling bottlenecks like contention and crosstalk, and avoid common pitfalls like underestimating response times during load simulations. By accurately modeling systems as either open or closed, engineers can better understand capacity needs and prioritize the specific optimizations that will most effectively improve scalability and user experience.

* **Performance Modeling Basics**: This theoretical approach involves representing real-world systems with mathematical models to predict response times, capacity needs, and server utilization without the cost of physical testing.
* **The "Hockey Stick" Effect**: According to queueing theory, response times remain relatively flat at low utilization but increase exponentially toward infinity as server utilization approaches 100%.
* **Queue Management Strategies**: To improve mean response time during overloads, systems can use reactive methods like setting maximum queue lengths, client-side back-offs, or uncommon approaches like CoDel (Controlled Delay) and LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) processing.
* **Service Time Optimization**: The PK (Pollaczek-Khinchine) formula indicates that queuing delay can be reduced organically by decreasing average service time (optimizing code) or reducing service time variability (batching or standardizing request sizes).
* **Open vs. Closed Systems**:
    * **Open Systems**: Requests are independent and random (e.g., standard web servers). Response time can grow unbounded.
    * **Closed Systems**: There is a feedback loop with a fixed number of clients (e.g., IoT sensors waiting for an ACK). Throughput is limited by response time, and response time grows linearly rather than exponentially.
* **Simulation Pitfalls**: Most standard load simulators mimic closed systems. If the actual production system is an open system, these simulations may significantly under-predict real-world response times.
* **Universal Scalability Law (USL)**: Systems rarely scale linearly when adding servers due to two main penalties:
    * **Contention**: Serialization of tasks and competition for shared resources (e.g., database locks).
    * **Crosstalk**: The coordination and consistency overhead required for servers to communicate with one another.
* **Retrograde Scalability**: In cases of high coordination overhead (crosstalk), adding more servers to a cluster can actually cause total system throughput to decrease.
* **Scaling Improvements**: Scalability can be enhanced by implementing smarter data partitioning, using aggregation trees to parallelize workloads, and employing fine-grained locking or MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control).
* **Practical Application**: Performance modeling is most effective when used alongside empirical methods like load simulation to interpret experimental results, validate system behavior, and prioritize high-win architectural changes.

title: Project to Product: How Value Stream Networks Will Transform IT & Business
description: How to Survive and Thrive in the Age of Digital Disruption using Product mindset (instead of project mindset) and the flow framework.
notes:
To thrive in the age of digital disruption, organizations must transition from project-based management and siloed proxy metrics to a product-centric **"Flow Framework"** that aligns business strategy directly with software delivery. Success requires bridging the communication gap between leadership and technology by focusing on the end-to-end flow of **features, defects, risks, and debt** to ensure sustainable innovation and long-term organizational health.

* **The Core Conflict:** Traditional enterprise IT suffers from a "broken layer" where business leaders manage via projects and cost centers while technology teams attempt to work via products and flow. This disconnect leads to failed digital transformations.
* **The Flow Framework:** A common language designed to bridge the gap between business strategy and technology delivery by measuring the flow of business value.
* **Four Flow Items:** All work in software delivery can be categorized into four mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive items:
    * **Features:** New business value pulled by the customer.
    * **Defects:** Quality improvements pulled by the customer.
    * **Risks:** Security, compliance, and availability work.
    * **Debts:** Technical debt and architectural improvements needed to sustain future velocity.
* **The Zero-Sum Game:** There is a finite capacity for flow. Over-prioritizing features at the expense of debt and risks eventually leads to a "death spiral," where defects rise and the ability to deliver new value collapses.
* **Shift from Project to Product:** Organizations must move away from temporary "projects" with fixed start/end dates and instead fund stable, long-lived "product value streams" that align with business results.
* **Key Flow Metrics:**
    * **Flow Velocity:** The number of flow items completed in a given time.
    * **Flow Time:** The total time elapsed from the moment work enters the value stream to its completion (taking the customer's perspective).
    * **Flow Efficiency:** The ratio of active work time to total lead time, identifying where work is sitting in wait states.
    * **Flow Load:** The amount of work in progress; high flow load is a leading indicator of burnout and decreased productivity.
* **Value Stream Architecture:** Just as software has an architecture, the "value stream network" (the tools and processes connecting specialists) must be intentionally architected to remove bottlenecks and dependencies.
* **Economic Turning Point:** We are currently in a "turning point" of the Age of Software. Companies that fail to master the new means of production (product-centric flow) face a "mass extinction event" similar to previous industrial revolutions.
* **BMW Analogy:** Modern software delivery should mirror advanced manufacturing plants (like BMW), where the entire physical and organizational structure is optimized for the continuous flow of products, not the completion of siloed tasks.
* **Empowering Teams:** Delivery teams should be empowered to set their own "Flow Distribution"—deciding how much capacity to allocate to debt and risk reduction versus new features to ensure long-term sustainability.

title: GOTO 2016 The Future of Software Engineering
description: Mary Poppendieck explores the transition from monolithic systems to **federated architectures** and **experimental, problem-solving teams** within a resilient, cloud-driven engineering culture.
notes:
To navigate the future of software engineering, organizations must shift from centralized, monolithic systems to federated architectures and autonomous, problem-solving teams that embrace continuous delivery and experiment-driven learning. This transition requires moving away from the pursuit of "defect-free" code toward building resilient, antifragile systems that can thrive within the distributed and uncertain environment of the cloud.

**Scaling and Architecture**
*   **Scale Wide, Not Tall:** Modern scaling involves building systems of computers operating in parallel managed by software, rather than simply getting a larger single computer.
*   **Federated Architecture:** Move away from monolithic databases, which act as central dependency generators, toward federated architectures with local data persistence to enable faster deployment [3-5].
*   **APIs as Products:** Treat APIs as the primary integrators rather than central databases; APIs should be hardened, versioned, and owned by specific teams [6-9].
*   **Stateless and Asynchronous:** Modern systems should prioritize stateless protocols and asynchronous, event-driven communication to handle the frequent failures inherent in cloud environments [9-12].

**Infrastructure and Cloud**
*   **Cloud Inevitability:** Economic and technical advantages make the cloud cheaper, more stable, and more secure than on-premise data centers, even for highly regulated industries like banking and medicine [13-15].
*   **Infrastructure as Code:** Utilize technologies like containers for standardized environments, software-defined networks, and infrastructure as code to manage distributed systems [16-19].

**Organization and Teams**
*   **Two-Pizza Teams:** Organize into small, autonomous teams (approx. 8 people) that own a service "cradle to grave," including design, testing, deployment, and production monitoring.
*   **Reduced Communication:** Use architecture to minimize the need for inter-team communication and dependencies, allowing for independent deployment without constant coordination [22-24].
*   **Problem-Solving Teams:** Transition from "delivery teams" that take orders to "problem-solving teams" that are given metrics and constraints and are responsible for finding the best solution [25-27].

**Resilience and Process**
*   **Antifragility:** Shift from seeking a "defect-free" state to building "antifragile" systems that recover quickly and grow stronger when exposed to stress or failure [28-31].
*   **Chaos Engineering:** Test resilience by intentionally killing production instances or injecting latencies to ensure the system can survive real-world failures.
*   **Continuous Delivery:** Implement a pipeline with automated builds, testing, and deployments; utilize trunk-based development with no branching to keep code always production-ready [33-35].
*   **Deployment vs. Release:** Decouple deployment (moving code to production) from release (turning a feature on for users via a switch) to reduce risk.

**Innovation and Design**
*   **Design Sprints:** Use five-day sprints to prototype and test ideas with real users before writing any code to avoid wasting development resources [37-40].
*   **Avoid Conformity Bias:** Use individual sketching and parallel idea generation rather than group brainstorming to ensure minority or "fragile" ideas are heard [40-43].
*   **Maintain Options:** Pursue a variety of ideas simultaneously and maintain multiple options as long as possible before settling on a final design.

title: Why Leaders Eat Last
description: This talk explores how great leaders foster trust and high performance by creating a **"circle of safety"** through self-sacrifice and a deep understanding of the **biological drivers of human cooperation**.
notes:
Effective leadership is the selfless choice to cultivate a **"circle of safety"** by prioritizing the well-being and security of others over personal gain, which fosters the deep-seated trust and cooperation necessary to overcome external challenges. By consistently sacrificing time and energy for the group, leaders trigger biological bonds that transform an organization into a resilient tribe where every member feels empowered to protect one another and innovate.

* **Leadership is a Decision, Not a Rank:** Leadership is a choice to look after the person to your left and right, regardless of your formal position in an organization.
* **The Circle of Safety:** A leader's primary responsibility is to create an environment where members feel safe from internal threats (politics, credit-stealing, fear of layoffs) so they can collectively face external dangers.
* **The Cost of Leadership:** True leadership requires the sacrifice of self-interest. Alphas are granted perks and status by the group with the expectation that they will be the first to run toward danger and protect the tribe.
* **The Biological Incentive System:** Human behavior is governed by four primary chemicals: Endorphins, Dopamine, Serotonin, and Oxytocin (EDSO).
* **Selfish Chemicals (Endorphins & Dopamine):** Endorphins mask physical pain, while Dopamine rewards goal achievement. Dopamine is highly addictive and can lead to destructive behaviors if unbalanced by social chemicals.
* **Social Chemicals (Serotonin & Oxytocin):** Serotonin provides feelings of pride and status through public recognition, while Oxytocin creates trust and long-term bonds through acts of generosity and the investment of time and energy.
* **The Danger of Cortisol:** Chronic stress in unsafe work environments leads to a constant drip of cortisol, which increases paranoia, inhibits empathy, and shuts down the immune system, causing long-term health issues.
* **Time and Energy vs. Money:** Human bonds are formed through the sacrifice of non-redeemable commodities like time and energy. Digital communication (email) is a tool for information, but human relationships require physical presence and direct interaction to release oxytocin.
* **The Social Contract:** Societies and organizations fail when leaders violate the contract of self-sacrifice—receiving the benefits of rank while sacrificing their people to protect their own interests.
* **The Power of Service:** Helping others (the 12th step of recovery) is the most effective biological mechanism for overcoming addiction and achieving lasting fulfillment.

title: The 3 Types of Unit Test in TDD
description: This TDD tutorial explores the three types of test with some simple examples to demonstrate each one. It then goes on to explore the difference between stubs, fakes, spies and mocks in testing and describe some common difficulties that people sometimes face.
notes:
Test-driven development serves primarily as a powerful design tool that forces developers to focus on the external interface and behavior of their code before implementation, ultimately helping to identify and correct design flaws through immediate feedback. By recognizing that there are only three essential types of tests—checking return values, state changes, or interactions—practitioners can simplify their approach and maintain a clean separation between what a piece of code does and how it works.

* **TDD as a Design Tool**: Test-Driven Development is primarily about driving software design from the perspective of a user, focusing on how code is consumed rather than how it is implemented.
* **Separation of Interface and Implementation**: TDD encourages separating the "what" (the interface and semantics) from the "how" (the internal details), which helps in hiding implementation "secrets" and improving modularity.
* **Public Interface Testing**: Tests should only exercise the code through its public interface. Avoid using "back doors" or breaking encapsulation, as tests should rely on the same level of abstraction as a standard user of the code.
* **Three Types of Test Outcomes**:
    * **Direct Response**: Testing the specific value returned or the exception thrown by a function.
    * **State Change**: Testing whether an operation correctly modified the internal state of the code (e.g., checking if a list size increased after adding an item).
    * **Interaction (Behavioral)**: Testing how the code interacts with external dependencies or other bits of code.
* **Terminology for Interaction Testing**:
    * **Stubs**: Simple, "dumb" pieces of code that return the same predefined value every time.
    * **Fakes**: Simplified implementations that contain some basic behavior or logic (e.g., a simple simulator or in-memory service).
    * **Mocks and Spies**: Tools used to record interactions during execution so the test can later verify if the correct calls were made.
* **Test Simplicity**: Aim for a cyclomatic complexity of one in tests. This means avoiding loops, conditions, or complex logic within the test code to keep it maintainable and clear.
* **Avoid Calculation Duplication**: Do not replicate the production code's logic within the test itself, as this couples the test to the implementation and fails to provide a definitive assertion.
* **Redundant Testing**: Avoid testing multiple input values if they don't exercise different logic paths or demand new behaviors from the implementation.
* **Design Feedback Loop**: TDD acts as a signal for design quality. If tests are difficult to write or if mocks become overly complex (e.g., "mocking a mock"), it is a clear indicator of tight coupling and poor design that needs refactoring.
* **Chicken Counting Rule**: In testing, use the "zero, one, or many" principle. Testing two instances usually counts as "many," making further iterations unnecessary and slow.

title: Honeycomb's secret to high-performing teams
description: Interesting conversation about high performance engineering organizations and how the pace of production is the heartbeat of the organization. They also discuss some very interesting ideas about how great teams generate great engineers, rather than the other way around.
notes:
The most important lesson is that **high-performing engineering teams prioritize learning from production through observability, supported by a rapid CI/CD pipeline (ideally within 15 minutes), to foster a curious, collaborative, and ownership-driven engineering culture focused on solving meaningful problems rather than just firefighting bugs**. Someone should remember to **be curious in production and invest in observability and rapid deployment to create a virtuous feedback loop and empower engineers to continuously learn and improve their systems**.

Here are the most important points of the presentation, based on the provided transcript:
- **Observability is defined as learning from production, going beyond just metrics, logs, and traces.** It involves being curious and observing software in novel ways, then sharing these observations with the team.
- The mathematical definition of observability from control systems theory is understanding internal system state by looking from the outside. This is increasingly relevant for complex systems like those with microservices.
- **The key to observability is instrumenting code to ask any question and understand any internal system state without prior knowledge**. You should be able to slice and dice already gathered context instead of adding new code for every new observation.
- Instrumentation should include a wide range of information, including parameters, runtime environment details, and high cardinality dimensions like user IDs. The philosophy is to include anything that might be useful in the future.
- There's a divergence between monitoring tools focused on infrastructure health and observability tools focused on whether the code is working and the user experience. Observability shifts the perspective to the end-user journey.
- **A healthy CI/CD pipeline with rapid deployments (ideally within 15 minutes) is crucial for high-performing teams**. This creates a tight feedback loop and fosters a strong sense of ownership among engineers.
- Shipping code frequently and safely should be as regular as a heartbeat to deliver value to users.
- **Observability allows engineers to have a continuous "conversation" with their code in production**, often using an IDE alongside observability tools.
- Finding bugs is the hard part, and observability is well-positioned to help with this by speaking the language of endpoints and variables.
- **Being curious in production is essential** for understanding how software behaves at scale, as many interesting bugs only appear in production.
- High-performing teams spend most of their time solving new and interesting problems that push the business forward, rather than firefighting bugs.
- The environment and team culture are far more powerful than individual knowledge in determining an engineer's performance. Great teams make great engineers, not the other way around.
- Software development is a socio-technical problem, and the majority of a system's knowledge resides in the heads of the people who work on it.
- If you're frustrated with your engineering team's performance, **take a long hard look at your CI/CD pipeline and aim for 15 minutes or less, and invest in observability**.

title: Podcast. Interview Ron Jeffries (Agile.FM)
description: Very interesting interview (darkscrum, agile adoption failures, number agile coaches vs the number of agile developers).

title: LISA17 - Scalability Is Quantifiable: The Universal Scalability Law
description: Do you know what scalability really is? It's a mathematical function that's simple, precise, and useful. REALLY useful. It describes the relationship between system performance and load. In this talk you'll learn the function (the Universal Scalability Law), how it describes and predicts system behavior you see every day, and how to use it in practice. I'll show you how to understand the function, how to capture the data you need to measure your own system's behavior (you probably already have that), and how to analyze the data with the USL. You'll leave this talk knowing exactly what scalability is and what causes non-linear scaling. There are two factors, and you'll start seeing those everywhere, too. As a result, when systems don't scale you'll know what kind of problem to look for, and you'll avoid building bottlenecks into your systems in the first place. Final note: this talk requires zero mathematical skill.
notes:
Scalability is a quantifiable mathematical function where performance is constrained by serialization and the quadratic costs of coordination, meaning that adding more resources can eventually result in decreased total output [1-3]. This Universal Scalability Law applies to both complex technical systems and human teams, highlighting that excessive communication and synchronization overhead are the primary drivers of system degradation.

* **Scalability is a formal mathematical function**, not a vague concept, and can be precisely quantified using the Universal Scalability Law (USL).
* **System behavior under load is nonlinear** and counterintuitive; small increases in utilization can lead to exponential increases in latency.
* **Queuing theory** illustrates that as a system approaches 100% utilization, latency follows a "hockey stick" curve, rapidly approaching infinity.
* **Capacity is not a fixed limit**; systems can operate beyond their peak capacity in a "degraded mode" or enter "retrograde scalability" where adding more load actually reduces total throughput.
* **Linear scalability is an ideal** that is rarely achieved in real-world systems, despite frequent vendor claims.
* **Amdahl's Law** defines the ceiling of performance speed-up based on serialization—the parts of a process that cannot be run in parallel.
* **Crosstalk (coordination overhead)** introduces a quadratic cost ($N^2$) to systems; as more nodes or threads are added, the effort to coordinate them eventually outweighs the benefits of parallelization.
* **The Universal Scalability Law equation** accounts for three factors: linear growth, serialization ($\sigma$), and crosstalk ($\kappa$).
* **USL parameters can be estimated** using simple external metrics like throughput and concurrency, making it possible to model a system as a "black box" without knowing internal service times.
* **Modeling allows for "virtual" load testing**, enabling engineers to forecast system behavior and identify bottlenecks beyond the limits of physical testing environments.
* **The law applies to human organizations**; communication overhead in large teams acts like crosstalk, reducing individual productivity and necessitating small, autonomous team structures.

title: Lean and agile at Toyota Connected
description: It is interesting to see how they are introducing agile in Toyota to compete with the tech companies and complement the excellence in production they have (with TPS).
notes:
To thrive in a complex and rapidly changing market, organizations must integrate the flow efficiency of **Lean** with the adaptive responsiveness of **Agile** to ensure they are both building products correctly and building the right products. This synergy enables businesses to maintain high quality and low costs while remaining flexible enough to innovate and stay ahead of the competition.

*   **Transition to Mobility:** Toyota is transitioning from a traditional automotive manufacturer to a "mobility company" to compete with high-speed tech rivals.
*   **Data-Driven Business:** Vehicles are becoming "web browsers on wheels," generating millions of data points daily. Data and AI are now core to the business strategy.
*   **Business Agility Definition:** Agility is the organizational capacity to respond to change rapidly. It is not limited to software but applies to manufacturing, finance, and leadership.
*   **Lean vs. Agile:** Lean focuses on "building the product right" (flow efficiency and waste reduction), while Agile focuses on "building the right product" (responding to customer demand). An organization needs both to be successful.
*   **Scrum as PDCA:** Scrum is viewed as a codified Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle. It is a planning framework, not just a delivery cadence, that enables empirical planning with rapid feedback loops.
*   **Evolutionary vs. Revolutionary Change:** While *Kaizen* represents continuous evolutionary improvement, agility represents *Kakushin*—a major revolutionary change in business operations.
*   **Customer First Philosophy:** Reaffirming the 1946 promise of delivering the highest quality at the lowest cost in the shortest lead time.
*   **Agile Leadership:** Leaders should adopt a servant-leadership mindset, moving away from command-and-control to support teams and prioritize customer needs directly.
*   **Cross-Functional Collaboration:** Success requires breaking down silos and creating cross-functional, multi-vendor teams to solve problems in real-time rather than through delayed ticketing systems.
*   **Shift to Cloud and OTA:** Moving from physical dealership software updates to instantaneous Over-the-Air (OTA) updates is essential to meet modern customer expectations.
*   **Complexity and Team Science:** Navigating "unknowns" requires studying complexity theory (such as the Cynefin framework) and applying team science to foster true interdependence rather than just grouping individuals.
*   **Scaling via Contextual Patterns:** There is no universal "scaling framework." Organizations must discover specific patterns that work within their unique context.
*   **Performance Gains:** Implementation of these methods has shown drastic results, such as reducing project delivery from 186 days to 69 days and server provisioning from 11 weeks to 2 days.

title: Maintainable Ep 161: How Small Can We Make This Problem
description: Maintainable podcast with Chad Fowler (The Passionate Programmer; ex-CTO of Wunderlist) on keeping software alive by touching it frequently, and making components small enough that they're disposable and trivially swappable.
notes:
On the Maintainable podcast, Chad Fowler (co-author of The Passionate Programmer, ex-CTO of Wunderlist, now CTO/partner at BlueYard Capital) argues that well-maintained software is software that keeps changing — and that the way to keep it healthy is to make each problem, and each component, as small as possible.

- Well-maintained software changes frequently at the behest of real users; a system that is alive and used can't stay healthy unless it's frequently touched (a stale repo isn't necessarily "done" — living systems need ongoing change).
- Make components small with a clear interface dictated by the system, so they can be swapped in and out without catastrophe — "how small can we make this problem" is the guiding question.
- War story: at Wunderlist (a to-do app that was really a multi-master distributed database with a real-time sync protocol), the Wunderlist 2 rewrite collapsed under load on a Christmas-Day launch, leaving a team too afraid to touch anything — the antithesis of healthy, frequently-changed software.

title: Business Mapping: Turning the lights on
description: Dan North on Business Mapping (built with Chris Matts): an experience-based, risk-driven way to scale delivery to hundreds/thousands of people — because Agile doesn't scale, scaling is a different problem.
notes:
Agile doesn't scale — it's a brilliant team-scale (7±2) solution to the software problems of the 1990s; scaling to 80/400/2000 people is a substantively different problem, and bolt-ons like Scrum-of-Scrums don't work. Business Mapping (Dan North with Chris Matts, from real engagements at a 1,500-dev product company and a 500-person bank) is a risk-driven way to make scaled delivery visible.

- It's a control vs risk distinction: SAFe/LeSS/DAD are about controls (checkpoints, feature trains — "holding the tail"); Business Mapping is about understanding where you are and managing risk ("being the dog").
- Effective change is a cycle: visualize (know where you are — turn the lights on; most change starts without knowing), stabilize (make the value stream consistent — consistently bad beats wildly variable, or optimization is just noise), then optimize (technologists jump straight here and skip the other two). Business Mapping lives entirely in "visualize" — it shows where you must form an opinion, not what to decide.
- Four maps: initiative mapping (pick a top-level KPI, e.g. reduce churn, and assert falsifiable hypotheses that initiatives will move it — kill them when disproved; a hypothesis is a statement you can disprove, not prove); demand mapping (programs of work on a cone of uncertainty, funded in "swags" = sweet wild-ass guesses ≈ a team for a week, mapped to the skills/systems each needs); skills mapping (people across six dimensions — developer/team/product/platform/department/org — with career aspiration as a first-class citizen); and delivery mapping (put people + work "in a bag and shake").
- Move people to the work, not work to people (lean: transporting work is waste) — like a pit crew swarming the car. Cross-functional/feature teams were originally a projection of that principle; scaling methods forget it and just slice work through fixed feature teams, creating the dependency/coordination/different-priorities problem.
- The constraint is not money: infinite money won't double your speed tomorrow. Access to people, specific skills, org structure, politics and geography are the real constraints (a theory-of-constraints problem — the movie-studio-needing-NYPD-units analogy).
- Fund for risk-adjusted return on capital: value isn't just ROI but WHEN you get it (bond pricing) — incremental delivery realizes value sooner and keeps value-at-risk low. And it tackles the human side: people feel "lost in Agile" ("I'm a team member" with no growth path); make skills and aspirations visible.

title: The Difference between Software Engineering and Manufacturing
description: In this interview, Don explains the basic difference between applying lean to a manufacturing process and to a digital/software product creation process.
notes:
Software engineering should adopt manufacturing's quantitative discipline for managing invisible inventory and optimizing batch sizes, but it must strictly reject the goal of minimizing variability [1-4]. Because variability is the essential "traveling companion" of innovation, the focus should be on leveraging uncertainty to exploit asymmetric payoffs, where high-variance outcomes provide the best opportunity for breakthrough success [3, 5-7].

* **Engineering methodologies as a continuum**: Engineering should not be viewed as a binary choice (0% or 100% adoption of a method) but as a continuum where different practices are applied as needed.
* **Batch size economics**: Reducing batch size is an economic tradeoff between holding costs and transaction costs. There is an mathematically optimum batch size rather than a purely philosophical "small is good" approach.
* **Variability and Innovation**: In manufacturing, minimizing variability maximizes value. In engineering, variability is the "constant traveling companion" of innovation; removing all uncertainty inevitably removes innovation.
* **Asymmetric Payoffs**: In scenarios like product development or competitive races, high variability (standard deviation) is often desirable because it increases the probability of hitting the extreme "upside" performance required to win.
* **Invisible Inventory**: Unlike manufacturing where inventory is physical and visible, engineering inventory consists of information, which is intrinsically invisible. 
* **Invisible Flow and Batch Size**: Because work is information-based, the flow of work and the size of batches are also invisible in an engineering environment, making them harder to track and manage.
* **Managing Information Flow**: Tools like Kanban systems are essential in engineering to make invisible inventory and work-flow visible and manageable.

title: Product Managers,Product Owners and the need for Real End User Validation
description: Rich Mironov highlights the critical need for direct user validation and closing the organizational gap between developers and customers to avoid the ultimate waste: building a product nobody wants.
notes:
Building the wrong product is the ultimate source of waste, which can only be avoided by prioritizing direct, continuous validation with real end-users over internal stakeholder opinions. Real success comes from reducing the distance between developers and users, allowing the whole team to collaboratively frame and solve genuine customer problems rather than just processing unverified requirements.

* **Building the wrong thing is the greatest source of waste** in software development; success is defined by creating products customers actually want to pay for, not just delivering on time.
* **Distinguish between problems and solutions**: Customers are experts on their own problems but are almost always wrong about how to solve them. Product managers must extract the "problem statement" and avoid simply writing down customer suggestions as requirements.
* **Direct user validation is mandatory**: Product managers should talk directly to 10 to 30 actual users—not just stakeholders, executives, or sales proxies—to identify patterns and avoid outliers.
* **Overcome cognitive biases**: Awareness of confirmation bias (seeking agreement), availability bias (relying on the last conversation), and authority bias (obeying the CEO’s "good idea") is critical to objective validation.
* **Product Management vs. Product Ownership**: The "Product Owner" role is often too narrowly focused on backlog grooming and story writing. Real product management requires significant time "outside the building" to understand market segments and user outcomes.
* **Reduce organizational distance**: The more handoffs and translations there are between a developer and an end user, the more value is lost. Minimizing this distance leads to better products and higher morale.
* **Solution design is a "team sport"**: Engineers should be involved in the problem-framing and architectural stages. Since developers are often the best problem-solvers, handing them finished "requirements" wastes their intellectual potential.
* **Organize by stable feature teams**: Teams should own specific parts of a product long-term to build deep expertise and accountability for technical debt, rather than being shuffled between temporary projects.
* **Product vs. Project mindset**: A product business focuses on selling identical bits to a mass market for profit, while a project business focuses on hourly utilization and custom work. Product management is essential for the former but often absent in the latter.
* **Engineering is a profit center, not a cost center**: Viewing software development as an "IT cost center" leads to outsourcing and cost-cutting that destroys product quality. High-performing companies treat engineering as a creative, value-generating engine.
* **Focus on outcomes over outputs**: Success is measured by user joy, adoption, and business value, not by velocity, ticket counts, or meeting arbitrary deadlines.

title: NEXT17 | Bruce Sterling | Live from 2027
description: Bruce Sterling explores the transition of the digital industry into a mature, regulated era by 2027, highlighting the death of Moore's Law and the urgent need for European leadership in ethics, social policy, and climate action.
notes:
As the era of relentless digital expansion and Moore's Law draws to a close, society must move beyond the exploitative "haze" of Silicon Valley's disruption toward a more realistic and humane future defined by decarbonization, social regulation, and "living in truth". Europe has a unique historical opportunity to lead this transition by prioritizing environmental resilience and civil rights over the fading, "old-fashioned" ideologies of the digital gold rush.

* The digital industry is maturing into a standard legacy industry, following the historical lifecycle of sectors like railroads or aviation rather than remaining a permanent revolutionary force.
* **Moore’s Law has effectively ended**, terminating the era of exponential leaps in processing power and bandwidth that drove the last 50 years of technical growth.
* Silicon Valley is undergoing a process of **"Detroit-ification,"** shifting away from genuine innovation toward "Uberization" and business models aimed at surveillance and the exploitation of an underclass.
* The current digital economy is defined by **wage stagnation and skyrocketing costs** for health and education, contrasted by record corporate profits for the top 1%.
* Standalone personal computers are becoming obsolete, as computing has shifted toward hardware that is permanently tethered to major corporate ecosystems like Google, Amazon, Facebook, and Apple.
* Many hyped technologies, such as **VR, AR, and drones**, are increasingly viewed as entertainment frills or military tools rather than drivers of broad societal progress.
* **Deep learning** is technically interesting but remains limited and is unlikely to evolve into "General Artificial Intelligence" within the next decade.
* Demographics are a fixed reality; Germany and other developed nations are transitioning into societies where **half the population is over the age of 50**.
* The future will be defined by **environmental decline**, with extreme weather events like "rain bombs," droughts, and floods becoming unavoidable physical realities.
* Europe has a strategic window to lead by **unifying its military, borders, and civil rights** while aggressively taxing and regulating global tech giants.
* The term **"digital" will eventually vanish** from common usage as the technology fades into the background of everyday life, similar to electricity or automation.

title: Rights and Responsibilities of a delivery team
description: Sandro Mancuso explores transforming delivery teams into autonomous problem-solving units by establishing a **working agreement that balances team rights with professional responsibilities** to shift focus from mere outputs to meaningful business outcomes.
notes:
To achieve true agility, delivery teams must evolve into autonomous problem-solving units that balance a clear set of rights with professional responsibilities to align technology directly with business outcomes. This shift requires moving away from simply completing backlog tasks toward sharing accountability for maximizing value and solving core business problems as a single, integrated team.

**Core Goal of a Delivery Team**
*   The primary objective is to continuously and efficiently enable business evolution.
*   Teams should focus on achieving business outcomes (e.g., increased subscribers, faster response times) rather than just technical outputs (e.g., finishing JIRA tickets or story points).
*   Continuous delivery should be maintained so the team is always ready to go to production, regardless of external deployment constraints.

**Team Structure and Roles**
*   The Product Owner (PO) must be an integral part of the team, not a separate entity or a "project manager" who simply hands down tasks.
*   Accountability must be shared; if a goal is not met, the entire team (including the PO) shares the responsibility.
*   The role of a full-time Scrum Master is often unnecessary for mature teams; teams should be capable of understanding processes and organizing themselves.
*   Organizations should avoid "cookie-cutter" models like the Spotify model unless they truly adopt the principles of squad autonomy and mission-alignment.

**Unified Backlog Management**
*   The entire team should maintain the backlog to ensure technical strategy and business direction are aligned.
*   A single backlog should contain functional requirements, operational requirements (formerly non-functional), bugs, and technical improvements.
*   Centralizing backlog control in one person (the PO) limits the solution space and creates a single point of failure.

**Rights of a Delivery Team**
*   **Team Structure:** The right to define how the team is composed and the "veto power" to reject new members who do not fit the team’s culture or needs.
*   **Strategy Access:** The right to access business strategy and communicate directly with stakeholders to understand the "why" behind requests.
*   **Process Autonomy:** The right to define and tailor their own working process based on the specific project context.
*   **Sustainable Pace:** The right to work at a pace that maintains health and quality over the long term.
*   **Accountability Boundaries:** The right to not be held accountable for things outside their control, such as estimates they didn't make or production environments managed by separate operations teams.
*   **Scope and Time Control:** If quality is non-negotiable, the team must have the right to control either the time or the scope of a project.
*   **Experimentation:** The right to dedicated time for targeted learning and experimentation to find better solutions.

**Responsibilities of a Delivery Team**
*   **Professional Service:** Providing honest, transparent, and respectful service to the sponsors paying for the work.
*   **Business Best Interest:** Acting as if it were their own money and company, advising the business on the best range of solutions.
*   **Maximizing Value:** Understanding the investment being made in the team and ensuring the value provided justifies the cost (ROI).
*   **Continuous Value Delivery:** Delivering work in small, frequent increments to allow for rapid feedback and adaptation.
*   **Enabling Planning:** Helping stakeholders plan by providing high-level, realistic "ballpark" estimates, even if they aren't firm commitments.
*   **Continuity:** Structuring work so that performance does not drop significantly when individual members are absent or leave.
*   **Excellence:** A commitment to technical excellence and creating a respectful, high-performing environment.

**The Shift from Delivery to Problem Solving**
*   Teams must move away from being "order takers" (delivery teams) toward becoming "problem solvers."
*   There needs to be a much larger intersection between product design and software design/architecture, rather than just connecting at the level of user stories.
*   The focus should shift from managing backlogs to identifying and solving core business problems.

title: a16z Podcast: Improving Leadership
Test Driven Development with Geepaw Hill
description: Clare talks to  Geepaw Hill about why he loves TDD so much and how he spreads that love to software teams all over the world.

title: How Stripe Invests in Technical Infrastructure
description: Will shares Stripe's approaches to prioritizing infrastructure as your company scales, justifying—and maybe even expanding—your company's spend on technical infrastructure, exploring the whole range of possible areas to invest into infrastructure, adapting your approach between periods of firefighting and periods of innovation, and balancing investment in supporting existing products and enabling new product development.
notes:
Effective infrastructure management requires transitioning from reactive firefighting to proactive innovation by strictly **limiting work-in-progress** and finishing high-leverage tasks to create the space needed for long-term planning. To sustain this, teams must treat internal infrastructure as a product by **listening to users**, setting clear performance baselines, and strategically balancing immediate user requests with platform quality and future-focused initiatives.

*   **Definition of Technical Infrastructure**: Any tool, service, or framework used by three or more teams that is business-critical.
*   **Work Quadrants**: Engineering tasks fall into four categories based on two axes: Forced vs. Discretionary and Short-term vs. Long-term. The "sweet spot" for infrastructure teams is maximizing long-term discretionary work.
*   **Escaping the Firefighting Cycle**:
    *   **Limit Work in Progress (WIP)**: Reduce concurrent tasks to ensure projects actually reach completion.
    *   **Finish Useful Things**: Completing one high-leverage task creates a "sliver of time" to reinvest in the next.
    *   **Automation**: Use service cookbooks to track requests and prioritize automating the most time-consuming manual tasks.
    *   **Problem Redefinition**: Use static analysis and system constraints to make certain classes of failures impossible.
    *   **Hiring**: If process improvements and automation fail to stop firefighting, the team size must be expanded to avoid permanent burnout.
*   **Strategic Innovation**:
    *   **Listen to Users**: Move beyond alert-driven communication by conducting regular coffee chats and qualitative surveys with internal teams.
    *   **Benchmarking**: Talk to peer companies to see how they solved similar problems; this can save years of wasted effort.
    *   **Validation**: Find the cheapest possible way to disprove a solution early. Tackle the most difficult use cases first to ensure the solution is viable for the entire organization.
*   **Maintaining Infrastructure Properties**: Invest in a balanced portfolio across Security, Reliability, Productivity, Usability, Efficiency, and Latency rather than focusing on a single metric.
*   **Proactive Scalability**: Convert unplanned scalability work into planned work by running continuous load tests against production to identify capacity limits months in advance.
*   **Investment Strategy Framework**:
    *   **User Asks (e.g., 40%)**: Directly supporting immediate needs of other teams.
    *   **Platform Quality (e.g., 30%)**: Maintaining baselines for reliability and security.
    *   **Key Initiatives (e.g., 30%)**: Driving long-term evolutionary or order-of-magnitude improvements.
*   **Organizational Courage**: Leaders must resist pressure for immediate fixes and maintain focus on systemic solutions, even when progress appears slow to outside stakeholders.

title: Advanced Continuous Delivery Best Practices (DEV317-R1)
description: (AWS re:Invent 2018) Some interesting insights to improve our delivery pipelines.
notes:
Successfully implementing advanced continuous delivery requires automating safety through rigorous health validations, regional segmentation, and pipeline governance to minimize deployment risk and ensure business continuity. Organizations should focus on tightening feedback loops and incrementally maturing their processes to achieve reliable, high-availability software releases at scale.

* **Levels of Automation**: Distinguish between **Continuous Integration** (source to pretest), **Continuous Delivery** (adds deployment with a manual gate), and **Continuous Deployment** (fully automated end-to-end).
* **Pipeline as Code**: Treat delivery pipelines as **Infrastructure as Code** by committing definitions to version control to track changes, monitor history, and restrict unauthorized access.
* **Fast Feedback Loops**: Ensure developers receive **immediate results** after a commit to minimize context switching and identify build or deployment failures instantly.
* **Functional Validation**: Move beyond simple "200 OK" health checks to **synthetic testing** that validates the application is functioning correctly (e.g., checking if specific UI elements or services are responding as expected).
* **Minimum Healthy Hosts**: Configure deployment groups to require a specific **percentage of healthy instances**; if the deployment causes the fleet to drop below this threshold, trigger an **automated rollback**.
* **Blue-Green Strategy**: Deploy to a **parallel environment** to validate health in isolation before shifting traffic, maintaining the original environment for a period to allow for an immediate cut-over if issues arise.
* **Blast Radius Reduction**: Segment production deployments into stages: start with a **Canary** (receives a small portion of live traffic), then progress to specific **Availability Zones**, and finally roll out to the entire **Region**.
* **Global Pipeline Orchestration**: Utilize **multi-region pipelines** to manage global deployments from a single location, improving end-user latency and ensuring high availability/disaster recovery.
* **Automated Governance**: Implement **compliance rules** (e.g., via AWS Config) to automatically block non-compliant pipeline changes or prevent transitions during restricted times like **code freezes** or off-hours.
* **Staggered Database Changes**: Decouple database and code releases by using **design patterns**—deploying additive schema changes before the code that uses them, and deploying code changes before removing old database structures.
* **Pre-production Stages**: Use a **pre-prod environment** that mirrors production hosts but only receives test traffic to verify that all dependencies and configurations are correct before any customer-facing deployment.

title: What Breaks Our Systems: A  Taxonomy of Black Swans
description: A lot of insights about how complex systems fail. Based on public postmortems can help us avoid some common problems.
notes:
This talk, recorded at QCon New York 2019, is an SRE-focused taxonomy of "Black Swan" outages by a speaker who describes herself as a former plain-vanilla software engineer turned SRE, who spent a long time at Google, wrote a chapter in O'Reilly's Google SRE book and contributed to Seeking SRE, and was at the time a "very shiny new production engineer at Slack" (she also campaigns against lethal autonomous weapons). Borrowing Nassim Taleb's "Black Swan" concept, she defines these as outlier events that are hard or impossible to predict and severe in impact, and notes that because systems are full of hidden correlation we see them more often than naive probability suggests. Her core thesis: by studying other people's public postmortems we can find recurring patterns and build tooling, processes, and resilience that turn unpredictable severe events into routine non-incidents. She distilled years of public internet postmortems of multi-hour outages into six failure categories, stressing throughout that she throws no shade at the organizations involved — sharing painful postmortems is a service.

* **Change is the biggest cause of outages, so canarying and continuous delivery are core defenses**: Citing Google's Sue Lueder, who studied outage causes for years, she says the biggest cause of production problems is change. Canarying — rolling a new binary or config to 1%, then 5%, then 10% while monitoring for errors, crash loops, or business-metric drops, and auto-reverting bad pushes — "is a defense against an entire class of problems." Continuous delivery helps because rollbacks are faster when the change is recent, watched, and understood by someone still present.

* **Category 1 — Hitting limits you didn't know were there**: Instapaper hit a hidden 2TB ext3 filesystem limit behind Amazon RDS, lost all writes, was down over a day and degraded for five days while re-importing data. Sentry maxed out 32-bit Postgres transaction IDs because vacuuming couldn't keep up, halting writes for most of a US working day (similar postmortems exist from Joyent and Mailchimp). SparkPost (May 2017) expanded its cluster and silently hit an undocumented AWS connection-tracking table limit from high short-lived DNS connections, blocking email for hours. Foursquare was down 11 hours when a MongoDB shard grew larger than machine RAM (back when MongoDB had a global lock), causing a query backlog while resharding under load. Platform.sh had a region down 4 hours when a third-party orchestration library funneled all ZooKeeper queries through one 64k pipe buffer that eventually filled and threw. Limits hit system resources (RAM), logical resources (pipe buffers, Postgres transaction IDs), and provider/filesystem limits — "very often they're very hard limits... that we can run into completely without warning."

* **Defenses for limits — load and capacity testing, and alerts as documentation**: She once spent ~15 months doing load testing and fixing what it found. Load tests must include write loads (read-only tests are "not very useful" because writes take locks); if imposing unusual workloads on a cloud provider, load-test that the provider can handle current load plus margin. Capacity-test by growing data stores past current size on a replica of prod, including ancillary stores like ZooKeeper, and exercise startup, shutdown, and resharding — not just happy-path operation. "The best documentation of a limit that you know about is a monitoring alert" — file a ticket (don't page) and link a runbook, since the alert may fire years later when you're gone: "embed the knowledge in your systems and not in your people as much as possible."

* **Category 2 — Spreading slowness from saturation**: Hosted Graphite (Feb 2018) went down from AWS problems despite not being on AWS — slow AWS-based customer connections hogged its HAProxy load-balancer connections until saturated, an accidental Slowloris (an attack the Iranians used heavily ~10 years prior against their own government infrastructure). Spotify (April 2013) overloaded a playlist microservice when another service spun up unplanned; rolling back didn't help because request queues stayed full, worsened by verbose logging (a "perfect storm" of factors), requiring a controlled restart behind a firewall. Square's Redis-backed auth system slowed to a crawl because clients retried Redis 500 times with no backoff, and since everything depended on auth, everything locked up.

* **Defenses for spreading slowness — fail fast, bound retries, observe saturation**: "The big defense against this is failing fast." Enforce request deadlines (no point in timeouts longer than ~100-200ms for internet users who'll get bored); retry maybe three times, "not 500, three"; use exponential backoff with jitter to avoid echoing waves of errors; and use the circuit breaker pattern to share unhealthy-backend state across requests and stop calling for a few seconds. Build dashboards around the golden signals latency and errors, plus saturation metrics. Utilization is what percentage of a resource is busy/full; saturation is excess demand (every thread in use with work queued). "Most of these distributed systems... stem from either an error... or saturation." Track both physical (RAM, CPU) and non-physical resources (connection pools, threads, locks, file descriptors). Utilization-saturation-errors comes from Brendan Gregg, whose blog and book Systems Performance she recommends.

* **Category 3 — Thundering herds (correlated demand)**: A thundering herd is a big spike of coordinated demand; correlation is why we see Black Swans more often than independent-probability math predicts. Sources include organic user demand (New Year's Eve, Black Friday, flash sales — e.g. the mobile game Puzzle & Dragons, whose monthly "magic stones" sale sent its payment service off the charts) and, more often, systems: mobile clients updating at midnight, cron jobs on the hour, big batch jobs (the Google "intern MapReduce"), and self-repair like a Voldemort cluster re-replicating data when a node drops. CircleCI (July 2015) was hit by a surge of queued GitHub webhooks when GitHub recovered, with heavy scheduling logic causing database contention. Mixpanel (Jan 2016) was intermittently down 5 hours from a perfect storm: an AZ down for maintenance, an unexplained load spike saturating disk I/O, and Android clients retrying with no backoff. Discord had two 2-hour incidents in one day when one presence-service instance disconnected (CPU soft lock from a spinlock) and everything reconnected at once, worsened by low service cardinality.

* **Defenses for thundering herds — expect them, degrade gracefully, queue, and test**: "Basically any... internet-facing service... can face this kind of thundering herd" — a 10k QPS service can see 500k QPS, so plan for it. Plan a degraded mode and decide what traffic to drop (e.g. free tier before paying tier); queue work that needn't be processed synchronously, using a cheap queue plus an asynchronous worker as a buffer so you neither drop incoming requests nor overload backends (most monitoring systems ingest metrics this way). Test what happens when you overload the system — does it shed load gracefully or lock up and contaminate the rest?

* **Category 4 — Automation interactions in complex systems**: At Google (one she witnessed firsthand), an engineer tried to disk-erase one rack but a regex accident logically erased the entire Google CDN by throwing away encryption keys; YouTube and the Play Store survived (slower for a couple of days) only because the CDN was a latency cache, not a capacity cache. Reddit's ZooKeeper migration broke when they disabled their ZooKeeper-dependent autoscaler, but automation meant to keep the autoscaler running turned it back on, and it took the site down. "One of the things [automation] can get done really well and really fast is taking down all of your stuff." Complex systems (multiple interacting parts, non-linear, with memory) are inherently prone to unpredictable behavior — she has "seen robots fighting with each other," e.g. two automations sloshing load back and forth until everything drained.
