* **Defenses for automation — constraint services, big red buttons, logging, and human primacy**: Build a constraint service that automation must ask before acting, setting lower bounds on remaining resources (e.g. always keep 2,000 web servers) or refusing changes to recently alerted services. Crucially, a constraint service must NOT limit human operators (only advise them) "because humans are smarter than the machines" and must be able to fix things when the robots have destroyed everything. Make automation easy to turn off via a "big red button" — a literal page or command — and test it monthly or quarterly (good drills for new on-callers). Have automation log all production changes to one searchable place (IRC, Slack, Logstash) so humans can see what it did.

* **Category 5 — Cyberattacks (the single most impactful outage in the deck)**: Maersk, the global shipping company, was infected by NotPetya ("probably [a] Russian warfare worm"), detailed not in a company postmortem but in a Wired article. It entered via an accounting system in their corporate office and leapfrogged into production because prod was not firewalled off from corporate systems; to stop propagation they had to turn off everything everywhere and couldn't even unload ships. It caused a ~20% hit to global shipping worldwide and cost billions. Lesson: minimize the worst-possible blast radius — segment production heavily, don't let the whole corporate network reach one big production environment, use proxies/jump hosts to access and SSH into prod, and validate and control what runs there. "This should be really, really sobering to anybody who has a setup where corporate machines can just talk directly to prod."

* **Category 6 — Dependency loops**: "Can you reboot all your services from scratch?" Older grown-up systems often can't, and simultaneous reboots are a bad time to discover a circular dependency (e.g. storage depends on monitoring to place shards, monitoring depends on storage). GitHub had a 2-hour outage when a power disruption rebooted much of a data center and a Redis cluster — assumed a soft latency dependency — had to be rebuilt while down, revealing it had become a hard dependency. Trello's API needlessly failed during an S3 outage because it ran a spurious check that the S3-hosted frontend assets existed, turning a frontend-only outage into an API outage too. Defense: layering — define a layer 0 that depends on nothing, with each higher layer depending only on lower ones; test starting infrastructure from scratch periodically (build a fresh staging environment with a full dataset and measure how long it takes); and continually test that soft dependencies (e.g. Redis) haven't silently become hard ones, for instance by turning Redis off for a few hours every month.

* **Cross-cutting practices and closing**: Beyond the six categories (others exist — networks, leap seconds, time-correlated issues), she recommends disaster testing, fuzz testing (to find data that crashes services), chaos engineering, and incident-management processes such as FEMA's, practiced regularly even on minor incidents and drills. Any on-caller should be able to escalate via a pager alias to pull in more technical people, a major incident response team, or executives. Plan out-of-band communication that doesn't depend on your own prod infrastructure — a phone bridge, IRC, or a laminated wallet card. Finally, prioritize this resilience work explicitly in the team's time budget (e.g. a set percentage each quarter); if you can't find the time despite known scary areas and near misses, your team may be overloaded.

title: Agile Essence and Fluency
description: As agile software has got more accepted and popular, it’s also been subject to a fair bit of misunderstanding. This talk focuses on the essence of agile software development, adaptive planning and people-orientation. Following this, a brief summary of the Agile Fluency model, which describes the typical path people follow as they learn and apply agile software development, is given.
notes:
Agile is fundamentally an **adaptive mindset** that embraces changing requirements and empowers teams to own their processes rather than adhering to rigid, predictive plans. To achieve true agility, organizations must commit to a long-term **cultural and technical shift**, moving beyond management rituals to master the technical practices that enable the delivery of real business value.

* Agile is a reaction against **predictive planning**, which treats software development like construction engineering by relying on fixed designs and stable requirements.
* In an agile mindset, requirements are assumed to be **unstable**, necessitating a process that embraces and manages inevitable change through iterations.
* Success is measured by **business value** and domain efficiency rather than the predictive metrics of being on time and on budget.
* Agile rejects **Taylorism (Scientific Management)**, which separates "planners" from "doers"; instead, it recognizes that software requires capable people who must define their own processes.
* There is no single "standard" agile approach; teams must adapt their practices to fit their specific members and the unique problems they are solving.
* **Retrospectives** are essential for continuous improvement, allowing teams to learn from their work and evolve their process over time.
* The **Agile Fluency Model** outlines stages of development: **Focusing** (management practices/visibility), **Delivering** (technical excellence/low defects), and **Optimizing** (organizational/business alignment).
* Transitioning to the **Delivering** stage requires a significant **skills shift** in technical practices such as test-driven development and continuous delivery.
* Implementing high-quality technical practices typically results in an **order of magnitude drop in defect rates**, which ultimately drives higher productivity.
* True agile transformation is a **multi-year investment** that requires a fundamental shift in culture and worldview, making it impossible to achieve in short timeframes like six months.

title: DOES17 London - The Key to High Performance What the Data Says
description: This talk presents the findings of the 2017 State of DevOps Report, emphasizing that high-performing organizations achieve both better throughput and stability. It explores how transformational leadership, technical practices, and lean product management contribute to IT and organizational performance.
notes:
High-performing organizations achieve superior throughput and stability simultaneously by leveraging transformational leadership and decoupled architecture, proving that speed and reliability are mutually reinforcing rather than a trade-off [1-5]. This success is sustained through a holistic, disciplined approach where technical practices and lean management drive the cultural transformation necessary for exceptional performance [6-9].

* **DevOps Performance Metrics**: High performers achieve 46x more frequent change deployments, are 440x faster from commit to deploy, have a 96x faster mean time to repair (MTTR), and a change failure rate that is five times lower than low performers.
* **Throughput and Stability**: High performance is not a trade-off; high-performing organizations maximize both speed (throughput) and reliability (stability) simultaneously. Low performers often sacrifice stability when attempting to increase throughput.
* **Transformational Leadership**: Leadership is a significant amplifier of performance. It is characterized by five key factors: vision, intellectual stimulation, inspirational communication, supportive leadership, and personal recognition.
* **Architecture as a Predictor**: Architecture is the strongest predictor of continuous delivery. Performance is driven by architectural outcomes rather than specific technologies, specifically the ability for teams to make changes, test, and deploy independently without outside permission or fine-grained coordination.
* **Automation and the J-Curve**: High performers do significantly less manual work across configuration management, testing, and deployments. Organizations often experience a "J-curve" where manual work initially increases as automation uncovers technical debt before performance radically improves.
* **Reverse Conway's Law**: Organizations should design their technical architecture to enable the desired organizational structure—specifically small, cross-functional, loosely coupled teams that can experiment and deploy independently.
* **Lean Product Management**: Performance is driven by working in small batches, making work flow visible, and empowering teams to experiment or pivot based on customer feedback.
* **Organizational Impact**: High IT performance predicts broader organizational success. High performers are twice as likely to exceed both commercial goals (profitability, market share) and non-commercial goals (customer satisfaction, mission objectives).
* **Cultural Transformation**: Culture is changed by altering behaviors and practices. Implementing technical practices and providing the right tools and leadership transforms the culture, which in turn drives performance.
* **Technology Agnostic**: These principles apply across all environments, including "brownfield" legacy systems, mainframes, and highly regulated industries. Success depends on investing in architecture and culture rather than just adopting new tools.

title: Software_Crasftsmanship (Barcelona CAS2014)
description: Very inspiring talk about the profesionalism as a developer. IMHO craftmanship is the real spirit of agile.
notes:
True agility requires a shift from strictly following processes to embracing software craftsmanship, where technical excellence and professional ownership ensure that code remains a valuable asset rather than a liability. Developers should focus on shortening feedback loops through disciplined practices like TDD and refactoring to provide continuous value and maintain the ability to adapt to business needs.

* **Agile as Feedback Loops:** At its core, Agile is about shortening feedback loops. While many organizations focus on the process (scrum, post-its, certifications), Agile fails without technical excellence because businesses can only move as fast as their developers can safely change the software.
* **The "Agile Hangover":** Many companies experience an "agile hangover" where they have improved processes but still suffer from long release cycles, frequent bugs, and hard-to-maintain legacy code because they neglected the technical side of development.
* **Software Craftsmanship:** This movement emphasizes **professionalism** and "well-crafted software" rather than just "working software." Well-crafted software is easy to maintain, a pleasure to work with, and evolves as the business grows.
* **Career Ownership:** Developers must take full ownership of their own careers and learning. Professionalism means not waiting for an employer to pay for books or training, but investing personal time and effort into mastering the craft.
* **Productive Partnership:** The relationship between a developer and a company should be a partnership of professionals, not a master-servant dynamic. Developers are stakeholders whose own reputations are tied to the success or failure of the project.
* **Value-Driven Technical Practices:** Practices like TDD, pair programming, and continuous integration should not be discussed as "technical tasks" with management. Instead, they should be framed as ways to provide **value** through immediate feedback and the reduction of waste and bugs.
* **Refactoring as Preparation:** Refactoring shouldn't just happen after code is written. The most important refactoring happens **before** starting a new feature to "prepare the system" so the new functionality can be easily slid into place.
* **The Fallacy of Quality Metrics:** Quality cannot be easily measured or defined. Management obsession with metrics like code coverage is often dangerous, as these numbers can be easily manipulated (e.g., writing tests without assertions) and do not truly reflect the health of the system.
* **Mentorship and Community:** A central tenet of craftsmanship is the "community of professionals." This involves mentorship, sharing knowledge, and helping younger developers improve, ensuring that skills and values are passed down.
* **Pragmatism over Dogma:** A good developer is a generalist who uses the best tools for the job. Software craftsmanship is not a "religion" or a set of rigid rules; it is about using the right practices in the right context to deliver the best possible value.
* **The Boy Scout Rule:** Developers should always leave the code cleaner than they found it. This constant, small-scale improvement prevents the "pile of sheet" from growing and keeps the software from becoming an obstacle to the business.

title: The nature of product
description: A very interesting interview to Marty Cagan. Very good insights about empowered teams, product management, and team organization.
notes:
Here are the most important points from Marty Cagan's presentation:
- **The fundamental difference between feature teams and real product teams:** Feature teams are handed a roadmap of prioritized features (solutions) to build, primarily serving the business. In contrast, real product teams are given problems to solve (customer or company problems) and are empowered to come up with the best solutions. Real product teams focus on outcomes (solving problems) rather than output (releasing features).
- **The drastically different role of the product manager:** In feature teams, the product manager often acts as a project manager or "cat herder," gathering requirements and ensuring delivery. In empowered product teams, the product manager is responsible for discovering solutions that are valuable and viable, working as a peer with designers (focused on usability) and engineers (focused on feasibility). The job is so different that Cagan suggests they shouldn't even share the same title.
- **Misconceptions about product management:** Many people mistakenly believe that product managers primarily write requirements (PRDs, user stories) and hand them off to designers and engineers for implementation. They also often think the job is just about cranking out features based on a roadmap. Cagan emphasizes that product management in successful companies involves deep collaboration and bringing unique skills to the team beyond just defining what to build.
- **Don't overinvest in problem validation:** While understanding the problem is important, **people buy solutions, not the problem**. Product teams should be careful not to spend excessive time validating a problem, especially if it's already well-understood, and instead focus on discovering a winning solution.
- **The true purpose of user research:** User research is not just about confirming that users like a prototype. The primary goal is to find all the reasons why users _won't_ use the product. It's evaluative, helping to identify flaws and areas for improvement. Furthermore, **product managers and designers must actively participate in user research sessions**; reports alone are often ignored.
- **Transitioning from a feature team to a product team is possible:** Individuals in feature teams can try a small experiment, proposing to their manager that their team operate as a product team for a quarter or two, focusing on solving problems and measuring success by outcomes.
- **Key skills for a product manager in an empowered team:** A product manager needs to be an expert in four areas: understanding users and customers, being an expert in the data (product usage, analytics), understanding all parts of the business (marketing, sales, monetization, compliance), and knowing the competitive landscape and industry trends. They also need to ensure the team has strategic context (product vision and strategy) and learn product discovery skills and techniques.
- **Concerns about the evolution of product management:** Cagan is worried about the proliferation of unqualified "product people," particularly in Europe with the misapplication of the "product owner" role by agile coaches lacking product experience. He also sees issues with poorly implemented product ops that might interfere with the core responsibilities of a product manager.
- **The three sacred responsibilities of a product manager:** These must never be delegated: **unencumbered access to users and customers, unencumbered access to engineers, and unencumbered access to stakeholders**. Companies that try to split the product manager role by limiting these accesses create significant damage.
- **The danger of scaling with process over leaders:** While scaling is challenging, relying too heavily on processes (like SAFe) instead of developing strong leaders is a significant risk and can lead to "repackaged waterfall" that is the antithesis of agile principles. Cagan echoes Steve Jobs' warning about the "disease of process people".
- **Marty Cagan welcomes hard questions:** He finds them inspiring for his writing and encourages people to email him with challenging product management questions they can't find good answers to. His resources can be found at svpg.com.

title: The Marty Cagan special - ProductTank #27 Singapore
description: An open discussion on Modern Product Management.  The talk contains a lot of interesting discussions during the Q&A. I think it covers all the underpinnings of modern product management.
notes:
True product management requires moving beyond administrative "product owner" tasks to become an empowered leader who solves business and customer problems through deep knowledge and rapid discovery. Success is measured by achieving meaningful outcomes and creating value, rather than simply delivering a roadmap of features.

*   **Product Manager vs. Product Owner:** The Product Owner role is primarily administrative and represents only about 10% of a Product Manager's job. A true Product Manager requires deep skill-building—often taking one to two years—ideally through coaching by experienced mentors.
*   **Core Competencies:** A successful Product Manager must possess deep knowledge in four specific areas: users/customers, data analytics, the business (including finance, legal, and sales constraints), and the industry (competitors and technology trends).
*   **The Four Risks:** Modern product management focuses on tackling four key risks upfront: Value (will customers buy or use it?), Usability (can they figure out how to use it?), Feasibility (can we build it with our current technology and time?), and Viability (does the solution work for our business?).
*   **Discovery vs. Delivery:** Discovery is the process of finding a solution that is valuable, usable, feasible, and viable using prototypes. Delivery is the separate effort of building a production-quality, scalable, and reliable product.
*   **Outcome over Output:** Success is measured by solving business problems (outcomes) rather than simply shipping features (output). OKRs should focus on business results, while traditional roadmaps of activities often fail to solve the intended problems.
*   **Collaborative Innovation:** True innovation rarely comes from customers or requirements documents; it typically comes from engineers who understand what is "just now possible" with technology. PMs, designers, and engineers must collaborate side-by-side rather than through a hand-off of requirements.
*   **Value Creation vs. Value Capture:** Product teams should focus the majority of their efforts on creating new value (discovery) rather than just capturing existing value through low-risk optimizations and minor tweaks.
*   **High-Frequency Iteration:** Effective discovery involves rapid experimentation. Great teams may test 50 to 200 ideas a month, often performing 15 to 30 iterations per week using prototypes to find a winning solution.
*   **Deep Work Requirement:** To perform the actual Product Manager role, an individual needs at least four hours of "deep work" per day, free from meetings and administrative project management tasks.
*   **Continuous Customer Research:** Teams should conduct at least three one-hour sessions with users every week to identify pain points and understand why they might *not* use a product.
*   **Role of Leadership:** The most important responsibility of product leaders (Managers, Directors, VPs) is coaching their Product Managers to become skilled. They are also responsible for setting the vision, strategy, and objectives that align multiple teams.
*   **Ethical Responsibility:** Beyond technical and business risks, product people must evaluate ethical risk—asking "should we build it?"—to consider the potential negative side effects on society and customers.

title: GeePaw Hill on Incremental Software Delivery
description: Pure wisdom on why working in small, safe steps is the most efficient way to work in software product development when we have environments of high uncertainty (which is almost always).
notes:
Drawing on the information in the YouTube video transcript, here are the most important points of GeePaw Hill's presentation on Incremental Software Delivery:

\*  \*\*The core argument of the presentation is that you should probably take many more much smaller steps when making changes, particularly when your app is already shipping.\*\* This is presented as a default and powerful piece of advice.

\*  Hill defines a \*\*"step" as a gap in time between two points of "readiness".\*\* At the "before" point, you are ready; during the step, you are unready; and at the "after" point, you are ready again.

\*  \*\*"Ready" means that at any given point, you have source code from which you can regenerate your running app without concerns.\*\* This definition explicitly excludes the requirement that the new program be better than the previous one and also ignores the cost of deployment. The focus is solely on whether deployment would cause a problem.

\*  The traditional approach often involves \*\*very large steps\*\*, potentially lasting weeks or even a month (e.g., 30-day stories).

\*  \*\*Hill argues for a better approach involving many more much smaller changes.\*\* He contends that the trade is deeply invested in the idea that more value comes faster from fewer and larger steps, but \*\*"that's not true".\*\*

\*  The theory of taking fewer and larger steps has \*\*two significant problems\*\*:
  \*  \*\*It does not take into account non-linearity.\*\* Longer steps don't just get bigger in effort linearly; they get "bigger bigger" due to factors like decreased certainty, reduced human mental bandwidth, and increased perturbation.
  \*  \*\*It does not take into account the inherent value in each step.\*\* This value is realized multiple times with smaller steps, whereas it's only captured once in a large step.

\*  \*\*There are three aspects to the inherent value of smaller steps:\*\*
  \*  \*\*Interruptability:\*\* Being in a "ready" state more frequently allows for easier interruption and resumption of work without significant loss of context.
  \*  \*\*Steerability:\*\* Frequent "ready" states enable better responsiveness to changing priorities, market situations, or urgent issues ("brushfires").
  \*  \*\*"Juice" (Dopamine):\*\* Completing smaller steps more often provides regular positive reinforcement and a sense of accomplishment.

\*  When faced with a big story, the first step is to \*\*"turn the ton of fan out of it"\*\* by removing unnecessary features or functionalities that are not immediately required for the core value. It's also crucial to shift the mindset from requiring improvement with every step to simply ensuring the step doesn't make the user's life worse.

\*  Articulating a clear definition of "done" can help in breaking down stories into smaller, manageable increments.

\*  Techniques like releasing new, unused code (e.g., CSS classes or non-accessible code) immediately can further enable smaller, more frequent deployments. This aligns with the principle of \*\*"open for extension closed for modification"\*\*.

\*  The prevailing pedagogy that focuses on greenfield development is misaligned with the reality that most developers work in brownfield environments, emphasizing the need for better teaching of techniques for changing existing code. Keeping methods ridiculously short (e.g., no more than five lines) can naturally lead to smaller, more manageable changes.

\*  The presenter advocates for a \*\*"human local oriented taken and iterative"\*\* approach to change, applicable not just to code but also to individuals, teams, and organizations.

\*  The core message is summarized by the analogies: \*\*"that's too much rice" and "mmm that's not enough garlic"\*\* for software development, which translates to "you should probably take many more much smaller steps then you're taking now".

\*  Overcoming resistance to taking smaller steps often involves \*\*creating experiences\*\* that demonstrate the benefits, rather than relying solely on arguments.

title: Sufficiently Advanced Monitoring is Indistinguishable from Testing
description: Interesting ideas about testing in production.
notes:
Advanced monitoring functions effectively as a form of testing by allowing teams to observe real-world behavior and leverage actual user activity to identify system failures. This approach enables a continuous feedback loop that transcends traditional development cycles to ensure long-term software quality and reliability.

*   **Monitoring as Testing**: The primary thesis is that when monitoring systems become sufficiently advanced and granular, they function as a form of testing by validating system behavior and detecting failures in real-time within the production environment.
*   **The "Silly Cycle"**: This concept identifies an inefficient development and testing loop—often involving repetitive cycles of manual testing, deployment, and reactive bug fixing—that advanced monitoring and observability are designed to break.
*   **"Use Your Users"**: This strategy advocates for leveraging real-world user traffic and behavior as the ultimate test suite, allowing the system to be validated against actual usage patterns rather than just synthetic or imagined scenarios [3-5].
*   **Testing in Production**: A critical technical practice that involves shifting validation efforts into the live environment to ensure system resilience and reliability under real-world conditions.
*   **Shortened Feedback Cycles**: High-level monitoring and observability provide rapid feedback to engineering teams, which is essential for maintaining developer productivity and supporting continuous delivery.
*   **Engineering and Operations Culture**: The presentation emphasizes integrating monitoring into the core engineering culture, moving away from treating operations and testing as isolated silos and toward a unified approach to system health.

The current sources provide the structure and high-level themes of the presentation but do not include the full transcript. If you would like more detailed insights or specific examples from Ed Keyes' talk, I can use the **discover_sources** tool to find a complete transcript or more in-depth summary for you. Would you like me to do that?

title: Integrated Tests Are A Scam
description: Integrated tests are a scam. You’re probably writing 2-5% of the integrated tests you need to test thoroughly. You’re probably duplicating unit tests all over the place. Your integrated tests probably duplicate each other all over the place. When an integrated test fails, who knows what’s broken? Integrated tests probably do you more harm than good. Learn the two-pronged attack that solves the problem: collaboration tests and contract tests.
notes:
An integrated test is any test whose pass/fail depends on many bits of behaviour at once, so when it fails you can't point to the cause. Relying on them is a self-reinforcing scam: they don't pressure your design, so you design more sloppily, make more mistakes, and write fewer of the isolated tests that would catch them.

- The real value of isolated (he prefers "isolated" over "unit", and "checking" over "testing") tests is design feedback: if a test is hard to write (23 lines of setup), the design has too many dependencies. TDD's goal is learning about design quality, not testing.
- The scam as a positive feedback loop of negative feelings: 100% of tests pass but a mistake ships → you add more integrated tests to fill the gaps → less design pressure → sloppier design → more mistakes + less time for isolated tests → even more likely that all tests pass yet mistakes remain. (He insists on "mistakes," not "bugs" — we made them.)
- Sloppy design = strong coupling: change a 4 to a 5 and you must hunt every 4 (his Connect-Five story with Uncle Bob: loops within loops, variables ii/jj/kk, comments in Cyrillic — "all you have to do is turn five into six").
- Combinatoric explosion: testing modules only as a cluster needs the product of their paths (3×5×7 = 105; refactoring can make it worse), and these grow faster than the exponential cost-of-change curve. You'll never write them all — you write somewhere between 1% and 80% with no idea where you are.
- The fix is to listen to the design pressure: program to interfaces, and make the interface valuable via its contract (the behaviour the functions guarantee, e.g. add then contains returns true).
- Replace integrated tests with two cheap halves. Collaboration/interaction tests (from the client's view, using mocks/expectations and stubs) check "does the client ask its neighbour the right questions and handle every answer?" — test the 0/1/many/lots/oops cases with stubbed responses rather than manipulating a real database. The other half (contract tests) checks the server honours the contract the client relies on — together they give the confidence integrated tests pretend to, without the combinatoric blow-up.

title: Technical leadership and glue work
description: Let's talk about how to allocate glue work deliberately, frame it usefully and make sure that everyone is choosing a career path they actually want to be on.
notes:
"Glue work" is essential technical leadership that ensures project success, yet it can be career-limiting if it replaces the quantifiable technical achievements required for promotion. To protect your career, you must deliberately balance these high-impact tasks with core technical work while ensuring your contributions are visible and aligned with how your organization evaluates success.

* **Definition of Glue Work:** Glue work refers to the essential but often invisible tasks—such as unblocking teammates, reviewing designs for gaps, onboarding new members, and improving communication—that ensure a software project's success.
* **The Recognition Gap:** While glue work is critical for shipping products, it is frequently undervalued or dismissed as "soft skills" rather than technical leadership, leading to engineers being passed over for promotion in favor of those with high "quantifiable" output like code.
* **Career Risk for Juniors:** Performing excessive glue work early in a career is risky. Because performance reviews often focus on individual technical contributions (coding), junior engineers who act as "glue" may fail to meet the specific technical bars required for senior roles.
* **Systemic Gender Bias:** Research indicates a significant imbalance in who performs this work; women are asked to do and volunteer for non-promotable "thankless" tasks (like organizing off-sites or taking notes) significantly more often than men.
* **Skill Development and Stagnation:** Technical skills are not fixed; they are developed through practice. Over-indexing on glue work can lead to "skill rot" in core areas like coding, making it harder to pass technical interviews or be perceived as a senior individual contributor later.
* **Actionable vs. Vague Feedback:** The feedback that an engineer is "not technical enough" is often non-actionable gatekeeping. Effective feedback should be specific, such as requiring a deeper understanding of distributed systems or specific architecture trade-offs.
* **Strategic Visibility:** To be recognized for glue work, it must be framed as a leadership choice driven by technical judgment. Engineers should create artifacts, such as design proposals or strategic documentation, to make their impact visible and undeniable.
* **The "Stop Doing Glue" Strategy:** If an organization refuses to reward glue work despite its impact, the most effective short-term career move is to temporarily stop doing it and focus exclusively on high-visibility, "unarguably technical" tasks to secure a promotion.
* **Managerial Responsibility:** Managers must ensure that non-promotable work is distributed fairly across the entire team and that senior-level career ladders explicitly require the technical leadership skills demonstrated through glue work.
* **Long-term Career Pathing:** Moving from an "engineer" title to a non-technical role (like project management) can close doors, as industry bias often assumes technical skills disappear once the "engineer" title is removed from a resume.

title: Old Is the New New
description: Very interesting remainder about the bases and about how to identify these core ideas and the new improvements based on them. Understanding this process can help us to improve even faster if we learn and go deep in the bases.
notes:
Kevlin Henney argues that beneath the constant churn of technology trends lies a steady continuity of ideas, and that software as a discipline suffers from a weak sense of history, living in "a constant state of astonishment and rediscovery." His thesis is that most ideas we treat as new are decades old, and that if we recognized this constancy we could focus on the genuinely novel parts of each innovation rather than repeatedly reinventing the wheel. He frames this through Al Rashid's quote: "Everything has been said before, but since nobody listens we must always start again."

* **A weak cultural sense of history** — Henney's central premise is that practitioners can understand a surprising amount of new technology by asking "what is this like?", because the underlying ideas recur; software's poor historical memory means we keep rediscovering and reinventing instead of building on what is known.
* **Garbage collection and lambdas are ancient** — Lisp (sketched late 1950s, first implementation 1960) had garbage collection; Algol 68 (the year is in the name) had it too, plus expression orientation, `if`/`fi`, the abbreviation `int`, and anonymous procedures capturing variables by closure — i.e., lambda expressions in a procedural language. He dates lambda calculus as object-oriented (per William Cook) back to 1932: "programming like it's the 1930s."
* **Patterns as aggressive disregard of originality** — Citing Brian Foote, Henney says most of what we build is unoriginal; the small bit of originality is what deserves real labor. Most problems are already solved and we are poor at communicating those solutions.
* **"Worse is Better" misunderstood** — Richard Gabriel's 1990 idea (a famously bad name) does not mean shipping any old crap; it means an under-featured product that is rock-solid, fast, and small. "Worse" applies only to completeness/scope, choosing the smallest scope first — an incremental, iterative approach that predates Agile and RAD.

### Small is a virtue
* **Simplicity enables replacement** — A simple implementation can be discarded, replaced, understood, and changed; that disposability is one of the most important properties of good design.
* **Ward Cunningham's wiki** — The original wiki was ~150 lines of Perl; small enough to rewrite yourself yet with massive potential for extension (MediaWiki, Wikipedia). A corporate team would have spent 18 months and produced something far larger.
* **Lyons' Unix kernel** — John Lyons' annotated Unix (~9,500 lines of kernel code) delivered a whole operating system; Henney has seen single classes bigger than that doing far less. Most systems just take data, change format, do calculations, and put it out.
* **Constraints breed elegance** — Quoting Ritchie and Thompson (1970), severe size constraints on Unix encouraged "not only economy but a certain elegance of design." Like asking an artist for "a picture of anything," no boundaries is the worst thing for creative quality.

### Design, modularity, and process
* **Design is iterative and intellectual, not production** — Andy Kinslow at the 1968 NATO conference already stated design is iterative; Douglas Ross warned that specify-then-do thinking causes most troubles, predating Royce's misattributed 1970 waterfall paper. Jack Reeves (1992) crystallized that programming is a design activity, and the only true production phase — copying software — is a minute fraction of cost (Naur/Randell, 1968).
* **Margaret Hamilton coined "software engineering"** — In the early-to-mid 1960s working on Apollo, to distinguish it from hardware engineering and treat all engineering as part of one systems process with no hierarchy; she developed fault tolerance.
* **Modularity by volatility** — Parnas (1972) proposed decomposing systems around difficult design decisions likely to change, hiding each decision from others — organizing by stability/instability, not domain. Henney notes you can even put module boundaries around team disagreements. Microservices, components (Szyperski, 1997), and SOA repeat these themes; the only real difference between services and microservices is the word "micro" — and scope inevitably drifts (Perlis: every program becomes "Rococo and then rubble").
* **Performance and I/O are first-class concerns** — "There is no code faster than no code" and "the fastest I/O is no I/O"; one team got a 3x speedup by eliminating unnecessary I/O after only achieving 10% in two years. Distribution fights the speed of light, which is "not in a config file"; if offered the option not to put something on the network, take it.

### Testing, concurrency, and paradigms
* **Testing and TDD are old** — Alan Perlis (1968): testing should be interlaced with design, not used after it. "Middleware" and "unit test" appear in the 1968 proceedings. Alfred Aho describes writing tests before new awk features in the 1970s — TDD before its name. Hoare's 1969 precondition/trigger/postcondition (Given-When-Then) underpins both methods and test structure.
* **Threads and synchronization are the wrong tool for speed** — Quoting Russell Winder, processes with message passing (Erlang, Occam/CSP, the Actor model from Carl Hewitt 1970-73) avoid shared-memory synchronization stress. Henney's barb: synchronization exists to eliminate concurrency — "if somebody says we're doing threads to make things faster, why are you using locks to make things slower?" Java's synchronized collections showed designers "had no idea what they were doing."
* **Technical debt misread** — Ward Cunningham's 1992 metaphor (like a mortgage) says a little debt speeds development only if paid back promptly with a rewrite; "every minute spent on not-quite-right code counts as interest." Cunningham assumed it would be obviously bad to just increase the debt.
* **"The spec writes the program" recurs forever** — The CommitStrip cartoon's dream of specifications writing themselves is answered by Henney: a specification comprehensive and precise enough to generate code is already called code. Object orientation (Kay: messaging, local state retention, late binding), functional programming ("Excel is the world's most popular functional language"), and the actor-as-object debate all show paradigms as a continuous landscape without boundaries.
* **The oldest coordination model** — Doug McIlroy's 1964 note proposed coupling programs "like garden hoses" — the Unix pipe, a concatenative/dataflow model with continuous input-output processes. It took six years for Ken Thompson to find the pipe symbol (1970); good ideas sometimes just take a while to implement.
* **Recognize what is genuinely new** — Henney closes that we know more than we credit ourselves for, and would progress further by paying attention to the past to identify the truly novel part of each idea — rather than the cynical consultant who just does a global find-replace (object → component → service → micro) on the same PowerPoint each cycle.

title: Why people believe weird things
notes:
Michael Shermer, director of the Skeptics Society and publisher of Skeptic magazine, argues that belief in the paranormal, pseudo-science and the supernatural stems not from stupidity but from predictable features of how the human brain processes patterns, faces, statistics and language. Using a string of concrete demonstrations, he shows that skepticism is less a fixed position than a method: a way of always asking "what's the more likely explanation?" and of keeping track of misses as well as hits.

* **Skepticism is a method, not a stance.** Shermer frames science as a verb—"a way of looking for natural explanations for all phenomena"—and rejects "science vs. religion" as a category error, comparing it to asking whether science and plumbing are compatible.
* **Hits get remembered, misses get forgotten.** Psychics, astrologers and tarot readers thrive because people recall the hits and forget the misses, whereas science keeps the whole database and checks whether hits exceed what chance predicts.
* **The Quadro 2000 Dowser Rod demo.** A $900 plastic rod with a Radio Shack antenna sold to high-school administrators to "dowse" for marijuana in lockers scored 50 percent in a two-box test—exactly a coin-flip.
* **Always ask: what's more likely?** Faced with a crop circle promoting skeptic.com, he asks whether multi-dimensional beings crossed interstellar space or a Skeptic reader used Photoshop—"before we say something is out of this world, we should first make sure that it's not in this world."
* **Cheap fakes need no technology.** His own "UFO" photo over Pasadena, shot with a throwaway Kodak Instamatic, is simply a Buick hubcap tossed off-camera.
* **Data and theory must balance.** Galileo, lacking a theory of planetary rings plus grainy data, concluded Saturn had "three bodies"; Huygens solved it in 1655 with both a good theory and finer data.

### Pattern-seeking and seeing faces
* **Humans are pattern-seeking animals programmed to see faces.** The 1976 "Face on Mars" sparked a movement to photograph the region as Martian architecture; the 2001 close-up degrades fine-grain data into coarse-grain, yet the face persists because evolution wired us to detect faces.
* **Religious iconography in random textures.** A Tennessee "nun bun," Our Lady of Guadalupe, the Virgin Mary on a São Paulo window, and a grilled cheese sandwich a Las Vegas casino bought on eBay for $28,500 (which looks more like a 1940s starlet).
* **The Clearwater Virgin Mary, investigated firsthand.** Walking around the back of the two-and-a-half-story image revealed the effect appeared wherever a sprinkler head met a palm tree—"a miracle of Mary, or a miracle of Marge?"

### Auditory illusions and priming
* **Backward-masking and the power of suggestion.** Playing Led Zeppelin's "Stairway to Heaven" backwards, the audience hears only "Satan" until he primes their brains with the supposed words—after which the hidden message becomes impossible to miss.

### Intelligent design and a hopeful close
* **"Then a miracle occurs."** Citing the Sidney Harris cartoon, invoking a miracle explains nothing and offers nothing to test—it is the end of the conversation for intelligent design, whereas placeholders like dark matter mark the beginning of science's causal chain.
* **Katie Melua and self-correcting science.** The singer's lyric "12 billion light-years from the edge / that's a guess" was corrected after Simon Singh noted we know it is 13.7 billion light years with error bars; Melua re-cut the song to the corrected version—a model of revising belief in light of better data.

title: From startups to big-business: Using functional programming techniques to transform line-of-business applications
description: CQRS, Event Sourcing, Microservices
notes:
Adopting functional programming and event sourcing allows organizations to build highly resilient, scalable systems where state is treated as an immutable stream of events, providing a "time machine" for data recovery. This architectural approach ensures that complex business logic remains maintainable and that systems can withstand and recover from catastrophic infrastructure failures.

* **Core Architectural Pillars:** The system is built on three main tenets: functional programming (specifically using F#), treating microservices as stream processors, and comprehensive event sourcing.
* **Command Processor Pattern:** Decouples intent from facts. **Commands** (e.g., *ReserveInventory*) represent an intent to change state and are validated against current state. **Events** (e.g., *InventoryReserved*) represent the immutable fact of what occurred.
* **Functional State Management:** State is reconstructed by defining a **Zero State** and using an **Apply Function** to fold a sequence of events into a current state. This allows for a "time machine" capability where state can be rebuilt for any point in time.
* **Snapshots for Performance:** To avoid rebuilding state from thousands of events, the system periodically saves **Snapshots** of the state at specific sequence numbers. The command processor only needs to load the latest snapshot and any subsequent events.
* **Granular Stream Design:** Defining event streams as narrowly as possible (e.g., *Item + Warehouse ID* instead of just *Item*) reduces contention, minimizes the cost of imposing order, and significantly increases total system throughput.
* **Optimistic Concurrency:** Every state update uses a **logical clock (sequence number)**. If a write fails due to a conflicting sequence number, the system re-retrieves the updated state and re-executes the command logic.
* **Scalable Event Storage:** Transitioning from specialized event store products to **Cosmos DB** provides the necessary performance, partitioning, and geo-replication required for global scale.
* **Hybrid Communication:** Use **Kafka** or Event Hubs for asynchronous background processing and **HTTP** for synchronous, low-latency customer interactions (like real-time checkout reservations) to ensure immediate feedback.
* **Discriminated Unions for Reliability:** Using F# discriminated unions for message types ensures exhaustive pattern matching; the compiler forces developers to handle every possible command or event type, reducing runtime errors.
* **Resiliency Through Immutability:** By treating inputs and state as immutable and using stateless functions for processing, the system is inherently more testable and resilient to data loss.

title: Our Responsibility to Defeat Mass Surveillance
description: Inspirational talk about some of our responsibilities as developers.
notes:
Martin Fowler and Erik Dörnenburg argue that software developers bear direct ethical responsibility for what their software does to users and to the world, and that mass surveillance is a grave challenge the profession itself created and must now help defeat. Building on the original agile vision of programmers collaborating on the "what" rather than just coding handed-down stories, they contend that as developers gain domain knowledge and influence they also take on responsibility for the consequences of their code. The core call to action: make encryption and privacy the default so that the "bothersome people" essential to a free society are not left exposed.

* **The agile vision was bidirectional, not top-down**: Fowler's biggest disappointment with the agile movement is that stories still flow one way, from business/analysts to programmers. The intent was a collaborative circle where developers, with domain knowledge, help shape requirements — illustrated by the Amazon programmer who built shopping-cart recommendations against the marketing VP's objection and proved via A/B tests it increased revenue.
* **Knowledge precedes responsibility**: Developers should learn fundamental design principles (true across technologies and time) and their business domain rather than chasing specific technologies. Knowledge is what lets them play the conversational role agile intended.
* **Dark patterns make developers complicit**: Software that nudges users against their own interest — sneaking insurance into a shopping cart, or a Windows printer driver that declares a cartridge empty while the same cartridge prints fine under Linux — implicates the developer who wrote it. "I was told to do this" is rejected as an excuse.
* **The "weak excuse" of needing a job**: Fowler stresses that Western software workers are privileged, earn above-average money, and can find new jobs easily, so "my boss made me do it" is a weak justification for building harmful software.
* **Responsibility extends to the world, not just users**: Developers should ask whether their work does good — not in a grandiose sense, but in providing genuinely useful services (even printer software helps someone print house paperwork). Crucially, what matters is not whether Fowler or Dörnenburg approves, but that each developer makes that judgment and owns the choice.

### Why mass surveillance matters

* **"I have nothing to hide" is flawed because it centers the self**: Surveillance harms those who annoy the powerful — investigative journalists exposing corrupt mayors/police, and activists driving social change. Examples: Alabama demanding NAACP membership lists during the Montgomery Bus Boycott to choke off funding to civil-rights activists.
* **Time changes what must be hidden**: Uganda's attempted law criminalizing homosexuality (and even non-denunciation) shows that information openly shared today can become dangerous later — and the internet does not forget.
* **Free societies depend on the "bothersome people"**: Without investigative journalists and activists, citizens cannot vote intelligently or drive the changes (women's vote, racial equality, LGBT rights) that troublemakers historically forced. Surveillance can shut them — or their funders — down.

### The email case and developer choices

* **Commercial motives diverge from user needs**: Email is built to make money (often for shareholders maximizing value), so the monetization model scans content for advertising rather than giving users the encryption they would choose if asked — email today is a postcard, not a sealed letter.
* **"Not my problem" is the recurring abdication**: Developers say "someone else will do it," business people say "not our target group," stakeholders invoke "free markets" — all restating that they accept no responsibility.
* **Centralization makes surveillance cheap**: The big five email providers hold ~42% of global accounts; in Germany the top eight reach ~88%, in the US four companies reach ~90%. A handful of legal arrangements exposes most of the world's email. The trend from proprietary clients to open-source (Thunderbird) toward Software-as-a-Service web mail reverses prior gains and moves data into others' clouds.
* **US cross-border data claims**: The Obama administration contended US companies must comply with warrants even for data owned and processed entirely abroad (Microsoft fighting it in court; IBM building a German data center). The worrying part is the idea was even floated.
* **Encryption itself is treated as suspicious**: Per Snowden revelations, GCHQ retained all encrypted email indefinitely on the bet that future compute could decrypt it. Using secure transport makes you stand out — so people who "have nothing to hide" and send plaintext make the bothersome people more conspicuous.
* **Make privacy the default to protect everyone**: Privacy is a civilizational achievement and should be the norm, not a special need; Tim Bray's framing of privacy as a dial, not binary. If most email is encrypted, surveillance costs rise and targets blend in. Surveillance also chills behavior (people self-censor knowing they are watched).
* **False positives and trajectories**: A 99.9%-accurate test would flag ~5,000 "terrorists" among Denmark's 5.6M people — almost all false positives. Combining opaque pricing (variable hotel/flight prices) with state data seeping into the private sector (the Stratfor/PETA/Coca-Cola hack, where a private intelligence VP knew of a classified FBI investigation) yields scenarios like insurers raising premiums based on email metadata suggesting hereditary cancer — invisibly, with no way to compare.

### What developers can do

* **Pixelated and the AGPL choice**: ThoughtWorks started an open-source, encrypted, decentralized email solution (then early-stage, on GitHub, not production-ready). As a US company they won't host it, shipping puppet scripts so organizations run their own servers. They chose AGPL over Apache 2 specifically because the SaaS model means changes are never "distributed" — AGPL forces source disclosure even for service modifications, keeping changes in the open.
* **The hard problem is UX, not crypto**: Encryption libraries largely work and the fundamentals aren't broken; the real challenge is user experience. So developers who think they can't contribute because they lack cryptography skills are wrong — UX, documentation, and ordinary work matter most.
* **Concrete actions**: contribute to privacy-increasing open-source projects; use and adopt encrypted email to raise the encrypted proportion of traffic; push HTTPS everywhere; educate family and friends. History (women's suffrage) shows seemingly impossible movements succeed through sustained effort.
* **"Responsible over maximizing gain"**: Echoing the agile manifesto, Dörnenburg frames the core decision: when achieving the most profitable business model requires a dark pattern, do we stay responsible first and then maximize gain, or maximize gain and not care? Mass surveillance is on the profession — technologists built naive plaintext email and HTTP without TLS, took the easy way out, then blamed users with RSA/DSA key complexity. The job is to make countermeasures so easy that ordinary users need do nothing special. It is our responsibility, our choice.

title: Napoleon DevOps and Delivering Business Value
description: The importance of leveraging DevOps short feedback loops to evolve continuously the system.
notes:
Mark Schwartz, AWS Enterprise Strategist and former CIO of US Citizenship and Immigration Services, argues that the biggest impediment to DevOps in large enterprises is not technical but organizational: DevOps shortens the lead time from code commit to production, yet it sits inside a business decision cycle that remains painfully long. Using Tolstoy's depiction of Napoleon at the Battle of Borodino, he shows that good outcomes depend not on doing things faster but on aligning the decision cycle with the speed at which reality actually changes.

* **The Napoleon analogy frames the core problem.** At Borodino, Napoleon directs the battle from a fort a mile away on a misty, smoke-filled day, relying on messengers who arrive with outdated news. In one scene he orders troops to cross a bridge the Russians have already retaken and burned. Tolstoy's point: Napoleon's orders have almost nothing to do with what is really happening on the battlefield.
* **The real impediment is an impedance mismatch between cycle times.** Napoleon has a long decision cycle while the action moves quickly, so he cannot control it. Enterprises have the same mismatch: DevOps drives lead times down religiously, but it is embedded in a business with a very long lead time.
* **DevOps only covers a small slice of the value stream.** It runs from code commit to deployment and use in production, with monitoring and telemetry confirming it works. The full business cycle — from idea to impact, or concept to cash — is far longer.
* **The long front end dominates.** Big enterprise change typically starts with recognizing business or mission needs, bundling them into a large project or program, building a business case, passing it through investment governance and a steering committee, and finding resources — only then is code written, with downstream work needed to harvest the value.
* **Speeding up only the DevOps part is not enough.** For large capital investments going through this drawn-out process, accelerating the part DevOps covers makes little difference; the entire lead time must shrink to preserve nimbleness, agility, and the ability to run experiments.

### The wrong mental model for uncertainty

* **Business cases assume a predictable world.** They project revenues and costs and assume the estimate lands reasonably close to reality. But when uncertainty is extreme — a 60% confidence interval might require plus or minus 200% — projecting numbers and deciding on them is the wrong mental model.
* **Plan-based execution becomes self-deception.** When decisions rest on a business case, executing exactly to plan (spend the budgeted million, return the projected ten million) is highly valued. In a world you know will not go as planned, pretending it will is willfully misleading yourself into bad decisions.
* **Governance exists for two real reasons.** Oversight processes — business cases, reviews, strategic alignment — exist to reduce execution risk and to allocate capital to the best opportunities. In Napoleon's uncertain world, making those detailed decisions in advance achieves neither well.
* **Uncertainty is opportunity, not just hazard.** When the unexpected happens it either destroys you or makes you better; the difference is your ability to seize it. Managing risk today means having the agility, speed, flexibility, and inventiveness to turn surprises into opportunities — so risk is essentially the same as not being agile across the whole value stream.

### Three models to shrink the long cycle

* **Product model.** Decentralize decisions into product teams that own their roadmaps, as AWS does across its ~125 services. Roughly 90-95% of the roadmap comes directly from customer requests; governance is local, with central concerns (like an imperative to cut costs and pass savings to customers) exerting influence rather than control.
* **Budget model.** Schwartz rejects the myth of separating "keeping the lights on" from "innovation" — much maintenance is actually innovation, since software keeps doing exactly what it did when bought, and changing it (often refactoring or a Strangler pattern) is remaking the decision that it still fits the business. Fund everything as ongoing maintenance via budgets pushed down the hierarchy, so teams control spend without long approval cycles or steering committees.
* **Objective model (e-verify at USCIS).** Anticipating e-verify would need to scale massively for political reasons — limited by the human, not the cloud, part of the system — the team set five business objectives (raise the 98.6% automated handling rate, raise the 70 cases-per-day adjudication rate, raise the 40% registration completion rate, plus two others).
* **Objectives passed to teams, not requirements.** Translating objectives into requirements adds risk (the requirements might be wrong) and ties the hands of innovators. Instead, cross-functional teams (developers, ops, infrastructure, testers, security, and business operations) were given an objective like "70 cases a day — make it go up," free to change business processes or write software, reporting the new number every two weeks.
* **Continuous, evidence-based reinvestment.** Every two weeks the oversight body re-decided whether to keep funding each objective based on results. When shopping-cart abandonment plateaued despite sensible efforts, the team recommended stopping investment and reallocating the money — surprising the committee, since projects never return money. The four-year project finished in about two and a half years and returned the rest.
* **Central control is preserved, not lost.** Decentralizing decisions to teams while agreeing on objectives gives the center more meaningful control than starting with a fixed requirement set, because resources can be diverted continuously based on what is actually working.
* **The lesson from Napoleon.** Shrinking cycle times is not about doing things faster — the army takes as long as it takes — it is about the quality of decisions. Napoleon decides badly because his decision cycle is out of sync with events; an enterprise must make its business decision cycle fast enough to keep good central control and judgment while the action moves quickly, which is what Tolstoy ultimately teaches us about DevOps.

title: How to Deliver Quality Software Against All Odds GOTO 2024
description: This podcast features Daniel Terhorst-North, a prominent figure in the software development world, reflecting on 20 years of industry changes and sharing his insights on topics ranging from Agile and DevOps to product management and organizational flow. Drawing on his experiences at Thoughtworks and beyond, Terhorst-North highlights the importance of connecting business needs with technical implementation and emphasizes the value of building evolvable systems with "simplexity" in mind.
notes:
Effective software delivery is a **socio-technical endeavor** that requires balancing technical excellence—such as continuous delivery and domain-driven design—with strong product management to ensure you are "baking the right cake" for the business. You should remember that the most impactful improvements in engineering come from **aligning code with the language of the domain** and fostering a human-centered culture where communication and organizational flow take priority over technical complexity.

*   **Agile Normalization:** Agile practices have become so ubiquitous over the last 20 years that they are now the industry standard, moving from a "maverick" approach to a normalized, everyday part of software delivery.
*   **Cross-Functional Teams:** The industry has shifted away from siloed gated processes toward cross-functional teams that handle a "full-ish stack" to deliver capabilities rather than just isolated components.
*   **Economic Impact of Architecture:** Efficient code and "green software" are economic imperatives; well-architected systems reduce server resources, electricity consumption, and operational costs, especially in cloud environments.
*   **Serverless Paradigm:** Serverless architecture should be viewed as "shared nothing" rather than strictly stateless. Optimizing the startup and shared components of functions can enable massive scaling at extremely low costs.
*   **Go-Style Testing:** Unlike the traditional xUnit school that stops at the first failure, Go's testing allows for a "roadworthiness" approach where multiple failures are registered in a single run, providing a comprehensive list of what needs fixing.
*   **Domain-Driven Design (DDD):** Incorporating the language of the business domain directly into the code makes complex business rules explicit and allows developers and stakeholders to communicate without needing a translator.
*   **Innovation through Necessity:** Many industry standards, such as CI/CD pipelines, blue-green deployments, and single deployable artifacts, were invented to solve high-pressure, "impossible" delivery challenges in real-world projects.
*   **Theory of Constraints:** Bottlenecks in software delivery often shift: once development becomes agile, the bottleneck moves to operations (release), and once operations is automated, it moves upstream to product management (idea batching).
*   **Product vs. Project Funding:** Project-based funding focuses on cost and perceived risk, while product-based funding acts like a venture investment, prioritizing continuous value delivery and reducing "value at risk."
*   **Socio-Technical Systems:** Programming is a socio-technical activity that is as much about human communication and team energy as it is about technology; technical excellence alone is insufficient for long-term organizational success.
*   **Baking the Right Cake:** High-performing organizations must balance engineering excellence ("baking the cake right") with effective product strategy ("baking the right cake") to avoid efficiently building the wrong thing.
*   **Redefining the "Best" Programmer:** Mastery in programming is not strictly tied to formal education; a computer science degree is neither a requirement for being a top-tier programmer nor a guarantee of expertise.

title: Story telling - The art of delighting your audience
Shared Mental Models Part 2
description: Part 2 of "Shared Mental Models"
notes:
Software development is far more than a technical craft or individual artistic expression; it is the practice of **"symmathesy,"** where diverse, collaborative teams learn together to create impactful systems that fundamentally reshape the world. This collective process transcends simple coding, functioning as a **living system** where people and software evolve together to achieve goals far greater than any individual could reach alone.

*   **Agile's Core Contribution**: The software industry's greatest contribution is the ability for heterogeneous teams to collaborate toward a common goal rather than just performing common tasks.
*   **Transition from Trade to Impact**: Similar to the Renaissance shift from guild-based tradespeople to artists, software is moving from simple technical proficiency to a focus on the individual distinction and the impact the work has on the audience.
*   **Medium vs. Product**: Programming languages (like Java or Pascal) are merely mediums—comparable to acrylic paint or marble—used to produce software that changes lives and human capabilities.
*   **Software is Not a Craft**: Complex systems like Facebook cannot be created through traditional one-on-one apprenticeship; they require thinking bigger about the entire organization and its goals.
*   **Software as the "Next Thing After Art"**: Software is a unique medium—more moldable than plastic and stronger than metal—that can impact millions of people instantly through scalability, representing a new evolutionary stage beyond traditional art.
*   **Symmathesy**: Software development is defined as the practice of "symmathesy," which is mutual learning within a living system. Both the creators and the software they build are part of an interconnected, evolving system.
*   **Collective Effort**: Innovation and progress are not the result of individual genius but the product of groups, including "mobs," teams, and communities working together to move ideas forward.
*   **The Role of Diverse Perspectives**: Creating meaningful systems requires the active participation of business people, designers, and product owners in the room with developers to ensure the software is truly useful and impactful.

title: The Joy of Building Large Scale Systems
description: Suhail Patel discusses the art and practice of building systems from core principles with a focus on how this can be done in practice within teams and organisations. Very interesting talk with many details about system implementation, taking into account the changes that have occurred in the hardware.
notes:
Modern hardware has become so incredibly fast that **software is now the primary bottleneck** in system performance, requiring engineers to adopt "**mechanical sympathy**" and leverage modern programming tools to eliminate long-standing inefficiencies. By updating legacy software patterns and embracing low-level optimizations, organizations can achieve massive gains in **speed, cost-efficiency, and environmental sustainability**.

* **Hardware-Software Gap:** Modern systems often fail to exploit the full potential of current hardware because they are built on lessons and design patterns from an era of spinning disks and slower networks.
* **The Bottleneck Shift:** While hardware latency and throughput have improved by orders of magnitude (e.g., NVMe SSDs, 25Gb networks, massive RAM), software has become the primary bottleneck.
* **Mechanical Sympathy:** High-performance engineering requires an understanding of how software interacts with hardware, specifically regarding CPU caches, memory alignment, and sequential versus random IO.
* **Environmental Impact:** Approximately 50% of data center greenhouse gas emissions are attributed to software and infrastructure inefficiencies, such as serialization overhead and redundant CPU cycles.
* **Thread-per-Core Architecture:** Moving away from "shared everything" designs to thread-per-core models reduces CPU context switches and synchronization overhead (locking), significantly improving tail latency.
* **Core Affinity:** Pinning network requests and application threads to specific CPU cores (using tools like IRQ affinity) can reduce tail latencies by over 70%.
* **Modern IO Interfaces:** Transitioning from traditional asynchronous IO to `io_uring` in the Linux kernel can lead to massive increases in throughput for both disk and network operations.
* **Systems Programming Languages:** Languages like Rust and Zig offer memory safety and performance close to the metal with significantly better developer ergonomics than C or C++.
* **Hot Path Optimization:** Replacing critical sections of code in high-level languages (like Python) with C or Rust modules can yield 10x to 75x speed improvements for specific tasks like data parsing.
* **Modern Runtime Features:** Utilizing updated runtimes and features—such as Java’s ZGC for sub-millisecond garbage collection pauses—can remove long-standing performance barriers without rewriting entire applications.
* **Production Profiling:** Low-overhead profiling (e.g., eBPF, Java Flight Recorder) should be run in production to identify where CPU time is actually spent, rather than relying on intuition.
* **Organizational Barriers:** Technical debt is often maintained by "faux barriers," such as organizational resistance to upgrading to the latest Long-Term Support (LTS) versions of languages and runtimes.
* **Complexity Reduction:** The most efficient and least complex systems are those that can perform the required workload on the minimum number of machines.

title: Climbing Off The Ladder, Before We Fall Off (spotify-happiness-retention)
description: Another good talk from Sopitify crew. This time focused on creative solutions to generate/maintain/discover the intrinsic motivation of each software/ops engineer at the company.
notes:
Chris Angove, a chapter lead at Spotify (managing 10-12 engineers), gave this talk at QCon New York 2014 after moving from Amplify (~120 engineers) to Spotify (then 600-700 engineers worldwide, with 40 million active and 10 million paying users). His core thesis is that the traditional linear career ladder is fundamentally broken for engineers because it promotes good, happy people out of the roles they excel at and enjoy. He argues that career growth should not require taking on more responsibility and control, and proposes a non-linear model of "add-ons" that lets engineers grow, gain recognition, and stay challenged without changing rung. He explicitly frames the talk as a work in progress and an invitation to start a conversation, not as a finished answer.

* **Alignment vs. autonomy as the organizational backdrop**: Spotify, agile since ~2007, found that "agile with a capital A" was getting in the way as it scaled past 150-200 engineers. Angove rejects the false dichotomy of strong-boss alignment vs. "do whatever, get shit done" autonomy, framing them as two axes. The goal is the top-right "innovative organization": high alignment and high autonomy, where management "presents the problem and requests that the team solves the problem."

* **The Spotify structure (tribes, squads, chapters, guilds)**: Tribes are like departments/areas of focus (e.g. partner tribe, music player tribe); squads are cross-functional teams with a PO; chapters group people of the same expertise under a chapter lead who mentors and handles salary/time-off but does NOT assign work (work comes from the squad); guilds are loose cross-cutting groups for sharing ideas to avoid siloing. The structure is a matrix, and Angove admits reality is messy: "in some cases our chapters are not well-balanced... we actually have chapters that cross-cut tribes, so now we're breaking some of our own rules."

* **The linear ladder's benefits are real but managerial**: Each rung is clearly defined, value to the company is legible to a CTO who doesn't know everyone, and promotion is an easy public way to deliver money AND respect/recognition. Angove concedes "the ladder isn't bad, it has its place, but it can't be the only thing," noting it ultimately "optimizes for the manager, not the engineer."

* **The ladder's core failure — promoting people out of what they're good at**: Engineers ready for promotion after 5-6 years are often told the next step is managing people, which many don't want; they hit a ceiling and either grudgingly manage badly or leave. "Maybe you're a great engineer, sorry, we have to move on now because we promoted you." There's also no way to try a role and fail safely: "I can't just go back to being an engineer. There goes any respect or responsibility I had, because now I've failed."

* **The math problem of flat structures**: Spotify's structure is only four steps from newest hire to CTO (engineer → chapter lead → tribe lead → CTO), with chapters of roughly 8-12 people. With a flat structure, "the only way to get a promotion is for me to quit, or one of my peers to quit" — 12 people and one job — pushing good engineers to leave for the startup scene.

* **The dual-track ladder is an improvement but still flawed**: Splitting into a management track and a technical track reduces skill/interest mismatches and keeps easy public recognition, but it still assumes growth means more responsibility, still accounts for groups not individuals (works for ~80%), and "gets muddled" — he references a Google thread where he "stopped counting after about 12 different titles."

* **The personal origin — his father**: Angove, the first in a blue-collar family to get a white-collar job, cites his father, who spent 45 years doing skilled labor in the auto industry, changed roles a dozen times, kept learning, became a valued mentor and "much more valuable at the end of his career than at the beginning" — without ever needing to become a manager. "Why is it so bad to plateau at being an engineering manager... why do we look at it as such?"

* **Add-ons: the proposed non-linear model**: The linear ladder remains (for the ~10% it suits), but engineers layer on "add-ons" — engineer-driven growth opportunities that must also deliver business value. The guiding principle: "role should be defined by institutional need, not career achievement" — hence one engineer level whether you have 17 years' experience or are fresh out of college. People typically run one or two active add-ons at a time.

* **Add-ons must be company-supported (the Amplify lesson)**: At Amplify a "merit badges" scheme failed because it gave engineers a checklist with no support — "we never once went up to an engineer and said how am I going to help you get there." Spotify's version requires manager involvement: defining the goal, a concrete success metric, and the help needed (training, workshops, and crucially TIME during the work week, not 60-80 hour weeks). Failure of an add-on must be acceptable: managers "can't freak out every time somebody tries something and it doesn't work out."

* **Example add-ons**: Speaker (the requirement is speaking publicly outside Spotify at least four times a year — Angove was working on this one himself), road manager (project management for rare large cross-office projects, since Spotify has no project-manager role), agile coach, technology evangelist (e.g. championing React), architect (Spotify has only two people with the architect title, who set alignment with little endowed authority while teams do their own architecture), mentor, technical writer, trainer, and open sourcer.

* **Run as a Spotify-style experiment, with guardrails against gaming**: "Create a hypothesis, create an experiment, measure it, then decide." This is "Add-ons Part Two" — the 2013 version didn't fail outright but got low adoption because it lacked the ability for engineers to create their own add-ons. Add-ons are deliberately NOT tied to fixed compensation (no "$5,000 per add-on") and there are no physical badges, to avoid people collecting add-ons competitively — "if you're doing 14, when are you getting your coding done?" The low manager-to-employee ratio is the check: "that's calling the bullshit, on both sides."

* **Closing call to action**: "We don't have enough people in engineering positions as it is, and if we're driving people from those positions, what the hell are we doing?" He stresses each company's implementation will differ by culture, structure, and size, but the underlying problem of promoting people out of jobs they're good at and happy with is common — and he asks the audience for their ideas to solve it together.

title: Theory of Constraints”, Agileee conference, 2009
description: A must for any software developer that wants to really understand Agile and Lean.
notes:
Applying Goldratt's Theory of Constraints (from The Goal) to software: pursue three measures at once — increase throughput, decrease inventory, decrease operating expenses — and relentlessly find and improve the system's bottleneck, because the bottleneck alone sets the throughput of the whole system.

- It's easy to hit two of the three measures and wreck the third (hire the whole world; or just close the doors). The discipline is doing all three together — and it's the lens for being "agile about being agile": if a practice change doesn't improve at least one without hurting the others, maybe don't do it.
- Inventory in software is costly and hard to quantify: dead code you must side-step and fear to delete (interdependence), and "unshipped features" — built but unused — that add inertia (Microsoft Word can't simplify because of its feature mass).
- There is always a bottleneck (the slowest part turning a feature request into a running tested feature); improving any non-bottleneck changes nothing. Improve the bottleneck by routing work around it, or cloning it (more machines, or teaching a second/third person).
- Agile practices serve these goals. Sitting together reduces human bottlenecks (spread knowledge), enables the "expert in earshot" pattern, and improves information flow (more paths = less chance any one becomes the bottleneck); specializing generalists add paths.
- System speed depends on the slowest part, not the fastest: raising a junior's level (or pairing the rock star to teach) helps throughput more than freeing the star to go fast alone (like making the least fuel-efficient cars 50% better beats doubling the most efficient).
- The design practices fall straight out of queuing theory: when a later step makes you redo an earlier one, move some of the later step earlier — testing→code becomes test-first, adding design gives TDD (the test is your first design decision), adding analysis gives BDD/ATDD. Design-up-front is a continuum; TDD just nudges you left of where you'd naturally over-design.
