* **Join at runtime in the client.** For one-to-n cases (one customer, many orders, e.g. an order-history page), query the customer service for the record then the order service for matching orders, joining in the application, what any multi-source web page already does.

### Transactions: workflows and sagas
* **Replace distributed transactions with sagas.** A monolithic database gives trivial ACID transactions across entities; once split into services this is hard. Do NOT use two-phase commit, it kills scalability. Instead model the transaction as a **saga**: a state machine of individually atomic steps connected by events (update A emits event, update B emits event, and so on). Rollback is achieved through **compensating transactions** that run in reverse, undoing each step back up the chain. More complicated than a DB transaction but a working existence proof of transaction-like behavior in a distributed world. Real-world analogues: payment processing across issuers and banks, and multi-level expense-approval workflows that can be rejected and re-approved up and down the chain.
* **Serverless fits saga steps.** Functions-as-a-service (Lambda, Cloud Functions) are an ideal fit for the lightweight, stateless, event-triggered logic in saga steps, often just 10-20 lines that consume an event and emit others.
* **No silver bullet.** Microservices and serverless are not the answer to every problem; they are tools in the toolbox. A healthy company combines microservices, serverless, and other techniques. The closing recommendation: small teams with clear responsibility, TDD and continuous delivery, a DevOps "you build it, you run it" culture, and microservices as the technical approach at sufficient scale.

title: Software Design: Why, When & How
description: Kent Beck on the questions Design Patterns left out — the why, when and how of software design — framed in economics, options, and three human skills (tolerance for ambiguity, waiting, and treating design as a social process).
notes:
Design Patterns taught us the "what" of software design (a vocabulary for configurations of elements), but Kent Beck argues the harder, neglected questions are why, when and how — and answering them well is mostly about economics and human skills, not catalogues of patterns. There are no simplistic rules.

- Software design is "beneficially relating elements" (elements at every scale — service to variable; Yourdon & Constantine's Structured Design gave us coupling and cohesion). Coupling = a change to one element forces a change to another; cost is dominated by rippling changes, so design reduces coupling — but reducing coupling has its own rising cost, so you aim for the minimum of the two curves.
- Why design at all: (1) economics — net present value (earn sooner, spend later, so defer design) vs option value (more ways to change the software is worth more, which argues for doing work sooner); (2) personal satisfaction (taming "that function"); (3) service to the next person who reads the code.
- When: design pays off the instant before you need the change. Forces pushing it LATER — NPV, opportunity cost, and lack of knowledge (premature abstraction is as bad as premature optimization; distrust the rule of three). Forces pushing it SOONER — option value, the batching/context-in-your-head economy, and the human need for closure. The balance differs per team, per project, per month.
- How: usually big changes in small, safe steps (each step yields feedback), but occasionally a giant leap when the steps would be too many/costly. In either case the critical risk-mitigation is running the old and new design simultaneously (e.g. a new pricing service run in parallel, ramping traffic and comparing results) — "over a weekend" is the warning phrase to fear.
- Three learnable skills programmers lack: tolerance for ambiguity (let the design stay undecided — "the suspense is terrible, I hope it lasts"); waiting deliberately (the opposite of ignoring — like an expert Go player who adds complexity rather than prematurely resolving it; let your code show archaeological layers and remove them when the time comes); and treating design as a social process (sequence the design to what the team can absorb; pick names for the reader). A hard-to-test thing is a design problem, not a testing problem.

title: Product Tank Madrid: Focus on Impact with John Cutler - World Product Day 2020
description: Excellent talk. John presents all the learnings he had during the last three years, using his great article as a starting point (<https://cutle.fish/blog/12-signs-youre-working-in-a-feature-factory>). Lots of knowledge and tips to introduce product thinking.
notes:
True impact in product development is achieved by shifting from a "feature factory" mindset of constant output to a culture of outcomes, where teams prioritize measuring for learning and demonstrating value through small, visible experiments. Success depends on reducing work-in-progress to create space for discovery and establishing a shared language of strategy that connects product behaviors directly to the broader goals of the business.

*   **Show, Don't Tell:** Most executives and team members have never seen a high-performing product team in action. To build belief, you must demonstrate success through small, rapid experiments rather than trying to convince others through theory or slides.
*   **Measure to Learn vs. Measure to Control:** There is a critical distinction between using metrics to grade, rank, or justify decisions and using them to reduce uncertainty. Focus on building a culture where measurement is a tool for improvement and collaborative learning.
*   **Side-by-Side Practice:** Impact-focused product development is a skill that requires hands-on practice. Experienced leaders should work directly alongside teams to model how to ask better questions and shape work, rather than deciding strategy in a silo.
*   **Manage Work-in-Progress (WIP):** High WIP is a primary driver of the "Feature Factory" symptoms. When teams are overloaded, they lose the patience and capacity to experiment. Visualizing work at the "mission" or "outcome" level is necessary to see how over-commitment impacts throughput and morale.
*   **Strategy as a Shared Language:** A product strategy is not a roadmap; it is a shared language around value. It must provide the connective tissue between specific customer behaviors (outcomes) and broader business goals like revenue or churn.
*   **Explain the "So What":** You must evangelize the business value of working in an outcome-oriented way. Since shipping a pre-agreed roadmap is often perceived as the "safest" path in a corporate environment, you must demonstrate why focusing on impact leads to more sustainable growth.
*   **Acknowledge and Unlearn:** Many companies become "Feature Factories" because that high-output approach initially made them successful. Moving toward impact requires acknowledging that past success while "unlearning" structures and incentives that no longer work as the product grows in complexity.
*   **Visualize the Mission:** To shift focus, move beyond tracking individual tasks and start visualizing the progress of higher-level missions and bets. This helps the entire organization understand the data and the purpose behind the work.

title: Altitude NYC 2018: Observability workshop
description: A practitioner's observability workshop: observability as a system attribute, the black/white/grey-box spectrum, signals as observations, and the three pillars (metrics, tracing, logging) as emergent consumption patterns.
notes:
Observability is best treated, least-controversially, as a system attribute (Baron Schwartz) — like resiliency or availability — so there are different techniques to achieve it, classified from a practitioner's point of view rather than by chasing a single perfect definition.

- It's an umbrella term with many framings: the union/superset of testing and monitoring (Cindy Sridharan), a manifesto of criteria (Honeycomb: don't pre-aggregate, high cardinality, structured logging), a superset of monitoring atop the testing pyramid, and a reactive/proactive split (alerting/overview vs debugging/profiling/dependency analysis). Going too far down the definitional rabbit hole becomes self-parody.
- Techniques sit on a black-box ↔ white-box spectrum: black box probes from outside with no special access (Nagios/Icinga, CPU/memory of a process), white box exposes internal state explicitly (statsd → Graphite, goroutine counts, GC pause times), with grey box in between (HAProxy stats, an HTTP proxy counting 503s in the data path).
- All signals are fundamentally the same: an observation = a number captured at a point in time with associated metadata (some implicit, some explicit). Even a log event fits — its key/values are metadata, the number is 1 (it happened), with a timestamp.
- With a magical zero-cost system that interpreted all raw observations at query/read time, you could stop there. Because no such system exists, we make engineering compromises — imbuing observations with semantic meaning to enable specific consumption workflows.
- Hence the three pillars — metrics, tracing, logging — understood not as fundamental types but as emergent patterns of consumption that, in turn, shape how we produce, ship and store the signals; they're the product of an optimization between how operators want to introspect and what technology can do at scale.

title: Success is a continuous journey
notes:
Richard St. John argues that the most dangerous moment in any career is the instant we believe we have arrived. His thesis is that success is not a one-way street with a finish line but a continuous journey, and that the same eight principles that get us to the top are exactly what we must keep doing to stay there.

* **The one-way-street fallacy is the root cause.** We treat success as a destination: we do everything that leads up to it, decide we've "made it," sit back in our comfort zone, and stop doing the very things that made us successful. From there "it doesn't take long to go downhill."
* **This is confession, not theory.** He grounds the talk in his own collapse, repeating "because it happened to me," turning an abstract warning into a personal cautionary tale.
* **He stopped working hard.** Reaching success he had pushed himself, then quit because he figured he could "just sit back and relax."
* **He stopped improving.** He had always tried to do good work, then decided he was "good enough" and didn't need to get any better.
* **He lost his ideas.** He used to do the simple things that generated good ideas, but as a self-styled "hot-shot guy" he assumed ideas should arrive like magic; instead the only thing that arrived was creative block.
* **He got distracted by money.** Having ignored the money to focus on clients, he found money pouring in and ended up on the phone with his stockbroker and real estate agent when he should have been talking to clients.
* **He drifted into work he didn't love.** Calling himself "the world's worst manager," he took on management anyway because he was president of the company, abandoning the work he loved.
* **Outward success masked inward depression.** A "black cloud" formed over his head; buying a fast car left him "faster but just as depressed." His doctor's dry line: money can't buy happiness, only Prozac.
* **Serving himself drove clients away.** Clients saw he was serving himself rather than them, took their projects elsewhere, and the business "dropped like a rock" until he and his partner had to let all employees go.
* **Hitting bottom freed him to do what he loved.** With no employees to manage, he returned to the projects he loved, worked harder, and climbed back up over a slow seven years.
* **Sustaining beats achieving.** Going back to following the eight principles made the business bigger than ever and dissolved the black cloud. Avoiding "success-to-failure syndrome" means continuously following the eight principles—because they are not only how we achieve success but how we sustain it.

title: Why we switched to serverless containers
description: Florian Forster, talked about why they switched to serverless containers. Zitadel has a really interesting workload that is both CPU intensive and latency sensitive.
notes:
ZITADEL hit Kubernetes' scaling-speed and cost limits and moved to "serverless containers" (Google Cloud Run) — plug in an OCI image and let the platform scale it from 0 to 1000 without managing nodes — while keeping the same containers their self-hosting customers run (dogfooding).

- "Serverless containers" = run a standard OCI image on a platform that scales it without you managing the underlying infrastructure (Cloud Run, Fargate, Fly.io); "serverless" is a disliked word but conveys "no servers to manage." For a startup, Kubernetes is too much abstraction to babysit.
- Why Cloud Run specifically: end-to-end HTTP/2 (needed for their gRPC APIs) — most offerings only did HTTP/2 to the CDN, not upstream — plus CPU-based autoscaling and scale-to-zero in low-traffic regions.
- The workload is CPU-bound (Bcrypt hashing ~0.8-1s, token signing) with "double-dip" daily traffic per region that flattens globally; SLO ~250ms for 80% of calls.
- Lean stack: Cloud Run + CockroachDB dedicated (managed — cheaper than self-running) + Google CDN/Global Load Balancer + Cloud Armor; Terraform + GitHub Actions; Datadog (reluctantly, coupled to Cockroach). Minimize "tool drag" — too many tools for one job kills focus.
- Keep "hot regions" warm (3-9 normally; can deploy to 26 in ~2 min) because a ~2s cold-region start feels bad to users, and smaller container images start faster.
- Usage-based pricing, not feature-locked or per-seat: a security product shouldn't charge extra for 2FA, and per-identity pricing makes customers stop creating identities — so charge for requests.
- Observability from existing data (logs, status codes, latency, traces) rather than active health checks, which only test happy paths and miss real-world conditions (extensions, proxies, mobile resets).
- Lessons: use turnkey/managed services to start (don't run your own Kubernetes or Cockroach without a strong reason); validate features with data before building (they over-built and are pruning); docs are the biggest pain point — "content is oxygen for the community."
- Don't build authentication yourself — it's not "two fields and a button"; the depth (OAuth threat model, XSS/CSP, hashing) is the problem, so use a battle-tested turnkey solution.

title: Responsible Engineers and Outcomes with Mary and Tom Poppendieck
description: Mary and Tom Poppendieck discuss about  "Responsible Engineers and Outcomes". They walkthrough concrete examples of what it looks like for lean teams to maximize outcomes and minimize outputs. While exploring these examples, they touch on customer interaction, feedback, the responsible engineer, and the single threaded leader.
notes:
True success in software development is found by prioritizing meaningful customer outcomes over mere feature outputs, which is best achieved through "responsible engineers" or "single-threaded leaders" who own the product vision and maintain direct feedback loops with users. This approach requires a commitment to continuous learning and the willingness to abandon obsolete processes to meet evolving market needs.

* **Focus on outcomes, not outputs**: Success should be measured by the impact on the customer and the market, rather than the number of features, stories, or lines of code completed.
* **Eliminate proxies**: Engineering teams should have a direct connection to customers and real usage data to determine their own priorities, rather than relying on intermediaries or "feature curators."
* **The Single Threaded Leader**: Successful product development requires a leader who has no other responsibilities besides the success of a specific service or product, possessing the authority to assemble the necessary team.
* **The Responsible Engineer**: Organizations should operate on the principle of responsibility, where engineers are fully accountable for the success and performance of their specific components.
* **Passion-driven leadership**: Leaders must be visionaries who evoke passion and give team members ownership. Since engineers are essentially "volunteers," they cannot be effectively managed through top-down commands.
* **Mini-startup team structure**: Teams should function as independent business units, including diverse expertise (technical, marketing, manufacturing) and a controller to manage the product's profit and loss (P&L).
* **Feedback-driven iteration**: An iterative approach is only effective if it includes a full feedback loop from actual user usage that directly informs the team's next steps.
* **Direct customer engagement**: Technical team members should meet directly with lead customers to understand their needs firsthand, as they are best equipped to translate those needs into technical capabilities.
* **Minimize "Code Junk"**: Large codebases are a liability. Every feature is a burden unless it is proven to add true value; therefore, teams should aim to maximize outcomes while minimizing output.
* **Continuous evolution**: Methodologies like Agile are not the end goal; organizations must constantly challenge and improve their processes, as practices can become obsolete within five to ten years.

title: Keynote: Crossing the River by Feeling the Stones
description: a longer version <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ek0aWj_rWYs>
notes:
True strategy requires high situational awareness through visual mapping of the competitive landscape, allowing organizations to navigate evolution and uncertainty by taking small, iterative steps. By "feeling the stones" as they cross the river, leaders can move beyond generic buzzwords to make context-specific decisions based on position and movement.

*   **Strategy is a cycle**, not a one-time plan, involving five factors: purpose, landscape, climatic patterns, doctrine, and leadership.
*   **Situational awareness** is critical for competition; business often relies on "magic frameworks" (like SWOT) and stories, whereas military strategy relies on visual, context-specific maps.
*   A true **map** requires an anchor (the user need), the position of components (the value chain), and a consistency of movement (evolution).
*   **Evolution** is a universal pattern where components move from Genesis to Custom-Built, to Product/Rental, and finally to Commodity/Utility through competition.
*   **Climactic patterns** are external forces that influence the landscape, such as the rule that everything evolves and that characteristics change as they do.
*   **Inertia** to change is a common pattern where past success with a specific business model prevents a company from adapting to new evolutionary stages.
*   There is **no "one size fits all" method** for management; Agile/XP is best for the Uncharted (Genesis), Lean for the transitional Product stage, and Six Sigma/Outsourcing for Industrialized commodities.
*   **Doctrine** refers to basic organizational principles, such as focusing on user needs, removing bias and duplication, and utilizing cell-based structures.
*   **Context-specific gameplay** allows for market manipulation, such as using open-source approaches to accelerate industrialization or using fear, uncertainty, and doubt (FUD) to slow it.
*   Technological shifts (like the move from servers to cloud) cause a **co-evolution of practice**, shifting focus from high Mean Time To Recovery (MTTR) and disaster recovery to low MTTR, continuous deployment, and DevOps.
*   **Serverless** represents the next stage of code execution as a utility, leading to new emerging practices and rendering previous practices like traditional DevOps as "the new legacy".
*   The philosophy of **"crossing the river by feeling the stones"** summarizes strategy: have a purpose and direction, but take small, iterative steps and adapt based on the landscape.

title: DRY Software Patterns & Microservices
description: In this episode, Dave Farley of Continuous Delivery explores DRY, Coupling and Microservices and how they interact. Dave takes a pragmatic software engineering approach to exploring the pros and cons of DRY on different scales and describes why the microservices example may be more complicated than it looks.
notes:
Software development requires balancing the benefits of the **DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself)** principle against the risks of **coupling**, as reducing duplication in large-scale systems like microservices often leads to dependencies that hinder independent progress [1-3]. Ultimately, coupling is a more dangerous "enemy" than duplication in complex architectures, making it essential to prioritize loose coupling over strict adherence to DRY across service boundaries.

* **DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself)** aims for a single, authoritative representation of every piece of knowledge within a system to simplify updates and maintenance.
* At a **local level** (within functions, classes, or modules), DRY is an essential practice for code quality and clarity.
* Duplication leads to **"behavioral drift,"** where different copies of the same logic diverge over time, making bugs harder to identify and fix.
* The primary trade-off for implementing DRY is a significant **increase in coupling**.
* In large-scale or complex systems, **coupling** is often a greater threat to agility and system health than duplication.
* **Microservices** rely on independent deployability; applying DRY across these boundaries creates developmental coupling that forces teams to coordinate releases and move in lockstep.
* Shared **"platform" or "common services"** code often inadvertently creates architectural coupling that slows down organizational progress by breaking multiple services when changes occur.
* **Versioning shared libraries** can reduce coupling by allowing services to use different versions of code, though this technically violates the DRY principle.
* Effective **decoupling** is a product of intentional design—such as using minimal abstract parameters or ports and adapters—rather than just the choice of technology.
* **Test-Driven Development (TDD)** is a valuable tool for identifying over-coupled dependencies and poor abstractions early in the development cycle.
* DRY should be treated as a **useful guideline for local code** but discarded between microservices, where managing coupling is the absolute priority.

title: Anzeneering a Journey to Humility Safety and Excellence
description: Great talk to understand how safety changes our behavior.
notes:
True excellence is achieved by establishing safety as an unwavering precondition rather than a temporary priority, integrating robust technical defenses with a culture of dignity and psychological safety. By fostering a blameless environment where individuals feel respected and empowered to take safe risks, organizations can improve performance, reduce failure, and enable people to make meaningful contributions.

*   **Safety as a Precondition:** Safety should not be treated as a priority, because priorities change. It must be a "precondition"—a requirement that is present before work begins.
*   **Definition of Safety:** Safety is the "freedom from unacceptable risk." What is considered "acceptable" is highly dependent on the specific context of the organization and environment.
*   **Anzeneering:** A term derived from "Anzen" (the Japanese word for safety) and "Engineering," referring to the process of making safety a core part of everyday work across physical, psychological, and technical domains.
*   **The Six Shields of Protection:** The practice focuses on protecting six core assets: money, time, information, reputation, relationships, and health.
*   **Safety as the Presence of Defenses:** Safety is not defined by the absence of accidents, but by the presence of defenses. High-performing organizations focus on building and maintaining these defenses rather than just reacting to failures.
*   **The Swiss Cheese Model:** Failures occur when "holes" (weaknesses) in multiple layers of defense align. Organizations must constantly look for and close these holes.
*   **Psychological Safety:** A safe culture encourages dissenting views, productive conflict, and the freedom to speak one's mind. Without psychological safety, teams cannot achieve "habitual excellence."
*   **Blame-Free Culture:** Blame is toxic and prevents learning. A healthy organization replaces blame and discipline with a focus on learning from failures and strengthening defenses.
*   **Paul O'Neal’s Three Questions for Greatness:** An organization has the potential for greatness if every employee can answer "yes" to these questions every day:
    *   Am I treated with dignity and respect by everyone I encounter?
    *   Am I given the tools, training, and encouragement to make a contribution that gives meaning to my life?
    *   Does someone I care about and respect notice that I did it (recognition)?
*   **Stop-Work Authority:** Every individual, regardless of rank, must have the authority to stop work immediately if they perceive an unsafe situation.
*   **Tailboarding:** A brief, pre-work ritual where the team discusses potential risks and safety measures for the specific task at hand.
*   **Agile as a Risk Reduction Tool:** Agile practices—such as short feedback loops, continuous deployment, and Minimum Viable Products (MVPs)—are inherently safety-focused because they allow for faster learning and "failing fast" before significant resources are lost.
*   **Never One Defense Away from Failure:** Systems should be designed so that no single error or worker action is the only thing standing between the system and a total failure.
*   **Habitual Excellence:** Excellence is not an occasional event but a habit formed through the consistent application of safety as a precondition and the fostering of a respectful, high-recognition culture.

title: WTF is Platform as a Product?
description: Matthew explains why the platform-as-product approach can be a game-changer for organisations building and running software-enabled products and services. Using ideas and patterns from Team Topologies—including Thinnest Viable Platform, team cognitive load, and the evolutionary team interaction modes—he explains how organisations like Adidas and Uswitch have successfully used the platform-as-product model to accelerate and simplify the delivery of software at scale.
notes:
To maximize engineering efficiency, organizations should treat internal platforms as **curated products** designed to reduce the cognitive load of development teams by providing a compelling, self-service experience that engineers choose to use. This approach prioritizes **developer experience** and streamlined delivery over mandatory compliance or complex technology for its own sake.

*   **Definition**: A digital platform is a foundation of self-service APIs, tools, services, and knowledge arranged as a compelling internal product.
*   **Engineers as Customers**: A platform is primarily a curated experience for engineers. Decisions should be driven by the developer experience (DevEx) and the needs of the internal teams.
*   **Treating Platform as a Product**: Apply standard product management and service management practices. This includes having a roadmap, using user personas, and marketing the platform internally.
*   **Optionality**: Adoption should be voluntary, not mandatory. If teams are forced to use a platform, it is a sign the product is not good enough. It should instead be the "path of least resistance."
*   **Reducing Cognitive Load**: The main purpose of a platform is to reduce extraneous cognitive load for streamline teams, allowing them to focus on their specific business domain.
*   **Thinnest Viable Platform (TVP)**: Keep the platform as small as possible to avoid "technology bloat." A platform can be as simple as a wiki page curating external cloud services.
*   **Interaction Modes**: Use "Collaboration" to discover and define service boundaries, "X-as-a-Service" for low-friction consumption at scale, and "Facilitating" to help teams bridge capability gaps.
*   **Evolutionary Design**: Expect the platform to change. Services should be added as technology evolves and decommissioned when they are no longer necessary or are replaced by superior external cloud offerings.
*   **Success Metrics**: Measure success based on the "four key metrics" from *Accelerate* (Lead Time, Deployment Frequency, Mean Time to Restore, and Change Fail Percentage) for both the platform and the teams using it.
*   **Feedback Loops**: Use user satisfaction metrics like Net Promoter Score (NPS) and internal surveys to ensure the platform is actually helping engineers build software more effectively.
*   **Responsibility Split**: Streamline teams remain responsible for the "non-functional" aspects of their services (security, scalability, etc.), while the platform provides the generic infrastructure and tools to simplify meeting those requirements.

title: How to Break the Rules
description: GOTO 2017
notes:
To truly exploit new technology, you must identify the specific limitation it diminishes and actively dismantle the legacy rules and organizational policies originally designed to manage that limitation. Realizing benefits requires replacing these outdated behaviors with new rules that allow the organization to fully leverage the technology's power.

* **Core Principle:** Technology brings benefit if and only if it diminishes a specific limitation. 
* **Exploitation vs. Adoption:** Organizations are generally good at adopting new technology but poor at exploiting it because they fail to change the rules that were designed for the previous limitations.
* **The Four-Question Framework for Exploiting Technology:**
    1. **What is the power of the technology?** Identify the specific capability it provides.
    2. **What limitation does it diminish?** Identify the bottleneck or constraint that the technology is designed to alleviate.
    3. **What rules enabled the management of that limitation?** Identify the policies, behaviors, and processes created to cope with the old constraint.
    4. **What new rules are needed?** Define the new behaviors and processes required to fully utilize the new capability.
* **The Rule Evolution Trap:** Rules created to manage limitations eventually evolve from simple practices into official policy, then into organizational structure, and finally into deep-seated culture.
* **MRP Case Study (Material Requirements Planning):**
    * **Old Rule:** Plan monthly and order in large batches because manual calculation is slow.
    * **The Trap:** Using computer systems to run calculations faster while still sticking to monthly planning cycles and large batches.
    * **The Benefit:** Realized only when organizations moved to frequent re-planning and smaller batch sizes.
* **ERP Case Study (Enterprise Resource Planning):**
    * **Old Rule:** Use cost accounting and siloed decision-making because real-time enterprise data is unavailable.
    * **The Trap:** Implementing ERP but continuing to measure success through local utilization and cost-cutting rather than holistic value flow.
    * **The Benefit:** Requires moving to throughput accounting and cross-silo collaboration.
* **Cloud & Virtualization:**
    * **Old Rule:** High governance, long procurement, and specialized silos because hardware is expensive and risky to manage.
    * **The Trap:** Applying traditional procurement and "high-ceremony" governance to virtualized, on-demand resources.
    * **The Benefit:** Treating compute as a reversible utility that allows for rapid, inexpensive experimentation.
* **Continuous Delivery:**
    * **Old Rule:** Manual gatekeepers and front-loaded risk management because fixing mistakes in production is expensive.
    * **The Trap:** Automating deployment but maintaining manual sign-off boards (CABs) and "authority" figures who act as bottlenecks.
    * **The Benefit:** Implementing self-evidencing processes where compliance and security are built-in, enabling small, frequent, and valuable increments.
* **Overcoming Resistance:**
    * Rules are often tied to human status and emotional attachment.
    * To break rules, you must make it psychologically safe to change and provide "graceful exits" for those whose roles or status are threatened by the new technology.
    * Dismantling old structures is a community and cultural challenge, not just a technical one.
* **Measuring Success:** Use experiments and control groups to prove a limitation is actually diminishing. If the technology is adopted but the limitation remains, the old rules are likely still in effect.

title: Shared Mental Models Part 1
description: This was an excellent talk, full of insights that prompted reflection. In this talk Jessica looks at how the shared mental models created while mob programming work throughout the team even when they are not actively mobbing. She also explores the other practices she’s found complementary in creating a high functioning team and how looking at your contribution from a generative (helping other create) vs a productive (what I created, myself) frame can lead to a happier, better and more productive team.
notes:
True excellence in software engineering is achieved when we shift our focus from individual productivity to **generativity**, recognizing that great teams synthesize great people through shared mental models and collaborative learning. You should remember that we function within a **symmathesy**—a learning system where the team and the software evolve together, meaning your growth as a developer is a direct product of the healthy, communicative environment you help create.

* **Great teams make great people.** Individual greatness is not the starting point but a side effect of being part of a high-functioning system. Great developers are "synthesized" by their environment and team.
* **Symmathesy** is a "learning system made of learning parts." In software, this is a **socio-technical symmathesy** where the people, the code, the running software, and the tools all learn and change together over time.
* **The Line of Representation** is the boundary between developers and the software they create. Because digital systems are not physical, developers must consciously build and maintain tools (logs, monitors, editors) to discover what the software is doing and build accurate mental models.
* **The Law of Downhill Invention and Uphill Analysis** states that it is significantly easier to build a complex system from scratch (downhill) than it is to understand or change a system built by someone else (uphill).
* **Legacy code** is defined as code that a developer can no longer "change" because they no longer hold the mental model of it in their head. It is no longer a "living" part of the symmathesy.
* **Mental models are always incomplete.** In complex systems, individual mental models are inevitably out of date or wrong. A team's primary task is spending the "coherence penalty" to align these overlapping models so they can communicate and act effectively.
* **Generativity over Productivity.** Rather than measuring individual output, team members should be evaluated on their "generativity"—the difference in what the team produces and what the team *becomes* because of that person's presence.
* **The "10x Developer" can have negative generativity.** A developer who works alone and changes the system faster than others can learn it prevents the rest of the team from building a mental model, effectively creating legacy code in their wake.
* **Mob Programming** is a vital practice for sharing mental models. It ensures that the system remains "alive" in the heads of multiple people simultaneously, allowing the team to move "downhill" together.
* **The "Invisible College" effect.** Innovation and creativity are rarely the result of isolated individuals; they emerge from groups of diverse people (a "Camerata") sharing common values, language, and problems.

title: GOTO 2016 • Stability Patterns & Antipatterns
description: Michael Nygard explores essential architectural patterns and anti-patterns for building resilient systems that handle faults gracefully and prevent cascading failures.
notes:
True system stability comes from accepting that failures are inevitable and designing architectures that isolate and dampen faults to prevent them from propagating into catastrophic, system-wide outages. You should remember that every integration point is a potential source of failure, requiring proactive measures like circuit breakers and bulkheads to protect the rest of the system.

* **Availability is a dependent variable** derived from **stability**, which is the architectural characteristic that allows a system to maintain its mission despite faults and errors.
* **Faults** (internal incorrect states) become **errors** when they produce observable incorrect behavior, eventually leading to **system failure** (loss of availability).
* **Integration Points** are the primary source of system instability. Every out-of-process call is a potential failure point that can hang or crash a system, often due to low-level issues like firewall timeouts or TCP retransmits.
* **Cascading Failures** occur because server nodes are highly correlated through shared software and load; a failure in one node often increases the stress on remaining nodes, leading to a total collapse.
* **Slow Responses** are significantly worse than fast errors. They tie up resources (threads, memory) across the entire calling chain, amplifying scaling issues and wasting work that often ends in a timeout anyway.
* **Circuit Breakers** prevent resource exhaustion by wrapping dangerous integration calls. They track failures and automatically cut off traffic to a failing provider for a "human-scale" period, allowing for recovery and immediate fallback responses.
* **Bulkheads** compartmentalize systems into isolated zones (e.g., separate thread pools, dedicated server clusters) to ensure that a failure in one area or for one client type does not sink the entire "ship."
* **Timeouts** are essential for every integration point to prevent infinite blocking. Developers must explicitly define what happens (retry, error, or queue) when a timeout occurs.
* **Fail Fast** by validating input parameters and checking the state of resources or circuit breakers at the beginning of a request to avoid consuming time and resources on operations destined to fail.
* **Decoupling with Asynchronous Middleware** (messaging/queues) allows systems to handle unbalanced capacities and remain available even when downstream providers are offline.
* **Marketing-Induced Self-Denial** occurs when successful promotions create massive, uncoordinated traffic spikes. Coordination between IT and Marketing, alongside the use of static landing pages and CDNs, is critical.
* **Scaling Effects** such as N-squared communication patterns (point-to-point messaging) or shared resource bottlenecks (firewalls, API gateways) often remain invisible until a system reaches production scale.
* **Chaos Engineering** (e.g., Chaos Monkey) is a valuable tool for forcing systems to become anti-fragile by injecting failures into production, uncovering "wicked errors" that cannot be replicated in traditional QA environments.
* **Unbounded Result Sets** from databases or services can lead to memory exhaustion; queries should always include limits and be tested against real-world data distributions.

title: Less stuff more happiness
notes:
Graham Hill argues that despite Americans having roughly three times the living space they did 50 years ago, accumulated possessions still overflow into a booming personal-storage industry while happiness has flat-lined. His thesis is that less stuff and less space can actually equal more: a smaller footprint, more money, more freedom, and more time. He proposes "editing" your life the way you'd edit anything else, keeping only what you truly love.

* **The space paradox**: Americans have triple the space of 50 years ago, yet have become such good shoppers that they need even more, fueling a 22 billion-dollar, 2.2 billion-square-foot personal storage industry.
* **Costs of accumulation**: The pile-up brings credit card debt, huge environmental footprints, and happiness levels that have flat-lined over the same 50 years.
* **The joys of less are familiar**: Most people have tasted them in a college dorm, a hotel room, or camping with basically nothing—delivering more freedom and more time.
* **Less stuff equals a smaller footprint**: Living with less is not deprivation but a way to shrink your environmental impact, save money, and add ease to life.
* **Life Edited project**: He started lifeedited.org and crowd-sourced the design of his own 420 sq. ft. Manhattan apartment (with Mutopo and Jovoto.com), drawing over 300 entries worldwide.
* **The wish list, done small**: He fit it all into that small footprint—home office, sit-down dinner for 10, room for guests, and his kite surfing gear—his "own little jewel box."
* **The economics of small**: Buying 420 sq. ft. instead of 600 saved him 200 grand immediately, and a smaller space means smaller utilities, saving more.

### Three approaches to living little
* **Edit ruthlessly**: "Clear the arteries of our lives"—let go of the shirt unworn in years, stem the inflow by asking "Is that really going to make me happier? Truly?" Own great stuff you'll love for years, not just stuff.
* **Small is sexy (space efficiency)**: Design for how things are used the vast majority of the time, not the rare event—why have a six-burner stove when you rarely use three? Favor things that nest and stack.
* **Digitize**: Paperwork, books, and movies can be made to "disappear," reclaiming physical space.
* **Multifunctional spaces and housewares**: A sink combined with a toilet, a dining table that becomes a bed, a side table that stretches to seat 10.
* **The winning render in action**: His apartment combines a moving wall with transformer furniture—a coffee table that grows to seat 10, an office that folds away, a bed that pops out of the wall, fold-down guest beds, even a movie theater.
* **The takeaway**: You don't need 420 sq. ft.; just consider editing down (3,000 to 2,000, 1,500 to 1,000). Ask whether a little life editing would give you more freedom and time, and "make room for the good stuff," because less might equal more.

title: Hibernate should be to programmers what cake mixes are to bakers: beneath their dignity.
description: Great lightning talk, provocative and inspiring.
notes:
A humorous polemic against generic frameworks (Hibernate/ORMs) using a baking analogy: cake mixes are stupid, and so are the frameworks we reach for to avoid understanding our own applications. It's easy to develop blind spots in our own domain, so looking at another domain (baking) illuminates them.

- Cake mixes are full of additives, taste worse, rob you of pride, creativity and knowledge — and don't even save time: you still crack the eggs and melt the butter; they only save you from measuring flour.
- Hibernate/ORMs do the same to programming: you write SQL anyway (and better than Hibernate's 300-line, runs-overnight queries), and you'd never let a teammate that bad at SQL near your database — yet we hand it everything and lose awareness of how our apps work. "We used to write algorithms, now we're writing annotations."
- The self-justifying trap: the more we use something, the more we assume it must be a good idea ("I'm not stupid, so this must be right").
- It's not anti-reuse: chefs happily buy bread, wine and cheese, and good single-purpose Java libraries that do one thing well are great. The target is generic frameworks that hide how the application works and bloat it (the childhood-obesity analogy).
- An appeal to architects: let developers solve problems in a custom way that meets the actual current demands of the problem and customer — you might be pleasantly surprised.

title: DevOps for the Database
notes:
Baron Schwartz, who has worked with databases for roughly two decades — first as a developer, then as a MySQL scaling consultant at Percona for about five years, and as founder of the database monitoring company VividCortex for six years — argues that the database is one of the last holdouts of DevOps and that the same principles, practices, and culture applied everywhere else in the codebase should be applied to the database. He is explicit that this is opinion rather than research (he cites a self-made survey filled out by more than 50 people, contrasting it with Nicole Forsgren and Jez Humble's keynote science). His core thesis: when you bring DevOps to the database you escape the iron triangle of "faster, better, cheaper, pick two" and can have all three, plus a virtuous cycle where stability leads to speed which leads to more stability. The structure of the talk covers what DevOps means for the database, what works, what fails, and where the DBA role goes.

* **A query is code, so a gatekeeping DBA owns code they can't control**: Without DevOps you get a DBA "responsible for code that they can't control because a query is code" — when you deploy your application you deploy queries into the database. This pushes DBAs into gatekeeping/change-control, creating bottlenecks, offloading developer work onto the DBA, diverting a highly skilled expert into "unskilled dependency work," and breaking the production-to-development feedback loop.
* **Three anchoring stories show multiplication vs. transformation**: A fast-growing regulated company just multiplied its DBA team in proportion to developers — "not a fundamental change, just a multiplication" — and quality didn't improve (lots of outages). A friend, sole DBA at a hot startup going from 20 to ~100 developers under "move fast and break things," tried to review every change before production; Schwartz told him "this is not going to work... it's not physically possible." A small media org gave its monitoring product to 17 overseas developers instead of hoarding it — "developers are smart, they can use a database monitoring product... we can't be the bottlenecks" — which he calls the "golden motion."
* **Developers must own how their system runs against the database in production**: They own the schema, the performance, and the overall workload, and must be able to debug, triage, and repair their own database outages — but "not every developer has to be able to do this alone in the most obscure database outages at 3 a.m. on a Sunday without calling in reinforcements." It's valuable to keep someone who understands buffer pools and latches.
* **Schema and data model are part of the same codebase and pipeline**: The schema is "part of the codebase, and it's not a separate codebase," version controlled and deployed through the same tooling, with migrations ideally automated. He flags automated schema migration as a hard last holdout because "data has inertia and resistance."
* **Charity Majors' two stages of DevOps**: Stage one was when operations people started doing development (infrastructure as code — Chef, Puppet — replacing automation that "was you pushing keys on the keyboard"). Stage two "is here when developers need to be able to do operations," which Schwartz says is especially important and overdue in the database arena.
* **Four core elements, starting with tooling**: people, culture, structure/process, and tooling — "not a DevOps adoption project, it's more of a process... continuous and ongoing." Tooling first because it's most concrete. The two tooling pillars: deploy/release (frequent automated database changes, eliminating manual toil like his old SQL Server job of copy-pasting stored procedures from dev to prod) and monitoring/observability.
* **Monitoring vs. observability are different things**: "Monitoring tells you if your systems are broken, observability tells you why they're broken and how to fix them." Monitoring is an activity of testing for predetermined conditions; observability is a system property letting you ask ad hoc questions from instrumented telemetry.
* **Knowledge-sharing tooling matters as much as deploy tooling**: Wiki pages, runbooks, playbooks, dashboards, chatbots. He cites Etsy's Deployinator with its "What to watch after a push" links and GitHub's "deploy confidence" wiki pages tied into alerts, so a 3 a.m. page can link to a dashboard saying "it might be this, it's often that."
* **Start small and don't break production**: "Don't bring down production" is the first rule — "it's okay to punch a hole in the boat, just make sure you punch it above the waterline." He invokes the EMT mantra "stabilize the patient, then transport" and the Chaos Engineering book: "if you know your system is going to break, you don't need chaos, you need to fix it." Pick one team, prove it out, ideally on something new and low-stakes rather than transforming painful legacy systems.
* **Culture is an emergent artifact you change via incentives**: "You can't operate on culture directly" — change processes, tooling, and especially incentives. "Show me who gets rewarded or promoted and I'll tell you your real culture." Per Charity Majors, operations work, carrying a pager, and being on call for your own code "shouldn't be low status" — make owning production behavior higher status.
* **Shared pain drives change**: A deal didn't close because only the miserable DBA — not the boss — knew he worked nights and weekends. Schwartz himself once wired old noisy Nagios alerts into PagerDuty and got robo-voice calls at 3 a.m. with infant children at home, and "nobody knew I was doing this... nobody cared." "If the pain is not shared, you're not going to make any changes happen." Leadership support from the "HIPPO" (highest paid person's opinion) is required, but leadership also comes from beneath via managing up.
* **Failure modes**: Bad/fragile tooling "not built by people who have experience running at scale" causes critical outages (and "critical career changes"); two separate pipelines for code and database changes create two distributed systems to coordinate; any friction makes people revert to the old way; vendors can support but "are not going to change anybody's culture from the outside"; and clinging to legacy DBA roles (per Sylvia Botros) where the DBA is "the only person with access, the go-to person" is itself the problem.
* **The DBA becomes a Database Reliability Engineer**: Just as sysadmin became SRE, database administration should become database reliability engineering. He recommends the books Accelerate (Forsgren et al.), this year's State of DevOps report (page 57), the Chaos Engineering book ("40 pages of brilliance"), Database Reliability Engineering (Charity Majors and Laine), and GitHub's open-source non-blocking schema change tooling as the best available. He notes advocating for change is a difficult but career-critical skill: "the people who grow in their careers are the ones who can clearly articulate what needs to be done, why, the benefit, and how to do it."

title: Making Work Visible: How to Unmask Capacity Killing WIP
description: Are you scrambling to meet deadlines only to find more requests piling up in your inbox? Hidden WIP is a major contributor to clogged value streams and overloaded employees, burdened with too many meetings and frustrating work routines. Making work visible highlights the problems leading to low productivity across the organization. In this talk, Dominica shares how to unmask the things that are killing your team’s capacity and their ability to optimize workflow.
notes:
To effectively manage work overload and consistently deliver business value, it is crucial to make all work, including unplanned tasks and dependencies, explicitly visible and to intentionally limit work-in-progress (WIP) to optimize workflow. By doing so, teams can identify bottlenecks, address conflicting priorities, and ensure a smoother flow of activities from start to finish.

- People often take on more work than they can capacity to do, leading to self and team overload, bottlenecks, and delayed business value delivery.
- Reasons for overload include a desire not to let the team down, the appeal of new projects over finishing old ones, underestimating task duration, and difficulty saying no, especially to superiors.
- **Making all work visible** is crucial to unmask capacity-killing overload, including planned, unplanned, and partially completed tasks.
- **Too much Work-In-Progress (WIP)** is the primary deterrent to smooth workflow (FLOW), causing scattered focus, interruptions, context switching, and significantly increasing wait times.
- Visualize and plan for **unplanned work** (emergencies, interruptions) by allocating capacity or creating dedicated visibility lanes, as it will never be eliminated entirely.
- Address **conflicting priorities** by establishing clear prioritization policies and using visual boards to show competing demands, facilitating conversations about what truly is the top priority.
- Expose **dependencies** between tasks, teams, or systems, as these significantly increase the likelihood of delays and contribute to WIP overload.
- **Intentionally limit Work-In-Progress (WIP)** to create a pull system, which gives teams permission to say no to new tasks when capacity is full, ensuring a smoother flow and more predictable delivery of business value.
- Gradually reduce WIP limits over time to find an optimal flow, adjusting them as team capacity changes.
- Leaders must be aware of their positional power, as team members are likely to accept new work even when overloaded, which ultimately hinders the delivery of business value.

title: SysAdmin to SRE: creating Capacity to make tomorrow better than today
description: Damon Edwards explains how to reclaim engineering time and improve operational reliability by transitioning from traditional systems administration to SRE through targeted **toil reduction and self-service operations**.
notes:
Creating the capacity to make tomorrow better than today requires a fundamental shift from manual firefighting to engineering work by systematically reducing **toil** through self-service operations. This approach reclaims the time necessary to move beyond repetitive, non-value-adding tasks and focus on building the **enduring value and reliability** that modern businesses demand.

*   **Time and Capacity**: The central goal of SRE is managing time to create the capacity necessary to make tomorrow’s operations better than today’s.
*   **SRE vs. SysAdmin**: Simply changing a job title without changing the nature of the work results in a "long-suffering Systems Administrator" who remains buried under toil.
*   **SLOs with Consequences**: SRE requires Service Level Objectives (SLOs) with real organizational consequences; if a service falls below its agreed-upon performance level, the entire organization must prioritize fixing the issue over developing new features.
*   **Definition of Toil**: Toil is manual, repetitive, automatable work that lacks enduring value and increases linearly as a service grows.
*   **Toil Limits**: SRE teams should track toil and aim to limit it to less than 50% of their time, ensuring the other 50% is dedicated to engineering work that reduces future toil or improves the business.
*   **Workload Regulation**: SRE teams must have the authority to regulate their workload, which can include requiring developers to maintain their own applications in production until they reach a required level of stability.
*   **Reliability Hierarchy**: Reliability begins with monitoring and incident response at the foundation, followed by post-mortems, testing, capacity planning, and finally product development.
*   **Starting with Toil Reduction**: While culture changes like SLOs are foundational, focusing on toil reduction through self-service is often the most practical and successful starting point for legacy organizations.
*   **Self-Service Operations**: Enabling self-service for common tasks like restarts, deployments, and health checks eliminates waiting times and constant interruptions for operations staff.
*   **Operations as Code**: Operational procedures should be integrated into the Software Development Lifecycle (SDLC), allowing them to be peer-reviewed and promoted to production with the same discipline as application code.
*   **Enhanced Compliance**: Self-service platforms improve compliance and security by replacing manual "theater of control" with automated, safety-proofed procedures and a clear audit trail of who ran what, where, and when.
*   **MTTR Reduction**: Empowering those closest to a problem (such as NOC teams or developers) with automated self-service tools can reduce Mean Time to Repair (MTTR) by over 90%.

title: TDD, where did it all go wrong. Summary
description: Short summary of the "TDD, where did it all go wrong" talk.
notes:
To maximize the value of Test-Driven Development, developers should focus on testing **observable behaviors at the API boundary** rather than individual classes or internal implementation details. This approach ensures that tests remain stable during refactoring and provide high coverage with a more efficient and sustainable test suite.

* Redefine a **"unit"** in TDD as a test that runs in **isolation from other tests**, rather than an isolated class or object.
* Focus testing on **behaviors** rather than implementation details or specific class operations to avoid fragile tests.
* Write tests against the **public API or module boundary** to achieve better coverage with fewer tests and increase overall productivity.
* Avoid testing implementation details so that **refactoring** remains easy; tests should only break if behavior changes, not when internal code is reorganized.
* Move **behavior-based testing** back into unit tests to reduce the cost and complexity associated with external Acceptance Test Driven Development (ATDD) tools.
* Maintain the **collaborative process** of ATDD (involving developers, QA, and customers) but capture the resulting requirements as behavior-based unit tests.
* Use the **Ports and Adapters** (Hexagonal) architectural style to separate the core domain logic from technology concerns like databases and web frameworks.
* Ensure tests drive the **creation of the API** by interacting with the system at the same level as external drivers like REST or messaging frameworks.
* Stop writing deep-domain tests that provide **no additional coverage** beyond what is already provided by API-level behavioral tests.

title: Evolutionary Architecture
description: This talk is a great introduction to the topic.
notes:
Rebecca Parsons (recorded at Agile Australia 2019) argues that long-term architectural planning is impossible in an era where business models, customer expectations, and technology change constantly and unpredictably. Her core thesis: instead of trying to predict where change will come from, we should build an "evolutionary architecture" that "supports guided incremental change across multiple dimensions." The mechanism is the architectural fitness function — an objective, testable characterization of what "good" means for a given system — which turns architectural governance from a process about implementation into one about outcomes. She develops the idea with Neal Ford, with whom she co-wrote a book on the topic.

* **Prediction is dead, change is unpredictable**: In the mainframe era you could have "five and even 10-year architectural road maps" because nothing changed fast. Now business model lifetimes shrink and the tech landscape churns — Neal Ford bets $100 nobody can name the JavaScript framework they'll use in two years "because it hasn't been written yet." Asked to predict the tech landscape in 10 years, she laughed, noting it had been less than 10 years since the iPhone.

* **Why "evolutionary" not "emergent" or "adaptable"**: She rejected "emergent" (her robust disagreement with Neal) because, unlike code, there is no universal definition of a good architecture — the emergent unicyclist just stays upright. She rejects "adaptable" because it's too close to "adapter": people respond by adding configuration parameters or plug-ins, presuming they know where change will come from. "You can — we can no longer predict where the change is coming from." Predicting wrong means more code, which is "harder to understand, you have more bugs," and "most likely, you got it wrong."

* **"Good" architecture is context-specific, not universal**: A retailer rejected a fully centralized architecture because of a hidden organizational memory — "what happens when we lose Scotland?" — after a past comms failure cut their home office off from Scottish stores for days. A trading system she worked on expected only "200 transactions a day," but each worth billions, so "what they cared about was never losing a message," not low latency or throughput.

* **The definition, word by word**: "Evolutionary architecture supports guided incremental change across multiple dimensions," and "each one of those words is important."

* **Fitness functions must be objective and testable**: Inspired by evolutionary computation's fitness/objective functions. "The single most important thing about this objective function is you and I will never disagree on what the answer is." So not "the code must be maintainable" (means different things to different people) but "cyclomatic complexity less than 30" or a naming standard. She illustrates the danger of a single-axis fitness function with a genetic-algorithm airplane wing that was aerodynamically more efficient but had crazy projections and likely couldn't survive takeoff, landing, or birds — they optimized only one aspect of "good."

* **Multiple dimensions, and you can't maximize them all**: The -ilities are many and conflict. "You cannot have a maximum level of encryption and maximize security at the same time" because more encryption costs compute and hits performance — high security and high performance are possible, maximizing both is not. She rejected the claim that it's "professionally irresponsible to ever deploy an application with less than five nines reliability": not every app needs five nines, and "it's expensive to get." One new -ility they propose is "evolvability" — though for a small enough app it may be cheaper to throw it away than to make it evolvable.

* **Governance becomes about outcomes, not implementation**: Agilists do care about governance — "we care about bad governance." Automated fitness functions are the basis for governance: "my system respects everything you asked of it because my build is green." The composition of all individual fitness functions is a system-wide fitness function that becomes "your target state architecture" — no longer "a block diagram that takes up three-quarters of that wall," and focused on outcomes (simultaneous users, response time, recovery time) rather than hows, which vary far more over time.

* **Concrete fitness function examples**: JDepend to ban cyclic dependencies and enforce layer coupling ("no one in a code review ever needs to think about cyclic dependencies again"); ArchUnit to fail builds on generic exceptions, bad interface naming, or to force everyone through a wrapper around an insecure library; consumer-driven contracts that let teams "cheerfully ignore each other" until a test breaks, maximizing independent evolution; synthetic transactions and resource/timing monitoring in production. Netflix's Simian Army is a "holistic continuous fitness function" running in production — e.g., checking RESTful endpoints are properly configured and kicking out those that aren't — so "no architectural or code review ever has to worry" about those things.

* **Free humans for human work**: The point of automating checks is so technologists focus on real tradeoffs — "I can't get my performance numbers high enough because I would need to use this caching strategy, but this caching strategy violates our data currency requirements for security" — "not, you just introduced a cyclic dependency, bad, bad, bad."

* **Continuous delivery is the non-negotiable foundation**: These techniques are "incredibly risky" without CD disciplines, because you may swap out radical parts of the architecture — like when GitHub finally rewrote its `merge` function (previously shelling out to git merge) because it wasn't scaling, using a framework they built called Scientist to test in production invisibly. The deployment pipeline is "the key": give the security department a slot so a zero-day in a library auto-fails builds and emails the exact owners, replacing the error-prone manual "scurrying around" that caused a high-profile US breach. "Deployments should be boring."

* **Applying it to legacy, pragmatically**: Identify the -ilities you're "most worried about" (not where you predict change), pick a few, define a fitness function to measure how far off you are, start with warnings not build-breaks, chip away, then flip warning to error so "we can't backslide." Be pragmatic: a disaster-area module untouched in five years isn't worth much worry — wrap it protectively or hope to throw it away. Continually re-evaluate fitness functions, because mergers, new EU regulations, or new expectations (augmented reality affecting how you build tractors) can make a fitness function no longer fit.

* **Data evolution is the most important technique**: "If you cannot evolve your data, you cannot evolve your system." DBAs had the best excuse for up-front work because production data requires migration. She strongly recommends the book *Refactoring Databases* (subtitle *Evolutionary Database Design*), which decomposes large schema change into three parts — schema change, data migration for that small change, and code access change — run one migration at a time to isolate problems (e.g., a magic field that meant something different between two dates), instead of "hundreds of thousands or millions of records failing for dozens of reasons."

* **Other techniques and resources**: Choreography (like microservices) gives evolvability but introduces failure modes absent under orchestration — "you probably don't want to do it unless you absolutely have to." There's a website with fitness function katas (typing `solution=true` or `s=1` reveals the answer). A favorite trick: hash an open-source license file in the build so you can "call the lawyer if somebody changes an open-source license" without needing NLP.

title: The Power Question
description: I want to ask this question to everyone... What would you do if you weren't afraid?  Very interesting talk and very interesting question
notes:
Empowering yourself and your organization requires a fundamental shift from fear-based thinking to a mindset of freedom, activated by consistently asking, "What would I do if I weren't afraid?" Overcoming the limiting influence of fear allows for better decision-making, higher performance, and the realization of your full potential and purpose in the world.

* **The Power Question:** Asking "What would you do if you weren't afraid?" shifts an individual from a feeling of powerlessness to one of power and enables a rapid mindset shift.
* **Prevalence of Fear-Based Thinking:** On average, humans have 60,000 thoughts per day, and 80% of those thoughts are negative or fear-based.
* **Fear as a Cognitive Barrier:** Fear is not an effective motivator; it causes the peripheries of the brain to shut down, leading to myopic thinking and poor decision-making.
* **Symptoms of Fear in the Workplace:** Stress, anger, bureaucracy, "fiefdoms," and command-and-control leadership are all organizational symptoms of underlying fear.
* **Freedom at Work Model:** This framework consists of three pillars: a freedom-centered mindset, freedom-centered design, and freedom-centered leadership.
* **The Importance of Mindset:** Most organizational change initiatives fail or are unsustainable because they attempt to change behavior or design without first addressing the underlying fear-based mindset.
* **Top Fears of Leaders:** Research indicates the three primary fears for executives are imposter syndrome, fear of failure, and the fear of being perceived as vulnerable.
* **Financial Impact of Freedom:** Companies that prioritize freedom over fear and control have demonstrated revenue growth nearly seven times higher than the average S&P 500 company.
* **Vulnerability as Strength:** Being vulnerable and authentic about emotions allows leaders to overcome the limitations of fear and empowers their teams to step up and support the organization.
* **Global Application:** Overcoming fear is essential for achieving full potential and purpose, not just personally and professionally, but also in addressing large-scale social and economic challenges.

title: JUXT Cast S5E4 - Special with Kent Beck
description: Interesting conversation about the concept of eventual business consistency (<https://tidyfirst.substack.com/p/eventual-business-consistency>) / bitemporality and about XP.

title: #ModernAgileShow 14 | Interview with David J Bland, Founder & CEO, Precoil
description: David J. Bland discusses applying Lean Startup principles and assumption mapping to cultivate a culture of rapid learning and innovation across both software and non-software industries.
notes:
Organizations must prioritize rapid learning through targeted experimentation and assumption mapping to identify and discard non-viable ideas before scaling, thereby saving significant resources and time. Success in this process requires a culture of psychological safety where leaders value evidence-based decision-making and celebrate the discovery of what doesn't work as a vital step toward achieving true customer delight.

* **Optimize for Learning over Scaling**: Minimum Viable Products (MVPs) should be designed to maximize learning rather than immediate growth. While the team determines what is "minimal," only the customer can decide if a product is "viable."
* **Cross-Industry Application**: Lean Startup and Modern Agile principles are not limited to software; they are successfully applied in automotive, fashion, consumer packaged goods, and service industries.
* **Shift to Mobility and Outcomes**: Companies are moving from selling specific products (like cars) to solving broader customer needs (like mobility or hygiene), requiring a focus on speed-to-delight.
* **Continuous Discovery and Testing**: Use fast feedback loops to test value propositions and assumptions before building. Slow feedback loops prevent informed decision-making and increase the risk of failure.
* **Safety as a Prerequisite**: Innovation requires a culture where it is safe to fail or pivot. Leaders must lead with questions rather than commands and ensure that stopping a non-viable project is viewed as a success, not a personal failure.
* **Financial Value of "Killing" Ideas**: Significant value is created by saving resources—sometimes tens of millions of dollars—by identifying and stopping bad ideas early based on evidence.
* **Diversity of Experimentation**: Teams should use multiple validation methods, including customer interviews, search trend analysis, landing pages, and paper prototyping, rather than relying on a single type of test.
* **Targeted Validation**: Experiments that target people actively seeking solutions (such as search-based advertising) often yield much higher conversion and better data than passive "push" advertising.
* **Assumption Mapping**: Use a framework to map assumptions based on **Desirability** (do people want it?), **Viability** (should we do it?), and **Feasibility** (can we build it?).
* **Focus on the Unknown**: Prioritize experiments on assumptions that are high-importance but low-certainty to reduce the biggest risks first.
* **Nurturing Intrapreneurs**: Corporations should identify and celebrate internal entrepreneurs, providing them with the tools and "superpowers" to navigate discovery and validation processes.
* **The "Andon Cord" in Knowledge Work**: Teams must have the authority to "stop the line" to reflect and improve without fear of retribution, ensuring quality and long-term revenue over short-term output.

title: The Software Industry's Evolution, Complex Architecture & Problem-Solving At Scale | Michael Nygard In The Engineering Room Ep. 35
description: A discussion between two experts on incremental design and architecture, including data mesh solutions, managing complexity, platform engineering, and the challenges of balancing innovation with stability

title: What We Got Wrong - Lessons from the Birth of Microservices
description: An expert retrospective on the birth of microservices at Google that argues for prioritizing organizational velocity and team communication over purely technical infrastructure goals.
notes:
Microservices should be implemented primarily to enhance **organizational velocity and team communication** rather than for purely technical reasons, while ensuring that service fragmentation remains purposeful and cross-cutting concerns are standardized. To manage the resulting complexity, teams must shift from relying on massive dashboards to an observability approach focused on **detecting critical service-level indicators** and systematically refining the search space for root causes.

* **The "Why" of Microservices**: The primary reason most organizations should adopt microservices is to reduce human communication overhead ("shipping the org chart"). Google's initial adoption was driven by technical computer science requirements, but for most, the goal should be increasing engineering velocity by minimizing team-to-team dependencies.

* **Velocity vs. Throughput**: Designing for massive system throughput often requires sacrificing features and engineering velocity. Foisting the complex horizontal scaling requirements of "planet-scale" services onto smaller projects creates unnecessary friction and slows down development.

* **Resilience on Commodity Hardware**: Google’s infrastructure (GFS, BigTable, MapReduce) was born from the necessity of building resilient software on top of cheap, unreliable hardware. This "cattle, not pets" mentality is foundational to modern distributed systems.

* **The Latency Cost of "Serverless"**: There is a significant performance gap between a main memory reference (nanoseconds) and a network RPC within a data center (microseconds/milliseconds). Breaking systems into the smallest possible functional units (FaaS) without considering these network costs can lead to performance failures.

* **"Ants" vs. "Hippies" Culture**: Complete team independence (the "hippie" ethos) leads to operational "train wrecks." Successful microservice architectures require an "ant" approach: teams are independent but must follow a strict "prefix menu" of global rules for cross-cutting concerns like security, authentication, and service discovery.

* **Observability over Dashboards**: Massive dashboards filled with "squiggly lines" are ineffective for root cause analysis because failures in microservices cascade across dependencies. Observability should focus on detecting critical Service Level Indicators (SLIs) and providing a guided process to refine the search space.

* **Alerting Strategy**: Organizations should alert on SLIs—the metrics that actually impact the consumer—rather than trying to alert on every possible root cause. Attempting to maintain thousands of lines of alerting configuration for specific failure modes is unsustainable.

* **Distributed Tracing Realities**: In a microservices environment, data volume grows geometrically (transactions multiplied by the number of services). This makes centralized logging and tracing expensive, often requiring aggressive sampling that can unfortunately reduce the visibility of low-throughput systems.

title: If Russ Ackoff had given a TED Talk.
description: This presentation is from a 1994 event hosted by Clare Crawford-Mason and Lloyd Dobyns to capture the Learning and Legacy of Dr. W. Edwards Deming.  Russ knew Dr. Deming and speaks here about the difference between "continuous improvement" and "discontinuous improvement" as seen through the lens of systems thinking.
notes:
Real improvement comes from managing the **interactions of a system's parts as a whole** rather than optimizing them individually, as the performance of the system depends on how its components fit together. Instead of merely seeking efficiency by "doing things right," true progress requires the **wisdom of "doing the right thing"** through creative redesign and systemic effectiveness.

* **Failure of Quality Improvement:** Most quality improvement programs fail because they are "anti-systemic" and not embedded in systems thinking.
* **Definition of a System:** A system is a whole consisting of interdependent parts where each part can affect the behavior of the whole, but no part has an independent effect.
* **Properties of the Whole:** The essential properties of a system are properties of the whole which none of its parts have (e.g., a car can move, but its engine cannot move itself).
* **Interaction vs. Sum:** A system is the product of the interactions of its parts, not the sum of the behavior of its parts.
* **The Pitfall of Optimizing Parts:** Improving the performance of parts taken separately will not improve the performance of the whole and can often lead to system failure.
* **Interdependence:** The performance of a system depends on how the parts fit together, not how they act individually.
* **Defect Reduction vs. Improvement:** Getting rid of what you don't want (defects) is not the same as getting what you do want. True improvement requires redesigning the system for the desired outcome.
* **Continuous vs. Discontinuous Improvement:** Continuous improvement is often just imitation of a leader. Leadership and progress require discontinuous improvement through creativity.
* **Efficiency vs. Effectiveness:** There is a fundamental difference between doing things right (efficiency/knowledge) and doing the right thing (effectiveness/wisdom).
* **The Goal of Quality:** Quality should be focused on effectiveness and value rather than just efficiency. Doing the wrong thing right is not as good as doing the right thing wrong.

title: Podcast Agile Uprising Jeff Gothelf | Sense and Respond/Lean UX
description: Agile Uprising podcast with Jeff Gothelf (Sense and Respond, Lean UX) on replacing feature roadmaps and binary goals with outcome-based roadmaps — teams reporting evidence and learning against behavioural metrics.
notes:
On the Agile Uprising podcast, Jeff Gothelf (co-author of Sense and Respond and Lean UX) argues for managing teams by outcomes rather than features — reducing cycle time, increasing learning, and giving leaders evidence instead of false certainty.

- Outcome goals are "muddier" than binary ones (if you were told to raise mobile commerce 15% and got 9%, did you succeed or fail?) — but that harder conversation is the point: the job is to reduce cycle time and maximise learning.
- Instead of end-of-project sign-off, teams report back on a cadence (monthly/quarterly): here are the experiments and discovery we ran, here's what we shipped and learned, here's the movement on the metric, and here's what we'll do next.
