Michael Feathers explores how AI and large language models will reshape software development practice, framing his observations (recorded 4 May 2023) as deliberately provisional predictions he may have to apologize for later. His central thesis is that generative AI turns precise, deterministic software specification into something closer to natural-language communication, which shifts where developers must invest attention rather than eliminating the discipline of development itself.

* **AI development is a communication challenge, not a magic solution**: Feathers pushes back on the hope that you can simply talk to a computer and get a finished, money-making product. Because LLMs fill in unstated details, they may guess right or wrong, exactly like human communication where a loosely stated request gets interpreted differently than intended. This is jarring for developers used to being terribly precise.
* **The hallucination problem stems from the temperature problem**: He explains that GPT systems inject randomness ("temperature") to create novelty, but the same mechanism that enables creativity also enables wrong answers and false implementations. Researchers are working to vary this so you get some creativity while staying more deterministic.
* **We are entering a long era of generate and test**: As AI generates code, developers must review and verify it doesn't hallucinate, just like scrutinizing a pull request from a fellow developer to see if it truly satisfies the need. Review becomes a much bigger deal in this paradigm.
* **QA becomes far more important**: After two decades of pushing testing toward automation, Feathers argues people will now have to do much more review and behavior-checking to confirm the running systems actually carry out the intended behavior.

### Modularity and system design under AI
* **The context window is the key constraint**: He describes the context window as the system's working memory, analogous to how many items a human can hold in mind at once. With GPT-4 he cites roughly 50 pages of text as the working limit, which bounds the size of systems you can effectively generate or refactor in one session.
* **Modularity may persist as a necessity, not just a human discipline**: Although LLMs are already good at judging cohesion and coupling and can rewrite ugly code with good engineering principles, the context window limit means whatever you build must fit within the size the system can process at once, so modular pieces remain necessary.
* **The escape hatch problem applies to AI code generation**: Like every code generator in history, AI-generated code needs holes left in place to call hand-written behavior. He frames frameworks as the table with shaped holes and libraries as the pluggable pieces, the two classic reuse mechanisms, which he believes don't disappear.
* **Will source code ever go away?**: Feathers values source code as a consultable specification of behavior. He notes neural nets already eliminated explainability in domains like medical imaging classification, where the incentive to use them outweighs the lost explanation, suggesting AI-built software we can't inspect is plausible, yet he expects source code to persist, especially under regulation and liability.
* **Virtual forks as a new modularity approach**: He proposes feeding a high-quality codebase to GPT and asking it to make behavioral modifications and export a subset, like an open-source fork but virtual, using behavior embedded in existing source as a basis for specifying new behavior.

### Conversational and test-driven development
* **Pseudolang as a glimpse of conversational development**: He describes an experimental human-readable spec called Pseudolang that, once pasted into GPT-4, teaches the model a syntax (constraints, instructions, an interpreter persona) it can then use. The system tolerates loose, imprecise input rather than demanding exact syntax, and the language itself was co-invented collaboratively with GPT-4.
* **TDD works well with GPT by supplying tests as guardrails**: Rather than asking for a function and reviewing whatever comes back, Feathers prefers providing tests and constraints up front and asking the system to produce an implementation that satisfies them, putting guardrails in place to constrain what gets built.
* **Easing back on precision is a hard adjustment**: Many developers chose the field because they prefer the precision of talking to computers over the misunderstanding risk of talking to people; Feathers admits he struggles to relax his precision since it isn't strictly necessary now, and programming may become more like communicating with a co-worker.

### Economic, legal, and cautionary notes
* **Cheaper software raises the bar rather than ending demand**: Recalling a 1990s friend who thought we'd soon have written all the programs we need, he argues humans always want more, so easier development will make us demand far more complex and capable systems than before.
* **The business and legal landscape is unsettled**: He flags open questions about market concentration around OpenAI/Microsoft and Copilot, US legal cases on whether IP can be retained on output trained on others' work, and predicts a thriving market of locally hosted LLMs for privacy and security, paralleling the early distrust-then-adoption arc of cloud services.
* **The entropic asymmetry is the core caution**: Citing Kent Beck noting his hard-won skills are declining in value, Feathers names his own counterpoint: it is always easier to make messes than to clean them up. We must understand what we've generated well enough to maintain it, and AI makes it easy to attempt ambitious systems far beyond our ability to support.

title: .Net Rocks! 1436 DevOps Readiness Assessment with Jez Humble and Nicole Forsgren
SE Radio 208: Randy Shoup on Hiring in the Software Industry
How conscious investors can turn up the heat and make companies change
description: In a talk that's equal parts funny and urgent, consultant Vinay Shandal shares stories of the world's top activist investors, showing how individuals and institutions can take a page from their playbook and put pressure on companies to drive positive change. "It's your right to have your money managed in line with your values," Shandal says. "Use your voice, and trust that it matters."
notes:
Investors should move from simply divesting their capital to actively engaging with corporate management to drive environmental and social improvements, as this pressure creates more meaningful global change than simply holding a "greener" portfolio [1-3]. By adopting the tactics of activist investors, conscious shareholders can leverage the correlation between ESG performance and financial success to transform companies from within.

* **Value Creation through Engagement:** Activist investors create value by compelling management to make changes, a methodology that "conscious investors" can adopt to drive environmental and social progress.
* **Correlation Between ESG and Profit:** A clear correlation exists between ESG (Environmental, Social, and Governance) performance and financial performance, proving that sustainable practices are a competitive business advantage rather than just a moral obligation.
* **Profitable Sustainability:** Companies can successfully align products with environmental goals—such as manufacturing shoes from ocean plastic—to target conscious consumer segments and generate profit.
* **Ineffectiveness of Divestiture:** Selling stock (divestment) is an ineffective tool for global change because it cleans an individual’s portfolio without forcing management to reform, often leaving shares in the hands of investors who do not care about ESG issues.
* **Engagement Over Divestment:** To achieve real-world impact, conscious investors must shift from simply walking away to active, constructive, and collaborative engagement with company management.
* **Senior-Level Involvement:** Effective corporate change requires senior-most investment professionals to lead ESG discussions with CEOs and boards, rather than delegating these tasks to junior staff in risk or investor relations departments.
* **Urgency of ESG Performance:** ESG underperformance should be addressed with the same urgency and high-level intervention as financial underperformance.
* **Power of the Individual:** Individual asset owners (those with pension funds, sovereign wealth funds, or endowments) have the right and the power to demand that their money be managed in line with their values by pushing fund managers to engage rather than divest.

title: How to Write Acceptance Tests
description: "How to write Acceptance Tests" describes the use of Automated Acceptance Tests, an important tool in evaluating our software. These tests are focussed on answering questions like "Does our software do what our users want and expect?". Software Development teams have been trying to achieve this insight for many years, with often poor results. Grounded in the ideas of TDD and BDD this Acceptance Test Driven Development (ATDD) approach is based in the idea of creating a Domain Specific Language (DSL) for expressing test cases.
notes:
To prevent fragile and unmaintainable acceptance tests, developers should implement a **four-layer architecture** that strictly separates the business-focused "**what**" of a system from the technical "**how**" of its implementation. By isolating system interactions within protocol drivers and using a domain-specific language, you ensure that test cases remain stable, readable, and resilient even as the underlying technology or user interface evolves.

* **Core Objective:** Write automated acceptance tests that focus on **what** the system should do from an external user's perspective, rather than **how** it works technically.
* **Executable Specifications:** Acceptance tests should be written before the code to serve as executable specifications that drive development.
* **Four-Layer Architecture for Test Organization:**
    * **Layer 1: Test Cases:** Written entirely in the language of the problem domain. They are highly abstract and describe business scenarios (e.g., "buying a book") without mentioning UI elements like buttons or text boxes.
    * **Layer 2: Domain Specific Language (DSL):** An implementation layer that provides a library of reusable domain behaviors. It handles parsing, default values, and aliases to make writing new test cases fast and efficient.
    * **Layer 3: Protocol Drivers:** The only layer that understands the technical details of the system. It translates abstract domain commands into real interactions, such as API calls, message generation, or UI manipulation (e.g., via Selenium).
    * **Layer 4: System Under Test (SUT):** The actual application being evaluated, ideally deployed in a production-like environment.
* **Separation of Concerns:** By isolating technical implementation details within the Protocol Driver layer, the test cases remain stable even if the system's underlying technology or UI changes.
* **Test Portability and Reuse:** This architecture allows the same abstract test case to be reused across different interfaces—for example, using one protocol driver to test a web interface and another to test a mobile app or a physical robot.
* **Internal vs. External DSLs:** While tools like Cucumber (Gherkin) can be used, internal DSLs (hosted within a regular programming language like Java) are often preferred for their access to standard programming tools and IDE support.
* **Fragility Reduction:** Decoupling the "what" from the "how" solves the common problem of fragile tests that break with every minor system change.

title: Architecture for Flow with Wardley Mapping, DDD, and Team Topologies
description: This talk illustrates the concepts, connects the dots between DDD, Wardley mapping and team topologies, and demonstrates how these techniques help to evolve a fictitious legacy system for a fast flow of change.
notes:
Achieving a fast flow of change requires a holistic integration of business strategy, software architecture, and team organization through the combined use of Wardley Mapping, Domain-Driven Design, and Team Topologies. This alignment ensures that the entire socio-technical system is optimized for overall performance and adaptability rather than just the efficiency of its individual parts.

**Holistic Framework**: Integrate Wardley Mapping (Strategy), Domain-Driven Design (Software Design), and Team Topologies (Organization Design) to create adaptive socio-technical systems optimized for a fast flow of change.

**Systems Thinking**: Focus on improving the performance of the system as a whole rather than local optimization of separate parts. A system's performance is determined by how its parts interact, not how they perform individually.

**Wardley Mapping for Situational Awareness**: Use maps to visualize the value chain and the evolution of components (Genesis, Custom Build, Product/Rental, Commodity). This helps determine where to build in-house, where to buy, and how to anticipate market changes.

**DDD Subdomain Strategy**: 
*   **Core Domains**: Provide competitive advantage; build in-house using agile methods.
*   **Supporting Subdomains**: Necessary but provide no edge; use off-the-shelf products or open source where possible.
*   **Generic Subdomains**: Standardized problems (e.g., authentication); outsource to utility suppliers or use commodity services.

**Bounded Contexts as Ownership Boundaries**: Decompose systems into modular bounded contexts that serve as clear ownership boundaries for teams, fostering autonomy and reducing communication overhead.

**Team Cognitive Load**: Optimize for flow by ensuring a team's responsibilities do not exceed its cognitive capacity. Teams can generally manage more components in the "commodity" stage than in the "genesis" stage due to lower uncertainty.

**Four Team Types**:
*   **Stream-aligned**: Focused on a continuous flow of work from a segment of the business domain.
*   **Platform**: Provides internal self-service tools and services to reduce the burden on stream-aligned teams.
*   **Enabling**: Helps stream-aligned teams bridge capability gaps.
*   **Complicated Subsystem**: Handles highly specialized parts of the system (optional).

**Interaction Modes**: Use specific modes—**Collaboration** (joint discovery), **Facilitation** (helping/teaching), and **X-as-a-Service** (predictable delivery)—and evolve these modes as components mature.

**Efficiency Gaps and Bottlenecks**: Identify and eliminate delivery bottlenecks (e.g., manual infrastructure) by moving to commodity services (e.g., cloud/serverless) to free teams to focus on core value.

**Team-First Transition**: Implement architectural changes (like migrating a monolith to microservices) by reorganizing teams first. Start with a platform team to explore new technologies, then incrementally refactor the application into stream-aligned teams.

title: O11ycast Ep. #15, Speaking of Serverless with Rachel Myers and Emily Nakashima
description: Interesting discussion about serverless development, customer-vendor relations, using logs correctly, and better understanding your data model.
notes:
Serverless is a spectrum (managed services for parts of your stack), not an all-or-nothing ideology — and it doesn't remove the need to observe or to own your service. You can offload components but never ownership, so instrument for end-to-end success, turn engineering problems into bounded support problems, and treat logs as a last resort.

- Serverless ≠ no monitoring and ≠ no ops: nobody can own your service for you because only you know what to look for; you must keep the glue between outsourced components in your head.
- The Parse cautionary tale: telling people "don't worry your pretty little heads about the backend" works until they hit the front page and a hidden full-table-scan bites — multi-tenancy means you can't see the query planner you depend on.
- "Instrument as if you were a serverless developer": care only about whether your code executes end to end, not CPU/load/memory; propagate IDs so support sees the same trace the customer hit.
- Observability turns open-ended engineering problems into bounded, repeatable support problems — debugging Parse went from hours/days to seconds/minutes by standardizing how you answer new questions.
- The logs debate: prefer structured events (initialize one wide event per request, enrich it, ship once) over many unstructured loglines; events contain metrics, not vice versa. Logs/strace are a legitimate last line of defense (and local logs help with co-tenancy), but having to SSH in to tail a file signals a tooling gap.
- Cheap observability tricks: feature flags (LaunchDarkly) to toggle detailed logging without redeploying; dynamic sampling done like a scalpel not a club (sample the boring traffic heavily, keep errors and suspect users — a histogram bucket ≈ a sampled event).
- OpenTelemetry's promise is standardizing collection into frameworks so every developer gets instrumentation by default instead of everyone reinventing the wheel.
- Software engineers on call is the "second coming of DevOps": ops become consultants, not gatekeepers; the goal is to make production safe to experiment in ("a glass castle needs to become a playground") — and you can probe an org's culture in interviews ("take me on a tour of your tooling", "what happened last time you asked for help?").

title: How To Design A Good API and Why it Matters
description: Every day around the world, software developers spend much of their time working with a variety of Application Programming Interfaces (APIs). Some are integral to the core platform, some provide access to widely distributed frameworks, and some are written in-house for use by a few developers. Nearly all programmers occasionally function as API designers, whether they know it or not. A well-designed API can be a great asset to the organization that wrote it and to all who use it. Good APIs increase the pleasure and productivity of the developers who use them, the quality of the software they produce, and ultimately, the corporate bottom line.
notes:
**Effective API design is an essential skill for every programmer that centers on creating intuitive, minimalist interfaces which are easy to use correctly and nearly impossible to misuse.** By focusing on modularity and excluding unnecessary features, developers can ensure their code remains a valuable, evolving asset rather than a restrictive long-term liability.

**Importance of API Design**
* APIs are among a company's greatest assets if designed well and greatest liabilities if designed poorly.
* All programmers are API designers because good programming is modular, and inter-modular boundaries are APIs.
* Thinking in terms of API design improves code quality, making units more composable and reusable.
* API design and language design are nearly identical; both create tools for programmers to express intent.

**Characteristics of a Good API**
* Easy to learn and use, even without documentation.
* Hard or impossible to misuse; it should force the developer to do the right thing.
* Easy to read and maintain code written against it.
* Sufficiently powerful to meet requirements, but no more powerful than necessary.
* Easy to evolve over time to meet future needs.
* Appropriate to its intended audience and terminology.

**The Design Process**
* **Gather Requirements Skeptically:** Extract the actual problems users need to solve (use cases) rather than accepting their proposed solutions.
* **Agile Specification:** Start with a short (one-page) specification. Agility trumps completeness in the early stages to allow for major refactorings.
* **Write to the API Early:** Code against the API before implementing it to test usability and find flaws.
* **Exemplary Examples:** Spend significant time on example code, as it will be emulated by users for years.
* **The Rule of Threes:** For Service Provider Interfaces (SPIs), support at least three implementations during design to ensure the API is truly flexible.
* **Cohesive Vision:** While satisfying stakeholders, the design should feel like the work of a single mind to maintain beauty and cohesion.

**General Design Principles**
* **"When in doubt, leave it out":** You can always add a feature later, but you can almost never remove one once it is in the API.
* **Minimize Conceptual Weight:** The number of concepts a user must learn is more important than the number of methods or classes.
* **Hide Implementation Details:** Do not let internal choices (like specific exceptions or hash functions) leak into the API, as this inhibits future changes.
* **Minimize Accessibility:** Make everything as private as possible to maximize information hiding and minimize coupling.
* **Names Matter:** Names should be self-explanatory, consistent, and symmetric. Avoid cryptic abbreviations.
* **Documentation is Essential:** Document every public class, method, parameter, and exception. Specifically document side effects and state space for mutable objects.
* **Avoid Transliteration:** When moving an API between languages, do not copy methods exactly; adapt the design to the patterns and conventions of the new platform.

**Class Design**
* **Minimize Mutability:** Classes should be immutable unless there is a clear reason otherwise. This makes them simpler, thread-safe, and reusable.
* **Liskov Substitution Principle:** Only use inheritance when there is a true "is-a" relationship.
* **Design for Inheritance or Prohibit It:** If a class is not explicitly designed and documented for inheritance (defining self-use patterns), make it final.

**Method Design**
* **"Don't make the client do anything the module could do":** Avoid requiring boilerplate code or repeated calls to accomplish a single task.
* **Principle of Least Astonishment:** The user of an API should never be surprised by its behavior; predictability is worth the extra implementation effort.
* **Fail Fast:** Report errors as soon as possible after they occur—ideally at compile time via static typing.
* **Programmatic Access:** Always provide a programmatic way to access data that is also available in string form to prevent users from having to parse strings.
* **Overload with Care:** Avoid ambiguous overloads where different methods might be called depending on how a value is cast.
* **Use Appropriate Types:** Favor interfaces over specific classes and use the most specific type possible for input parameters.
* **Consistent Parameter Ordering:** Use the same order for parameters across similar methods to prevent silent bugs.
* **Avoid Long Parameter Lists:** Ideally, limit parameters to three or fewer. Use helper classes or the Builder pattern for complex configurations.
* **Avoid Null Returns:** Return zero-length arrays or empty collections instead of null to prevent unnecessary null-pointer checks in client code.

title: Accelerating large engineering organisations with Internal Platforms
description: Interesting talk on how to accelerate an engineering organization by creating an internal platform team with product focus. The talk is full of interesting examples.
notes:
Accelerating large-scale engineering requires treating internal developer platforms as **products** that prioritize developer experience and standardized **"golden paths"** to minimize cognitive load and manual toil. By focusing on self-service capabilities and the seamless integration of tools, organizations can achieve massive **multiplier effects** in efficiency, security, and delivery speed across all autonomous teams.

**Core Concepts & Efficiency Model**
*   **Engineering Efficiency Formula:** Efficiency is defined as (Total Engineers - Platform Engineers) × Boost Factor, accounting for the law of diminishing returns.
*   **Resource Allocation:** Data suggests approximately 25% of an engineering organization should be dedicated to platform teams to optimize the "boost factor" for product teams.
*   **The Four Key Metrics (DORA):** High-performing teams are measured by Deployment Frequency, Lead Time for Changes, Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR), and Change Failure Rate.

**Platform Principles**
*   **Platform as a Product:** Move away from a ticketing/infrastructure mindset toward a self-service model with high-quality documentation, tutorials, and clear Service Level Objectives (SLOs).
*   **The Golden Path:** Use the Pareto Principle to identify the 80% of common use cases and make "the right thing the easiest thing to do."
*   **Focus on Glue and UX:** The platform should not compete with cloud providers (AWS, etc.) but rather integrate and "glue" them together to create a seamless end-to-end developer experience.
*   **Thinnest Viable Platform:** Prioritize a thin, reliable, and highly usable layer over a broad, complex set of poorly maintained services.
*   **Toil Observation:** Identify and automate repetitive, low-value work (upgrades, migrations, rebasing) to unburden product teams.

**Operational Strategies**
*   **Transparent Migrations:** Strive for "no migrations" where the platform evolves underneath the developer's feet without requiring manual intervention or pull requests.
*   **Multiplier Effects:** Centralized platform changes create economies of scale (e.g., migrating a logging provider was found to be 15x cheaper when done through the platform vs. individual teams).
*   **Escape Hatches:** Provide clear, public APIs that allow teams with legacy or specialized needs to leverage specific parts of the platform without being forced into an all-or-nothing adoption.
*   **Data-Driven Insights:** Use an event bus to collect data from the entire lifecycle (code to production) to provide teams with cost transparency and performance facts.

**Lessons Learned**
*   **Blast Radius Management:** Avoid single, massive clusters which act as single points of failure. Instead, use multiple smaller, partitioned clusters.
*   **Disposable Infrastructure:** Treat clusters as disposable entities to maintain "hygiene," prevent "special snowflake" configurations, and increase iteration speed.
*   **Portfolio Management:** Regularly decommission obsolete internal tools that have been commoditized by the industry to reduce cognitive load on teams.
*   **Culture of Experimentation:** Platform teams must adopt product development mindsets, including the ability to fail cheaply and conduct A/B testing on infrastructure features.

title: Kubernetes Impact with Clayton Coleman
description: SE Daily interview with Clayton Coleman, lead engineer of OpenShift (Red Hat), on Kubernetes' shift toward stability at scale — etcd, autoscaling, monitoring — and why platform-as-a-service is the productive interface between developers and complex clouds.
notes:
In this Software Engineering Daily interview, Clayton Coleman — lead engineer of OpenShift, Red Hat's Kubernetes-based PaaS — discusses how Kubernetes, now running clusters with hundreds of thousands of containers, has shifted its core contributors' focus toward stability, and why platform-as-a-service is the missing layer that makes enterprises productive on complex clouds.

- Stability over features: the architecture goal is to "do the minimum we need" and keep Kubernetes simple — round it off so people build on top or alongside it; "if Kubernetes is done to a 95% level, people can rely on it and focus on what drives their business."
- etcd is praised as "the simplest possible thing that works" — Google adopted it off the shelf rather than its internal Chubby, and etcd3 was redesigned with CoreOS using lessons from etcd2; in HA configurations the masters tolerate node failures and rarely cause outages.
- They explore pulling the Prometheus monitoring system into Kubernetes itself, weighing the architectural pros and cons; autoscaling and monitoring are the areas making Kubernetes easier to operate.
- PaaS as interface: it sits between the cloud provider and the developer shipping code, giving an opinionated, more productive, multi-cloud-portable way to use complex clouds — Coleman is surprised more organizations haven't adopted some form of PaaS. He closes on the selfless open-source community that keeps the software going.

title: Rethinking growing engineers in the age of AI
description: Meri Williams explores the urgent need to rethink engineering growth in the age of AI, advocating for a shift from manual coding tasks toward early tech leadership, systems thinking, and a "healthy paranoia" that allows leaders to "surf" the waves of technological change rather than be overwhelmed by them.
notes:
Engineering leaders must proactively **redesign training models** to replace the "deliberate practice" once provided by repetitive junior tasks now handled by AI, focusing instead on accelerating **foundational skills, systems thinking, and code review** much earlier in an engineer's career. Ultimately, rather than resisting technological shifts, organizations should embrace a sense of **"healthy paranoia"** and teach their teams to "surf" the waves of AI by prioritizing critical thinking and a deep understanding of why systems work or fail.

- **Disruption of Entry-Level Roles:** AI coding assistants are performing the well-scoped, repetitive tasks traditionally assigned to junior engineers, leading to a significant reduction—estimated at 75%—in entry-level hiring across the industry.
- **Loss of Deliberate Practice:** Traditional junior work provided "accidental" deliberate practice through repetitive tasks and immediate feedback; because AI now handles this work, leaders must find new ways to help engineers build foundational skills like debugging and recognizing "code smells".
- **Accelerated Career Expectations:** Junior engineers are being required to perform "tech lead" functions, such as systems thinking and code review, much earlier in their careers because AI allows them to handle larger stories and complex tasks immediately.
- **New Required Skillsets:** Beyond coding, engineers must now prioritize critical thinking, effective AI prompting, and "healthy paranoia" to verify AI-generated outputs against documentation and real-world behavior.
- **AI as an "Endlessly Patient Mentor":** Junior staff are increasingly using AI to explain code and troubleshoot, which provides constant support but lacks the wisdom of human experience and may reduce traditional coaching interactions.
- **Bridging the "Unconscious Competence" Gap:** Senior engineers often struggle to teach because they perform expert tasks automatically; they must learn to actively deconstruct their knowledge and explain the "why" behind design decisions rather than hiding behind "best practices".
- **Adapting Training Models:** Rather than resisting AI, engineering leaders must redesign training to focus on foundations and mentorship, adopting the philosophy that while you "can't hold back the ocean" of technological change, you can "learn to surf".

title: The GIST Framework
description: In this talk from #MTP Engage Manchester consultant Itamar Gilad takes us through his GIST (goals, ideas, steps, tasks) framework.

title: Designing for Habitability
description: Tips and tricks for creating platforms that truly accelerate the pace of innovation in companies.
notes:
**Effective software platforms must be designed as habitable products that prioritize developer enablement and trust over control, offering an optional "paved road" that users choose because it genuinely simplifies their work.** This approach ensures that platform teams remain focused on creating value and supporting autonomous, productive teams rather than enforcing restrictive governance.

* **Habitability in Software:** Systems should be designed so that users can understand their construction and make changes comfortably and confidently. Decisions should prioritize making the daily lives of developers easier and more productive.
* **Platform as Enablement:** The mission of a platform team is delivery enablement, not just owning technology. Their goal is to help stream-aligned teams focus on delivering value by reducing their cognitive load.
* **Product Mindset:** Treat the platform as a product. This includes having a product owner, identifying specific user needs, and conducting outreach and training rather than operating in a silo.
* **The "Paved Road" Metaphor:** Provide a curated, smooth experience with guardrails for common tasks. This path should be the easiest way to get things done, but it should remain a road rather than a "railway track."
* **Optionality:** A platform should be optional to use. Forcing adoption removes the incentive for the platform team to make the tool easy to use and can lead to shadow IT or talent attrition.
* **Self-Service and Trust:** Platforms must embrace self-service via APIs. Resistance to self-service often stems from a lack of trust, which ultimately slows down the organization and creates bottlenecks.
* **Universal Space Design:** Similar to skyscraper architecture, platforms should provide a central core of essential services while leaving the surrounding "space" flexible for developers to reconfigure based on their specific needs.
* **Avoid Tooling-Based Governance:** Implementing governance solely through restrictive tooling leads to control-oriented platforms that developers will eventually bypass. Governance should be about clarity and support, not just saying "no."
* **Curated Experience:** Instead of exposing the full complexity of ecosystems like Kubernetes, platform owners should provide an appropriate level of abstraction and curation that fits the organization's specific skills and problems.
* **Socio-Technical Focus:** Software is a system of both technology and people. If the platform does not focus on the human experience and building trust, the technology itself will fail to deliver its intended benefits.

title: Teal Organizations
description: How they are trying to be a teal organization at Codurance. Very interesting talk.
notes:
True organizational agility and empowerment are achieved by replacing traditional hierarchies with a culture of **radical transparency and decentralized decision-making**, where every team member is trusted with full business information to actively shape the company’s future. Success lies in fostering a **trust-based environment** where individuals are not just employees but active contributors to the organization's organic evolution and shared purpose.

**Teal Organization Framework:** Organizations evolve through stages—Red (Impulsive), Amber (Conformist), Orange (Achievement), and Green (Pluralistic)—leading to Teal, which focuses on self-management, wholeness, and evolutionary purpose [1-4].

**Decentralized Decision-Making:** Command-and-control structures are replaced by "Initiative Circles," where any employee can propose a change if they can convince at least two others to join the effort [5-7]. 

**The Advice Process:** Decisions are made by those who care enough to participate; those who do not join an initiative implicitly accept the outcome, though they can start a new initiative later to propose further changes [8-11].

**Radical Financial Transparency:** All employees have 24/7 access to detailed company financials, including client-specific revenue, monthly expenditures, and the status of the company's three-month survival buffer [12-14].

**Open Salaries:** To ensure fairness and eliminate workplace politics, every employee’s salary—from junior apprentices to founders—is visible to everyone in the company [15-17].

**Inverting Core and Support Functions:** Support roles like HR, sales, and finance exist only to serve core engineering functions; developers are encouraged to handle management and sales tasks to prevent support silos from dictating company rules [18-21].

**Context-Dependent Practices:** Organizational practices are not universal; they must be adapted to the specific context of the company to serve core values like business agility and craftsmanship [22-24].

**Talent Investment:** A strong learning culture is maintained through apprenticeship programs where the company pays people to learn, treating talent development as a core business strategy.

**Wholeness and Relationships:** Building personal, family-oriented relationships among staff fosters empathy, making it easier to navigate professional difficulties and performance issues.

**Evolutionary Purpose over Fixed Missions:** Traditional mission and vision statements are often "empty words"; instead, the organization should "sense" its direction organically, allowing its people to reshape the company as they grow [27-29].

**Organic Growth:** Success is defined by doing meaningful work and maintaining a sustainable environment rather than hitting arbitrary headcount or revenue targets.

**Managing Informal Hierarchies:** While formal hierarchies are removed, informal hierarchies based on expertise and influence remain; leaders must consciously step back from initiatives to avoid disproportionately influencing outcomes

title: Improving Software Flow Build Stuff
description: Great presentation, in which Randy, starts from the 5 ideals of the Unicorn project (Locality and Simplicity, Focus, Flow, and Joy, Improvement of Daily Work, Psychological Safety, Customer Focus) to describe what we can do as technical leaders and as engineers to improve our ability to build and deliver software.
notes:
To achieve high-performing software delivery, organizations must cultivate a **virtuous cycle of continuous improvement** and **psychological safety**, where modular architecture and focused flow allow teams to prioritize meaningful customer outcomes over mere technical output. Success depends on the insight that **improving daily work is as vital as the work itself**, requiring a disciplined, blameless approach to problem-solving and a commitment to diverse, inclusive collaboration.

*   **Locality and Simplicity**: Architectural changes should be local and isolated to prevent breaking distant parts of the system. This principle drives the evolution from monoliths to modular microservices, as seen at companies like eBay, Twitter, and Amazon [1-3].
*   **Conway's Law**: A duality should exist between modular architecture and modular organization, where small, autonomous teams manage individual services to allow for independent design, development, and deployment.
*   **Focus as a Predictor of Excellence**: Cognitive control—the ability to focus—is the best predictor of high achievement and individual success. Practices like mindfulness or meditation can help train this attention.
*   **The Flow State**: High performance is achieved when individuals are in a "flow state," where there is a perfect balance between the work challenge and the person's skills.
*   **Maker vs. Manager Schedules**: Developers (makers) require long, uninterrupted blocks of time to maintain flow, while managers often operate on fragmented schedules. Leaders should protect developer time by avoiding meetings scheduled in the middle of these work blocks.
*   **Limiting Work in Progress (WIP)**: Teams should focus on fewer tasks at once and release incremental units of work to get faster feedback and reduce the risk of abandoning large amounts of unfinished work [11-13].
*   **Improvement Budget**: Teams should break the cycle of technical debt by allocating a dedicated budget (often 20–30%) for improving tools, builds, and infrastructure rather than negotiating every small fix.
*   **The Improvement Kata**: Improving daily work is even more important than doing daily work itself. Use the "Plan-Do-Check-Act" cycle to conduct small experiments that iteratively improve the system.
*   **Psychological Safety**: This is the most critical factor for team success; it allows members to take interpersonal risks and bring their "whole selves" to work without fear of punishment or humiliation [19-21].
*   **Blameless Postmortems**: After an incident, teams should focus on systems-level learning—improving detection, diagnosis, and prevention—rather than finding individuals to blame.
*   **Diversity and Decision-Making**: Diverse and inclusive teams make measurably better business decisions and deliver stronger results than homogeneous teams.
*   **Outcomes Over Outputs**: Success should be measured by business results (revenue, profitability, market share) and customer metrics rather than just the volume of code produced [25-27].
*   **Disciplined Problem Solving**: The core job of an engineer is to solve customer problems; sometimes the most efficient solution involves process or assembly line changes rather than writing new code [28-30].

title: Product Roadmaps with Bruce McCarthy
description: A talk about Product Roadmaps and how changing how businesses see roadmaps will help teams build more impactful software for their customers.

title: The New Faces of Continuous Improvement | Dev Interrupted Engineering Panel
description: A very interesting panel on high performance teams, engineering culture, continuous delivery, etc.
notes:
Engineering organizations thrive when they prioritize **rapid feedback loops** and **"paved paths"** that minimize developer toil, allowing teams to focus on creative problem-solving rather than administrative drudgery. Success is sustained by a **leadership culture of trust and empathy** that uses human-centric metrics to value well-being and autonomy as much as technical output.

**Workflow and Feedback Loops**
*   **15-Minute Feedback Cycles:** Aiming for a 15-minute feedback loop is a cornerstone of high-performing teams. Rapid cycles keep the original intent fresh, while longer cycles lead to context decay, batching of changes, and a loss of individual ownership.
*   **Paved Paths:** Developing a "paved path" or "golden path" maximizes developer efficiency by providing curated components and automated workflows, allowing engineers to focus on their domain rather than infrastructure.
*   **Intentional Innovation:** While engineers should be free to deviate from the paved path, they must own the support for those choices. Successes from these deviations should be brought back into the central paved path for the whole organization.
*   **Guardrails over Rules:** Use flexible guardrails rather than stringent rules to manage risk, particularly as a company scales.

**Data and Metrics**
*   **Beyond Activity Tracking:** Avoid the trap of measuring only activity (like lines of code). Use the **SPACE framework** to track Satisfaction, Performance, Activity, Communication/Collaboration, and Efficiency/Flow.
*   **DORA Metrics:** Deployment frequency, cycle time, change failure rate, and mean time to recovery provide a vital baseline for understanding organizational health.
*   **The "Fifth Metric":** Track how often teams are alerted outside of business hours. Frequent interruptions to personal life drain "life force" and lead to burnout and turnover.
*   **Metrics as Tools, Not Weapons:** Data should be used to help teams identify bottlenecks and improve, never for individual performance grading or compensation adjustments, which leads to gamification.

**Culture**
*   **Professional Dream Team:** High-performing organizations should be viewed as professional dream teams rather than "families." This emphasizes autonomy, mastery, and meaning while maintaining professional boundaries.
*   **Context over Control:** Leaders should provide the necessary context for teams to make independent decisions rather than exerting top-down control.
*   **Radical Transparency:** Foster a culture where information is shared broadly by default to empower decentralized decision-making.
*   **Authenticity and Vulnerability:** Leaders build trust by being authentic and vulnerable. Admitting to "having a bad day" or being "not okay" makes it safe for others to do the same, reducing anxiety.

**Leadership and Management**
*   **Leaders as Multipliers:** A true "10x engineer" is not just a high individual performer but a leader who multiplies the capabilities and behaviors of those around them.
*   **Tending the Sociotechnical System:** The primary job of technical leadership is to maintain the sociotechnical system (builds, deploys, and processes) so engineers can spend their time on business value rather than toil.
*   **Coaching from C to A:** Managers should shift from doing the work themselves to coaching and mentoring their team members, focusing on scaling their own skills through others.
*   **Leading with Empathy:** Effective leadership requires walking in the shoes of both developers and customers to identify and fix friction points.
*   **Forcing Time Off:** Leaders must actively push teams to take contiguous time off to prevent burnout, recognizing that loyal employees often work themselves to a point of desensitization.
*   **Diverse Staffing:** Prioritize staffing diverse teams to bring a wealth of competing opinions and experiences to the table, preventing the pitfalls of monoculture development.

title: Leading Teams For Silicon Valley Tech Giants | Randy Shoup In The Engineering Room Ep. 7
description: This conversation explores the architectural evolution of Silicon Valley tech giants from monoliths to microservices, highlighting the importance of customer focus, asynchronous communication, and continuous delivery for achieving scale and velocity.
notes:
Successful software engineering at scale requires **evolving architecture—starting with a monolith and transitioning to decoupled, asynchronous systems**—to match the changing needs of the business and its customers. Technical leaders should focus on creating a **"paved road" of platform services** and adopting **continuous delivery practices** that prioritize customer outcomes and team productivity over rigid technical dictates.

* **Start with a Monolith:** In the startup phase, when a team is searching for product-market fit and iterating rapidly, a monolith is the ideal choice. It allows for easier refactoring, local performance calls, and simpler deployments without the networking and operational complexities of microservices.

* **Architectural Evolution:** Architecture should be viewed as an evolving "skeleton" rather than a static decision. Major companies like eBay, Google, and Netflix all began as monoliths and evolved through multiple architectural phases (e.g., monolith to "mini-apps" to microservices) as they scaled to meet business needs.

* **Customer-Centric Architecture:** The primary job of engineering is to solve customer and business problems. Architecture should be "isomorphic" to the problem domain, using the customer’s view of the world as the organizing force for the system's structure.

* **Three Dimensions of Decoupling:** Effective system design involves decoupling across three planes:
    1. **Domain boundaries:** Dividing sub-domains using concepts like Bounded Contexts.
    2. **Complexity types:** Separating "essential" domain logic from "accidental" complexity like infrastructure and platform concerns.
    3. **Time (Asynchrony):** Decoupling operations so they do not need to occur simultaneously.

* **Asynchrony and Scalability:** Most web-scale systems should favor asynchronous, event-driven communication and eventual consistency (the AP solution in CAP theorem). This reduces system complexity, increases availability, and provides more degrees of freedom for scaling individual components.

* **Strategic Consistency:** Reserve synchronous transactions and strict consistency (CP) for the rare cases (often less than 5%) where they are strictly required by the domain, such as the core bidding logic of an auction where a canonical order is essential.

* **Platform Engineering as a Product:** At scale, organizations should invest in internal platform teams that treat other engineers as customers. This "vendor-customer" relationship focuses on building a "paved road"—a set of tools and frameworks that are strictly better and more productive for developers to use than building their own bespoke solutions.

* **Data-Driven Performance (DORA Metrics):** Engineering excellence can be scientifically measured and improved using four key metrics: deployment frequency, lead time for changes, change failure rate, and mean time to recovery (MTTR). Improving these "engineering outcomes" has a direct causal relationship with business success, profitability, and market share.

* **The Velocity Initiative:** To increase organizational speed, identify and systematically remove technical and process bottlenecks (e.g., long build times, manual security reviews, flaky tests). Turning these impediments into a platform team's backlog allows for continuous delivery improvements.

* **Small Batch Sizes:** Reducing the unit of work fits better into human cognitive limits and lowers the transaction cost of deployments. Smaller batches make it easier to identify the source of failures and allow for safer, more frequent releases.

* **Advanced Release Techniques:** Modern software delivery at scale leverages techniques such as "traffic mirroring" (shadowing production traffic for black-box testing) and automated "canary rollouts" that use monitoring data to automatically promote or roll back new code.

title: Agile Book Club: Evolutionary Design (with Kent Beck)
description: This was one of the best talks I've heard on software design in a long time. Excellent points on evolutionary design, testing, technical practices, etc. A full three quarters of an hour of wisdom.

title: Second Generation Lean Product Development Flow
description:
An introduction to Lean Product Development Flow given by Don Reinertsen at Adventures with Agile in London. 
This talk is a must to understand modern product development (Flow, uncertainty, Little's law, cost of delay, the value of feedback, queues, batch size, etc).
notes:
Effective product development requires shifting focus from maximizing resource utilization to managing the **economic flow of information** by quantifying the **cost of delay** and reducing **batch sizes**. To drive innovation, organizations must move beyond traditional manufacturing logic to actively manage **invisible queues** and exploit **variability** through fast feedback loops that allow for timely, evidence-based pivots.

*   **Focus on Economic Models:** Product development involves interacting variables that require quantification to reach system-level optimums. Use life-cycle profit as the primary metric to translate disparate variables (like cycle time and unit cost) into a single unit of measure.
*   **Cost of Delay (CoD):** Quantifying the cost per month of delaying a project is essential. Even rough analytical estimates are superior to intuition, which typically varies by a 50-to-1 range among team members.
*   **Managing Invisible Queues:** Unlike manufacturing, engineering queues consist of invisible information. Overloading capacity causes a nonlinear explosion in queue size and cycle time; for example, reducing capacity by 25% at high utilization can double or triple cycle times.
*   **The Utilization Trap:** Operating at near-100% utilization is efficient for machines but disastrous for flow in processes with variability. Successful product development requires maintaining a capacity margin to manage queues.
*   **Exploiting Variability:** While manufacturing seeks to eliminate variability, product development relies on it for innovation. By using asymmetric payoff functions—similar to financial options—teams can achieve high upside with limited downside.
*   **Batch Size Reduction:** There is a direct relationship between batch size and cycle time. Reducing batch sizes accelerates feedback, improves quality, and reduces risk.
*   **Lowering Transaction Costs:** To economically enable smaller batches, teams must focus on reducing the transaction cost of the process (e.g., automating software testing or deployment).
*   **WIP Constraints:** Starting work sooner does not mean finishing sooner. Controlling Work-In-Progress (WIP) is the most effective way to reduce cycle time, as defined by Little's Law (Cycle Time = WIP / Departure Rate).
*   **Weighted Shortest Job First (WSJF):** Prioritize work by dividing the Cost of Delay by the duration of the task. This ensures the maximum economic benefit is produced per unit of time consumed.
*   **Fast Feedback Loops:** Rapid feedback creates "embedded options" to pivot or shut down failing initiatives early. This improves the overall economics by preventing the waste of resources on low-value features.
*   **Shortened Planning Horizons:** High-speed development improves requirement accuracy. It is easier to define what a customer needs six months from now than three years from now.
*   **Decentralized Decision Rules:** Create simple economic decision rules (e.g., "any engineer can spend $300 to save one pound of weight") to allow large teams to make aligned, system-optimal decisions without constant management oversight.

title: Evolutionary Architecture as Product @ CircleCI
description: Robert Zuber discusses how the evolution of software development since 2011 has driven the evolution of CircleCI's architecture. From the explosive adoption of Docker to the steady rise of microservice architectures, the changing demands of software engineering teams have proven to be deeply coupled with the structure of their service–far more than they anticipated when they started the business.
notes:
Robert (Rob) Zuber is CTO of CircleCI; he joined when the company was about 20 people, mostly engineers, and was a co-founder of a business acquired by CircleCI (whose founder is now CircleCI's CEO). In this QCon San Francisco 2019 talk, his core thesis is that architecture in a product company must evolve continuously because the customer — software developers — is "whimsical," constantly adopting new tools and platforms that the architecture must absorb. He drives toward three takeaways: design for change, simplify the basics so you can keep your head up and look ahead, and think like a product manager. He illustrates each with concrete, often painful, stories from CircleCI's history rather than presenting a clean end-state.

* **The customer is whimsical and drives the architecture**: Zuber opens with io.js — forked from Node over frustration with Joyent's stewardship around Thanksgiving 2014, screamed about by angry customers by February 2015, supported by CircleCI two weeks later, then remerged into the Node Foundation by June 2015, after which "this platform no longer existed." His point: "this is my customer" — developers love trying new technologies, and those choices dictate what CircleCI must support. "The way that our system is architected defines our ability to support this kind of whimsical behavior."

* **The mono-image and its accidental simplicity**: In 2014-2015 CircleCI ran a Clojure monolith on large AWS hosts that received webhooks, scheduled work, and ran builds in LXC containers (the pre-Docker precursor) all from "one single image" — a "mono-image" containing everything anyone might want. It "sounds like a terrible idea, but it was actually a fantastic idea" because it created great operating simplicity. Built on Ubuntu 12.04, it handled multiple language versions via RVM/Virtualenv/NVM, but could hold only one version each of MySQL, Postgres, Mongo, Redis.

* **Single-database-version pain and "version numbers are never semver"**: A patch upgrade of Neo4j changed root-user authentication and instantly broke every Neo4j user's tests — "everyone's favorite place to massively change core behavior is in the last dot of the version number." They mitigated with an internal tool called "pseudohacks" that altered container state after spin-up. Customers stuck between MySQL 5.5 and 5.6 would uninstall and reinstall the DB at the start of each build, adding 2-3 minutes to build times measured otherwise in tens of seconds.

* **Scaling crisis from giant images**: As more language versions were added, the container image grew larger, and bringing a machine online eventually took 45-50 minutes — crippling for a system whose daily traffic more than doubles and spikes sharply. After GitHub once failed to deliver webhooks for hours then dumped them all at once, scaling that cliff was nearly impossible. Around May 2015 they hired a "human scaler" on a 3-month contract because the auto-scaler had stopped working; after four extensions they made the role full-time and hired two more. Adding Ubuntu 14.04 meant running two parallel "one true container" systems with all the same problems duplicated.

* **The 2.0 rewrite enabled composition, not just one more language**: The platform rebuild split the monolith into a small cluster of services (receive hooks, schedule, hand off, collect output) plus a local agent that composes the exact Docker containers a customer requests at runtime (e.g. "Ruby 2.6.5 on Stretch, this MySQL, a down version of Redis"). They build their own DB images for better internal layer caching and disable fsync since durability is pointless for a container used 30-60 seconds. "Did we design for change? No" — but the result could support "any combination of tools that our customers wanted to use."

* **Simplify the basics so you can keep your head up**: Using his kids' soccer training as a metaphor, players keep their head up to read the field only because they are so proficient with the basics. "In software, we love to tie our shoelaces together" — making easy problems hard for ourselves so we can't focus on what's ahead.

* **The ClojureScript/Om frontend trap**: Early 2014 CircleCI chose ClojureScript (with the Om framework, a wrapper around React) on the theory they'd be better at frontend using their backend language. Om's creator David Nolen later built Om Next; CircleCI tried to adopt it iteratively and ended up with "two state models inside of our frontend," gave up and tried switching to React iteratively, ended up with three, and finally just rewrote the whole thing. The frontend was so stuck that the workflows feature shipped in the backend while the user dashboard still showed the old jobs/builds domain. On StackShare, React showed 7,856 companies and 18,312 developers, while Om's adoption was effectively CircleCI and a couple of ex-employees — when they asked the Clojurians Slack for Om help, "we got links to our own code."

* **Don't chase the step-function advantage**: "Please don't believe" that an obscure cool technology no one else is smart enough to use will make you faster. "The thing that's going to keep you moving fastest and delivering real value to customers is ignoring these desires" and focusing on tools that let you easily build customer value.

* **You are a product manager (the complexity argument)**: Citing John Ousterhout's "A Philosophy of Software Design" — "if we want to make it easier to write software... then we must find ways to make software simpler" — and Moseley & Marks' "Out of the Tar Pit" distinction between essential complexity ("inherent in and is the essence of the problem as seen by the users") and accidental complexity (everything else). The chain: if your job is to reduce complexity, and you can only identify essential complexity by understanding the user problem, "then you are a product manager." His kitchen-remodel story (forgetting to order cabinets with a 6-8 week lead time) inverts to make the engineer the architect who must see patterns and stay ahead of what's being asked.

* **The Docker driver story — engineers must own product direction**: Docker originally ran on LXC, so CircleCI executed Docker builds inside LXC containers via the Docker LXC driver. In March 2014 Docker launched Libcontainer ("re-implemented LXC in Go"); in late summer 2015 they deprecated the LXC driver, forcing CircleCI to tell customers to stay on Docker 1.9. "Nobody cares about the internals of Docker. This is our job" — and this was "below our product team," something engineers discussed daily but PMs didn't understand. The eventual fix was a remote Docker engine talking over to a system holding the customer's persistent store, after intermediate detours (tarring caches to S3, which was slower than just pulling images; giving raw VMs, which lost the prebuilt environment). All done "under duress" while waiting for the product team to prioritize it.

* **Elixir echo of io.js, and a realistic stance on speed**: Late 2016, an Elixir user complained builds went "from 20 seconds to 10 minutes and nine seconds" — "if you're going to shame someone publicly, shame them with precision" — yet ended with "CircleCI is awesome, though." After Zuber offered early access on a Friday night, by Saturday morning the same user's friend J Treguna had builds down to 45-50 seconds. "If you expect your CI platform to run your tests at exactly the same speed as your laptop, your CI prices will go up."

* **Q&A — balance, convincing PMs, evaluating new tech**: On avoiding over-engineering: surviving companies must stay on the "scrappy end"; invoke the rule of three ("don't abstract a system until you've had to do it twice"); "if we were building CircleCI 3.0 right now, I'd probably be fired." The 2.0 migration was done incrementally — pull off a tiny bit of traffic with minimal change, learn, repeat — because "simple is easier to change... even if you didn't design for that specific change." On convincing product to invest in tech upgrades, he recommends "cost of delay" from Reinertsen's "Principles of Product Development Flow": convert technical work into business/financial impact (dollars or euros) so the discussion becomes about a shared model/data rather than competing narratives — "product will win" an opinion argument. On adopting non-mainstream tech: start from the problem, not the technology; weigh total cost not just early delivery cost (UIs are "all in" while a service backend lets you try something on one service and throw it out cheaply when it fails).

title: Engineering Productivity @Google
description: Interesting talk on how engineering productivity is organized at google
notes:
To maintain agility and innovation at scale, organizations must treat engineering productivity as a dedicated discipline that uses data-driven automation and metrics to optimize the entire developer workflow. By shifting from a traditional QA model to one where developers own the quality of their code, companies can remove friction and empower their teams to ship high-quality products faster.

*   **Definition and Scope**: Engineering Productivity focuses on optimizing the entire developer workflow—from authoring and experimentation to production and monitoring—to remove frustration and toil.
*   **Scale and Throughput**: The discipline supports over 2 billion lines of code and roughly 60,000 daily code submissions, managed by a dedicated team of 2,000 productivity engineers.
*   **Operational Ratio**: Google maintains an average ratio of 18 developers to 1 productivity engineer. This model shifts quality ownership to the developers rather than relying on a separate QA department.
*   **Cycle Time Goals**: A primary target is maintaining sub-30-minute end-to-end cycle times for developers to check in and verify their changes.
*   **Evolution of Focus**: The discipline transitioned from basic CI/CD and release automation (2004–2010) to addressing "the last mile," including code complexity, IDE intelligence, and large-scale refactoring.
*   **Metrics and Instrumentation**: Everything is instrumented, including Change List (CL) latency, release frequency, rollback rates, code coverage, code review latency, and production outages.
*   **ML-Driven Productivity**: Machine learning is used to make ROI-based decisions, such as identifying which tests to skip or run based on historical failure rates and the likelihood of catching a bug.
*   **Core Roles**:
    *   **SETI (Software Engineer, Tools and Infrastructure)**: Builds the core tools and infrastructure.
    *   **Test Engineers**: Technical engineers focused on systems thinking, gap analysis, and coverage analysis.
    *   **Release Engineers**: Focus on automating the push to production.
*   **Adoption Strategy**: New tools and practices are adopted through partnership and data-driven evidence of reduced toil rather than top-down mandates.
*   **Hardware-Software Challenges**: Increasing focus is placed on the complexity of integrating software across diverse ecosystems like Android, wearables, and home automation.
*   **Closing the Loop**: Production data and assertions are fed back into the development workflow to ensure systems are not just "up" but behaving as expected.

title: se-radio-episode-234-barry-oreilly-on-lean-enterprise
description: A interesting interview about the book Lean Enterprise and the application of Lean and Agile principles to drive innovation and product creation at our "disruptive" era.

title: The Biggest Problem With UI
description: This talk explains why UI/UX design should be treated as an integral software design choice rather than a static specification, advocating for development teams to own the UI to ensure the system accurately reflects the user's mental model.
notes:
**UI/UX design must be treated as a core software design choice** rather than a static specification, ensuring the resulting system effectively reflects the user's mental model. Organizations should move away from siloed design teams and instead **empower streamline development teams to own UI responsibility**, using design experts as enabling teams to foster autonomy and improve overall usability.

- **The separation of UI design and backend development** is a significant organizational mistake that fosters transactional working, limits innovation, and results in lower-quality solutions.
- **UI/UX design is an integral part of software design**—it is a choice of how a system solves a problem rather than a requirement defining what the system needs to do.
- **A real user interface is an interactive system** that relies on the user’s mental model of the entire system; usability cannot be divorced from the code or the assumptions made during development.
- **Using UI mockups as static specifications** creates development bottlenecks and prevents teams from focusing on the true behavioral goals of a feature.
- **UI specialists should function as "enabling teams"** that support and coach stream-aligned teams to build their own capabilities rather than doing the work for them or directing it.
- **Stream-aligned development teams should own the responsibility for UI**, ideally handling 80% of typical UI tasks independently and relying on experts for the most exploratory work.
- **Autonomy in decision-making** is a key predictor of building better software faster; centralizing UI design often removes this autonomy and creates dependencies.
- **Design consistency is best achieved through reusable assets**, such as component libraries, stylesheets, and clear guidelines, rather than through centralized design silos.
- **Specifications should focus on behavioral needs** using techniques like Behavior-Driven Development (BDD) to ensure the system meets user needs, rather than just matching visual mockups.

title: What It Takes To Be A Software Engineer
description: Great and concise description of what software engineering is and the forces that apply to our profession.
notes:
Based on the video transcript** ...** and our previous conversation linking Dave Farley's ideas to Agile principles, the most important points of the presentation are:
• **Redefining Software Engineering:** Farley argues that software engineering is neither overly bureaucratic nor simply programming. Instead, it is a **pragmatic and scientifically rational approach focused on delivering working software**. He emphasizes that it encompasses much more than just writing code, including requirements, design, testing, deployment, monitoring, and production.
• **The Unique Nature of Software Development:** Unlike the production of physical goods, software development's primary challenge is **design, not production**, because copying digital information (the output of software) is essentially free** **. This makes software development fundamentally a discipline of **exploration and discovery**.
• **The Paramount Importance of Learning:** To excel in this discipline of exploration and discovery, software engineers must become **experts at learning**, drawing parallels with the scientific method.
• **Key Characteristics of a Learning-Focused Approach:** Farley outlines five crucial aspects of optimizing for learning in software engineering:
◦ **Iteration and Feedback:** Working in **small steps** to gather frequent **feedback** to ensure progress** **. This is a core tenet of Agile methodologies.
◦ **Working Incrementally:** Building systems through **many small steps**, allowing for mistakes and facilitating easier course correction. This aligns with Agile's iterative and incremental nature.
◦ **Working Experimentally:** Approaching changes as **experiments** by forming hypotheses, making predictions, and testing them in production. This resonates with Agile's emphasis on experimentation and continuous improvement.
◦ **Empiricism:** Recognizing that models don't need to be perfect and that learning from surprises allows for **incremental enhancement** of systems. This supports Agile's adaptive nature.
• **The Necessity of Managing Complexity:** Software systems, even seemingly simple ones, are inherently complex. Therefore, mastering techniques for managing this complexity is crucial in software engineering.
• **Principles for Managing Complexity:** Farley presents five interconnected ideas to effectively manage complexity:
◦ **Modularity:** Breaking down systems into independent, manageable pieces.
◦ **Cohesion:** Grouping related elements together.
◦ **Separation of Concerns:** Ensuring each part of the code has a distinct responsibility.
◦ **Abstraction:** Creating simplified models to understand and focus on specific parts.
◦ **Coupling:** Managing the dependencies between different parts of the system to prevent changes in one area from negatively impacting others.
• **The Benefits of Applying These Principles:** By focusing on learning and effectively managing complexity through these principles, teams can build software that is **easier to understand, easier to test, easier to change, and thus easier to incrementally evolve into something better **. This directly supports the goals of Agile development.
• **These Principles as the Definition of Good Software Engineering:** Farley concludes that applying these ideas is what truly defines software engineering and enables the creation of better software faster** **. If these principles are not being applied, then whatever is being done likely doesn't qualify as proper software engineering.

title: The Software Team Leader Manifesto
description: Roy Osherove's Team Leader Manifesto: a team leader's purpose is to grow the team to self-organization by matching leadership style (command / coach / facilitate) to the team's phase (chaos / learning / self-organizing).
notes:
The missing link in failed Agile adoptions is the team leader: most are appointed without training and default to keeping the status quo and putting out fires. The manifesto's core: "great teams are grown, not hired," so a leader's real purpose is to grow the team to self-organization — and to make themselves obsolete as the bottleneck.

- Measure yourself by the team's growth: ask each week whether each person learned a new skill. A flat line means you're not doing your job. A self-organizing team can solve its own problems (even "my machine is too slow") without you — if they must come to you, you're a bottleneck.
- Growth means challenging people out of their comfort zone (real fear = real learning). Learning a new framework in your stack doesn't count; jumping into a different culture does — Osherove left his CTO role to intern in a Ruby/Vim/Mac shop where his ego meant nothing. "You always want to be where you feel stupid."
- You must make time to learn (the book Slack): a new skill takes ~2-10x longer at first, so adding 20% to estimates fails — budget closer to double. Most teams have no learning time, so they go to a TDD course and never actually do TDD.
- Match leadership style to the team's phase (these are zones a team moves between, not one-way stages): chaos = no time to learn, so be a commander/dictator who shields the team and clears time (when the ship is sinking the captain gives orders); learning = time exists and is used, so be a coach who teaches people to solve their own problems ("what are you going to do about it?"); self-organizing = facilitate and keep them in the zone. Treating a self-organizing team like chaos (or vice versa) breeds unhappiness.
- Beware chaos addiction: fixing fires without tests works once, so you keep doing it — breaking the cycle means taking a scary stand to remove commitments and create learning time.
- Lead people, not machines: every software problem is a people problem — fix the skill, not just the code. Spend at least 50% of your time with the team, learn to confront people (Osherove's terror at coaching a slow typist, who happily learned touch-typing), and practice congruence (weigh self, other, and context — the story you tell yourself about a "malicious" colleague is an excuse to avoid talking to them).

title: Master Class with Marty Cagan
description: A great presentation on skilled product teams and leading product organizations. The questions at the end are also very interesting.
notes:
True innovation in product development occurs when leaders provide **strategic context** and empower cross-functional teams to solve problems, rather than stifling them with rigid processes or top-down control. To succeed, organizations must move beyond "fake agile" rituals and focus on the first principles of **continuous discovery**, frequent delivery, and fostering "missionary" teams that are deeply connected to their users.

* **Principles over Process**: Successful product companies prioritize first principles and culture over formal, rigid processes like SAFe, which are often described as "waterfall in disguise" and prioritize predictability over innovation.
* **Empowered Product Teams**: Organizations should move from "feature teams" or "delivery teams" (mercenaries) to "empowered teams" (missionaries) that are given specific problems to solve rather than a fixed roadmap of features to build.
* **The Empowered Engineer**: The most critical factor for consistent innovation is the empowered engineer. Engineers should be involved in the "what" and the "why" of a product, not just the "how" (coding).
* **Direct Access to Users**: Product teams (Product Managers, Designers, and Engineers) must have direct, unmediated access to real users and business stakeholders to understand risks and needs.
* **Frequent Releases**: True agility is defined by frequent, reliable, and decoupled releases—at minimum every two weeks—to maintain customer value and enable continuous learning.
* **Lead with Context, Not Control**: Management should provide "strategic context"—vision, strategy, and clear objectives—allowing teams the autonomy to discover the best solutions instead of using command-and-control tactics.
* **Addressing the Four Product Risks**: Product discovery must focus on reducing four key risks: Value (will customers buy/use it?), Usability (can they figure out how to use it?), Feasibility (can we build it with our tech/time?), and Viability (does it work for our business?).
* **Managers as Coaches**: A manager's primary responsibility is to coach their team on substantive skills (e.g., product management or design) to ensure they are competent and trustworthy enough to be empowered.
* **Thinking through Writing**: High-performing cultures often use written narratives (such as 6-page documents) to force deeper thinking, clearer logic, and more accountable decision-making than is possible with slide presentations.
* **Three Pillars of Transformation**: To become a product-led company, an organization must change how it builds (Continuous Delivery), how it solves problems (Product Discovery), and how it decides which problems to solve (Product Strategy).

title: How To Multiply Your Time
description: TEDxDouglasville
notes:
Rory Vaden argues that conventional time management is broken because it is purely logical (tips, tricks, apps, calendars) while the way people actually spend time is emotional, driven by guilt, fear, worry and anxiety. He reframes the entire problem: there is no such thing as time management, only self-management, and the real leverage comes from "multipliers" who give themselves emotional permission to spend time today on things that create more time tomorrow.

* **Time management is emotional, not just logical** — Inspired by his partner's 2-year-old daughter Haven begging "no work today," Vaden realized feelings of guilt, fear, worry and frustration dictate how we spend time as much as any to-do list.
* **There is no time management, only self-management** — You cannot manage time; it continues whether we like it or not. The only thing you can manage is yourself.
* **The three eras of time management thinking** — Era 1 (1950s-60s industrial revolution) was one-dimensional efficiency: do things faster. Era 2 (late 1980s, Stephen Covey's Time Management Matrix) added a second dimension, prioritizing by urgency and importance.
* **The hidden limitation of prioritizing** — Nobody talks about the fact that prioritizing creates no new time; it merely bumps item #7 on your list to #1. Efficiency is a hamster wheel; prioritizing is borrowing time from one activity to spend on another, like juggling.
* **The third dimension: significance** — Multipliers add a third calculation. If urgency is "how soon does something matter," significance is "how long is it going to matter." The 3-dimensional question is not "What's the most important thing I can do today?" but "What can I do today that would make tomorrow better?"
* **The premise for multiplying time** — You multiply your time by giving yourself the emotional permission to spend time on things today that give you more time tomorrow. The significance calculation changes everything.

### The Focus Funnel

* **Eliminate — the permission to ignore** — The first question is "Can I eliminate this? Is it even worth doing?" Next-generation time management has more to do with what you don't do than what you do. Perfection is achieved not when nothing more can be added but when nothing more can be taken away. Anything you say no to today creates more time tomorrow.
* **You are always saying no to something** — A multiplier told Vaden it is futile to try to never say no, because every time you say yes to one thing you simultaneously say no to an infinite number of others.
* **Automate — the permission to invest** — "Can I automate the task?" Setting up online bill pay costs 2 hours you never have, but saving 30 minutes a month breaks even in 4 months and yields ROTI (Return On Time Invested) thereafter. Automation is to time what compounding interest is to money; wealthy people think about money the way multipliers think about time.
* **Delegate — the permission of imperfect** — "Can I teach someone else how to do this?" The objection "they can't do it as well as I can" is only true absent the significance calculation; over time they master the task just as you did.
