* **The component techniques:** Progressive delivery packages practices not in the standard continuous delivery definitions — canarying (sending a small amount of traffic to test "does the canary snuff it"), A/B testing, and blue-green deployments (two identical environments, shifting traffic gradually). These differ from "press the button and everybody is going to experience this new service."

* **Two core notions:** Progressive delivery has "release progression" (gradually moving traffic over) and "delegation" (understanding user populations and putting the user or business in charge of when change happens).

* **Cloudflare's dogs, canaries, and pigs:** Moving past "cattle and pets," Governor describes Cloudflare's user-population taxonomy from CTO John: dogs are loyal paying customers protected from breaking changes; a rotating "canary city" (could be London, Munich, or Oslo) gets the janky stuff first; and "pigs" are people who signed up with stolen credit card information and get all the broken rollouts first — "there's a lot of signal in those complaints, even though they are thieves."

* **The business angle:** Continuous delivery and "debugging in production" (Charity Majors, Honeycomb.io) sound "absolutely freaking terrifying" to businesses. Progressive delivery appeals to them: Comcast's Greg Otto worried about rolling a breaking service to 30,000 customer service agents. Governor argues IT is shifting from being "the boat anchor" on the business.

* **Compartmentalization and isolation:** He praises Werner Vogels' essay on "10 years of compartmentalization" at AWS — you want firebreaks, not a homogeneous network where change propagates "like a wildfire." Everything needs isolation so people can move quickly and safely. Subbu Allamaraju's work on Expedia outages reinforces that "things break when you change things" — there's a reason Amazon doesn't do production deploys at Thanksgiving.

* **Adopters and tooling:** Datadog's Jason Yee built canary/A/B infrastructure on Istio and Kubernetes, accounting for geography (Japanese users may use an app differently) and timing. Grubhub does canary deploys to small cities first. Weaveworks (Stefan Prodan) built Flagger, a traffic-shifting tool with thresholds (move 5%, then 10%, then let Kubernetes roll across the fleet). CloudBees (Carlos Sanchez) calls it "the next step after continuous delivery."

* **Feature flags:** Building on Pete Hodgson's ThoughtWorks "Feature Toggles," Governor highlights LaunchDarkly (Adam Zimman describes them as "fancy if statements" — "If this is a beta user, give them this experience"), Split (split.io), and Wix's Petri. LaunchDarkly began thinking it was about developer experience but realized "it's actually about the service management experience."

* **Kubernetes consumption and homogeneity:** Google open-sourced Kubernetes (Docker declined to take it — "a bit like saying, 'No, we don't want to sign the Beatles'"), but Google's homogeneous network (Borg) is unlike others'. Target runs Kubernetes in stores and IBM Cloud Private exists because customers "don't have homogeneity of target environment" — both built polling systems for progressive delivery of Kubernetes itself, since long-term support is "dying" and currency matters for security.

* **Observability, GitOps, and abundance:** Google's SRE book signals are latency, errors, traffic, and saturation. Service meshes enable rollbacks, failovers, and service routing with built-in observability — traces, logs, and metrics (Cindy Sridharan, Charity Majors, Jaana Dogan). GitOps stores desired state in Git (Boeing stores user IDs in Git and rolls out via pull requests). Progressive delivery is enabled by "cloud abundance" — a Netflix engineer wanting 10 instances might spin up 100 and discard the 90 that don't meet performance characteristics. The ultimate goal is amazing developer experience that also serves user and customer experience.

* **Q&A — tooling maturity:** Asked whether a small team of 5-10 can do progressive delivery without a dedicated team, Governor's "easy answer is no" — current adopters have big focused teams — but he predicts "within the next 12 to 18 months, we're going to see much better automated tooling for canarying." He also noted state changes (data incompatible with prior versions) make rollbacks hard, reinforcing testing with "a small constituency first," and that incident management data (PagerDuty, the VictorOps/Opsgenie acquisitions by Atlassian) is "a gold mine" for closing the feedback loop.

title: Evolutionary Architecture with Neal Ford
description: software engineering daily

title: Curing Agile Hangover with Craftsmanship
description: Mashooq Badar and Sandro Mancuso on how UBS used software craftsmanship to cure the "agile hangover" — the technical and morale problems a Scrum adoption exposed but didn't solve.
notes:
Adopting Scrum at UBS fixed the process but, four years on, left them with more problems than they started with: 4-hour builds, unreliable/costly tests, mounting tech debt, low morale and an "us and them" mentality. The agile adoption was all process and no attention to the technical side — software craftsmanship is the counterbalance.

- The software itself is the key asset: process is only an enabler. Treat software as an asset whose lifespan you extend (that's where ROI comes from) by keeping a constant velocity, instead of building "a big piece of crap" you decommission after five years and rewrite the same way.
- Craftsmanship isn't just clean code and TDD — its cornerstones are a community of professionals, productive partnerships (vendors included, not second-class), and proud professionals who care (your reputation is on the line).
- Real empowerment: experts in delivery, architecture, production and engineering were placed at director level alongside the business, all equal — developers could even fire their Scrum Master.
- Fixing the inverted test pyramid: they had heavy, expensive system tests (and "unit tests" that tested nothing but code-coverage tools). They rebuilt a thin set of system tests for major flows only, pushed testing down to component level via BDD, and invested in well-crafted unit tests — cutting builds from ~4 hours to under 10 minutes.
- Quality everywhere: boy-scout rule, pair programming as the norm, GitHub-flow with short-lived branches where a different pair (ideally a different location) reviews and merges — spreading knowledge and a shared quality bar. Reframe legacy as "a great challenge," not someone else's mess.
- Continuous delivery by cross-pollinating dev and production-services (rotating people through the L3 support team), automating the manual release steps, and working toward zero-downtime deployment and continuous deployment (release on merge to master).
- Invest in people: permanent in-house coaches/mentors who lead by example (months with each team), internal and external communities of practice, a rigorous hiring process (pair-program on real code, not online tests — applied to vendors too), and a 10% improvement tax shown to the business each sprint. The goal: rebalance so both process and well-crafted software enable each other.

title: 5 Things That Waste Time & Money On A Software Project
description: This interesting video identifies common pitfalls in software development, such as not building what users want, using big teams, delaying feedback, chasing features over quality, and relying on manual regression testing, and advises optimizing for learning, fast feedback, and automated testing to improve productivity and reduce waste
notes:
The most important points from the presentation "5 Things That Waste Time & Money On A Software Project" are:
- **Building what users don't want is a waste of time and money**. It is difficult to know what the right things to build are because even when customers are asked what they want, they may not really know. Optimizing the development process to allow for many ideas and early identification of bad ones is key. Getting ideas into the hands of real users early provides fast feedback to determine if they are good or bad.
- **Large teams are not as effective as small teams**. Smaller teams are dramatically more productive.
- **Delaying feedback is costly and risky**. Working in smaller steps provides more opportunities to learn and reduces risk. Continuous integration and validation after each small step provides clarity and keeps the software in a working state.
- **Chasing features over quality is a common mistake that reduces production speed**. Prioritizing feature production over everything else keeps the work queue full, but leaves no room for urgent work and applies pressure to cut corners. Allowing space for work that isn't directly related to features helps maintain or increase the pace at which new things can be done.
- **Manual regression testing is slow, inefficient, low quality, and a misuse of human skills**. Regression testing should be done by machines via automated tests. This encourages design choices that result in more testable and better design systems.

title: Hammock Driven Development
description: Inspiring talk that seems to explain the two modes of thinking applied to problem-solving. Like Thinking fast and slow for developers :).
notes:
Rich Hickey delivers an experience report (explicitly not a methodology or science) on how he tackles hard problems, arguing that the most expensive and damaging software defects are not implementation bugs but problems of misconception: not understanding what we are doing before we do it. His central thesis is that real problem solving requires deliberate, distraction-free thinking that engages both the analytical waking mind and the synthesizing background mind, and that the hammock is the metaphor (and tool) for the latter.

* **Misconception, not implementation, is the real enemy.** The cheapest place to fix bugs is at design time. Testing, type systems, and QA address implementation defects, but nothing in our toolset catches a bad idea about what we are building.
* **Solve problems, don't build features.** A feature is just an attribute, the chrome knob, not the purpose of the car. Feature lists carry no guarantee of solving any real problem. Dig underneath what users ask for to find their actual problem, then reconcile the best solution with their request.
* **Avoiding a problem is not solving it.** Developers love being clever and feel great when they find a way around an obstacle, but working around a problem is not the same as solving it.
* **Problem solving is a practiced skill, not a personality trait.** Hickey points to Polya's *How to Solve It* (1945), while noting software lacks the mathematical proof that lets you know when you are truly done. Would you rather get good at methodology X, or good at the general skill of solving problems? The latter has more leverage.
* **State the problem out loud and write it down.** The seed of solving a problem is stating it: "This problem is X, therefore Y." Vast amounts of software get built where no one ever wrote down what problem it was supposed to solve.
* **Understand the problem deeply before choosing a solution.** Enumerate the facts, requirements, context, and constraints. Crucially, write down what you know you don't know: if your design page has no question marks, you are missing a step.
* **Study other solutions, then crucify them.** Looking at how others solved similar problems is the number one way to start one increment ahead of the best known work. Be critical: dismantling the last person's idea reveals the next best idea and the things they never wrote down.
* **Real tradeoffs require at least two solutions.** What people call a "tradeoff" is usually just the part of their software that sucks. A genuine tradeoff means comparing at least two solutions and weighing what is good and bad about each.

### The waking mind vs. the background mind
* **The waking mind is tactical and analytical.** It is excellent at immediate decisions and at criticizing ideas, but it tends to climb the nearest hill, finding the local maximum without noticing the higher hill elsewhere. It cannot solve a hard problem in ten minutes of staring at a screen.
* **The background mind is strategic and synthesizing.** It makes connections, draws analogies, and handles abstraction. Most non-trivial problems are solved here, and what feels like on-the-fly brilliance is usually the background mind regurgitating work it already did.
* **You must deliberately feed the background mind.** The point of all the listing, reading, and analysis done while awake is to assign tasks to the background mind. You won't solve a problem in your sleep if you didn't think about it hard enough while awake for it to become an agenda item.

### Focus, the hammock, and the process
* **Extreme focus demands getting away from the computer.** Design work needs intense focus, and computers are terrible sources of distraction. The hammock's hidden virtue: you can close your eyes and no one bothers you. Focusing means dropping balls and seeming far away.
* **Overcome the 7±2 limit by writing everything down.** Write down all the bits — facts, constraints, unknowns, critiques of competitors, a sketch of your solution — so you can shift arbitrary sets of seven items in and out of your head.
* **"Mind's Eye time": recall with no input.** After surveying your notes, step away, sit with no input, and close your eyes without sleeping. This switches you from reception to recall, pulling the parts of a large problem in and out of mind and forcing them onto the background mind's agenda.
* **Sleep on it, and amortize the loading time.** Wait at least one night on important decisions; sleep sorts out what matters and finds hidden relations. Loading up a problem can take an hour to a full day, so work one thing per day; if stuck, switch projects rather than staying stuck.
* **Capture results, prefer small answers, and don't fear being wrong.** A small answer is one of the most telling signs it is probably good. The goal is confidence to attempt things you have never done before. When facts and requirements change, redo the work and change your mind without apology — above all, do not be afraid of being wrong.

title: Fail Better: Radical Ideas from the Practice of Cloud Computing
description: Tom highlights radical ideas from _The Practice of Cloud System Administration_ on embracing failure to build resilient distributed systems, focusing on economic resiliency, performing risky procedures frequently, and creating a blameless culture.
notes:
To build truly resilient distributed systems, organizations must move away from trying to prevent all failures and instead learn to **fail better** by shifting reliability from hardware to software, performing risky procedures frequently to build confidence, and fostering a blame-free culture that prioritizes learning over punishment. This paradigm shift treats inevitable outages as opportunities to improve system safety and operational maturity rather than as reasons for blame.

* **Distributed Computing Definition**: Cloud computing is fundamentally distributed computing. While it offers superior scale, memory, and throughput, it introduces greater risks, makes failures more visible, and makes automation mandatory.
* **Software-Defined Resiliency**: Reliability should be achieved through software architecture rather than expensive hardware redundancy (like high-end RAID or dual power supplies). Software-based resiliency is free to deploy once written and allows for the use of cheaper, more efficient hardware.
* **Frequency Reduces Risk**: Risky procedures, such as disaster recovery failovers, should be performed frequently. Regular practice surfaces hidden bugs, ensures documentation is accurate, and eliminates single points of failure in team expertise.
* **Small Batches Principle**: Delivering software in small, frequent increments is significantly safer than large "Big Bang" releases. Small batches are easier to debug, have a smaller failure surface area, and improve employee motivation through faster feedback and instant gratification.
* **Recency of Use**: The risk of a process is inversely proportional to how recently it was last performed. Systems that are not regularly exercised (like data restores or failover protocols) are high-risk because their current operational state is unknown.
* **Blameless Culture**: Outages should be treated as learning opportunities rather than reasons for punishment. Firing or blaming individuals for failures discourages transparency, stops communication, and causes staff to hide small problems until they become catastrophic.
* **Postmortem Accountability**: Organizations should use detailed, blameless postmortems to document what happened and identify specific long-term fixes. This shifts the focus from "blame and shame" to accountability and systemic improvement.
* **Proactive Failure Induction**: Intentionally trigger failures (e.g., using "Chaos Monkey") during controlled business hours. This ensures that resiliency mechanisms work as intended while the team is available and focused, rather than waiting for failures to occur at inconvenient times.
* **Feature Flags**: Implement new code with toggleable flags to decouple deployment from activation. This allows for safe testing in production and provides an "instant off" switch to disable features that are buggy or not scaling properly.
* **Continuous Integration and Deployment (CI/CD)**: Automate testing and deployment to gain the confidence needed to release software frequently. This approach is applicable to both modern cloud environments and legacy enterprise systems.
* **Lean and DevOps Origins**: These practices are modern applications of lean manufacturing principles, focusing on efficiency, the reduction of waste, and continuous improvement.

title: DevOps Patterns Distilled: A Fifteen Year Study of High Performing IT Organizations
description: This talk distills 15 years of research into the **"Three Ways" of DevOps**, illustrating how high-performing organizations leverage **flow, feedback, and continuous learning** to achieve extraordinary technical outcomes and business value.
notes:
Adopting **DevOps principles** allows organizations to break the chronic conflict between speed and stability, enabling them to achieve both **high-speed delivery and world-class reliability** simultaneously. By prioritizing continuous flow, rapid feedback loops, and a high-trust culture of experimentation, teams can pay down technical debt and drive superior business performance while creating a more **humane and productive work environment**.

* **The Core Conflict**: DevOps addresses the "downward spiral" in IT where fragile artifacts and technical debt lead to unplanned work, missed promises, and "intertribal warfare" between development and operations.
* **The Three Ways**:
    * **The First Way (Flow)**: Focuses on the left-to-right flow of work from Development to Operations. The goal is to shorten lead times by automating environment creation and deployments, making them available on-demand.
    * **The Second Way (Feedback)**: Focuses on the right-to-left flow of feedback. This involves shortening and amplifying feedback loops to prevent problems from moving downstream and enabling quick detection and recovery.
    * **The Third Way (Continuous Learning)**: Focuses on creating a culture of experimentation and learning. This includes doing "painful things more frequently" to make them easier and injecting faults to build resilience.
* **Business Value of High Performance**: High-performing organizations deploy 30 times more frequently and 8,000 times faster than their peers. They are twice as likely to exceed goals in profitability, market share, and productivity.
* **Lead Time as a Key Metric**: Lead time (the time from code commit to production) is a primary predictor of quality, customer satisfaction, and employee happiness.
* **Decoupling Deployment from Release**: High performers separate the technical act of deploying code from the business event of releasing a feature to customers.
* **The "Andon Cord" in IT**: Encouraging anyone to "pull the cord" and stop the flow of work when a defect is found prevents the accumulation of technical debt and ensures long-term build tempo.
* **Shared Pain and Accountability**: Moving to a "you build it, you run it" model—where developers carry pagers and manage their own code in production—aligns goals and improves code quality.
* **The 20% Rule for Technical Debt**: Organizations should allocate 20% of all development and operations cycles specifically to paying down technical debt to avoid a future where 100% of time is spent on maintenance.
* **High-Trust Culture (Westrum Model)**: Performance is predicted by a generative, high-trust culture where messengers are trained rather than shot, failures lead to inquiry, and responsibilities are shared.
* **The Bottleneck Principle**: In any value stream, there is only one bottleneck. Any improvement made anywhere else is an illusion. The bottleneck typically moves from environment creation to deployment, then to testing, and finally to architecture.
* **Resilience through Fault Injection**: Intentionally causing failures in production (e.g., Chaos Monkey) ensures that systems and teams are prepared for real-world outages, transforming "fear into boredom."

title: What makes a good developer
description: Inspiring lightning talk about the importance of humanities, empathy for users and information management as the basis of our profession (beyond pure technology and knowledge about logic or mathematics).
notes:
Christin Gorman, a programmer of over 20 years, challenges the common belief that being a good developer is fundamentally about being a math-and-science whiz. Drawing on her own daily experience in Java codebases, she argues that the skills that actually matter—organization, clear communication, patience, mastery of the basics, and genuine care for users—are almost entirely missed by how the industry perceives, recruits, and trains developers.

* **Programming is wrongly lumped in with math and STEM.** At school her kids only encounter programming as part of math class, reinforcing the idea that you must be a "sciencey, math, geeky tech person" to be any good at it. She fits that stereotype herself, but argues it is misleading and actively harmful.
* **We are failing our users.** The whole point of software is to solve a human need, yet we constantly produce software that frustrates people, especially software people are forced to use at work. Being perpetually "roped in" to help confused friends and family is a symptom of how badly usability is handled.
* **We don't even understand our own code.** Entering any new codebase feels like "what is all this crap"—confusing structure, bridges to nowhere, contraptions nobody understands. She invokes the WTFs-per-minute metric: a couple is fine, but the level of confusion in real codebases is not normal for a profession with four-to-five-year degrees.
* **The industry hides behind convenient excuses.** When code is bad we blame "morons" and claim we need smarter developers, or we blame the old stack (the JSP monolith) and claim we need the new framework. This drives endless gigantic rewrites that reinvent software users already hate; after 20 years she has seen these rewrites repeatedly fail to deliver.
* **What we actually do is delegate work, like management.** We type instructions for the computer the way we'd delegate to people—deciding what roles and responsibilities create value. Real codebases have hundreds of thousands of lines, so the core skill is deciding how to group things: what classes and functions are needed, what data they require, how they communicate.
* **The job is closer to management plus documentation than to a STEM field.** Organizing a large codebase is like organizing a company, except the staff (the computer) does exactly what you literally say. You must communicate intent in written form so the computer, your teammates, and future-you can understand it.
* **The computer is an idiot that does exactly what you said, not what you meant.** The image of an employee who writes "Merry Christmas" on both cakes when told to captures the gap between literal instructions and intent.
* **Patience and perseverance are the single most important skill.** Programming delivers constant negative feedback, and the defining trait of a good developer is the refusal to give up through repeated failure.
* **Bad perceptions push good people out.** Framing the field around math makes people who think "I'm not good at math" self-select out. In 20 years she's used real math maybe twice, proving the math requirement is a myth.
* **Recruit for the skills that actually matter.** Instead of "are you good at math," ask: are you good at organizing and categorizing, at explaining things, do you care about clear and concise communication?
* **Spend less time on the next framework and more on the basics.** Learn how to reason about code, make it easy to test, what data structures are useful, and how to organize a codebase so information flows well—master the basics and you can write good systems in any language.
* **Care about human impact.** The field needs more human interest—caring about the effect systems have on users and society, taking accessibility seriously, knowing your users and listening to them—because serving people is the entire point.

title: Artificial Intelligence seen from the software development lifecycle perspective
description: Great introduction to the differences between traditional software development and the development cycle with AI models. Nerea introduces concepts such as Continuous training, model deployment, MLOps, and collaboration between data scientists and software engineers. Highly recommended for software engineers looking to delve into these topics and collaborate more closely on AI-based feature development.
notes:
Successfully transitioning AI from a laboratory environment to production requires the **integration of DevOps culture into the data science lifecycle**, a practice known as **MLOps** [1-3]. This shift demands **cross-functional collaboration** between data experts and software engineers to automate the unique, iterative processes of continuous training and model monitoring [4-6].

*   **AI development follows a scientific trial-and-error approach** originating from academia, which differs significantly from traditional software development cycles.
*   **Machine learning code is only a small fraction** of a complete production system; the majority of the technical debt and effort lies in configuration, data collection, serving, and monitoring.
*   **MLOps (Machine Learning Operations)** adapts DevOps culture to AI, dividing the lifecycle into three main phases: **design and data exploration, model development and training, and automated operations**.
*   **Continuous Training (CT)** is a unique requirement for AI that allows models to automatically retrain on new input data to prevent performance degradation over time.
*   **Continuous Integration in AI** must include the **validation of data and schemas** alongside traditional code testing to ensure high-quality inputs for the pipeline.
*   **Streamlit and Hugging Face Spaces** are recommended tools for data scientists to quickly create **front-end demos and simple prediction services** without extensive web development knowledge [8-10].
*   **MLflow** serves as a central repository for **model registry and experiment tracking**, allowing teams to log parameters, metrics, and versions of their models for better traceability.
*   **Scaling AI requires full automation** through tools like **Kubeflow (open source) or Vertex AI (cloud-based)**, which manage complex pipelines including metadata stores and automated triggers for retraining.
*   **Model Cards** should be used as "readme" files for AI models to explain their training, deployment, and performance in plain language for the rest of the software team.
*   **Interdisciplinary collaboration is essential**; data scientists, data engineers, and DevOps professionals should be integrated into the same teams rather than working in silos [16-18].
*   **Monitoring is critical** for production models to track latency and detect when **bad actors or faulty data** are polluting the neural network [18-20].

title: Ep. #29, Testing in Production with Glen Mailer of CircleCI
description: An interesting discussion about testing in production and rethinking socio-technical systems from the ground up.

title: The Value of Flow 14 09 17
description: Great explanation for flow efficiency for software delivery
notes:
The most important lesson conveyed is that **real efficiency comes from optimizing the flow of work** (flow efficiency) through a system, rather than focusing on keeping individual resources busy (resource efficiency). Maximizing resource utilization often leads to significant waiting times, large queues, and ultimately **destroys throughput and creates waste**.

- **Real efficiency comes from optimizing the flow of work** through a system (flow efficiency), not by maximizing individual resource utilization (resource efficiency).
- **High resource utilization destroys throughput** by creating significant waiting times, large queues, and context switching, ultimately leading to drastically increased cycle times.
- The process for achieving flow efficiency involves a three-stage cycle: **Visualize** (map the process, identify value-adding vs. waiting time), **Stabilize** (reduce variability, limit work in progress), and **Optimize** (make small changes, measure, and iterate).
- Most business processes have a **staggering amount of waiting time, often exceeding 90%**, due to work sitting in queues or being subject to schedules.
- **Underutilized time in a flow-efficient system is valuable**, serving as "Kaizen time" for learning, helping others, and improving the system.
- Identify and manage **bottlenecks** by using the narrowest point in the process to set the cadence for the entire system (Theory of Constraints).
- Implement a **pull system** where work is pulled by the next stage as capacity becomes available, rather than being pushed or dumped.
- Focusing on flow leads to **predictable delivery, reduced waste**, and the ability to deliver value faster and more consistently.

title: Full Cycle Developers @Netflix
description: Greg Burrell presents Netflix’s journey from siloed teams to their Full Cycle Developer model for building and operating their services at Netflix. He discusses the various approaches they’ve tried, the motivations that pushed them to keep evolving, and the lessons learned along the way.
notes:
Greg Burrell, an engineer who has been at Netflix for over 13 years (hired in 2005 into the experimental streaming group, evolving from tester to DevOps to SRE to the developer productivity group), explains how Netflix's edge engineering organization arrived at the "full cycle developer" model. His core thesis is that organizing software work into specialized silos (dev, test, NOC/operations) creates local efficiency but heavy cross-cycle friction, and that the answer is engineers who own the entire software life cycle. He frames this as a return to the startup-mode sense of end-to-end ownership and empowerment, but made sustainable at scale through tooling, staffing, training, and prioritization. He grounds the argument in Netflix's concrete history (2005 startup mode, 2007 specialized teams, 2011 hybrid model, 2014 first-principles reset, 2018 full cycle) and is candid that the model is not for everyone and is explicitly not a cost-saving measure.

* **Scale of edge engineering in 2018**: Netflix grew from under 1 million streaming subscribers 10 years prior to over 137 million, from US-only to over 190 countries, and accounts for 15% of downstream global Internet traffic per Sandvine. Edge engineering builds and operates more than 50 applications with only about 80 developers, deploys daily, and has "zero test teams and zero dedicated operations teams."
* **The evolution through models**: Started as a startup-within-Netflix (2005) where "everyone wore multiple hats"; adopted specialized teams in 2007 (devs throw changes "over the wall to test," then change-request tickets to a 24/7 Network Operations Center guarding the data centers); moved to the "hybrid model" in 2011 after migrating to the cloud (devs deploy and are on call, plus a DevOps team and a company-wide operations team called "core").
* **Pain point — lack of context**: Developers and testers knew the applications but not production; DevOps and the NOC knew production but not the applications. This was patched with high-overhead communication: change logs ("dumping a bunch of commit messages into a text file"), handoff meetings, and tangled email threads, "like trying to unravel a ball of yarn or that box of Christmas lights in your garage."
* **Pain point — lengthy troubleshooting**: Ops could describe symptoms but not reach root causes; devs could theorize root causes but lacked production access to validate. "We did troubleshooting via conference call," producing emails of "100,000 lines of log messages" and a high mean time to resolution (MTTR).
* **Pain point — lossy feedback cycle**: Operators applied quick fixes and workarounds instead of routing problems back to developers. The memory-leak example: DevOps wrote a script to reboot servers periodically rather than fixing the leak, a bug filed but de-prioritized once "nothing was on fire," so "it was months before we got that memory leak fixed" and some developers never even knew it existed. "The only people feeling the pain were the operations teams, and of course our customers."
* **Silos illustrated**: The Xbox-team anecdote — a finished server build sat unreleased while people walked "from cube to cube" to locate it, blocked because monolithic servers forced all changes to be tested together; one tester's reaction: "Dude, we just did a release like 2 weeks ago. What are we going to do, one like every month or something?"
* **The 2014 first-principles reset**: Rather than throwing more people at the problem (which "just lead to bigger silos or more links in the email chain"), the team asked how to get back to "systems thinking," "end-to-end ownership," optimized feedback loops "so our developers feel that pain and then remove it," and the empowerment of startup mode "in a way that's sustainable at our scale and complexity."
* **The full cycle developer model**: Developers are responsible for all areas of the software life cycle and "evaluate all problems from a developer perspective" — e.g., a developer who has done deployments builds better deployment automation, or creates a tool to handle support requests.
* **Five things needed to make it work**: (1) a mindset shift away from "everything else not my problem"; (2) tooling plus centralized specialist teams (storage, security, AWS) that act as "force multipliers"; (3) adequate staffing — emphatically "this is not a cost savings measure," since dumping multiple teams' work on developers is "a recipe for developer burnout"; (4) ongoing training (shadowing, boot camps) to build "production muscles"; (5) commitment and prioritization, treating "all areas of the software life cycle" as "first-class deliverables" rather than accumulating operational backlog and technical debt.
* **The "paved road" tooling**: Newt (Netflix workflow toolkit) generates a skeleton app with latest best practices, tests, Jenkins jobs, Spinnaker pipelines, dashboards and alerts — turning a week-long setup into minutes. Spinnaker (built with Google and open source) handles CI, canaries, and production deploys; Atlas is the open-source time-series database for health; alert templates/packs simplify monitoring. Teams may go "off the paved road," but the road is "proven, well-integrated, and growing."
* **Trade-offs acknowledged**: Not for every team (UI/front-end and CDN teams keep dedicated operations specialists); some developers just want to specialize or are tired of "carrying the pager"; increased cognitive load, interruptions, and more priorities to balance, mitigated by on-call rotations. When edge switched models "we didn't let anybody go" — testers and DevOps engineers moved to other teams. The upside is empowerment and end-to-end satisfaction.
* **Governance and Q&A details**: On-call is a rotation, not every weekend ("fixed by a developer in their pajamas on the couch"); deploys use canaries and region-limited rollouts to "limit the blast radius" — "very little yolo"; quiet periods around Christmas, Thanksgiving/Black Friday, the Oscars, and the Super Bowl. The mantra is "freedom and responsibility" — "that doesn't mean freedom from responsibility." Managers provide "context, not control," surfacing partner/upstream/downstream needs while the team self-organizes priorities. Teams are typically 4 to a dozen people to avoid single-person "knowledge islands" and low bus factor; tooling should be built bottom-up from what "developers need," and future aims include easier opinionated tooling, better metrics, and removing human judgment from deploys via Spinnaker/Kayenta canary analysis.

title: #NoEstimates
description: Allen Holub advocates for the **#NoEstimates** approach, arguing that traditional software estimation is a **wasteful ritual** that causes organizational dysfunction and demonstrating how **counting stories** provides more accurate business projections with significantly less effort.
notes:
Traditional software estimation is a wasteful and dysfunctional form of guesswork that should be abandoned in favor of simply **counting completed stories** to make accurate business projections. By prioritizing high-value work through tools like story maps, organizations can eliminate the stress of arbitrary deadlines and focus on **continuously delivering value** to the customer.

* **Estimates are guesswork:** Software estimation is inherently based on guesses because it involves implementing things that haven't been done before, making past performance an unreliable indicator for new tasks.
* **Estimation causes dysfunction:** It leads to irrational schedules, unsustainable work paces (burnout), and project failure. Managers often treat "wild-ass guesses" as binding contracts.
* **Deadlines destroy agility:** As soon as estimates become deadlines, the goal shifts from creating value to hitting time targets, which often results in building the wrong product.
* **The "Software Crisis" is an estimation crisis:** High failure rates in industry reports (like the Chaos Report) are often evidence of our inability to estimate accurately, not an inability to build software.
* **Story points are often misused:** Originally designed to obscure time and focus on difficulty, story points are frequently and incorrectly converted back into hours or days by management.
* **Velocity measures the environment, not the team:** Velocity is a ratio of perfect working conditions to reality. Low velocity is usually caused by organizational obstacles and bureaucracy rather than team "slacking."
* **Long backlogs are wasteful:** Anything beyond the top few dozen stories is "wild fantasy" that will change or never be built. Estimating or re-estimating items at the bottom of a large backlog is a complete waste of time.
* **Lean management shifts the power:** Control over time should belong to the people doing the work. The manager's role should flip from controlling schedules to a support role focused on removing obstacles for the team.
* **Estimation is pure waste:** According to lean principles, if an activity doesn't put value into the customer's hands, it is waste. Estimation produces no software and provides no value to the user.
* **Projections over estimates:** Businesses need projections to decide whether to kill a project early or add resources. These projections should be based on actual data, not guesses.
* **Counting is as accurate as estimating:** Data shows that simply counting the number of stories completed is just as predictive for project completion dates as complex Fibonacci-based story pointing, while taking significantly less time.
* **Horizontal planning via Story Maps:** Use story maps to plan by prioritizing thin slices of functionality across all user activities. This ensures a functional, valuable product is available as quickly as possible without relying on time-based estimates.

title: Ep. #4, Everything is an Experiment with Charity Majors and Liz Fong-Jones of Honeycomb
description: In episode 4 of Unintended Consequences, Yoz and Kim speak with Charity Majors and Liz Fong-Jones of Honeycomb about scaling teams and company values. They share how their team makes space for curiosity and learning as a group, and why this has been so important for the Honeycomb platform.
notes:
Observability means being able to ask any new question of your system and follow the trail of breadcrumbs to novel failures — not the vendor "three pillars" of metrics/logs/traces. The same socio-technical thinking applies to scaling a company: small lean teams, explicit values used as a decision tool, and a blameless culture that democratizes debugging.

- Real observability lets you interrogate the system for failures you've never seen before; staring at predefined dashboards and pattern-matching with intuition isn't debugging. Most outages go permanently unexplained because tools make novel failures too hard to understand.
- Goodhart's law ("when a measure becomes a target it ceases to be a good measure"): internal productivity/utilization metrics get gamed (Google running make-work jobs to keep machines), whereas SLOs are more resilient because they measure customer happiness.
- Socio-technical systems: you can't optimize people and tools separately — tools shape behavior, and you need both technical and human literacy to run production well.
- Power of small teams: Honeycomb at ~15-20 engineers covered enormous surface area and ran circles around a 200-engineer competitor; constraints and focus beat empire-building, and observability stops teams wasting half their cycles thrashing.
- Company values (hire adults, fast-and-close-to-right beats perfect, everything is an experiment, feedback is a gift, with style) only work with shared understanding; Honeycomb workshops and stress-tests them ("how could this value be misused?").
- Values are a decision tool — they free people to act as you would when you're not in the room — and they matter most under stress (e.g. where you spend money: healthcare, not kombucha fountains).
- "Everything is an experiment" (EIAE): every decision can be unmade, so just try something and move on; pair this with blameless retrospectives that distill lessons (and stories) across the org.
- The product goal is to democratize debugging — make the tool an outsourced brain so anyone can follow how the expert investigated, instead of expertise being locked in one person's scar tissue; the next investment to make that real is design.

title: Output vs. Outcome & Impact
description: Product thinking moves our focus from fast output to outcome and impact. This video is a great summary of this thinking.
notes:
Jeff Patton's simple mental model for product development: don't measure yourself by how fast you ship output — measure the outcome for users and the impact on your business. Your job isn't to build stuff faster, it's to change the world by paying attention to the people who use (or should use) your product.

- Most process models start with ideas → features/specs/requirements → something shippable. That whole left side — what we obsess over as cost, time and scope — is OUTPUT.
- But it really starts earlier, by paying attention to the world as it is: today's users who are unhappy, angry, confused or frustrated (or, for a startup, people using other products). That attention is what generates the ideas.
- OUTCOME is what happens when the work ships: measured not in cost/time/scope but in people's behavior — do they see, try, use and keep using it, and are they satisfied? (People differ, so some are less pleased than others.) Nobody ever loved a product because it shipped on time.
- IMPACT is the longer-term benefit to the organization — measured the traditional way (return on investment, market share, brand awareness). It only comes because users and customers got their outcome first; as more people adopt, the company's metrics (revenue, cost) move and people inside the building get happy.
- Strategy, at its heart, is deciding which customers and users to focus on — guided by the company's vision and the metrics it watches. Output is necessary, but outcome and impact are what actually matter.

title: How to Measure Success for Development Teams
description: Dave Farley explains how to use **DORA metrics**—specifically **stability and throughput**—to move from guesswork to **evidence-based experimentation** for measuring and improving software development team performance.
notes:
To improve software development effectiveness, teams should move from guesswork to **evidence-based experimentation** by tracking trends in **stability** (defect rate and recovery time) and **throughput** (lead time and deployment frequency). These DORA metrics provide a scientific way to measure the actual impact of any technical, cultural, or organizational change on a team's performance.

* **Evidence-Based Decision-Making:** Move away from guesswork and toward an experimental, evidence-based approach to software development.
* **Three Primary Goals:** Focus on delivering useful impact to users, maintaining high software quality (stability), and achieving efficient delivery (throughput).
* **Stability Metrics (Quality):**
    * **Defect Rate:** Tracking the frequency of defects introduced at different stages, such as after commits, acceptance tests, or production release.
    * **Time to Recover:** Measuring how long it takes to restore a stage or production environment after a defect is detected.
* **Throughput Metrics (Efficiency):**
    * **Lead Time:** The time duration between committing a change and having it ready for release.
    * **Deployment Frequency:** How often code changes are actually released into production.
* **Performance Correlations:** High performance correlates with specific technical practices, including committing to mainline multiple times per day, high levels of automated testing, and frequent production releases.
* **Measuring Change Effectiveness:** Use these metrics to objectively evaluate the impact of any change, whether it involves new technology, tools, team structures, or cultural shifts like stand-up formats.
* **Automated Tracking:** Deployment pipelines should be used as the primary platform to automatically capture and monitor these success indicators.
* **Trend Analysis:** Prioritize tracking trends within the organization to determine improvement over time rather than focusing on specific static percentage benchmarks.

title: Reliable Messaging Without Distributed Transactions
description: Particular CEO, Udi Dahan, describes how you can still do reliable messaging without using distributed transactions
notes:
Achieving system reliability without distributed transactions requires recording all outgoing message intents within the same database transaction as the business logic to ensure data and communication remain consistent. This approach mandates the use of stable message IDs to allow for reliable deduplication by subscribers and necessitates a testing process that accounts for potential failures at every single line of code.

* **Distributed transactions (DTC)** are often unsupported in modern environments such as **Azure Service Bus**, **Amazon SQS**, or **RabbitMQ**, necessitating alternative reliability strategies.
* A common but **insufficient solution** is simply checking a message ID table before processing; this fails to handle crashes that occur during or after message dispatch.
* Performing a **Bus.Publish** inside a business logic transaction is risky because messages can escape even if the database transaction later rolls back, leading to **inconsistent data**.
* Conversely, calling **Bus.Publish** after a transaction commits is also risky, as a server crash before the dispatch results in a **missing message** that should have been sent.
* The reliable alternative is to **record the intent to publish** (including the payload and destination) in the **same database transaction** as the business logic.
* A handler must then read this "outbox" table and **replay the activities** against the queuing system to ensure everything that needs to go out actually does.
* It is critical to **explicitly set and control Message IDs** when replaying dispatches so that subscribers can successfully **deduplicate** messages on their end.
* This architecture moves the system from "exactly-once" delivery (provided by DTC) to **"at-least-once" delivery**, requiring idempotency in downstream handlers.
* True reliability is only achieved through **rigorous testing** that simulates process crashes at every single line of code to verify correct state recovery.
* Avoid building custom queuing or file systems; instead, rely on proven infrastructure designed to handle these non-deterministic failure scenarios.

title: Why Your Software Team CAN’T Scale
description: In this episode, Dave Farley, author of the best selling books Continuous Delivery, CD Pipelines and Modern Software Engineering, looks into how to organise software at scale, and how the Team Topologies model works and helps us to design organisations that can produce better software faster.
notes:
Scaling software development effectively requires structuring small, autonomous, **stream-aligned teams** that minimize cognitive load and cross-team dependencies to mirror the desired decoupled architecture of the system. This organizational design prioritizes team autonomy and the elimination of handoffs, ensuring that communication structures support rather than hinder productivity and code quality.

* **Small teams (5–9 people)** are significantly more productive and produce higher quality code than larger teams due to reduced **cognitive load**.
* The **team is the primary unit of work**, not the individual; it should own responsibility and work as a unit toward shared goals.
* **Conway’s Law** dictates that organizational communication structures determine software architecture; teams should be structured to prefer **loose coupling** and **autonomy**.
* **Stream-aligned teams** are the primary team type, aligned with a continuous flow of work for a specific business domain or organizational capability.
* Autonomy is critical to success; stream-aligned teams should have **minimal to zero handoffs** and handle their own architecture, testing, and releases.
* **Enabling teams** help stream-aligned teams acquire missing capabilities or specialized knowledge by lending experts and teaching the team.
* **Complicated subsystem teams** reduce cognitive load for others by taking responsibility for technically specialized, complex parts of the system.
* **Platform teams** exist to provide internal products that enable stream-aligned teams to work with substantial autonomy.
* High-functioning organizations maintain a **high ratio of stream-aligned teams** (e.g., 6:1 or 10:1) to ensure most teams focus on delivering direct business value.
* Organizations should avoid the anti-pattern of **lack of team autonomy**, as the ability to progress without external coordination is one of the strongest predictors of success.

title: Laloux Culture Model and Agile
description: An overview of Frederic Laloux's Reinventing Organizations and how it relates to Lean and Agile Adoption.
notes:
The success of Agile and Lean methodologies depends on evolving an organization's culture beyond traditional hierarchy and profit motives toward a "Green" or "Teal" paradigm centered on shared values, empowerment, and a living-system mindset. While newer organizational models better navigate modern complexity, lasting transformation requires aligning leadership's cultural perspective with these collaborative practices rather than treating them as mere process improvements.

*   **Red Paradigm (Metaphor: Wolfpack)**: Operates through command authority and a division of labor where a powerful leader ensures survival through fear and compliance.
*   **Amber Paradigm (Metaphor: Army)**: Focuses on long-term goals, stability, and strict hierarchy supported by formal roles and strong processes.
*   **Orange Paradigm (Metaphor: Machine)**: Prioritizes competition, financial profit, meritocracy, and objectives-based management; it is the dominant paradigm for modern large corporations.
*   **Green Paradigm (Metaphor: Family)**: Centers on shared values, employee engagement, and customer delight, favoring empowerment and culture over strategy.
*   **Teal Paradigm (Metaphor: Living System)**: Built on self-management, "wholeness" (allowing employees to bring their full selves to work), and an evolutionary purpose.
*   **The Advice Process**: A Teal decision-making method where any member can make a decision after seeking advice from those impacted, rather than relying on top-down authority or consensus.
*   **Agile and Lean Rooting**: These movements are natively rooted in the Green paradigm; they often struggle in Orange organizations because they are treated as productivity tools rather than cultural shifts.
*   **Vertical vs. Horizontal Transformation**: Vertical transformation (moving between colors) typically requires the top leader's drive, whereas horizontal transformation improves efficiency and innovation within the current paradigm's boundaries.
*   **Nested Evolution**: Newer organizational paradigms are better equipped to handle complexity but are cumulative, meaning they include and can utilize the ideas of the older paradigms when necessary.

title: Great teams make great engineers (not the other way around)
description: Insightful quote :)
notes:
Prioritize building supportive environments and robust systems that empower everyone to succeed, because a great team ultimately shapes the quality of its engineers rather than individual talent defining the team’s success. Investing in a culture that optimizes for learning and safe delivery ensures that any engineer can perform at a high level and grow within the group.

* **Great teams make great engineers:** The environment and group dynamics are far more powerful influences on performance than an individual's personal knowledge of data structures or algorithms.
* **Environmental impact on performance:** Any engineer joining a team will typically align with that team's performance level—whether higher or lower—within three to six months.
* **System-driven success:** Focus should be placed on building systems that make it easy for engineers to ship code quickly and safely, rather than relying on "heroic" individual efforts.
* **Failure as a learning opportunity:** Mistakes should be treated as opportunities to optimize for learning. Technical guardrails and "circuit breakers" should be implemented to prevent failures from cascading.
* **Core Methodologies:** High-performing engineering cultures leverage Lean-Agile, Continuous Delivery, Systems Thinking, and DevOps to maintain flow and productivity.
* **Technical Practices:** Emphasis is placed on Small Safe Steps (3s), rapid feedback cycles, and trunk-based development to minimize risk and improve quality.
* **Platform as a Product:** Internal platforms and infrastructure should be treated as products to enhance developer experience (DevEx) and engineering scalability.
* **Cultural Focus:** Success is driven by engineering craftsmanship, diversity, and a culture that prioritizes simplicity and technical leadership.

title: Better value sooner safer happier
description: Very funny and interesting talk about culture.
notes:
Jon Smart distills lessons learned the hard way about leading ways-of-working change in large organizations, opening with a satirical "David Brent" CEO parody of how transformations go wrong (a Gantt chart, an 18-month countdown, a copy-paste "Spotify model" imposed with no input, certifications, and empty empowerment). His core thesis is that agile, lean and DevOps are means, not ends: the real goal is Better Value Sooner Safer Happier, and change must be invited rather than inflicted, achieved big through small, and treated as continuous improvement that is never done.

* **Focus on outcomes, not on a capital-T Transformation.** Telling people exactly how they must work ("complete empowerment and absolutely no choice") is not empowering; pivot toward the outcomes you actually want.
* **Better Value Sooner Safer Happier (BVSSH) as the universal outcome set.** Better = quality; Value = unique OKRs (revenue, market share, diversity); Sooner = lead time, throughput, flow efficiency, time-to-learning; Safer = continuous compliance, "agile not fragile"; Happier = colleagues and customers, a more humane way to work that counters burnout.
* **These measures balance each other.** You cannot cut lead time at the expense of quality, safety or happiness without it becoming visible; mediocre performers in the State of DevOps report dropped lead time but quality fell.
* **Empower within guardrails via minimal viable compliance.** People are intrinsically motivated to pursue BVSSH however they want, as long as they stay within the guardrails. The answer isn't always agile or DevOps — sometimes it's funding, finance, or even "smaller waterfalls."

### Achieve big through small
* **Avoid achieving big through big.** A big-bang transformation follows a large Kübler-Ross curve, and ~two years to climb out of the trough — but the half-life of the CIO is around the bottom of that trough, so leadership changes and reverts before recovery.
* **Use lots of little J-curves.** Many small changes get you above current performance quickly. "Think big, start small, learn fast" — zoomed out over 5-7 years it becomes an S-curve. Humans have a limited velocity to unlearn and relearn, so manage leadership expectations down for the first 12 months.

### Invite change over inflicting change
* **Resistance and "convincing" are signals you're doing change wrong.** Using Rogers' diffusion of innovation: innovators are motivated by scarcity, while the early and late majority are motivated by social proof.
* **Run a two-pronged strategy.** Start small with natural innovators, keep them at the bleeding edge (an "exemplar club"), and generate social proof for the majority from "what have we done here."
* **Focus on champions, ignore the critics.** Investing in champions creates a vacuum that pulls fence-sitters off the fence; eventually laggards become the odd ones out, and being the odd one out (scarcity) is exactly what a laggard least wants.
* **Beware railway fake movement (Eisenbahnscheinbewegung).** Renaming everything sprints, stand-ups, scrum masters and post-its can feel like progress while underneath it's #nochange: new labels on the same old behaviors.

### Leadership and direction
* **The leadership team is team number one.** Starting at the grassroots hits a "grass ceiling": bubbles of agility in a sea of waterfall. Senior leadership must keep the elastic band stretched positively, with psychological safety, generative culture, and intent-based leadership (David Marquet's "Turn the Ship Around").
* **Build the right thing (lean portfolio management).** Agility disconnected from strategy is "headless chicken syndrome" (75% of features rarely or never used). Pivot from projects to product, to long-lived teams on long-lived value streams, and from predictive solutions to nested outcome hypotheses and bets.
* **Build the thing right (speed and control).** A proven at-scale process takes quarterly business outcomes as the unit, with an "engage" checkpoint (strategy alignment) and a "validate" checkpoint (mandatory controls like encryption and GDPR) — then up to a hundred deploys a day. Multidisciplinary compliance teams identify with the value stream ("I am mortgages," not "I am information security"); 300+ standards were codified into risk stories tooled into Jira. Minimal viable compliance scales controls to risk.
* **Go slower to go faster — you're never done improving.** Use the Toyota Improvement Kata: set direction (BVSSH), grasp the current condition, set a ~3-month target condition, then run safe-to-fail experiments — if you're not failing, you're not trying hard enough. "Impediments are not in the path, impediments are the path" (Theory of Constraints). Human-systems entropy means processes and control grow back, so organizations need people who own the system of work and clear impediments.

title: The entity Microservice Trap (you may be doing it wrong)
description: GOTO 2016. Very interesting because lot of the advantages of the microservices are only available when using asynchronous messaging.
notes:
Fred George argues that microservices are at the same hype-and-disappointment cusp agile reached fifteen years ago, and that the most common way teams adopt them — carving the system along entity (database) lines — is precisely the trap that will produce the coming wave of microservice failures. His thesis is that the goal of microservices is wicked speed (from requirement to production), achieved by killing the central database and building behavior-oriented services, not by recreating CRUD-over-the-network entity services.

* **The central data structure as bottleneck.** George traces a lineage from Fred Brooks ("get the data structures right and the code writes itself") to the 1990s relational database sitting in the middle of the world. The corollary is that you can't change that central structure without making all surrounding code suspect, and a DBA/architect gatekeeper makes changes "like asking for the firstborn child."
* **The entity microservice trap.** The natural first move is to break the database along entity lines because teams are good at database design and it's reinforced by Conway's Law and REST. But this just recreates the same central-data coupling problem one level up.
* **REST as database access language.** In its pure form REST is just insert/update/delete/select verbs — guidance pushes teams to make each service behave like a database with CRUD interfaces, producing data-oriented rather than behavior-oriented services.
* **Where does the business logic go?** Once you have entity services, gluing them together forces a choice between an orchestration/moderator service (which becomes a god service with massive methods) or stuffing logic under the data Rails-style. All answers lead to new tar balls.
* **The performance and CAP wall.** Replacing database tables with entity services means joins now happen across the network via REST — pulling every client and every order record over the wire instead of using stored procedures and indexed joins. CAP theorem compounds this. SOA hit exactly this wall.
* **Coupling (connascence) is the real enemy.** Citing Jim Weirich's talk on connascence as a "unified theory of software," George reads it as a hierarchy of coupling with a rule of locality: the closer things are physically, the more you can couple them; if you want true separation you need the weakest possible forms.

### Complex problems need eventual consistency
* **Cynefin framing.** Using Dave Snowden's framework, George distinguishes complicated problems (rule-based — accounting, bank balances — needing strict consistency) from complex problems (no reliable cause-and-effect — financial markets, ad targeting, recommendation engines) which are fuzzy.
* **Kill the consistency requirement.** Because complex problems are fuzzy ("should I loan you money? maybe"), eventual consistency is fine. The organizational obsession with perfectly synchronized entities is an unnecessary restriction that must be eliminated.

### The right approach: behavior-oriented services
* **Behavior, not data.** A real service does something and returns a conclusion drawn from analysis — not "tell me your age." Ask the real question ("are you going to die too soon?"). It's fine for several services to hold somewhat duplicated data.
* **Break the database first.** The successful migration pattern (Chad Fowler at 6Wunderkinder, Gilt Group, Hailo) was to tear the giant database apart into small pieces first, then build behavior-oriented services around them.
* **The persistent event bus.** His favored architecture replaces the operational SQL database with a persistent event bus: all raw data is published (storage is cheap) and can be reanalyzed endlessly, while small per-service databases hold only just-enough cached data. A reporting/data-warehouse store stays because SQL and reports work well together.
* **The offer-engine example.** His "share of wallet" advertising scenario shows each microservice as several containers around a tiny purpose-built store: an offer service caching bids in Redis for ~300ms, and a membership service using a key-value store rebuilt nightly by a cron ETL. Touching one service's database only forces changes to that service's code.
* **Verdict from the workshops.** Almost everyone who attends arrives saying their company "does microservices" and leaves realizing they're doing entity-based services and hitting trouble. His closing advice: if you're building entity-based microservices, stop now and rethink — you'll hit a wall.

title: NUMMI (2015)
description:
The interesting history of the NUMMI plant and the joint venture between GM & Toyota. 
A good story to understand some of the lean principles. There is a previous version of the podcast with other interviews at https://www.thisamericanlife.org/403/nummi-2010

title: Adaptive Planning Beyond User Stories
description: Gojko gives a masterclass on how to focus on impacts, understand the need, and improve product decisions. Good ideas to postpone decisions, focus on behavioral changes, and be cautious with metrics. An essential talk for any product developer.
notes:
Agile success requires shifting from treating user stories as fixed requirements to viewing them as **survivable experiments** aimed at achieving specific **behavior changes** [1-4]. By prioritizing **measurable outcomes over feature outputs**, organizations can transform unexpected challenges into competitive advantages and ensure they are not building "Vasa ships" that sink immediately upon delivery [5-8].

* **The Disconnect in Requirements:** User stories were originally intended as tokens for discussion, but have become the de facto standard for rigid requirements. This creates a gap between business goals (often described in PowerPoints/Roadmaps) and what teams actually deliver.
* **Story Card Hell:** Accumulating hundreds of user stories in a linear backlog (like in JIRA) leads to "story card hell." This linear thinking assumes certainty and removes the flexibility that agile is supposed to provide.
* **The Value of Flexibility:** In most industries, flexibility is a premium service people pay for. In software, teams often have to fight for it, even though flexibility is essential to survive unexpected changes in technology, competition, and human behavior.
* **Linear vs. Adaptive Planning:** Linear plans fail because they cannot account for unpredictable dimensions: local context changes, time-based shifts (like a competitor releasing a free API), and unpredictable human behavior. 
* **The Vasa Ship Lesson:** A project can be delivered on "time and budget" and still be a disaster. Flexible scope is dangerous if it isn't guided by a bigger picture or business outcome, leading to a product that "sinks" upon release.
* **Palinski’s Principles for Adaptive Planning:**
    * **Variation:** Try many different ideas because it is impossible to know upfront what will work.
    * **Survivability:** Conduct experiments on a scale that won't bankrupt the company if they fail.
    * **Selection:** Actively identify what works and discard what doesn't.
* **The GPS Analogy:** Software planning needs "turn-by-turn navigation." This means making the cost of replanning cheap and using "automatic position finding" to see how far the team is from the actual business destination.
* **Focus on Outcomes over Outputs:** Teams often measure success through velocity (output), which is just a measure of time. True success should be measured by outcomes—specific changes in user behavior.
* **The "Five-Minute" Rule for Stories:** Before starting a user story, ask "How differently will the user work after this is done?" If you cannot define a specific behavior change, the story is likely a waste of time.
* **User Stories as Small Bets:** Shift the perspective of user stories from "small chunks of scope" to "small bets" or "survivable experiments." This helps business stakeholders understand that some ideas will fail, and that’s part of the learning process.
* **Hierarchical Backlogs:** Move away from linear lists toward hierarchical structures like Impact Mapping or Story Mapping. These tools visualize assumptions and allow teams to discard remaining stories once a business goal is reached.
* **Four Principles for Great Results:**
    * Ensure everyone understands the measurable business goal.
    * Focus on delivering outcomes and impacts rather than features.
    * Make decisions on what to do next based on immediate feedback from how the software is actually used.
    * Cultivate a culture where people care about the impact of their work, not just the completion of tickets.

title: SE-Radio Episode 355: Randy Shoup Scaling Technology and Organization
description: interesting interview about how to scale technology, team and culture.

title: AWS re:Invent 2019: Innovation at speed (ARC203)
description: This talk explores how organizations can accelerate innovation by transforming culture, adopting integrated product teams, and leveraging technical patterns like serverless and chaos engineering to minimize time to value.
notes:
Organizations achieve rapid innovation by prioritizing **time to value** and transitioning from rigid, centralized cycles to a continuous delivery model powered by decentralized, autonomous teams. To succeed, leadership must align organizational culture and incentives with modern technical practices, such as cloud-native architecture and chaos engineering, to ensure that high speed is balanced with system resilience.

* **Shift from Annual to Continuous Cycles**: Modern IT has moved from annual product releases to continuous innovation driven by mobile, IoT, and online sales channels.
* **Digital Transformation**: Success requires direct customer knowledge, personalized products, and the ability to manage connected products throughout their lifecycle.
* **Innovation Blockers**: Innovation is typically hindered by centralized decision-making, lack of trust, inflexible policies, and misaligned financial incentives.
* **Culture of Purpose**: High-performing cultures focus on giving employees purpose and freedom rather than micromanagement, emphasizing "freedom and responsibility" and good judgment.
* **Integrated Product Teams**: Move from temporary project teams to long-term product ownership where the same team handles business, development, and operations ("you build it, you run it").
* **Skills and Incentives**: Focus on training existing staff rather than just hiring. Implement systemic incentives for employees to gain new qualifications in emerging technologies.
* **Time to Value**: The fundamental metric for innovation is the time it takes for an idea to reach a customer. High-performing organizations measure this in minutes rather than months.
* **Continuous Delivery**: Deploying small, frequent changes reduces risk, speeds up repairs, and minimizes work in progress. One day’s worth of work or a single line of code is an ideal deployment size.
* **Decoupling Code and Features**: Use feature flags and A/B testing to separate the technical act of deploying code from the business act of launching a new feature.
* **Evolutionary Architecture**: The best modern architectures are designed to evolve rapidly and absorb new services rather than being "frozen" or perfectly tidy.
* **Cloud Native Principles**: Prioritize pay-as-you-go models, self-service APIs, global distribution, and high resource utilization (targeting 40-50% vs. the traditional 10-15%).
* **Serverless First**: Use serverless for rapid prototyping and gluing services together. Use containers for custom code, long-running jobs, or highly predictable high-traffic workloads.
* **Observability**: Smaller units like microservices and functions are inherently more observable. A system is observable if its internal state can be determined by its inputs and outputs.
* **Hazard Analysis**: Instead of just predicting component failures, analyze the "hazards" in the information flow between controllers (automated or human) and processes.
* **Chaos Engineering**: Move from passive disaster recovery to active resilience by intentionally introducing failures (e.g., Chaos Monkey) to ensure systems can self-heal.
* **Operational Learning Cycles**: Establish weekly mechanisms (like the AWS "roulette wheel" meeting) to review metrics and share operational failures across all teams to ensure collective learning.
* **Board and Finance Alignment**: Boards must move away from short-term risk minimization and capex-heavy models to support the opex and high-ownership structures required for cloud innovation.

title: o11ycast - Ep. #80, Augmented Coding with Kent Beck
description: Explore augmented coding with Kent Beck, discussing how AI agents transform software development, the essential role of observability and testing for control and understanding, and the future impact on engineering careers.
notes:
The most important lesson conveyed is that **augmented coding, powered by AI agents, represents a fundamental shift in software development that requires developers to adopt new practices focused on influence, feedback loops, and process refinement rather than traditional control**.
Someone should remember that working with these powerful, non-deterministic systems means **relying heavily on constraints, testing, and observability** to guide, understand, and ensure the desired outcomes of the code generated by agents, acknowledging that deep line-by-line understanding of all code may become less feasible. It's a time for experimentation and continuously refining the "making" process alongside building the product itself.


- **Augmented Coding is a Fundamental Shift:** The core concept is that AI agents are transforming software development, moving beyond traditional coding to "augmented coding" where developers create the tools and workflows alongside the product itself. This process involves working with **non-deterministic systems sensitive to initial conditions**, which means results can vary even with similar prompts. It allows developers to **explore more ideas more easily** by automating minutiae like programming language syntax and dependencies.
- **Control Shifts to Influence and Feedback Loops:** With AI agents, direct line-by-line control of code becomes less feasible and perhaps less important. Instead, developers must rely on **constraints, testing, and observability** to guide and understand the agents' behavior. **Testing acts as a crucial "super ego"** providing validation and preventing undesirable actions like commenting out failed tests. **Observability** provides essential feedback on emergent properties like performance or error rates in production, necessary because we let go of deep understanding of all the generated code.
- **The Process ("Making Making") is as Important as the Product:** Working with agents requires **continuously refining the process** itself, such as iterating on prompts, defining rules, and establishing workflows like automatically running tests and committing code. **Experimentation is key**, and it's important to have the discipline to iterate in smaller steps, back up, and restart when necessary, rather than getting stuck in a "go-to loop" or falling prey to the sunk cost fallacy.
- **Significant Impact on the Engineering Career:** Augmented coding raises questions about the future of engineering roles, particularly for **junior developers**, potentially limiting opportunities for "legitimate peripheral participation" where foundational experience is gained through assisting senior engineers. However, it may also lead to the rise of a new generation of **"native augmented coders"** who are uniquely skilled at leveraging these tools in ways current developers might not fully grasp.

title: Life is easy. Why do we make it so hard?
description: TEDxDoiSuthep Inspirational talk
notes:
In this TEDx talk, Jon Jandai recounts his journey from a poor village in Northeastern Thailand to Bangkok and back, arguing that modern life is hard only because we choose to make it so. His core thesis is that the four basic needs—food, house, clothes, and medicine—should be cheap and easy for everyone, and that true civilization means making them accessible rather than difficult.

* **The seduction of "success":** As a kid in his village, everything felt fun and easy, but when TV arrived people told him he was poor and needed to chase success in Bangkok. He internalized feeling poor and left to pursue it.
* **Bangkok was the opposite of easy:** He worked at least eight hours a day yet could only afford a single bowl of noodles or fried rice per meal, and slept in a small, hot, overcrowded room. This contradiction—producing a lot but never having enough—made him start questioning everything.
* **University as "destructive knowledge":** He found university boring and noticed most faculties taught what he called destructive knowledge—architects and engineers learn to cover mountains and the Chao Praya Basin with concrete; agriculture teaches how to poison the land and water.
* **Remembering the old rhythm of work:** He recalled that nobody in his childhood village worked eight hours a day; people worked two months a year—one month planting rice, one month harvesting—leaving ten months of free time, which is why Thailand has so many festivals.
* **Free time as self-knowledge:** With abundant time, people had time to understand themselves and see what they truly wanted—happiness, love, enjoyment—then expressed that beauty by carving knife handles or weaving baskets, crafts now lost to plastic.
* **Quitting and returning home:** He dropped out of university and returned to live as he remembered. Working just two months a year he harvested four tons of rice, while his family of six eats less than half a ton, leaving a surplus to sell.
* **Fifteen minutes of gardening feeds six:** On less than half an acre he spends 15 minutes a day tending over 30 varieties of vegetables—more than six people can eat—with a surplus for market income.
* **Building a house in two hours a day:** Believing a "stupid" person who never got good grades could never own a house, he tried earthen building instead—two hours each morning, 5 to 7 a.m., gave him a house in three months. His cleverest classmate also took three months but went into 30 years of debt; Jandai jokes he gained "29 years and 10 months of free time."
