* **Anyone can build a house:** He now builds at least one house a year, has no money but many houses, and his only problem is choosing which to sleep in. Thirteen-year-old kids make bricks and build a library in a month.
* **Clothing and following fashion:** He once spent a month's savings on jeans to look like a movie star, but the mirror showed the same person. For 20 years he has bought no clothes, living off leftovers visitors leave behind.
* **Like versus need:** When buying something, ask whether you buy it because you like it or because you need it. If it's because you like it, that's the wrong reason—a realization that made him feel more free.
* **Sickness as a reminder:** He came to see sickness not as bad but as a signal that he did something wrong, a prompt to stop and return to himself, healing using water, earth, and basic knowledge.
* **Pun Pun and saving seed:** He co-founded Pun Pun in Chiang Mai. Its main aim is saving seed, because seed is food and food is life—"no seed, no freedom, no seed, no happiness."
* **A learning center to make life easy:** Pun Pun's second purpose is being a learning center to relearn how to make life easy and to be together, countering the conditioning to disconnect and rely only on money.
* **Civilized versus uncivilized:** His central conclusion is that food, house, clothes, and medicine being cheap and easy is civilization; making them hard is uncivilized. He calls the present—full of universities and clever people yet ever-harder living—the most uncivilized era.
* **Being normal in an abnormal world:** Birds build a nest in a day or two and rats dig a hole in a night, while clever humans spend 30 years for a house. He chooses to be "normal," because the choice between an easy life and a hard one ultimately depends on you—and the only thing he can manage is his own mind.

title: The Journey to Continuous Delivery
description: Modern agile and how to introduce it. (good ideas about, flow, value, and teams).
notes:
Dan North, who has worked in IT for around 25 years (six-plus as a programmer, eight years at ThoughtWorks from 2002–2009 pioneering Continuous Delivery patterns and accidentally inventing Behavior-Driven Development, and the last five years as an independent consultant in large organizations), argues that the goal of Continuous Delivery is not frequent releases, build automation, or standardized tools, but to increase business agility. He defines business agility as "the speed and effectiveness with which we can adapt to new information," where lead time (wall-clock time from a need being expressed to it being delivered) is "the only game in town." The talk presents a repeatable improvement cycle — visualize, stabilize, optimize — applied across the whole organization, not just technology. North stresses that real transformation requires bringing commercial, management, operations, and governance functions along, since technology is the easiest and most mature part.

* **The goal is business agility, not frequent releases**: North debunks each plausible goal in turn with field stories. A media company released monthly but the items in each release had wildly variable ages (6 months, 8 months, 18 months old). His advice: "drop back to quarterly releases, but make sure the stuff in each release only started that quarter," so you can guarantee nothing is older than 12 weeks, build SLAs and stakeholder trust, then squeeze the cycle shorter.

* **Automation has no value without knowing where to automate**: At a post-government organization, "Keith" automated the build but it had reduced the path-to-live by only 45 minutes out of a multi-week process. Measuring with Post-it notes on a wall showed him "what to go after"; he then halved the lead time, and kept improving on each visit because he finally had visibility.

* **Standardized processes and tools assume a homogeneous estate**: At the biggest bank (formed by merging many smaller banks over decades), Scrum worked in London (mostly fungible Java/Scala/Python work) but failed in New York, which had a heterogeneous estate of 40–60 different systems doing the same thing. When only one person knows a system, cross-dependencies appear: "I get stuck waiting on her, which sucks for me. And she gets dragged out of what she was doing... which sucks for her."

* **Scrum solves 25-year-old problems**: Scrum was "game changing" when it brought multi-year releases down to multi-month, but "not much has changed in Scrum Land" — "They replaced the word commitments with forecasts... about 5 years ago." It assumes fungible standard teams and that long-lived stable teams are good, which only holds if work is homogeneous over time; otherwise "whatever setup you have now is by definition going to be the wrong setup in six months' time."

* **Continuous Delivery matters for both response speed and recovery speed**: It impacts how fast you can respond to a need and how fast you can recover. North shifts the guiding metric from MTBF (mean time between failure) to MTTR (mean time to recovery), citing John Allspaw (then CTO of Etsy) who noted MTBF breaks down across 10,000 hard drives — you must "design and architect as though something is broken."

* **Netflix's chaos tooling enforces resilience**: Chaos Monkey switches off a live production server somewhere every minute; Chaos Gorilla takes out an Amazon availability zone (run on a scheduled weekend); Chaos Kong takes out an entire Amazon region once a quarter — "every single server on US West is just switched off, and switched off hard." The rule each time: "No one's allowed to notice." North notes finance and healthcare lag far behind a video-streaming company on this duty of care.

* **Handoffs are the single biggest thing slowing us down**: The original cross-functional team principle was to bring representatives together so "the work doesn't need to leave the team," but it got corrupted into a generic team template. Other slowdowns: external dev/test environment provisioning, onerous release governance and sign-off, end-of-year release freezes ("living in fear!"), commoditized/offshored manual testing, and manual deployment and troubleshooting.

* **Cultural resistance, not technology, blocks automation**: At a US company's UK office ~12 years ago, the team had fully automated build and deploy after about 8 months, but US DBAs and ops engineers refused the button. The team eased them in via a script that required typing "yes", then just Enter, then nothing — because "they feel they're losing control" and believe "this automated process is less rigorous than a human being typing things, because people never type the wrong thing, ever."

* **Start where you are and pick a few things — the rule of 63**: Don't "automate all the things." North's rule: entering a new context you see "63 things that need fixing" — "Pick three. Suck up the other 60." Choose what hurts most through evidence, go after one bottleneck, and scale out only as you build confidence and trust.

* **Visualize, stabilize, optimize**: Visualize is "turning the lights on" via value stream mapping (he recommends the "Value Stream Mapping in 5 Minutes" YouTube video and Event Storming; cites Liz Keogh and Don Reinertsen's inactivity principle, "watch the work product, not the worker"). Stabilize brings system variance under control — "it doesn't matter if you're eye-wateringly bad at something, as long as you're consistently eye-wateringly bad." Optimize uses the Deming/Kaizen cycle (plan, do, check, adjust), framing experiments as "bets" and always asking "How are you going to measure that?"

* **Reframe problems as desired outcomes**: Using NLP language of being "at effect" (world happening to you) versus "at cause," turn "takes ages to get an environment" into "self-serve provisioning of environments." At a bank, a two-day change had a two-week lead time with five days of effort spent on release documents; they scripted the document to be generated from Jira and Subversion as a build artifact, "creating more than double the capacity by making some pointless work go away."

* **Dramatic, compounding business impact and the 18F example**: At one bank cut from 600 to 400 technology staff, obsessing about lead time let 400 people produce double the business impact of the former 600 within a year, then double again the next year — four times the throughput in two years. Process improvement is "the last transformation you'll ever do" because it never ends, making maturity models "fundamentally broken." Finally, healthcare.gov (Obamacare) cost a billion dollars and crashed; 18F rebuilt it in weeks at a fraction of the cost. They found 325 documents of regulations (mostly ops), built the cloud.gov platform (FedRAMP) so that running on it makes you de facto compliant with 269 of 325 documents, 41 more are generic, leaving ~15 simple checklist items (source control, linking requirements to code, tests to features). Closing advice: not everyone is Google or Netflix — "borrow their tools, but don't try and be like them"; "resist web-scale envy" ("If your data fits on my phone, it's not big"); "Start tomorrow, pick one thing, measure it."

title: Anthropic CEO Declares Programming Over, We Respond
description: The talk discusses reactions and scenarios related to the Anthropic CEO's prediction that AI will soon write essentially all code, proposing challenges to this claim and exploring different potential futures for software engineering roles and processes
notes:
Here are the most important points discussed in the presentation regarding the Anthropic CEO's prediction about AI writing code:
- The talk is a direct **response to the prediction by Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei** that AI will be writing 90% of code in 3-6 months and **essentially all code in 12 months**.
- The speaker, Ry Myers, states they **don't personally believe this extreme prediction** but emphasizes that it's a **situation worth taking seriously** due to the significant investment and attention such narratives receive.
- While the CEO was speaking to a general audience and potentially using terms like "coding" and "programming" interchangeably, the speaker argues that **"coding," "programming," and "software engineering" are not interchangeable concepts** in practice. Software engineering involves challenges of **scale, managing real-world needs and risks, and is a social process**, with coding being only a part of it. Improving coding speed doesn't automatically speed up the entire engineering process proportionally.
- The speaker proposes **specific, challenging tasks for current AI systems** that they argue are orders of magnitude _easier_ than replacing all code, using them as challenges to the CEO's prediction:
    - Successfully **replacing the software running on a single Mainframe**. This is highly relevant as 50% of the US GDP is tied to companies still dependent on mainframes.
    - **Rewriting the highly stable and well-documented TeX command** (a complex 1982 codebase under 40,000 lines) into a modern language like Rust. TeX is still core to academic papers and textbooks, is exceedingly well-documented (even using "literate programming" to generate both program and book).
- The presentation outlines **six potential scenarios for the future of software engineering** in the context of AI, noting that these are part of the current conversation:
    - **Extreme Completion:** AI acts as advanced autocomplete, streamlining work without fundamentally changing roles.
    - **Dev-ocalypse / Innovator's Paradise:** Traditional programmer roles are essentially eliminated.
    - **Abstraction Leap:** A significant shift to much higher levels of abstraction in building software.
    - **Uncharted Waters:** New, currently unknown programming paradigms emerge.
    - **Review Economy:** Humans primarily spend their time reviewing and debugging AI-generated code, becoming a bottleneck.
    - **Infinite Pile of Garbage:** Premature adoption of low-quality AI systems creates an unsustainable, intractable software infrastructure (an "ecological disaster scenario").
- These scenarios can interact; for instance, attempting the Dev-ocalypse might lead to the Infinite Pile of Garbage if not done carefully.
- The speaker's personal preferred future scenario leans towards a **combination of Extreme Completion and Abstraction Leap**, suggesting developing better building blocks like Domain Specific Languages (DSLs) in concert with advanced completion tools.
- The speaker notes that debates around AI replacing software roles can sometimes feel like a **"thinly disguised labor dispute,"** raising questions about how society treats people whose skills may become less in demand.
- A crucial takeaway is that **software engineers have the expertise and a say in shaping the future**. The future is not an inevitable "meteor" but something that society, including software engineers, will **actively build**.
- The ultimate goal should be to deliberate on **what society wants out of software** (e.g., quality, tractability, value) and work together towards that desired future, rather than passively accepting a predicted one.

title: Lightning Talk: Certified Really Agile Practitioner (CRAP)
description: Ironic (and sad) vision about digital transformation and the misunderstood agility that is unfortunately common these days.
notes:
True transformation requires moving beyond **"cargo cult" agility and superficial frameworks** that prioritize rigid milestones and output-driven metrics over meaningful cultural change. To succeed, organizations must **reject bureaucratic, "one-size-fits-all" prescriptions** and instead focus on fostering a mindset centered on flow, value, and continuous learning.

*   **Run digital transformation as a project** with a fixed start date, an 18-month countdown, and detailed Gantt charts.
*   **Mandate a single model** (such as the Spotify model of squads and tribes) across the entire organization regardless of context.
*   **Use milestones to predict output** at the specific point in time when you know the least about the work.
*   **Utilize high-pressure language** like "deadlines" and "drop-dead dates" as primary motivational tools for performance.
*   **Conduct aggressive HR restructuring** by letting project managers go and making all other staff reapply for new "agile" roles.
*   **Overlay new labels on old structures** by imposing squads and tribes on top of existing organizational charts without changing underlying behaviors.
*   **Align tribes by job function** (e.g., a "Dev Tribe" and an "Ops Tribe") rather than optimizing for the flow of value.
*   **Implement "rank and yank" incentivization**, rewarding the top 10% with bonuses and firing the bottom 10% within specific job roles.
*   **Rename traditional project management** by using the word "sprint" inside a Gantt chart and maintaining Big Upfront Design (BUFD).
*   **Prioritize compliance over risk**, seeking "Maximum Possible Compliance" and defaulting to "no" for any new requests.
*   **Force cloud technology into legacy controls**, disabling the majority of cloud features to make them fit 1950s-era organizational constraints.
*   **Treat digital transformation as a "factory"** where failure is not tolerated and experimentation is forbidden.
*   **Tie OKRs (redefined as "Output and Key Results") to bonuses** and use Red-Amber-Green (RAG) status reporting.
*   **Drive competition via individual metrics**, specifically targeting velocity, lines of code, and developer productivity algorithms to fire worst performers.
*   **Use maturity models as a target**, setting levels 1 through 4 to signify that an organization has "arrived" at its destination.
*   **Require mandatory "Certified Really Agile Practitioner" (CRAP) training** for all levels of leadership and staff.

title: Anatomy of a Production Kubernetes Outage
description: A great example of a complex outage and the learning derived from it.
notes:
Reliability in complex systems requires architecting **"defense-in-depth" fallbacks** to maintain core services during failures, coupled with a **transparent culture** that treats every outage as a vital opportunity for collective learning and community engagement. Underpinning this is the need for proactive testing through chaos engineering and monitoring that makes critical platform health data impossible to ignore.

**Incident Overview**
* Monzo Bank experienced a significant production outage lasting **1 hour and 21 minutes**.
* Despite the backend failure, the majority of customer payments continued to succeed due to a **defense-in-depth** architectural approach.

**Technical Root Causes**
* **gRPC Library Bug:** A bug in the gRPC client library (embedded in the Kubernetes API server) was triggered by connection resets following an etcd cluster upgrade.
* **Version Incompatibility:** A breaking change in Kubernetes 1.6 changed how empty endpoint sets were represented in JSON. The version of Linkerd in use did not expect a nullable field, leading to **null pointer exceptions**.
* **The Trigger:** An engineer had scaled a non-production service to zero replicas the previous day, creating the empty endpoint set that eventually triggered the Linkerd crash loop.

**Operational and Human Factors**
* **Compounding Errors:** Engineers initially misdiagnosed the routing issue as a known minor bug and restarted all Linkerd pods. This worsened the outage because the pods could not pull configuration from an already struggling API server.
* **Monitoring Gaps:** While monitoring identified the initial service failure, it did not provide enough visibility into the fact that the recovery actions (restarting pods) were actively killing the platform.
* **Information Accessibility:** The lack of easily accessible operational data led to poor decision-making during the high-pressure incident.

**Key Lessons and Best Practices**
* **Defense-in-Depth for Reliability:** Monzo utilized a fallback system at the "edge layer" (connected directly to the MasterCard network) that could authorize transactions offline and replay them once the backend recovered.
* **Chaos Engineering:** The outage could have been preempted by chaos engineering practices (e.g., using tools like Chaos Monkey or Cube Monkey) to test how the system handles node failures and pod restarts in production.
* **Culture of Transparency:** Defaulting to transparency and publishing public post-mortems fosters community support and helps identify low-level root causes through external peer review.
* **Post-Mortem Analysis:** Conducting blameless post-mortems is essential for evolving architectural patterns and improving incident response protocols.

title: As work gets more complex, 6 rules to simplify
description: Cooperation, trust and other "imprescindible" skills for the actual work... and how this idea of classical management is a great skill for a kind of work that now is completly obsolete.
notes:
Yves Morieux argues that the two classic pillars of management, the hard (structure, processes, systems) and the soft (feelings, interpersonal relationships, personality), are obsolete in the face of business complexity, and that this obsolescence is the common root cause of both disappointing productivity and chronic disengagement at work. His answer is "smart simplicity": six rules that make people cooperate so organizations can manage complexity without becoming complicated.

* **The two enigmas share one root cause.** Across more than 500 companies, productivity stayed disappointing despite all the technology, and engagement stayed low despite all the affiliation events and leadership programs. Both trace back to the obsolete pillars of management.
* **Complexity vs. complicatedness.** Complexity is the legitimate new set of business requirements (quality, cost, reliability, speed); complicatedness is the self-inflicted proliferation of structures, processes, KPIs, scorecards, committees and interfaces that the hard approach piles on for each new requirement.
* **The hard approach manufactures complicatedness.** An automotive engineering division is a five-dimensional matrix, each cell hiding a 20-dimensional matrix, with a dedicated function (Mr. Noise, Mr. Petrol Consumption) for every requirement instead of getting people to work together.
* **Mr. Repairability shows the futility of structural fixes.** When repairability became a requirement, the hard answer was a new function, process, scorecard and incentive on top of 25 other KPIs. Diluted across 26 KPIs within ~20% variable pay, it moved compensation by 0.8% and changed behavior by zero, while still costing coordination with 25 other coordinators.
* **Organizations are nervous systems, not skeletons.** The real solution is not drawing boxes with reporting lines but the interplay—the connections, interactions and synapses, the nervous system of adaptiveness—rather than the skeleton of boxes.
* **Cooperation economizes resources.** When people cooperate they use less of everything; when they don't, you need more time, equipment, systems, teams, stock and working capital, which employees end up absorbing through super-individual effort—producing stress, burnout and disengagement.
* **Both pillars fail at fostering cooperation.** The hard approach can only add boxes (a bank's middle office turns one front/back-office problem into two plus its own cost). The soft approach—making people like each other—is wrong and counterproductive: Morieux keeps two TVs precisely because he loves his wife and wants to avoid imposing tough tradeoffs, showing that more affection leads people to avoid the relationship-straining cooperation.

### The six rules of smart simplicity
* **Rule 1 — Understand what others do.** Go beyond boxes and job descriptions to the real content of others' work; the designer who knows a misplaced wire forces removing the engine to reach the lights.
* **Rule 2 — Reinforce integrators.** Integrators are existing managers given the power and interest to make others cooperate, achieved by removing layers and removing rules (the bigger the firm, the fewer rules it should have, granting discretionary power instead of an Encyclopedia Britannica of rules).
* **Rule 3 — Increase the total quantity of power.** Empower everyone to use their judgment by giving people more "cards," the critical mass needed to take the risk of cooperating rather than withdraw and disengage.
* **Rule 4 — Increase the shadow of the future.** Create feedback loops exposing people to the consequences of their actions; the firm told design engineers that in three years they would move to after-sales and own the warranty budget, so problems would "explode in their face"—far more powerful than 0.8% variable pay.
* **Rule 5 — Increase reciprocity.** Remove the buffers that make people self-sufficient so they need each other; many "second TVs" at work create no value and only provide dysfunctional self-sufficiency.
* **Rule 6 — Reward those who cooperate, blame those who don't.** Citing LEGO CEO Jorgen Vig Knudstorp, blame is not for failure but for failing to help or ask for help—making it in one's interest to be transparent about real weaknesses and forecasts.
* **The real battle is against complicatedness.** Applying these rules reshapes organizational design, financial policies and HR practices, letting firms manage new complexity without getting complicated—more value at lower cost. The real battle for leaders is not against competitors but against their own bureaucracy.

title: Continuous Delivery In Agile
description: Jez Humble dismantles the "continuous delivery won't work here" excuses (regulated, not websites, legacy, people) — the real obstacles are only culture or architecture.
notes:
Jez Humble takes on the universal objection — "continuous delivery sounds great, but it won't work here" — and shows the stated reasons aren't real obstacles. CD fundamentally means making releases boring: any time of day, zero downtime, by pressing a button.

- The four excuses ("we're regulated," "we're not building websites," "too much legacy," and his unfavourite, "our people are too stupid") are all wrong. The real obstacles are only two: your culture sucks, or your architecture sucks.
- CD is a risk-management process: a deployment pipeline produces, as a byproduct, exactly the information auditors want — auditors love it. He met the most restrictive, rigid compliance regime he'd ever seen (the US federal government) and CD still worked.
- Architecture and teams: prefer trunk-based development over long-lived branches (the HP LaserJet story — a branch per printer line meant a week to commit to trunk, six weeks of manual regression). CD decouples teams, and by Conway's Law you organize the organization around the architecture (co-located, cross-skilled teams) so you're not waiting on approvals to get anything done.

title: The problem with Product Ownership
description: Great talk to understand the tension between Agile IT and the classic management that continue thinking that IT is a cost center. Very interesting to understand why Agile IT can not be archived using a classic command & control approach.
notes:
Mark Schwartz (AWS enterprise strategist, former CIO of US Citizenship and Immigration Services) argues that the Scrum product owner role, far from healing the historic divide between IT and "the business," actually perpetuates the old waterfall mindset of controlling the technical people. His thesis: the product owner has become a loose-coupling interface that sits between the business and the team, keeps developers unaccountable for outcomes, and prevents the kind of joint, mission-driven team that agility was always supposed to deliver. He offers an alternative he ran at USCIS: charge a truly cross-functional team with measurable goals and no requirements.

* **The IT-vs-business divide is a historical accident, not a law of nature.** Schwartz traces it to the late 1970s arrival of expensive computers and the "Igor" stereotype: the geeky technologist nobody understood, treated as an outsider. Managers' core anxiety became "how do I keep control over these computer people?"
* **Waterfall institutionalized an arm's-length contractor relationship.** Because you couldn't trust IT, you wrote a big requirements document, negotiated an estimate, handed it over, and held them to contract — which made outsourcing feel natural.
* **Scrum shrank the divide but kept its premises.** The big IT-vs-business relationship was miniaturized onto a team: a product owner (from the business) decides what's valuable and tells the technical team, who are then on the hook only for delivering the requested output — not the outcome.
* **Sprint commitments are the same control reflex in new clothing.** Asking the team to commit to two weeks of work and checking up after is the waterfall control instinct, not a genuine change in thinking.
* **The product owner becomes a bottleneck and a proxy.** It is hard to get a good product owner; the best business experts are guarded by their managers. And a product owner from one part of the business sees their job as extracting work from IT for their constituency, so the backlog grows endlessly and the team can never be reassigned.
* **The Igor stereotype is no longer valid.** Technical people generally want to do the right thing and care about useful outcomes.

### The USCIS alternative: goals, not requirements
* **Make the whole team accountable for outcomes, not output.** Accountability means owning an outcome and getting the satisfaction of delivering it — not being blamed if it fails.
* **Replace ~100 documents and 13 stage-gates with five measurable goals.** For E-Verify scaling, they threw away four years of requirements (a sunk cost) and defined five quantifiable goals (raise the 70-cases-per-day throughput, raise the 98.6% automation rate, fix the signup process where only 40% completed). The business case became: spend ~$1M per goal — is it worth it?
* **Requirements are a risk, not an asset.** Translating an objective into requirements adds the risk that those requirements won't achieve the objective. He cites a Microsoft finding that roughly a third of ideas help the goal, a third do nothing, and a third actively hurt it.
* **The team decides how; management only sets the number.** Each goal went to one cross-functional team (~10 people) with development, testing, operations, plus business and policy expertise. They were told: do whatever raises the number, and show results day one. Anything not aimed at raising the number was forbidden.
* **Oversight became a recurring conversation about the metric.** Every two weeks the team reported the number; every month overseers could keep funding, stop, or reallocate. When a metric plateaued despite good effort, they gave the money back.
* **The product owner stays, but loses positional authority.** A real subject-matter expert was on the team and was critical; what he removed was the special position with authority to unilaterally decide what's valuable. Expertise lives in a T-shaped team.
* **Results: production impact on day one, project done early.** A four-year plan finished in two and a half years. Schwartz frames this as what agility actually means — a genuinely joint team where IT and business are the same thing, rather than Scrum and XP stopping halfway by keeping the old control premises.

title: NDC2012 Programmer Anarchy
description: Fred George on "Programmer Anarchy" at Forward: removing managers, BAs, PMs and QA so empowered programmers self-organize, build tiny services and optimize for speed as a competitive advantage.
notes:
Fred George describes "Programmer Anarchy" — how Forward (a London company doing ~£55M revenue with ~55 employees, £1M/employee) stripped away the development process and let empowered programmers run the show, treating speed as a competitive advantage and leveraging technology hard.

- Remove the roles: no managers, no project/iteration managers, no business analysts, and QA sits on the business side. The point of having no managers is so a programmer can rewrite something three times without anyone micromanaging — no story estimates, no tracking.
- It runs on trust and business metrics: throwaway code is fine — if 100 lines are wrong, rewrite and redeploy rather than protecting them with unit tests; watch the business metrics to see if it worked.
- Opportunistic and speed-driven: small services (an early form of microservices), rapid deployment, and a willingness to do "strange things" because they make money. The "anarchy" name is deliberate — programmers, close to the business, pick and prioritize their own work rather than being fed stories.
- The model demands an extra level of trust from the business (no estimates, no micromanagement) and is presented as an experience report, not theory — its success depends on the unusual practices, not despite them.

title: Cultivating Production Excellence - Taming Complex Distributed Systems
description: A good talk about operating system at scale.
notes:
Liz Fong-Jones is a developer advocate and site reliability engineer at Honeycomb, drawing on her time as an SRE at Google across roughly 10 teams and many Google Cloud customers. This QCon London 2019 talk uses a fictional company, "QCorp," that "bought DevOps" in a box from a vendor to argue that tooling alone cannot fix operational pain. Her core thesis is that QCorp adopted a tools-first approach when it should have adopted a people-first approach, because "people are the ones who operate systems and we're operating systems for people." She introduces "production excellence" as a learnable set of skills and a method for building sustainable, appropriately reliable systems that don't burn humans out, built on four pillars: SLIs/SLOs, observability, collaboration, and structured risk analysis.

* **The tools-first trap is real and self-inflicting**: QCorp ordered all the "alphabet soup" (Kubernetes, CI/CD, PagerDuty, full production ownership, infrastructure as code) and got walls of vendor dashboards nobody knew how to read, noisy alerting waking grumpy engineers, unpredictable deploys (green builds that broke in production from monolith/microservice interactions), and a hero culture where senior sysadmins hoarded knowledge. "No amount of tooling is going to solve this problem."

* **Culture trumps everything**: Citing Randy Shoup, she argues "culture trumps technology" and bad culture can overwhelm even the best-intentioned people and processes. Tools without a plan behave "like the brooms in the movie Fantasia" — they multiply and create worse problems to mop up.

* **Production excellence must be intentional and inclusive**: "You don't get production excellence by accident." Take an honest look at where you are, build a roadmap, act on long-term not short-term results, and secure "air cover" to do things right. Changing culture involves the whole team plus adjacent stakeholders — marketing, sales, executives, finance, customer support, and whoever operates your service.

* **Pillar 1 — Measure "too broken" with SLIs and SLOs**: Systems are always failing, like a lawn where individual brown blades don't matter — you care about the aggregate. Service-level indicators bucket events as good or bad (e.g., a request that returns success quickly enough; a batch row fresher than 24 hours). Exclude ineligible events (a botnet flood of 403s is "not even eligible"). Availability = good events / eligible events.

* **A good SLO barely keeps users happy**: Set a window (a month to three months, not a day, because "users and customers have memories") and a target. Example: "99.9% of my events will be good, measured over the past 30 days... HTTP 200 served with less than 300 milliseconds of latency." The right threshold is where a user who hits an error just says "Meh" and presses refresh.

* **Error budgets drive both alerting and business decisions**: Error budget = 1 minus the SLO (99.9% over a million events = a thousand bad events allowed). Compute the burn rate — "is the money flying out of my bank account, or is it just a slow drip?" — so not everything is an emergency: wake someone if you'll exhaust budget within hours, queue it for the next working day if it'll last days. Budgets let you answer "is it safe to push this risky experiment?" or "do I need to go multi-region?"

* **Start measuring something, iterate over time**: "You can't act on what you don't measure, so start by measuring something, anything" — e.g., load balancer logs. Don't try to match the Financial Times' synthetic monitoring out of the gate. SLOs are never one-and-done: if an outage isn't caught, fix the SLI; if users expect four nines but you offer three, re-architect and creep the target up over months; if a significant outage draws no complaints, your SLO may be too high and can be relaxed. Check in with product managers every three to six months.

* **Pillar 2 — Observability for novel failures**: Outages "may rhyme, but they're not identical" and you can't predict what will fail — N services talking to each other means N-squared failure modes, so don't try to instrument every case. Engineers must debug novel cases in production specifically, since "all of our users' pain comes from our actual production environments." Using Colin's definition: a system is not observable if you must push new code or refactor your database schema to answer a question about its inner state. Explain events in context, find the variance between failed and successful events to form hypotheses (borrowed from Ben's talk).

* **Mitigate first, debug later**: Feature flags and rollbacks (referencing Edith's talk) let you mitigate impact immediately and debug afterward. "A well-designed automated system... will fail, it'll correct itself, and someone the next business day can look at the telemetry" — self-healing design removes the need to wake people at night.

* **Pillar 3 — Debugging is collaborative and never happens alone**: In microservice/full-ownership environments your team has dependencies and dependents, so even small outages need cross-team work. Customer support, given the right tools, can investigate production themselves. It's also an interpersonal skill — finger-pointing makes outages harder, so teams must work well both when calm and under pressure (referencing Andrea's psychological safety, Type 1 vs Type 2 thinking, and growth mindset).

* **Sustainable operations and humane on-call**: "It's not okay to feed... the gears of the machines, with the blood of human beings." The EU Working Time Directive limits on-call even with extra pay. Flexibility helps — trade night-time on-call away from someone with a small child and take it yourself; managers shouldn't hold daytime on-call that interrupts one-on-ones. Production involvement (tickets, customer support) need not all look like on-call, and on-call need not be 168 hours straight. "If you don't sleep, you can't be creative, and creativity is essential to engineering."

* **Documentation and killing hero culture**: "Leave ourselves cookies, not traps" (quoting Tanya Reilly). Write the right amount, keep it organized, and commit to maintaining it rather than brain-dumping content that goes stale. Sharing knowledge removes the single bottleneck person; don't over-laud heroes who solve things alone or shame people who escalate. Reward curiosity and teamwork, and practice with game days or "wheels of misfortune."

* **Pillar 4 — Structured risk analysis prioritizes work**: Outages rhyme, so identify common patterns. Use deliberate Type 2 thinking, not gut instinct — like a bridge whose deck is failing now versus columns that might collapse in a 30-year earthquake, fix what's urgent. Quantify risk by frequency (time between failures) and impact (percentage of users), and control blast radius by pushing to 1% of servers at a time. Tie risks back to the SLO/error budget for a concrete business case to pause features and fix bugs — but "actually make sure that you do the work," since action items left on a board accomplish nothing.

* **Lack of observability and collaboration are systemic risks**: Poor observability dramatically magnifies time to recover; poor collaboration lengthens outages and time to detect — if support gets dismissed with "that's a problem between keyboard and chair," they stop reporting real incidents. The closing call: "season the alphabet soup with production excellence."

* **Q&A — disk-fill leading indicators**: For a predictable leading indicator (disks filling toward exhaustion that reliably crashes the system), a short-term alert is fine, but watch the operational load — at her large cloud company there was a rule of "no more than two incidents per shift" because that's all one can thoroughly investigate. Better: make the system resilient, e.g., restart the VM rather than let data accumulate, since "it's always easier to clean up an old replica and start a new replica than... clean up a disk that's been filled up."

* **Q&A — pair programming not required**: Production excellence does not specifically require pair programming. It requires that during an outage people are comfortable working together — looking over shoulders, sending graphs back and forth, bouncing ideas — though pairing and code reviews are good practices that don't directly bear on operations.

title: Istio motivations with louis ryan
description: Louis Ryan on the motivations for Istio: how Google's API infrastructure evolved through mobile and cloud traffic shifts toward a service mesh of sidecar proxies and a control plane.
notes:
A service mesh externalizes the cross-cutting concerns every service needs — retries, timeouts, load balancing, observability, security — out of per-service library code and into a sidecar proxy (Envoy) driven by a central control plane. Louis Ryan traces how Google's API infrastructure evolved through mobile and cloud traffic shifts to this model, which became Istio.

- Google's API infra evolved in three phases: V1 (cross-cutting logic as libraries inside each service), V2 (a central proxy — but a single point of failure coupling all APIs), V3 (a sidecar proxy per service instance, scaling horizontally with the service).
- Two traffic shifts drove it: mobile (an explosion in the number/cardinality of public-facing APIs needing auth, quotas, rate limiting) and cloud (clients on the same network as low-latency infra APIs like memcache/bigtable — orders of magnitude more chatty traffic).
- Separate the data plane (the dumb, fast proxy that moves traffic) from the control plane (which holds policy/config) — proxies go faster the less they do, and the two scale independently.
- What the mesh does for every service instead of hand-rolling per language: retries, timeouts, load balancing, circuit breaking, chaos injection — plus security via mutual trust (client knows the server is real, server knows who the client is) using workload identities the orchestrator issues.
- Platform-agnostic: shipped on Kubernetes first (sidecars in pods) but works on VMs, Mesos, Nomad, bare metal — most companies run more than one orchestrator.
- Mixer is the standardized integration point: swap Prometheus/Datadog for telemetry or Zipkin for tracing; telemetry is batched and pushed, while policy checks are cached in Envoy (they repeat, so they cache well).
- Reliability is layered: the control-plane config API only needs ~two nines (it just changes behaviour), but the runtime path (Envoy to Mixer/pilot) must hit four-to-five nines — via horizontal scaling plus Envoy-side caching so it degrades gracefully when the control plane is briefly unavailable.
- At extreme scale the sidecar model stops making sense (ML/TPUs on dedicated hardware) — push functionality into the application/hardware there; but the sidecar covers ~90% of typical enterprise workloads.

title: Resilient Predictive Data Pipelines
description: Interesting talk about different kind of data pipelines. Interesting examples about designs using AWS services and a description about using airflow to define/operate workflows.
notes:
Sid Anand, recorded at QCon London 2016, has worked ~15 years in Silicon Valley at web-scale companies focused on high-availability and data infrastructure, and about a year before the talk joined Agari, an email security SaaS startup. He is a maintainer of Airflow (open-sourced by Airbnb ~9-10 months earlier) and co-chair of QCon San Francisco. His core thesis: predictive data pipelines deserve a place in a high-availability track because, unlike ETL pipelines feeding internal data warehouses, predictive pipelines serve end customers in real time and a bug has a very high blast radius. He argues you should design predictive pipelines around four goals — scalable, available, instrumented/monitored, and quickly recoverable — and optimize for mean time to recovery rather than mean time between failures.

* **Two classes of pipeline, very different stakes**: ETL pipelines feed a data warehouse consumed by the business/analysts (ad impressions/clicks at YouTube/Facebook, sales metrics at Amazon/eBay/Etsy, product success and recommendation-algorithm A/B attribution) with acceptable downtime of "one day" or even "one to three days" over a weekend. Predictive pipelines (friend/colleague recommendations at Facebook/LinkedIn, product recommendations and dynamic search ranking at Amazon, fraud prevention) serve consumers directly, so downtime opens "a window of opportunity for you to be taken down by a fraudster."

* **The blast radius of a data-pipeline bug is the real problem**: "bugs happen" in any system, but a bad commit writing garbage data means you must (1) fix the bug, (2) clean up potentially large amounts of bad data already committed depending on ingest rate, and (3) clean up downstream systems — e.g. search indexes that already read and indexed the bad data.

* **Scalable means infinitely scalable, limited by the weakest link**: don't scale for X users or X data; the system should absorb growth. A pipeline is "a chain and the weakest link... is going to essentially determine the scalability of your system" — every component must be independently, infinitely scalable, and the same logic applies to availability.

* **Instrumentation centers on correctness and timeliness SLAs**: "a data pipeline that loses data is not very trustworthy" — it must not lose, corrupt, or duplicate data. Cached intermediate results need a "defined acceptable staleness criteria" because people lose track of how stale cached data is and it degrades output quality. In fraud prevention, "if it's a late result, it's a useless result," and a late result also delays everything behind it, breaking downstream SLAs.

* **Build the Jeep, not the Rolls-Royce**: a Jeep is designed to be beaten up and quickly repaired (a Marine Corps team can deconstruct and reconstruct one in four minutes); a Rolls-Royce is built for mean time between failures and "is going to be in the shop for weeks" if pushed past its design boundaries. Since "you don't know what challenges that data pipeline's going to face," optimize for low mean time to recovery.

* **SQS as the scalable, available, pull-based message queue**: Agari runs on AWS; Anand calls SQS "one of Amazon's best technologies" and notes at Netflix they logged "billions of events per day" to it. It is low-latency (generally sub-second, no published number), highly scalable and available, but trades away strict ordering — "it's called a queue, but it's not really FIFO, it's approximately FIFO," so "you should never depend on" ordering.

* **SQS protocol — visibility timers, redrive, and DLQs**: a consumer reads message M1, SQS makes it invisible (starting a visibility timer), the consumer commits contents to a DB and acks (canceling the timer). If processing fails, the timer expires and another consumer can pick it up — "a message can be consumed more than one time. But it will always be consumed." A configurable redrive policy (e.g. after ~10 timeouts) transactionally moves the message to a dead-letter queue (DLQ) so it doesn't clog the front of the queue. Default retention is 4 days; "it costs you nothing" to set the 14-day maximum, which they always do so messages don't disappear if consumers are silently down.

* **SNS complements SQS**: Simple Notification Service is a push-based topic service — "whereas SQS ensures that each message is seen by at least one consumer," SNS distributes via topics.

* **Idempotency and upserts are mandatory**: because acks can be lost and messages reprocessed, "every stage in the pipeline should be idempotent." A database needs transactions and upserts, not inserts ("inserts are going to create duplicates"). For ordering-sensitive updates, put the full row in the message to replace, and guard against stale overwrites with an updated timestamp and a WHERE condition: "don't update if I'm updating it with stale data."

* **Airflow ties monitoring, alerting, and loss-tracking together**: the community had ~30 companies in production, 100+ contributors, 2500 commits, and was being moved toward Apache (champion plus two of three mentors). Airflow handles intermittent failures via retries, supports community-contributed operators/hooks (S3, Google Cloud, Vertica, lambda, EMR), and lets you add custom monitoring graphs. Separate Spark jobs count records at each stage (collector, post-collector, post-agg, post-DB); Airflow compares the counts, computes loss per stage, and alerts (on-call + Slack with a link to the stack trace) when data is lost or a message lands in a DLQ.

* **Recoverability scorecard and remaining gaps**: scalable and available are "two green checkmarks" using these components plus Airflow scheduling; instrumentation/monitoring/alerting done via Airflow. Auto-scaling lets them roll back a buggy image in about a minute, and they can spin up the next larger EMR cluster or the next bigger DB (via Terraform) under load. Weak points: the DB "is usually the thing that falls over," Spark images are hard to roll back ("we usually have to dive in and find the problem"), and Spark is not auto-scaled — you must size executors/memory yourself.

* **Stage vs production and the sandbox for onboarding**: two environments, stage and production, with stage receiving the production feed plus test data so changes (including Spark jobs) are validated there. New customers' data streams go into a stage/sandbox first because "we don't know yet if we can handle their scale" with Spark not auto-scaled; they scale up Spark/DB, warm up models, and promote to production "within a few days."

* **The current pipeline is batch; real-time is the next iteration**: the collector drops data on S3 every 15 minutes to generate hourly mail-flow analysis. The next product is a real-time pipeline where "every single message" flows through, built on Kinesis with AWS Lambda — described as "another 15 minutes" of material not covered in the talk.

title: Episode 548: Alex Hidalgo on Implementing Service-Level Objectives
description: The episode examines how to define error budgets and policies to influence engineering work, how to tell if your project is under or over budget, and how to respond to being over budget, as well as how to derive value from using up excess error budget.

title: Small Batches - PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)
description: Adam Hawkins explains Deming's PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle and how scientific thinking shows up in everyday software delivery, from TDD to continuous deployment.
notes:
Scientific thinking is the through-line from lean manufacturing to lean software delivery, and Deming's PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) is its simple, fractal feedback loop: "experience teaches nothing without theory," so always state a prediction, act on it, and check the result — otherwise you never actually learn.

- Lean sees only "target conditions" — challenges like Ohno's "do it with two fewer people" map directly to software goals like "cut build time by five minutes" or "handle 3x traffic on the same hardware."
- Plan = write down your model of current reality and the expected outcome; a plan without a prediction can't be checked, so the loop can't continue.
- Do = execute the plan faithfully without deviating; Check = compare results to the prediction and ask why (right, wrong, or unexpected) to generate new knowledge; Act = let the checks drive the next move (iterate, abandon, or standardize).
- The common failure is "PD-PD": planning and doing but never checking — e.g. shipping features that are never validated against user metrics in production.
- PDCA works at every scale, from C-suite to frontline, and is the engine of the "high-velocity edge": set standards, improve via kaizen guided by PDCA, then ratchet standards higher.
- Lineage: Deming adapted it from Shewhart's PDSA (Plan-Do-Study-Act), took it to Japan (Toyota), and it became part of lean; OODA is a related military loop.
- It's already embedded in daily practice: TDD is PDCA (plan = write the test, do = write the code, check = run tests, act = refactor or repeat), and so is continuous deployment (small change → pipeline → telemetry → next iteration), with jidoka stopping the line as early as possible.

title: Ep. #9, High Performance DevOps with Jez Humble (o11ycast podcast)
description: Jez Humble on the Accelerate State of DevOps research: the elite performer cluster, why the team (not the individual) is the unit of performance, and how leadership drives culture through practices.
notes:
There is no fixed DevOps skill set — the one universal is solving problems and learning what you don't know (a growth mindset). The Accelerate research shows the team, not the individual, is the unit of performance, and that transformational leadership is what lets teams adopt the technical practices that, in turn, build a high-performing culture.

- DevOps skills are fungible: it's about solving problems, asking for help, and trying things in non-catastrophic ways — "all the things you didn't need to know when you used Heroku."
- The State of DevOps cluster analysis found an "elite" group (~7%) deploying on-demand many times a day, lead time and restore time under an hour, low change-fail rates, and ~3.5x higher availability than low performers.
- The team is the unit of performance; individual variance is overwhelmed by team/org effects (Cockcroft: "I get them from you"). A-players are people with a growth mindset, not 10x rockstars (the all-A-players company was Enron).
- A "misguided"/risk-averse cluster deploys rarely with heavy change management to prevent failure — and so has terrible time-to-restore when something inevitably breaks; the shift is from MTBF to MTTR (Allspaw), testing in production, and small batches.
- It's just queue theory: high transaction cost forces big batches, which makes you risk-averse; functional outsourcing (separate test teams) is pervasive but bad — elite performers almost never use it.
- Observability and monitoring both predict software-delivery performance and reduce burnout; what's crucial is a feedback loop from production back to business decisions.
- Change comes from a sense of urgency plus effective transformational leadership: leadership enables the practices (CD, observability, WIP limits, small batches), and the practices are how you change culture (Deming: a good person in a bad system loses).
- The methodological breakthrough is using psychometrics to study software teams scientifically, since randomized controlled trials are impractical.

title: How to Build Observable Distributed Systems
notes:
Recorded at QCon London 2018, Pierre is a Site Reliability Engineering Manager at Poppulo, a small-medium company in Cork making internal-communications software, with a 50-person engineering department and a 3-person SRE team whose model is to give teams tools and practices to operate their own applications rather than operating them for them. His core thesis is that production should be treated not as the end goal but as the starting point: systems will never be immune to failure, so instead of pouring all effort into pre-production testing and validation, teams must build observability to understand systems once they are live. He argues observability complements monitoring, is exponentially more important for distributed systems, and rests on a culture of trust. He walks through health checks, metrics, logging/structured logging, and tracing, explaining what each is good and bad for and how they combine.

* **Production is the start, not the end**: "we have to see production not as the end goal, but as actually the starting point." Practices like TDD, integration/end-to-end testing and staging all happen before production, but systems are "never going to be immune to failure" — quoting Charity Majors, if you fool yourself into believing you can build the perfect thing, when failure happens "we're not ready for it, and it's going to be failing really, really hard."

* **Distributed systems make this exponentially worse**: decoupling a monolith into many distributed, auto-scaling applications talking over an unreliable network creates "so many more places where things actually can go wrong" — before you didn't know how things could fail, now there are simply far more places to fail.

* **Observability vs. monitoring**: monitoring is based on alerts and a known concept of "normality" — thresholds, what normal looks like. Observability covers "everything else," the scenarios never thought about. Quoting Baron Schwartz, they are complementary: monitoring tells you if your system is working, observability gives you the tools to "ask the right questions" and investigate when it isn't — adopting the scientific method of forming a hypothesis and proving or disproving it.

* **Four techniques, not three**: the common pillars are metrics, logging and tracing, but Pierre adds health checks as "level zero" — a high-level red-light/green-light visibility tool that is "table stakes."

* **Health checks answer escalating questions**: is it running? Can it do its job (dependencies available, enough resources)? Is it saturated? Implementation can be a simple network broadcast, a TTL-based registry in a highly-available store like ZooKeeper or etcd, or — the now-typical approach in systems like Kubernetes — an HTTP /health endpoint returning 200 or 500 plus a body of debugging info (services, workload, dependency statuses).

* **Be careful what "healthy" means**: about three years ago Poppulo was "overzealous," making a service report unhealthy if ZooKeeper or the right number of Kafka brokers weren't available. The result: the service "just kept restarting all the time" — health checks were too expensive (connecting to Kafka on every hit) and were "almost DDoS-ing our infrastructure" against ZooKeeper. The deeper lesson: if a dependency goes down it shouldn't necessarily mark you down, or you get cascading failures and "took down our infrastructure just because we tried to make it resilient."

* **Three levels of metrics must work in tandem**: system metrics (CPU, memory, disk — long mastered by sysadmins), application metrics (API error rates — only recently adopted), and business metrics (conversions, items in basket, purchases). In an incident you must correlate them: "maybe our sales are going down in the last hour because we're getting more 500 errors in that particular service because the network latency increased between our data centers." Cloud-native dynamism (auto-scaling, multi-region clustered databases, short-lived instances) means you can no longer just SSH into a server for metrics.

* **Poppulo relies heavily on Prometheus, but it isn't for everything**: Prometheus polls discovered endpoints ("what's your metrics, what's your metrics"), stores time-series, feeds Grafana dashboards, and drives alert rules. But not every metric deserves an alert — alerting should be limited to user-impacting symptoms, while un-alerted metrics (e.g. memory usage) remain valuable for investigation.

* **Prometheus fails at high cardinality**: an audience poll showed almost nobody puts user IDs or email addresses in Prometheus — "don't, because that doesn't work." Time-series databases aren't built to decompose to fine granularity (e.g. the error rate for one specific customer among 100,000), and they can't keep all data forever because real-time rule evaluation must stay fast. For long-term business analysis ("what was this customer buying last year") metrics are the "worst world for everybody."

* **Logging handles high cardinality but must be aggregated, searchable and correlated**: just centralizing logs isn't enough — quoting the line that "we turned our monolith into microservices so that every outage could turn to a murder mystery," millions of unsearchable logs make it "a very, very long game of Cluedo." The key is a trace/request ID that follows a request through every service, letting you filter "a few log messages within a million" and pinpoint where the exception happened — which also removes reliance on treacherous, possibly-misaligned timestamps.

* **Structured logging unlocks debugging power**: since logs are cheap text, bundle rich context into JSON instead of a bare message — service/instance name, owning team (bounded context), commit/build number, runtime version (e.g. exact minor Java version), region/node, and high-cardinality fields like customer/user (with GDPR care — "probably don't put email addresses in there"), plus a request ID for tracing. In Kibana this let Poppulo isolate a spike to a specific customer and see that "up until 5:00 nothing happened" — impossible without that cardinality. Pierre predicts structured logs/events "are going to get a lot more love."

* **Tracing (OpenTracing via Zipkin at Poppulo)**: shows request flow over a timeline; Zipkin itself assigns the trace ID that correlates everything, and a trace breaks into spans (e.g. a 172ms request spawning two downstream calls, time spent in Cassandra vs MySQL). Within the first half-day of using Zipkin (about a year ago) they "found two or three things" they had no idea were happening; it caught a request fanning out to ~500 other requests, and proved a suspected DNS problem was real ("taking 10 seconds on DNS resolution") despite ops insisting "not a chance it's DNS."

* **Tracing's downsides**: you must "know a little bit what you're looking for" and often instrument more after seeing a problem once, then wait for it to recur. Retrofitting tracing into an existing system is expensive and leaves "black holes" where traces stop and can't be correlated — a year in, Poppulo still hasn't instrumented everything. Advice: if you're starting fresh with little legacy, add it upfront because it's "very cheap" then; service meshes like Istio help but you still must propagate the trace.

* **No single right tool — the known-knowns to unknown-unknowns spectrum**: monitoring and metrics "are not a substitute for understanding the way your system works." Left side (known-knowns, automated, machines): health checks and alerting metrics for resiliency and orchestration (restart on unhealthy, wait until ready). Right side (unknown-unknowns, humans on-call): metrics for querying, tracing, logs, events, structured logging — "we can't decide upfront what we're going to be needing, so provide as much context, as much information."

* **Usability and trust are paramount because humans are on the other side**: an internal SRE survey found Zipkin powerful but most people didn't know how to use it, and Kibana was hated and distrusted (traced to the pain of self-managing Elasticsearch clusters); without trust people revert to SSHing into machines. Aim for simple or "no-touch" instrumentation — e.g. Kubernetes and Fluentd auto-attaching labels (commit, service, team) so developers get power "for free."

* **Observability requires a blameless culture**: it assumes people genuinely want to know things; if knowing "is going to come back to haunt you, or come back to blame you," it's "all undone for nothing." Trust operates at multiple levels — a team trusts it knows its own system, operators trust they can gather the right info, and the company trusts "that as engineers, we actually know what the hell we're doing."

* **Visibility serves more than engineers, and curbs vanity work**: distilled metrics let Poppulo's front-line support answer customers without handing off to engineering and even get ahead of problems (e.g. a big customer about to run a large project that might cause trouble today). Observability also disciplines refactoring — instead of "endless, meaningless, aimless refactoring" for vanity, back the belief that code is wrong with data, instrument it if the data is missing, and verify afterward whether the change actually improved things. Closing: "production is just the start... Testing is important... but maybe don't spend all your time on it. Keep some for instrumenting your services."

title: The resource utilization trap
description: Henrik Kniberg explains the resource utilization trap, how it impacts our ability to deliver and what we can do about it.
notes:
Prioritizing 100% resource utilization leads to zero flow and stalled delivery, so you should focus on optimizing the flow of work through a pull system before attempting to improve efficiency. Remembering that keeping people busy is not the same as delivering value is crucial for avoiding the "resource utilization trap" and ensuring consistent customer delivery.

**Key Points of the Presentation**

*   **The Resource Utilization Trap**: Many organizations mistakenly prioritize keeping every employee 100% busy, viewing any idle time as wasted resource cost.
*   **100% Utilization Equals 0% Flow**: When a system is 100% utilized, flow time becomes infinite and throughput drops to zero. This is compared to a traffic jam where the road is fully utilized but no one is moving.
*   **Flow vs. Utilization**: The primary focus should be on "Flow" (how quickly work reaches the customer) rather than "Utilization" (how busy the workers are).
*   **Push vs. Pull Systems**: 
    *   **Push**: Forcing work into the system to keep people busy creates bottlenecks and stops delivery.
    *   **Pull**: Teams pull work only when they have actual capacity, which maintains a steady flow and improves delivery speed.
*   **The Lean Approach**: Optimize flow first to establish a baseline for delivery. Only after flow is optimized should you attempt to increase resource utilization, ensuring that any increase does not sacrifice the flow time.
*   **Productivity Paradox**: Focusing on keeping people busy results in a bunch of busy people but zero delivery to the customer. Focusing on flow results in faster delivery and higher overall throughput.
*   **Key Metrics**: 
    *   **Flow Time**: The time it takes for a single request to be delivered.
    *   **Throughput (Velocity)**: The volume of work delivered in a specific timeframe. 
*   **Optimization Results**: Using a pull system can maintain the same fast flow time as a low-utilization system while doubling resource utilization and throughput.

title: An Introduction to Modern Product Discovery
description: The best talk I show about these modern techniques.
notes:
Effective product development requires a shift from focusing on feature output to prioritizing **continuous discovery**, where cross-functional teams engage in weekly learning to identify opportunities that drive measurable customer and business outcomes [1-4]. Using mental frameworks like the **opportunity solution tree** allows teams to ensure they are solving the right problems that deliver actual value before committing to the delivery process [5-8].

* **Product Discovery vs. Delivery**: Discovery encompasses the activities used to decide what to build, while delivery focuses on the work required to build, package, and ship the product.
* **The Goal of Discovery**: The primary yardstick for successful discovery is the speed of learning. The objective is to learn as quickly as possible if a potential product or feature is the right thing to build before investing heavily in delivery.
* **Shift from Output to Outcomes**: Teams should move away from being "feature-obsessed" (output) and instead focus on the measurable impact they are creating for customers and the business (outcomes).
* **The Opportunity Solution Tree**: A visual mental representation for discovery that structures thinking into four levels:
    * **Desired Outcome**: A clear, quantitative metric that defines success (e.g., increasing engagement by 10%).
    * **Opportunities**: The "problem space" consisting of customer pain points, needs, and desires. Exploring this space is where product strategy happens.
    * **Solutions**: Potential ways to address the identified opportunities.
    * **Experiments**: Small, fast tests to verify if a solution actually delivers on the opportunity and drives the desired outcome.
* **Continuous Discovery**: A practice where the core product team (typically a Product Manager, UX Designer, and Tech Lead) conducts small research activities—such as customer interviews and prototype tests—every single week rather than doing large, infrequent research projects.
* **Collaborative Discovery**: Discovery is most effective when the people building the product are the ones doing the research, ensuring they stay close to the customer’s context.
* **Evidence-Based Decision Making**: Avoid making decisions based on a single data point (one interview or one A/B test). Instead, look at the "meta-analysis" of multiple research activities and sets of data to inform the best path forward.
* **Tool Neutrality**: Frameworks like Lean Startup, Design Thinking, and Jobs to be Done are all tools that serve the same underlying goal: learning faster to drive better outcomes. The value of a new tool is determined by whether it helps the team learn more quickly than their current methods.

title: Keynote PyCon 2017
description: Python:  Lisa Guo, Hui Ding Instagram migration to Python3.6
notes:
Executing a large-scale migration to Python 3 through incremental, version-compatible updates on a single master branch proves that major infrastructure modernization can be achieved without interrupting product delivery or user experience. This strategy prioritizes developer velocity and leverages modern language features to gain significant performance efficiencies while maintaining a solid foundation for future growth.

* **Scale and Stack:** Instagram successfully scaled to over 700 million monthly active users using a Django and Python-based infrastructure.
* **Why Python:** The language was chosen for its developer-friendliness, large talent pool, and ability to allow engineers to focus on business logic rather than language specifics.
* **Developer vs. Code Velocity:** Instagram prioritizes developer productivity over pure code execution speed, believing the framework's performance is rarely the initial bottleneck for growth.
* **Motivations for Python 3 Migration:**
    * **Type Hinting:** Standardized typing helps manage large codebases and improves developer productivity across multi-language teams.
    * **Performance (AsyncIO):** Native support for asynchronous I/O allows fewer processes to handle more concurrent requests, reducing CPU starvation and memory overhead.
    * **Community Support:** Aligning with the latest releases ensures access to new features, security updates, and easier contributions back to the open-source community.
* **Migration Strategy:**
    * **Single Master Branch:** They avoided long-lived branches, instead making the master codebase compatible with both Python 2 and Python 3 simultaneously.
    * **Incremental Changes:** Used automated "modernize" tools to apply specific fixes across the entire codebase rather than migrating file-by-file.
    * **Strict Testing:** Every commit was required to pass unit tests for both Python versions before being merged.
    * **Staged Rollout:** Deployment moved from developer sandboxes to employees and then slowly to the general user base.
* **Technical Challenges Encountered:**
    * **Unicode/Strings:** Handling the strict separation of strings and bytes in Python 3 required creating helper functions to maintain compatibility.
    * **Serialization (Pickle):** Incompatibilities between Python 2 and 3 "pickled" data required splitting Memcached key spaces to prevent cross-version communication errors.
    * **Iterators:** Changes where functions like `map` return iterators instead of lists caused subtle bugs that required manual conversion back to lists in specific logic flows.
* **Migration Outcomes:**
    * **CPU Efficiency:** Achieved a 12% CPU win on the Django web tier.
    * **Memory Savings:** Realized a 30% memory saving on the Celery (async) tier.
    * **Latency Reduction:** AsyncIO adoption reduced user request latency by 30% to 40%.
* **Engineering Culture:** The migration succeeded by following mandates of zero user impact and zero slowdown in product feature launches, utilizing a "small and focused diffs" philosophy.

title: "Unembedding" embedded systems with TDD: benefits of going beyond the make-it-work phase
description: Interesting talk about the benefits of using TDD in embedded systems. The talk is full of useful tips, patterns, and strategies very useful in both embedded and non-embedded environments. Special mention to how Francisco uses mutation testing in the development process.

title: How Finding & Fixing Faults is the Path to Perfection
description: Knowing how to get smarter, faster matters a lot :)
notes:
Steve Spear, author of the seminal HBR paper "Decoding the DNA of the Toyota Production System," argues that the path to perfection runs through the aggressive pursuit of fault. His central thesis is that knowing how to get smarter, better, and faster matters enormously, that learning is fundamentally easy because it only requires seeking out problems, and that the genuinely hard part is building the discipline and leadership to find fault, act on it, and spread what is learned.

* **Learning velocity is the real differentiator**: The gap between organizations that learn exceptionally well and those that learn at a normal pace produces profound, sometimes 90-to-1, differences in outcomes.
* **The Toyota transformation as proof**: Toyota entered the US in the late 1950s with the Toyopet, an abysmal car made at one-eighth the world productivity standard. By seeking out problems relentlessly, it reached world-standard productivity by the mid-1960s, double by the late 1960s, and by the mid-1990s ranked one or two in every significant category on affordability, reliability, and time to market.
* **Finding fault is the necessary trigger**: Recognizing "that sucks" is the first informer that you do not understand something and must do something differently. Toyota institutionalized asking "why are we even doing it this badly?" instead of accepting the status quo.
* **The worker as world expert on the problem**: Whoever sees and understands a previously unresolved problem becomes the world expert on that situation. Time must be built into work not just to do the job but to see problems, solve them, and share what is learned.
* **Right answer faster, not just first to market**: The goal is being first to market with the *right* answer. Toyota redesigned major models in half the standard two-year cycle at half the cost.

### The hybrid case: same problem, 90-to-1 results
* **Diverging from a shared challenge**: When the market demanded double the fuel efficiency, everyone first tinkered at the edges (aerodynamics, lighter materials) and got it wrong. The right answer was the hybrid: an electric motor for acceleration plus a combustion engine for steady state, coordinated by software.
* **A ten-year head start compounds**: Toyota's Prius beat Chevy's Volt to market by a decade, cycling the technology through six to eight rounds and diversifying across 24 platforms. Toyota sold ~9 million hybrid units versus ~100,000 Volts on the identical problem.

### Leadership and the safely-seeking-fault dynamic
* **Rickover and the nuclear Navy**: Hyman Rickover built atomic power on the same learning dynamic, designing work so people could see what was wrong, find out why immediately, and tell someone else. The result: a perfect US Navy submarine and reactor safety record since 1955.
* **The Soviet counterexample**: Facing the same physics, the Soviet program could not safely raise problems, and Spear could fill a slide with their failed submarines. The difference was purely the learning dynamic, enabled or blocked by leadership.
* **Operational failures that get ignored, not swarmed**: He cites a nurse confusing near-identical heparin and insulin vials, and NASA's Challenger and Columbia, where known problems were normalized and waved away until fatal.

### Midway: rehearsing instead of stress-testing
* **Locked-in assumptions left unchallenged**: The Japanese Navy showed up at Midway with double everything yet lost — losing "no later than 1929" when the Admiralty froze its doctrine around a single assumption and never revisited it.
* **War game as rehearsal, not stress test**: Before Midway they used a war game to *rehearse* their plan; when a junior officer kept winning by fighting realistically, the referee blew the whistle and swapped him out rather than learning from the loss.

### Practicing for the outliers
* **Apollo did not improvise**: Armstrong's seemingly heroic manual landing was not improvisation; the crew had practiced screw-up scenarios in the lunar module simulator. Competency must be built in the outliers, or you get failure instead of heroics.

### Daily habits to seek fault
* **Advocate less, invite critique more**: Instead of selling a plan's virtues, say "here's the best I could do, now tell me what's wrong."
* **Ask what is not working, then why**: Replace "how's it going?" (which invites "fine") with "what's not working in the situation we created for you?" then "why isn't it working, what can we learn, change, and teach?"
* **Tooling for dispersed, mobile workforces**: His team built mobile software — a portable digital andon cord — so a person with a problem can raise a ticket in seconds to whoever owns that type of problem, instead of sweeping it under the rug until it explodes.

title: Jim Womack on Lean Thinking: Past, Present & Future
description: Jim Womack offers a comprehensive retrospective and prospective view of Lean thinking, exploring its evolution, current relevance, and future potential for a "second lean leap".
notes:
Jim Womack's central message emphasizes that the "first lean leap" has been exhausted, necessitating a "second lean leap" where the next generation must drive a deeper, more resilient application of Lean principles by transforming management practices, embracing technology, and fostering employee loyalty to ensure its continued relevance and sustained value creation. This evolution involves changing both beliefs and practices to convert "muda" (waste) into value, making Lean a long-term commitment rather than a short-game fix.

- Lean is a way of thinking and practice, empirically tested through PDCA/A3, aiming to create more value with less resource.
- The "first lean leap" (1990-pandemic) is exhausted, marked by lack of sustainability, misblame of JIT for shortages, loss of relevance to tech and green movements, and erosion of employee loyalty.
- Lean is inherently fragile; it disappears if people don't believe in it and managers don't practice it.
- Modern management, focused on KPIs and top-down directives, has created less value than hoped and contributes to a rework loop rather than true improvement.
- A "second lean leap" is necessary, driven by the next generation, to regain relevance and apply Lean principles more deeply.
- Reasons for optimism include geopolitical shifts enabling "lean shoring," creative engagement with the tech world (e.g., Lean Tech Manifesto), an elaborated lean management system, and Toyota's continued success and innovation in areas like EVs.
- Lean can be a great enabler for green initiatives by making them cost-affordable.
- JIT protects against normal variation, while "recovery methods" (like those led by OMDD at Toyota) are crucial for Black Swan events.
- The "bank of muda" is still open, allowing for the conversion of waste into value to boost productivity.
- The transition requires changing both beliefs and practices regarding Lean.
- Toyota's sustained success is built on commitments like never laying off permanent employees (since 1950), making Kaizen during downturns, and practicing daily management (real-time problem resolution) and hoshin (big strategic improvements).
- Concurrent engineering (considering alternative countermeasures) and simultaneity (integrating product and process development) are key to rapid time to market.

title: Lunch & Learn How to Misuse DORA DevOps Metrics
description: Interesting presentation in which bryan describes an agile/devops transformation, telling us about mistakes and successes. Interesting learnings, tips, and ideas.
notes:
Effective continuous delivery relies on using metrics as health indicators to relentlessly uncover and remove organizational friction rather than as targets to measure or control people. True improvement comes from shrinking batch sizes to expose delivery problems, fostering a culture of trust, and prioritizing the delivery of actual business value over vanity metrics.
