- Reframe requests around learning: when a PM wants a new marketing partnership, don't ask "what new tech can we try?" (that grows batch size and WIP) — ask "how quickly can we go and see if this actually increases signups?"
- In the learning mode some practices become non-negotiable: model work as hypotheses, work in small batches, and use continuous delivery to match the required velocity of learning.
- The goal is to discover what and how to do — and discovery may legitimately conclude that a standardized, known-territory approach is now the right answer.
- All organizations face open-ended problems; you win by continually out-learning the competition (how Japanese carmakers beat American ones). Farley: "assume what you know is wrong... then figure out how wrong it is."

title: Acceptance Testing Is the FUTURE of Programming
description: This talk explores a radical idea that acceptance testing, rather than being just a way to verify software, may be the next step in the evolution of how we program computers, using AI to generate code from detailed specifications
notes:
Acceptance testing is emerging as a higher level of abstraction in programming, allowing developers to shift from manual implementation to precisely defining system behavior for AI to execute. This approach addresses the inherent ambiguity of natural language and the randomness of AI by using executable specifications to ensure software remains predictable and verifiable.

*   **Programming as Specification:** Programming languages serve as tools for thinking that allow developers to provide precise specifications of system goals, rather than just being a means for computers to understand the world.
*   **The Difficulty of Description:** The primary challenge in software development is not the act of writing code, but the ability to create a sufficiently detailed and accurate description of what the system should achieve.
*   **AI as a Compiler:** In the context of AI-assisted development, the human's prompt effectively becomes the program, while the AI acts as an advanced compiler translating those instructions into executable code.
*   **The Reproducibility Problem:** Unlike traditional deterministic programming, AI code generation is non-deterministic, meaning outputs can vary based on model updates, randomness settings, or changes to the AI itself.
*   **Acceptance Testing as a Solution:** Automated acceptance tests, acting as executable specifications, provide the precise and reproducible instructions necessary to define system behavior for AI implementation.
*   **Shifting Abstraction Levels:** This approach shifts the developer's role from writing implementation mechanics to specifying desired behaviors, representing a higher level of abstraction similar to the leap from Assembly to high-level languages.
*   **Integration into Modern Workflow:** Acceptance testing is a practical, existing technique that integrates seamlessly into modern development workflows, unlike previous failed attempts at high-level abstraction like model-driven development.
*   **The Need for Human Guidance:** Current AI models require significant "virtual handholding" and coaching from experienced developers to ensure generated code is compatible with the intended infrastructure and requirements.
*   **Efficiency Trade-offs:** Experienced programmers may currently spend more time prompting, debugging, and correcting AI errors than they would spend writing the same code manually from scratch.
*   **The Risk of "Cheating":** A major unresolved challenge is preventing AI from finding ways to "cheat" the tests—passing the specification without actually solving the underlying problem correctly.

title: Passion, Grace, & Fire - The Elements of High Performance
description: Josh Evans talks about the elements of high performance: passion, grace, fire, and what matters when trying to build and shape teams for high performance. He focuses on a period at Netflix when demands on engineering to quickly deliver multiple, parallel, large-scale technical transformations was the norm. The transformations enabled a global, scalable, reliable, successful streaming platform.
notes:
Josh Evans, who spent the formative part of his career at Netflix (1999-2016) including managing the playback services team from 2009-2013 and later worked at GitHub, delivered this talk at QCon San Francisco 2019. He is now starting a consulting firm called Technical Leadership Strategies. The title "Passion, Grace, & Fire" comes from an Al Di Meola song and album by three jazz-fusion guitar virtuosos (Al Di Meola, John McLaughlin, Paco de Lucía). Evans maps the three words to the elements of high-performing teams: passion is motivation/drive, grace is skills/experience/how you carry yourself, and fire is tenacity and delivery. His core thesis is that leaders inspire high performance not by empowering people but by building an ownership-oriented culture that gets out of their way, igniting passion, fostering grace, and demanding fire.

* **Ownership-oriented culture is the goal**: "seeks to foster a strong sense of ownership in every employee" so each person feels like an owner of the company — achieved by igniting passion, fostering grace (developing new skills/experience), and demanding fire (accountability, making sure things get delivered).
* **Money does not motivate creative work (Daniel Pink)**: Citing an MIT study from Pink's book, Evans notes that for manual, repetitive, algorithmic work paying more yields incremental improvement, but for cognitive, creative, problem-solving work, "you actually will demotivate people by trying to give them a bonus to do better work." Drive is instead fueled by autonomy, mastery, and purpose. Open source is the example: people work nights and weekends unpaid because the work matters and lets them develop skills.
* **Formative experiences also matter (Evans's own addition)**: His love of printmaking taught him love of craft and "the zone"; discovering science fiction (Asimov, Heinlein, Frank Herbert) at age 11 gave him a love of inventing the future — both shaped a tech career he never planned, after stumbling into tech support at Borland and building a tool called QuickSolver.
* **People already have power — don't "empower" them (Patty McCord)**: McCord, Netflix's Chief Talent Officer for 14 years and author of "Powerful," used "empower" with sarcasm; "the goal of the company is to remind people that they walk in with power and then create conditions for them to exercise it... It's more about getting out of the way than it is about doing something proactive."
* **Context, not control — the Netflix management strategy**: Supported by four pillars: extreme transparency, deep delegation, radical honesty, and true accountability. Transparency example: Netflix shared the bulk of its quarterly business review documentation (hundreds of pages) company-wide via roadshows and Q&A so every employee knew nearly as much as VPs and the CEO.
* **Deep delegation means clean handoffs to the deepest level**: "You don't want managers designing systems. You want engineers who deeply understand how to do that doing the work." Each manager fully owns their team's success and composition (hiring, onboarding, coaching, vision, strategy, roadmaps). This is the basis for "You build it, you run it."
* **Radical honesty and true accountability**: Feedback should be "blunt and direct but compassionate" — "You are acting as a mirror." Per the Netflix culture deck, "average performance results in a generous severance package," while those who excel are rewarded with more responsibility.
* **Mature, fully formed adults**: A character trait, not an age trait — Evans has met amazing mature people in their mid-20s and immature people in their 60s-70s. Such adults are humble, introspective, hungry for feedback, have integrity, see themselves as stewards of the business, are brave and direct with peers, and exercise good judgment ("you want to be right most of the time"... not "throwing spaghetti against the wall").
* **Scaling a team through promote-from-within and delegation (2009-2013)**: Overwhelmed by simultaneous demands (devices, internationalization, cloud migration, scale), Evans nearly quit; his boss said "it's because you panicked that you're the right person for the job... because you care." He delegated deeply, promoting internal stars: Philip (ex-Lawrence Livermore, led the Wii project, then single-handedly owned the iPad launch — "That's real ownership"), Julie Pitt (streaming server / internationalization, known for leaving code better than she found it), Ranjit Mavinkurve (Silverlight cloud migration), and Greg Orzell (an original author of Chaos Monkey).
* **Hiring via Patty McCord's "algorithm"**: "Is what this person loves to do, that they're extraordinarily good at doing, something we need someone to be great at?" — mapping cleanly to passion (what/why they love it and do we need it), grace (skills, experience, cultural fit), and fire (track record of delivery).
* **Situational interview questions to uncover passion, grace, and fire**: Origin story; a project they were proud of (do they ground it in business impact?); how they'd choose between competing offers (what makes them tick); technical fundamentals at scale (data structures, concurrency, locking, bottlenecks) plus a holistic system design; conflict with a coworker (do they solve it directly or call to power?); hardest feedback received and given; most painful personal failure (introspection); and turning around a failing project (tenacity) and owning a project end-to-end.
* **Results over four years**: The team grew from six high-performing engineers to 40, shipped on thousands of device types, drove features (profiles, device authentication, Encrypted Media Extensions for HTML5 DRM), internationalization (alternate audio/subtitles, launches in Canada/Latin America/Europe), and completed cloud migration in a record two years. Foundational work (e.g., Mohit Vora's CDN control plane) later let Netflix launch in 136 countries at once (excluding China, Iran, North Korea). All the people he highlighted went on to become high-performance leaders elsewhere (Workday, Stitch Fix, Twilio, GitHub).
* **Homework for the audience**: As individuals — know your origin story, what sparks your passion, whether your company's mission aligns with your values, and whether you get transparency and autonomous mastery; if opportunities are too limited, consider whether you'd grow better elsewhere. As leaders — have a clear mission/culture people can opt into, a roadmap with awareness of gaps, a team aligned with the mission, the practice of context-not-control, fully-formed adults, and mastery opportunities for each member; and if your company isn't ownership-oriented, ask what you can do to change that.

title: Vibe coding in prod | Code w/ Claude
description: A discussion on how to safely practice "vibe coding" (letting AI generate code while forgetting the code exists) in production by acting as the AI's Product Manager and focusing on verification of leaf nodes and abstraction layers.
notes:
Focusing on the product's existence rather than the code itself is essential for safely capitalizing on AI's exponential growth in code generation, requiring engineers to act as the AI's Product Manager by verifying abstraction layers, setting clear requirements, and concentrating AI-generated work on leaf nodes of the codebase to manage technical debt. This shift allows teams to leverage models capable of generating a week's worth of work without becoming a bottleneck by demanding review of every single line of code.

- **Vibe Coding Defined:** Vibe coding is defined as fully giving into the vibes, embracing exponentials, and, crucially, forgetting that the code itself even exists.
- **The Exponential Imperative:** The length of tasks AI can handle is doubling every seven months; to leverage this exponential growth and avoid becoming a even exists.
- **The Exponential Imperative:** The length of tasks AI can handle is doubling every seven months; to leverage this exponential growth and avoid becoming a bottleneck, engineers must find a way to responsibly use AI for large chunks of work without reviewing every line of code.
- **The Product Focus:** The solution to safe vibe coding in production is to **forget the code exists, but not the product exists**.
- **Engineers as Product Managers (PMs):** When vibe coding, the engineer acts as the AI's Product Manager, needing to set clear requirements, specifications, and constraints for the AI (Claude).
- **Verification through Abstraction:** Safety is achieved by finding and verifying an abstraction layer that can be checked without needing to understand the underlying implementation, similar to how managers oversee experts or how software engineers trust compilers.
- **Mitigating Technical Debt:** Focus vibe coding on **leaf nodes** of the codebase—parts that nothing else depends on and are unlikely to change—to contain any AI-generated technical debt.
- **Protect Core Architecture:** Human engineers must still deeply understand, protect, and heavily review the core architecture, trunks, and underlying branches of the system, ensuring they remain extensible, understandable, and flexible.
- **Verifiable Checkpoints:** Design the system and changes with easily human-verifiable inputs and outputs, and stress tests, to build confidence in correctness and stability without reading all the code.
- **Effective Prompting:** Spend time (e.g., 15-20 minutes) collaborating with the AI to gather guidance, explore the codebase, build a plan, and capture necessary context into a single, comprehensive prompt before letting the AI execute the plan.
- **Learning with AI:** AI tools can accelerate learning by enabling engineers to take many more "shots on goal" (trying new features/architectures) and by acting as an always-available pair programmer to explain libraries, decisions, and code it generated.
- **TDD and Testing:** Utilize end-to-end, general, and minimalist tests, often reading the tests first, to provide verifiable checkpoints and guidance for the AI, even if the implementation is generated.

title: Software Engineering podcast: Episode 473: Mike Del Balso on Feature Stores
description: Mike Del Balso co-founder of Tecton discusses Feature Stores and how it helps operationalize Machine Learning.
notes:
Here are the most important points of the presentation about feature stores:
- A **feature store is a data platform designed to manage the data pipelines required for production machine learning applications**. It organizes the data inputs (features) that models use to make predictions and helps productionize these data pipelines.
- Machine learning applications consist of **models and data pipelines that feed input signals (features) to those models**.
- Operationalizing machine learning models presents significant challenges, including the need to manage **complex data pipelines from various sources (real-time, batch, streaming)**, the requirement for substantial **engineering support**, and the **duplication of effort** when data scientists and engineers rebuild pipelines for production.
- **Feature stores aim to solve the problem of duplicated effort** by providing a centralized way for data scientists to define and register features. Once a feature is defined, the feature store handles its productionization, reducing the need for extensive engineering involvement in the core data science iteration loop.
- **Key benefits of a feature store include:**
    - **Faster development and productionization of ML models** by enabling data scientists to deploy independently.
    - **Reuse and sharing of high-quality, production-ready features** across different machine learning projects, avoiding redundant work and ensuring consistency.
    - **Reduced engineering costs** associated with building and maintaining custom data pipelines for each ML use case.
    - **Improved data quality and consistency** by standardizing feature definitions and pipelines.
    - **Simplified training data generation** by handling historical backfilling and point-in-time correctness of feature values, mitigating data leakage.
- A feature store typically comprises several components, including:
    - **Transformations:** Defining the data pipelines to compute features.
    - **Storage:** Both an **offline store** for historical feature values used for training and retraining and an **online store** (low-latency, key-value) for serving the latest feature values to models in real-time.
    - **Serving Layer:** An interface (e.g., REST API) for models to query and retrieve features.
    - **Feature Catalog:** A searchable repository of registered features with associated metadata (owner, trust level, etc.) to facilitate discovery and reuse.
- Feature stores often **integrate with existing data infrastructure** (data warehouses like Snowflake or Redshift, streaming platforms like Kafka, key-value stores like Redis or DynamoDB) rather than creating proprietary storage layers.
- Feature stores help with **knowledge management** related to data governance and compliance by enabling the tagging of data resources with sensitivity information, though the enforcement of policies typically resides with data teams.
- The future of feature stores includes further growth and education, as well as potential advancements in **automatic feature creation, and the integration of third-party or external feature sources**.

title: Love Your Work
description: A powerful exploration of how true fulfillment and organizational success are built on a culture of trust, mutual care, and the selfless pursuit of helping others realize their own value.
notes:
True fulfillment and professional success are found by shifting the focus from self-interest to the selfless care of others, which fosters a culture of deep trust and collective resilience. Leaders must prioritize building the confidence and value of their people over meeting metrics, as individuals who feel supported are naturally inspired to collaborate and innovate toward a shared destination.

- The Impact of Workplace Culture on Outcomes: Preventable failures, such as medical errors in hospitals, are often rooted in a lack of camaraderie and care among staff rather than a lack of technical skill or equipment.
- Management vs. Leadership: Many organizations are run by number crunchers who focus on metrics, whereas true leadership involves caring for the people doing the work so they, in turn, can care for the customers.
- The Biological Basis of Fulfillment: True fulfillment comes from the exertion of time and energy for the benefit of another person. This social behavior releases oxytocin, which strengthens bonds and inspires others to act generously.
- Trust Through Vulnerability: Trust is not built overnight; it requires taking the risk to be vulnerable. In high-performing teams like the Marines, members trust each other because they know their peers would sacrifice for them.
- The Role of Confidence: Before individuals can effectively help others, they must have self-confidence. Leaders are responsible for building this confidence by helping employees recognize their own value and strengths.
- Leadership Responsibility: A leader's job is not to ensure people meet deadlines, but to provide the training, support, and safety for people to fail, learn, and realize they are more capable than they think.
- The Problem with Shareholder Value: The shift in the 1980s toward prioritizing investors over employees and customers led to the normalization of mass layoffs and increased selfishness in business strategy.
- Building Authentic Relationships: Trust is built by being honest about intentions. Relationships should be cultivated without an immediate expectation of something in return, as transactional kindness undermines trust.
- Destination vs. Route: Having a clear, idealistic destination (the Why) allows teams to use their creativity to overcome obstacles. Without a clear destination, teams focus only on counting steps (metrics) and are easily halted by challenges.
- The Ripple Effect of Small Acts: Small, selfless actions inspire observers to perform their own acts of kindness, eventually reaching a critical mass that can shift an entire culture.

title: The Well-Balanced Programmer
description: Inspiring talk for any developer. JB gives good insights and fundamental career advice for any software development professional.
notes:
To become a truly effective and sustainable professional, a programmer must balance technical excellence with disciplined work organization and a deep understanding of human psychology. By identifying personal bottlenecks and shifting focus from simply writing code to managing workload and navigating complex human interactions, one can significantly reduce stress and deliver more value.

*   **The Three Themes of the Well-Balanced Programmer:** Software development is not just about writing code; it requires a balance between writing software, planning the work, and navigating interpersonal relationships.
*   **Theory of Constraints (ToC):** Every system has a bottleneck that limits its throughput (the rate of producing value, measured in "running tested features"). Focus your efforts only on improving the bottleneck, as improving any other part of the system provides no overall benefit.
*   **"Too Much to Do" vs. "Not Enough Time":** The phrase "I don't have time" leads to learned helplessness. Reframe the problem as "having too much to do," which empowers you to find solutions like doing less or maximizing work not done.
*   **Evolutionary Design:** Design decisions should be made as close to the moment of implementation as possible. Upfront design is often flawed because it occurs when you are at your peak ignorance regarding the system's actual needs.
*   **Working in Tiny Steps:** Use techniques like Test-Driven Development (TDD) and refactoring to work in small, manageable increments. If you make a mistake, slow down and take even smaller steps to reduce stress and maintain a clean codebase.
*   **The Casino Technique:** Allocate a small percentage (start with 10%) of your work time to experiment with new techniques and learning. Gradually increase this "spare capacity" until you find the limit where it starts to impact perceived performance, then take one step back.
*   **The Two-Minute Rule:** If a task takes less than two minutes (120 seconds), do it immediately. This prevents small tasks from cluttering your mental space or your long-term plan.
*   **The Inbox Technique:** Write down every distracting thought or new task on a piece of paper immediately to get it out of your head. This allows you to maintain deep focus on your current task without the fear of forgetting other responsibilities.
*   **Mono-tasking and Focus:** Work in uninterrupted bursts (such as the Pomodoro Technique). Most non-emergency communication can wait 15–30 minutes while you complete a cycle of focused work.
*   **The Fundamental Irony of Interaction:** In human interactions, a person's response is instant, culturally conditioned, and perfectly rational based on the "significance" (interpretation) they have assigned to your actions. 
*   **Offer Alternative Interpretations:** Since you cannot easily change someone’s conditioned response, change the "significance" by offering them a different way to interpret your behavior.
*   **Self-Debugging:** Use interaction models to analyze and improve your own behavior rather than trying to "fix" others. Identifying personal bottlenecks like being judgmental or impatient is key to professional growth.
*   **E-Prime for Communication:** Practice using English without the verb "to be" to avoid labeling and judging people or situations, which helps in maintaining better professional relationships.

title: Shaped by demand: The power of fluid teams | Daniel Terhorst-North | LDX3 London 2025
description: Daniel Terhorst-North presents demand-led planning as a framework for building fluid, autonomous teams that self-organize quarterly to balance delivery, discovery, and Kaizen based on real-time organizational demand.
notes:
Organizations can achieve true business agility by **shaping fluid, autonomous teams to fit shifting demand** rather than forcing work into static structures, relying on shared values and clear "rules of the road" to enable rapid team formation. By allowing people to **self-organize around pitched work**—balancing delivery, discovery, and continuous improvement—companies foster higher engagement and a stronger connection to the value being delivered.

- **Business agility** is the process of designing an organization so it can effectively respond to feedback, understand its customers, and execute its goals.
- Effective organizational design builds the best structure to perform the best work for the present moment, acknowledging that **structures must change** as the nature of work evolves.
- Work is categorized into **Delivery** (features), **Discovery** (learning and risk management), **Kaizen** (continuous system improvement), and **BAU** (running the business).
- The belief that teams require long "stable" periods to jell is based on flawed research; teams with shared values and goals can become productive within **hours or days**.
- **Demand-led planning** flips traditional structures by shaping teams to fit current demand rather than forcing work into pre-existing team silos.
- Quarterly planning cycles should shift focus between delivery, discovery, and Kaizen, with the rule that **none of these categories should ever be zero**.
- **True autonomy** means a team has the skills, knowledge, experience, authority, and resources (the "plane") to consume work without being blocked by external delays.
- Optimal team sizes range from **three to ten people** to avoid the overhead of constant meetings or internal friction.
- Knowledge is spread by **"bumblebees"** who move between teams, ideally limited by a **3x3 rule**: no more than three teams per person and no more than three such individuals per team per quarter.
- High engagement occurs when work is pitched by product or technical leads and individuals are allowed to **choose the projects** they find most valuable.
- Teams should establish **"Big Hairy Audacious Goals"** (BHAGs) that are aspirational and encourage maximum progress without the threat of punishment for falling short.
- Collaborative planning sessions should be high-energy and conclude with social activities like **"breaking bread"** to foster a sense of community and culture.

title: Foundations of Modern Product Organizations
description: This talk explains the keys to the success of digital product organizations. Technology is an essential part of any business today, not just a cost center.
notes:
To achieve true digital transformation, organizations must shift from a feature-delivery mindset to an outcome-driven model where autonomous "missionary" teams are empowered to solve strategic problems rather than just execute roadmaps. This transition requires strong product leadership to provide clear direction through strategy while fostering a culture of continuous discovery to ensure the business creates and captures genuine customer value.

* **Value-Centric Purpose**: An organization exists solely to create, deliver, and capture value. Every employee must understand their specific role in this value stream, and the primary focus should be on creating excellent products rather than focusing exclusively on financials or board meetings.

* **Digital Paradigm Shift**: True digital transformation requires moving beyond "digitizing processes" or adopting agile frameworks. It necessitates viewing technology as a profit center and a core business enabler rather than a cost center or a service provider to "the business."

* **Missionary vs. Mercenary Teams**: Modern organizations must shift from "mercenary" feature factories—which simply execute assigned tasks—to "missionary" teams that are empowered with the autonomy to solve specific business problems and are held accountable for outcomes rather than outputs.

* **The Strategy Kernel**: A robust product strategy consists of a diagnosis (identifying the critical challenge), a guiding policy (the overall approach to overcome obstacles), and a coherent set of actions (coordinated steps to implement the policy). Strategy is about choosing which problems to solve to realize a vision.

* **Product Leadership Responsibilities**: Leaders must provide direction (intent and strategy), management (resource allocation and coaching), and inspiration (motivation and team development). The head of product should be a peer to other top executives.

* **Team Topologies**: Organizational structure should be flexible and aligned with value streams. The four primary team types are:
    * **Stream-aligned**: Direct value creators responsible for a single product or user journey.
    * **Enabling**: Help other teams acquire missing capabilities.
    * **Platform**: Reduce cognitive load by providing internal services.
    * **Complicated Subsystem**: Handle highly specialized or mathematical components.

* **Continuous Product Discovery**: Organizations must redistribute effort from "delivery" to "discovery." This involves ongoing experimentation and validation to decide what to build—and what to kill—before committing resources to full-scale development.

* **Strategic Alignment over Arbitrary Goals**: Tools like OKRs should not be used to set arbitrary targets or execute roadmaps. Instead, they should be used for strategy deployment and alignment once an organization is already structured around products and value streams.

* **Outcome-Oriented Mandates**: Teams should be given strategic problems to solve rather than business cases or roadmaps. This grants them the ownership to diagnose situations and design the best tactics to achieve the desired business impact.

* **The Learning Mindset**: Modern product success requires accepting that the future is unpredictable, most initial ideas will fail, and customers are experts in their problems but not the solutions. The goal is to move from "business push" to "market pull."

title: Yes, I Test in Production (And So Do You)
description: A great talk about the need for testing in production and the approach we can use.
notes:
Charity Majors is an infrastructure engineer (self-described as the first infra engineer who joins early-stage products) who was the first backend engineer at Parse, which was acquired by Facebook. In this QCon San Francisco 2018 talk she argues that "testing in production" has been unfairly turned into a punchline, when in reality everyone tests in prod whether they admit it or not. Her core thesis: the software development lifecycle is not a binary "in prod / not in prod" switch but a continuum, and shipping to prod without validating what you actually deployed is just as negligent as not testing at all. She contends that distributed systems are hostile to staging, that most teams chronically underinvest in the tooling that makes production safe, and that mastery of production should be a non-negotiable mark of a senior engineer.

* **"Testing in production has gotten a bad rap"**: She blames the popular meme ("I don't always test my code, but when I do, I test it in production") for making people treat testing as either/or. Her position is "I always test my code, and then I test it in production, too" — "it is just as negligent, in my eyes, to ship to prod and not validate that what you thought you deployed is what you actually deployed as it is to not test it at all." Forced to choose one, she'd choose validating in prod.
* **The lifecycle is a continuum, not a switch**: Deploying is "more like turning on an oven and starting it to bake" — testing starts when you write code and "doesn't end until that line of code is pulled from prod." The longer code runs in production, the more environments and traffic it's exposed to and the more confidence you can have in it.
* **We are all distributed systems engineers now**: She contrasts the "humble" LAMP stack with Parse's 2015 architecture (a few hundred MongoDB replica sets running queries written by developers worldwide) and a national electrical grid as the mental model for failure. Failures are either hyper-local ("a tree falling over in Cleveland on Main Street" — you can't unit-test or predict it) or only visible when you zoom way out for systemic patterns (her example: bolts from a 1982 factory rusting 10x as fast, only those deployed January–March).
* **The shift from monitoring to observability**: Monitoring assumes you can predict how systems fail; observability accepts you can't. "This is the key lesson of distributed systems: it's never actually up" — at best it "asymptotically approaches being up." "If your tools are telling you that everything is working, your tools are not good enough."
* **Staging is a black hole for your time**: Distributed systems have "an infinitely long list of things that almost never happen... except you have 15 impossible things happening a day." Cloning staging to match prod is a time sink. Even a perfect staging clone won't catch a typo at deploy time, because "every deploy is actually a unique exercise of the combination of your deployment tools, the artifact, and the infrastructure as it exists in that moment."
* **The 80/20 argument**: You can catch the majority of bugs with ~20% of the effort (she credits a "Papers We Love" talk on this). Large classes of bugs only surface when code talks to real data; you can't mirror production users to staging due to security, traffic, scale, and concurrency. Even capture/replay — the "gold standard," which she built three times for load-testing database performance — "still is not going to tell you what's going to happen tomorrow."
* **Deploy tooling is your most important code, yet teams give it to the intern**: "The process of shipping your code, the code that does the work of shipping your code, is the most important code that you have." Her two-sentence summary: "take a fat chunk of that time that you're currently sending off to staging and reinvest it in the tooling around your production systems." Put senior engineers on deploy tooling, code-review deploy scripts, and bring designers in so the safe path is easy and the dangerous path is hard.
* **Reframe "testing" as experiments**: If bosses dislike the word "test," call them experiments. Things that belong in prod: blue-green deploys, canaries, behavioral testing, load tests (she notes companies that run a constant 20% load test in prod, giving them 20% spare capacity if they tip over), data-problem testing, chaos engineering, beta programs, and most deploy tooling itself.
* **Concrete safety tooling**: Feature flags ("a gift from God, or LaunchDarkly") — if a feature is twice as slow but only internal developers have it flagged on, "that is a huge win." Canaries — ship to one node, then 10, then more (Facebook always shipped to Brazil first). Always ship to internal users first. Shadow systems that fork traffic (she cites "Go-Turbine" and Linkerd, and a Parse Go rewrite that diffed old vs. new results) and Istio. Throttles, partial rewrites, and "velvet ropes" to cordon off bad actors or failing replica sets. And: "Please don't build your own" — invest in companies building these as a service.
* **Failure is when, not if**: "Never say if it fails." She prefers many small breaks users don't notice that give the team practice over rare big ones. End-to-end checks on money-making paths that look "flaky" are usually exposing real infrastructure problems your users already experience — "I give you 99 to 1 odds that the problem was not with the check."
* **Production proficiency defines a senior engineer**: "No one should be promoted to senior software engineer unless they are proficient in production" — able to write, deploy, and debug their software "not in an artificial sandbox, but... in production through the lens of their instrumentation." She advocates removing SSH access (an anti-pattern because it means your tooling wasn't good enough and you only see one node of many) and notes engineers can't take down a SaaS observability system, so they should feel safe exploring — "we get a dopamine hit when we figure something out."
* **On regulated industries / compliance (Q&A)**: "Compliance" is often an excuse for fear of change rather than a real blocker (crediting Adam Jacob's talks). Advice for conservative orgs: don't call it "testing in production" — call it "hardening production" or "building guardrails," recruit allies, find painful small wins, and earn technical credibility. Also: operational data should contain no PII/PHI — "if there is, that's a bug" — so engineers can have full access to how the system is used without crossing protected-data streams.

title: Want More Value Faster? Take Many More Much Smaller Steps
description: A must-talk for anyone trying to do lean/agile software development. The talk delves into the reasons why the strategy of using small, safe steps is the right one to steadily evolve a software system. I am very much aligned with this approach to software development.
notes:
Drawing on the information in the YouTube video transcript () and our conversation history, here are the most important points of GeePaw Hill's presentation:
- **The fundamental message is to embrace significantly smaller and more frequent steps in all areas of change, including software development.** This is presented as a core principle, likened to "that's too much rice" and "that's not enough garlic" ().
- **The traditional belief that fewer, larger steps are more efficient is challenged.** Hill argues that this perspective is based on an oversimplified view of software development as linear and predictable, which it is not due to factors like changing requirements and the self-modifying nature of software.
- **Smaller steps offer substantial intrinsic advantages that are often overlooked.** These include:
    - **Enhanced Interruptability:** Frequent "ready" states allow for easier pausing and resumption of work.
    - **Improved Steerability:** Smaller increments enable quicker adaptation to new information and changing priorities.
    - **Intrinsic Benefit:** Each completed small step provides a sense of accomplishment and positive reinforcement ("the juice"), contributing to motivation and rhythm.
- **A "step" is defined as a transition from a "ready" state (where the software can be deployed without major issues) to another "ready" state through a small, shippable change.** The focus is on minimizing the time spent in an "unready" state.
- **The immediate goal of a step doesn't always need to be delivering direct end-user value.** Instead, the criterion should be ensuring a "non-negative user experience" – not making things worse – while moving towards a larger goal.
- **Directed parallelism, the strategy of centrally managing multiple large parallel efforts, is inherently complex (NP-hard) and does not scale effectively.** Smaller, independent steps allow for more organic and less overhead-intensive parallel work by multiple individuals or teams. This relates to concepts like **Teams** and **team topologies** mentioned in the topics.
- **Rework avoidance, the central tenet of many traditional software development processes, is ultimately more costly than embracing changeability.** Smaller steps, supported by practices like **testing** and **Continuous Delivery**, facilitate easier and safer adaptation to evolving needs. **tdd** (Test-Driven Development) is highlighted as a key enabler of changeability through automated consequence detection, context switching support, and collaboration.
- **Adopting smaller steps requires unlearning certain traditional concepts like rigid pre-commitment to a final target, hierarchical command and control, and additive productive reproduction reasoning.** It also necessitates learning new skills, such as **tdd**, path-focused design (ensuring each step is shippable), and effective collaboration techniques like pairing, mobbing (or **MobProgramming**), and swarming. These practices are relevant to topics like **Agile**, **XP**, and **Culture**.
- **The principles of taking many more much smaller steps are not limited to code but are applicable to personal growth, team dynamics, and organizational change.** The core idea is about fostering a continuous, iterative approach to improvement in any domain. This aligns with concepts like **Lean Software Development** and the **Lean Startup**.
The topics listed in the sources, such as **Agile**, **XP**, **Devops**, **Continuous Delivery**, **Software Design**, **Lean**, **Testing**, **Microservices**, **Teams**, and **Architecture**, are all related to the practices and principles discussed in the presentation, which advocates for a more incremental and adaptive approach to software development that these topics often support.

title: Systems Thinking for Developers
description: Great explanation of how system thinking arises and its basic concepts. System thinking is a fundamental tool to work with/in complex systems such as software systems.
notes:
To grow from a technical coder into an impactful engineer, one must look beyond narrow logic and "correctness" to understand the **circular feedback loops and human relationships** that define software as a socio-technical system. Embracing this **systems thinking** allows developers to influence broader organizational outcomes and prioritize the long-term health and sustainability of the system over simple technical wins.

*   **Systems Thinking and Circular Causality:** Modern software development is rooted in the 1946 Macy Conferences, which introduced **cybernetics** and the idea of feedback loops where actions and perceptions constantly adjust one another.
*   **Logic vs. Context:** Effective engineering requires balancing **rigorous logic** (represented by John von Neumann) with **communication and context** (represented by Gregory Bateson). While logic handles the "how," context determines the "why" and the meaning.
*   **Meta-messages:** Every technical interaction, such as a pull request comment or an error message, contains a **meta-message** regarding the relationship between the participants. Ignoring this context can lead to frustration or toxic team cultures.
*   **The Thundering Herd and System Perspective:** Solving technical issues like server overloads requires **widening the perspective** beyond a single client. Implementing exponential backoff with **jitter** is a systems-level solution that accounts for multiple interacting components.
*   **Reinforcing Loops:** These are "spirals" that can be **virtuous** (e.g., high trust leading to better collaboration and cleaner code) or **vicious** (e.g., fear of deploys leading to larger, riskier releases and more failures).
*   **Balancing Loops:** These mechanisms **restore stability** and keep a system near a desired state. Examples include unit tests, Kubernetes restarts, and healthy code reviews that prevent technical debt.
*   **Symmathesy:** Software systems are **learning systems made of learning parts**. The system is not just the code, but the combination of the software and the people who interact with and change it.
*   **Developer Progression:** Growing from junior to senior and principal levels involves **widening the scope of care**. It requires shifting focus from narrow code implementation to the broader organizational, social, and community systems.
*   **Zooming In and Out:** A core skill for developers is the ability to **rapidly shift perspective** between highly formal logic (what to type next) and the wider socio-technical system (is this the right thing to build?).
*   **Moral Imagination:** Engineering is **never apolitical**. Pure rationality can lead to destructive outcomes (like Mutually Assured Destruction). Developers must exercise **moral imagination** by caring about security, potential for abuse, and the long-term impact of their work.
*   **The Goal is Continuity:** In both software and life, the objective is not a final "win" that destroys the environment, but the ability to **continue playing**—to keep learning, improving, and sustaining the system.

title: hanselminutes Podcast Accelerate: The State of DevOps with Dr. Nicole Forsgren
description: Hanselminutes with Dr. Nicole Forsgren on the Accelerate State of DevOps research — rigorous, vendor-agnostic science showing DevOps practices predict performance, and that industry (even highly regulated ones) doesn't change the result.
notes:
On Hanselminutes, Scott Hanselman talks to Dr. Nicole Forsgren about the Accelerate: State of DevOps report — and what makes it different from a typical vendor white paper: it's rigorous, academic-level, vendor- and tool-agnostic research (its sponsors include direct competitors like AWS, Azure and Google Cloud).

- It's research, not marketing: PhD-level statistical rigour over 30,000+ data points worldwide, deliberately tool-agnostic so the findings aren't selling anything.
- Headline finding: industry doesn't matter. There's no statistical evidence that being in a highly regulated field (finance, telecom, healthcare, government, insurance) changes whether DevOps / agile / digital-transformation practices work — she tested each industry separately, not just "regulated vs not."
- This kills the "it won't work for us, we're too regulated / too special" objection with data rather than opinion.
- The deeper challenge: accepting that something intuitively correct (your gut says banking is too regulated to move fast) can be demonstrably wrong once you measure it.

title: How to Go from Fear Driven to Freedom-Centric organizations
description: Podcast The Future Organization.  With Matt Perez from Nearsoft, a software development company with a great culture

title: Beyond Features: rethinking agile software delivery
description: Maybe we've been thinking about delivery all wrong. Maybe features aren’t the point after all. Maybe there are other kinds of work that we should recognise, schedule and track as first class citizens. Maybe this could take some of the uncertainty out of the delivery process, and give us back our sanity. Maybe.
notes:
The core message challenges the software industry's prevailing obsession with features and predictability, advocating for a paradigm shift to "software surgery" where the ultimate goal is to sustainably achieve business impact with minimal intervention, prioritizing discovery and continuous improvement as equally vital forms of work alongside feature delivery. This approach emphasizes curing problems and delivering results, even if it means no software at all, rather than merely cutting code or following rigid plans.
- **Shift from Feature Obsession**: The software industry often obsesses over delivering features and maintaining predictability, rather than focusing on actual business impact.
- **Agile's Misinterpretation**: While the Agile Manifesto emphasized individuals and interactions, working software, customer collaboration, and adapting to change, current practices often prioritize processes, tools, detailed plans (backlogs as Gantt charts), and contract-like commitments (sprint commitments, velocity).
- **Predictability as Primary Optimization**: Agile methods, developed in the 1990s, historically optimized for predictability to counter the long, unpredictable nature of traditional projects. Shorter batch sizes (sprints) were introduced to build confidence and make delivery more foreseeable.
- **Flawed Civil Engineering Metaphor**: Software development inappropriately adopted metaphors and practices from civil engineering (architects, builds, quantity surveyors, front-loading risk), which are suited for static, large-scale construction rather than adaptive software.
- **New Metaphor: Software Surgery**:
    - **Goal**: Sustainably minimize lead time to **business impact (results)**, even if it means delivering no software at all.
    - **User Desire**: People seek to resolve problems (e.g., be well, have an itch scratched), not necessarily to undergo surgery or build a solution.
    - **Minimal Intervention**: Aim for the smallest possible intervention to achieve the desired outcome (like keyhole surgery).
    - **Professional Competence**: Professionals should use proven techniques, be prepared for unexpected issues, and prioritize curing the underlying problem.
    - **Beyond Cutting**: Software work, like surgery, involves significant study, theory, and practice to understand _what_ to do and _where_ to act, not just the act of coding. The "first rule of surgery is don't cut" applies to software: seek non-software solutions first.
- **Three First-Class Types of Work**: All three are essential and should be planned, tracked, measured, and celebrated.
    1. **Features**: The observable capabilities a computer provides. (Often the only thing measured, leading to skewed perceptions of progress).
    2. **Discovery**: Experimenting, exploring, and reducing uncertainty to identify the right problems or the most effective solutions. It involves learning and surfacing unknowns.
    3. **Kaizen (Systems Thinking)**: Continuous, small-scale improvements to the _system of work_ itself (e.g., learning, automation, process changes, relationship building). The goal is to make the work system more effective.
- **Rethink Engagement and Professionalism**:
    - **Business Stakeholders**: Re-engage by focusing on delivering tangible business impact and acting as consultants to diagnose needs, rather than merely executing feature requests.
    - **Management**: Recognize and support all three types of work (features, discovery, Kaizen) for accurate capacity planning and visibility.
    - **Software Professionals**: Cultivate a mindset of continuous learning, specializing in areas, and developing broader skills like influencing and negotiating, which are crucial for true professionalism beyond just coding.
    - **Software as a Tool**: Software is a means to an end (business impact), not the end itself. The ultimate objective is "curing," not merely "cutting code."

title: GOTO 2020 • When To Use Microservices (And When Not To!)
description: Interesting conversation about tradeoffs to be considered for using a microservices architecture.
notes:
**Microservices should be treated as a strategic choice to achieve specific outcomes** like independent deployability or organizational autonomy rather than a default technical standard, as they introduce significant complexity and cost. To truly benefit from this architecture, an organization must also **evolve its culture from centralized control toward a model of distributed responsibility and team empowerment.**

* **Focus on Outcomes:** Microservices should be a conscious choice made to achieve specific benefits or outcomes, such as scaling or independent deployability, rather than adopting technology for its own sake.
* **Monolith as the Default:** The default architecture should be a single-process monolith. Microservices introduce the complexities of distributed systems and should only be used when there is a compelling reason to move away from a simpler topology.
* **Independent Deployability:** A primary goal is the ability to change and deploy one service without requiring changes to any other part of the system. This reduces the scope of releases and enables zero-downtime deployments.
* **Data and Processing Isolation:** Microservices are highly effective for partitioning sensitive data and the logic that acts upon it, which simplifies governance, auditing, and compliance with regulations like GDPR.
* **Organizational Autonomy:** As organizations grow, coordination becomes exponentially harder. Microservices enable teams to work, make decisions, and roll out software independently, reducing the need for cross-team synchronization.
* **Avoiding Distributed Monoliths:** Organizations must ensure that "theoretical" independent deployability is practiced in reality. If services must always be deployed together, it is a distributed monolith, which combines the downsides of both architectures.
* **Information Hiding:** Success depends on hiding as much information as possible within a service boundary. A smaller interface makes it easier to change internal logic without breaking other services.
* **Service Boundaries as Enforcement:** While code-level modules are often easily violated, the physical process boundary of a microservice makes it difficult to bypass encapsulation, forcing better architectural discipline.
* **Data Decomposition Challenges:** Breaking apart a relational database is often the most difficult task. It involves breaking referential integrity and moving from ACID transactions to the more complex world of eventual consistency.
* **Required Organizational Change:** Microservices struggle in "command and control" environments. Success requires a shift toward decentralized decision-making and a willingness to evolve organizational structures alongside the technical architecture.
* **Cost and Complexity:** Microservices rarely save money in the short term; they are an investment that "buys options" at the price of increased operational complexity and the need for new skill sets.

title: Surviving Continuous Deployment in Distributed Systems
description: A good description about the techniques required to deploy incremental changes and Trunk Based Development in a distributed system (expand-and-contract, outside-in with feature toggles).
notes:
Successfully implementing continuous deployment requires prioritizing backward compatibility through techniques like feature toggles for new behavior and the expand-and-contract pattern for refactoring. Developers should meticulously plan their release sequences and treat internal service boundaries as formal contracts to ensure that every commit is safe to deploy immediately without breaking production.

* **Continuous Deployment Prerequisites**: Success requires a strong technical foundation, including a robust TDD culture, zero-downtime deployment capabilities, infrastructure as code, and comprehensive automated testing.
* **Internal System Contracts**: Treat internal boundaries between distributed components (e.g., UI to API, API to Database) as formal contracts to ensure compatibility during independent deployments.
* **Deployment Order Principle**: To maintain production stability, deploy "Producers" before "Consumers" (e.g., update the API before updating the UI that relies on it).
* **Outside-In for New Features**: Implement new functionality starting from the UI and moving inward. Use **Feature Toggles** to decouple deployment from release, hiding incomplete logic from users until all layers are ready.
* **Inside-Out for Refactoring**: Use the **Expand and Contract (Parallel Change)** pattern when refactoring. Start from the innermost layer (Persistence/API) and move outward to the UI.
* **Expand and Contract Phases**:
    1. **Expand**: Introduce the new interface/capability alongside the old one.
    2. **Migrate**: Update consumers to use the new interface.
    3. **Contract**: Remove the old, deprecated interface.
* **Data Loss Prevention**: Avoid "naively" deploying database migrations and code changes simultaneously. Use **Preemptive Double Writes** (writing to both old and new columns/tables) to ensure data integrity during the transition period.
* **Preparatory Refactoring**: For tasks involving both new features and refactoring, refactor the system first to make the feature addition trivial, then implement the new functionality.
* **Story Planning**: Before coding, create a plan detailing the specific sequence and number of releases required to keep the system retro-compatible at every step.
* **Cognitive Overhead**: Continuous deployment increases the number of releases and cognitive load because developers must constantly ensure every change is backward compatible with the live environment.

title: Developer Productivity Engineering: What's in it for me?
description: This video features Trisha Gee, presenting on Developer Productivity Engineering (DPE). She explains the principles and practices of DPE and emphasizes how it can improve developer experience and efficiency.
notes:
True organizational productivity is achieved by shifting focus from individual "rockstar" developers to building **10x organizations** through **Developer Productivity Engineering (DPE)**, which systematically removes toolchain bottlenecks like slow builds and flaky tests. By prioritizing **fast feedback loops** and **observability** through automation, teams can eliminate creative friction and ensure that the developer experience is efficient, reliable, and focused on solving complex problems.

* **Developer Productivity Engineering (DPE) Definition**: A practice focused on maximizing developer productivity by using automation and observability to remove bottlenecks, friction, and "toil" from the software development lifecycle.
* **The "10x Organization"**: Research indicates that productivity is less about individual "rockstar" developers and more about the environment. The best organizations are roughly 11 times more effective than the worst ones due to better tools, fewer interruptions, and faster feedback loops.
* **Developer Time Allocation**: Studies show developers often spend only about 32% of their time actually writing code; the remainder is consumed by meetings, maintenance, and waiting for builds or tests.
* **Acceleration Technologies**:
    * **Build Caching**: Reusing outputs from previous builds if inputs haven't changed. This applies to both local environments and remote CI environments to avoid redundant compilation.
    * **Predictive Test Selection**: Utilizing machine learning to identify and run only the subset of tests (typically around 30%) likely to be impacted by specific code changes.
    * **Test Distribution**: Parallelizing test execution across multiple remote agents to significantly reduce the time required for large test suites.
* **Efficient Troubleshooting with Build Scans**: Implementing observability for the build process itself. Build scans provide a shareable, permanent record of build environments, dependencies, and errors, eliminating the "it works on my machine" problem.
* **Eliminating Flaky Tests**: Flaky tests are considered worse than no tests because they undermine trust in the toolchain. Organizations should proactively detect, retry, and fix or delete intermittent tests to ensure build results are reliable.
* **Production-Level SLAs for Tooling**: The development platform should be treated as a production environment. Build times and tool reliability should be monitored with the same rigor as user-facing applications.
* **Continuous Improvement**: Build and test cycles should not just be "fast enough" but as fast as they can possibly be. This requires ongoing measurement of local build times and proactive problem-solving to prevent "process entropy."
* **Business Value**: Improving developer productivity directly impacts the bottom line by increasing delivery speed (revenue) and improving resource efficiency/reducing CI costs (savings).
* **Developer Experience**: The ultimate goal of DPE is to remove "manual rubbish" and provide developers with the creative space and focus needed to solve complex problems.

title: Testing and Refactoring Legacy Code
description: In this live coding session, Sandro will present many techniques that will help you to efficiently retrofit tests to legacy code and then refactor it to show the business logic more clearly.
notes:
Safely modernizing legacy code requires a methodical approach of incrementally retrofitting unit tests—using "seams" to isolate hardwired dependencies—before refactoring the logic into a clear, behavior-driven narrative. This process, supported by frequent commits and automated tools, ensures that code evolves into a readable "story" while maintaining continuous stability and professional craftsmanship.

* **Legacy code definition:** Legacy code is primarily defined as code without tests. The goal is to retrofit unit tests until the code is covered before attempting to refactor or add features.
* **Risk mitigation:** Avoid manual changes to production code before tests are in place. Use automated IDE refactorings to isolate dependencies, as they are less likely to introduce human error.
* **Testing strategy (Shallow to Deep):** Identify the "landscape" of the code. Start by testing the "shallowest" branches (the shortest paths to a method exit) to build knowledge and input data incrementally before tackling the "deepest" branches.
* **Creating "Seams":** To isolate hardwired dependencies (like static methods or global state) without breaking the public interface, extract the dependency into a package-private method and override it in a local "testable" subclass for the test.
* **Test Storytelling:** Enhance the readability and intent of tests by using constants and domain language (e.g., using "Guest" instead of `null`) to explain the rationale behind test cases.
* **Test Data Builders:** Use builders to manage the creation of complex objects. This protects tests from changes in constructors and makes the setup logic far more readable and less brittle.
* **"Tell, Don't Ask":** When refactoring, look for "Feature Envy" (where one class performs logic on another's data). Move that logic to the data-owning class so you tell the object what to do rather than asking for its internal state.
* **Guard Clauses:** Use guard clauses to handle validation and edge cases early. This flattens nested `if/else` structures and separates preconditions from the core business logic.
* **Level of Abstraction:** Elevate the language of public methods to high-level English that describes business behavior, hiding implementation details (like list creation or specific validations) in private methods.
* **Small Safe Steps (3s):** Work in tiny increments: one move, run tests, commit. Frequent commits make it "cheap" to revert bad decisions and facilitate better collaboration during pair programming.
* **Coverage as a Side Effect:** Code coverage should be treated as a natural side effect of gaining confidence in the code's behavior, not as a target to be gamed or mandated by management.
* **Professional Responsibility:** Refactoring and testing should be integrated into the single "unit of work" required to deliver a feature. Avoid separating them into different tasks that management can choose to skip.

title: Lead Like the Great Conductors
description: Formas de liderazgo, control,  autoridad en distintos directores de orquesta. Por supuesto, aplicable a otras entornos.
notes:
Conductor Itay Talgam dissects what the gesture of conducting actually reveals about leadership, arguing that the conductor's real job is not to produce sound but to enable other people's stories to be heard at the same time. Through video clips of great conductors he maps a spectrum from rigid command-and-control to "doing without doing," showing that the best results come when control stops being a zero-sum game and players become genuine partners.

* **The magical moment is a trap of ego.** A tiny gesture turns orchestral chaos into music, tempting the thought "it's all about me." Talgam insists it isn't—if it were, he'd just teach the gesture. The Vienna Philharmonic barely even looks at the conductor.
* **Happiness comes from enabling others' stories.** The happiness of a visibly happy conductor comes from letting many stories sound at once: the orchestra as a body, the audience as a community, the individuals, and unseen people who built the hall and the Stradivarius instruments.
* **Riccardo Muti — over-clarity and sanction.** Muti is commanding, "maybe a little bit over-clear." His gesture conveys the instruction and the sanction—what happens if you disobey—justified with "I'm responsible" (responsible to Mozart), so the only story told is Mozart as Muti understands it.
* **Control that crushes partners backfires.** All musicians of La Scala signed a letter telling Muti to resign: "You don't let us develop. You're using us as instruments, not as partners." He had to resign—command-style control works only up to a point.
* **Richard Strauss — control by the book.** Strauss's "Ten Commandments for Conductors" include "if you sweat by the end of the concert you did something wrong" and "never look at the trombones—it only encourages them." Turning the score's pages, he signals "play by the book," suppressing the performer's own story.
* **Herbert von Karajan — spiritual but firm control.** Eyes closed, fluid hands, no clear downbeat. The Berlin Philharmonic doesn't know exactly when to play, so they look at each other and the first players lead the entrance. Karajan says the worst damage he can do is give a clear instruction, because it would prevent the listening to each other an orchestra needs.
* **"You start when you can't stand it anymore."** Karajan's reply to a London flute player asking when to begin. The music lives only in Karajan's head; players guess his mind under tremendous pressure—control that paradoxically forces dependence on the maestro.
* **Carlos Kleiber — opening space.** Kleiber's gesture says "I'm opening a space for you to put in another layer of interpretation." It works like a rollercoaster whose track exists only in the players' heads, making them partners who build the ride as they take it—exhilarating but exhausting.
* **Authority is still there when needed.** When a trumpet player errs repeatedly, Kleiber's body language effectively says "wait for me after the concert." Authority matters—but authority alone is not enough to make people partners.
* **Control as non-zero-sum.** Conducting a soloist, Kleiber is 100% present yet not commanding—"rather enjoying what the soloist is doing." By creating both the process and the conditions, the oboe player stays completely autonomous, happy and creative; everyone's control adds up in partnership to the best music.
* **Leonard Bernstein — starting from meaning.** Bernstein's whole face carries the music; when the meaning is pain, he visibly suffers. The baton leaves his hand entirely—now the player tells the story and briefly becomes the storyteller the whole community listens to.
* **The goal: "doing without doing."** Kleiber supplies process and conditions, Bernstein supplies meaning; combine process and content and you reach the point of doing without doing. Talgam closes with his friend Peter's line: "If you love something, give it away."

title: Software delivery in small batches: Dr. Deming
description: This episode highlights the significant impact of Dr. W. Edwards Deming on lean manufacturing, Total Quality Control (TQC), and the subsequent development of DevOps in software delivery. Deming's focus on holistic thinking, continuous improvement, and understanding the system laid the groundwork for lean principles, which later influenced the emergence of DevOps, revolutionizing software development and operations.
notes:
"It all goes back to Deming." The lineage of DevOps traces straight back to W. Edwards Deming's 1950 visits to post-war Japan: build quality into the process instead of inspecting it afterwards, treat the organization as a system, and pursue continuous improvement through scientific thinking — ideas that flowed through Toyota and lean into modern software delivery.

- Deming shifted Japan from after-the-fact inspection ("you burn the toast, I'll scrape it") to building quality into processes via Statistical Process Control, insisting change must come from top management and that the country be seen as one system.
- His teachings seeded Toyota: Taiichi Ohno developed just-in-time, kanban (pull-based work), and TQM, culminating in the Toyota Production System and the "Japanese Miracle"; the 1970s fuel crisis let Toyota and Honda capture the US market.
- The chain of influence: Goldratt's The Goal (1984, theory of constraints) → the term "lean" coined in The Machine That Changed the World (1990) → Agile (2000s) → DevOps (mid-2010s), with The Phoenix Project (2013) deliberately echoing The Goal.
- DevOps is Deming's systems thinking applied to software: dissolve the Dev/Ops silos that removed chances to learn, and optimize the flow of value to the customer (The DevOps Handbook, 2016).
- Deming's System of Profound Knowledge has four parts: theory of knowledge, understanding of variation, understanding of psychology, and appreciation of a system (every system has an aim; components optimize for the whole, not themselves).
- The Toyota Way (2001) rests on two pillars — respect for people and continuous improvement — both tracing directly back to Deming.
- Transformation begins with the individual: once you internalize profound knowledge you can keep widening the system you improve, from yourself to the organization to global society.

title: Simplifying The Inventory Management Systems at the World’s Largest Retailer Using Functional Programming Principles
description: Havens shares his views on what makes great architecture great. He details what happened when an API call required 23 other synchronous procedures calls to return a correct answer. He discusses the challenges of managing inventory at Walmart, how one implements event sourcing patterns on that scale, and the functional programming principles that it depends upon. Lastly, he talks about how much category theory you need to know to do functional programming and considerations when creating code in complex systems. It is recommended to first watch the talk https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=n5S3hScE6dU or listen to the podcast https://itrevolution.com/the-idealcast-episode-22/

title: How To Build Big Software With Small Agile Teams
description: Dave Farley explores the trade-off at the heart of scaling-up and describes useful techniques to allow you to scale your big software projects.
notes:
To scale software development effectively, organizations must prioritize team autonomy by intentionally decoupling technical systems and organizational structures to allow small teams to make independent decisions and deployments. This approach optimizes for speed and efficiency by treating coupling as a primary constraint to be minimized through strategies like bounded contexts and independent deployability, even when it requires sacrificing system-wide consistency.

*   **Team autonomy** is a primary predictor of software development success, directly correlating with improved stability, throughput, and organizational profitability.
*   Scaling large organizations effectively requires many **small, autonomous teams** rather than a single large, tightly coordinated group.
*   Autonomy is defined by a team's ability to make **large-scale changes** to design and architecture and to **deploy independently** without external permission or fine-grained coordination.
*   The fundamental trade-off in organization design is between **scalability/efficiency** (high autonomy, low consistency) and **normalization** (low autonomy, high consistency).
*   Organizational coupling is often driven by **code sharing**; the cost of sharing code between teams is significantly higher than maintaining it within a single team.
*   Teams should be aligned with **Bounded Contexts** to ensure inter-team boundaries reflect natural divisions in the problem domain.
*   Inter-team communication should be abstracted to the level of the **problem domain** rather than focusing on technical or tactical details.
*   The **"Don't Repeat Yourself" (DRY)** principle is beneficial within a team but should be avoided across teams to prevent harmful technical coupling.
*   Technical insulation can be achieved through **"Ports and Adapters"** and by translating all external inputs to protect the team's internal logic.
*   **Independent deployability** is a strategic goal; every team should ideally be able to release its work without needing to test it against other teams' systems.
*   Use **contract testing** to validate API interfaces and design public APIs to hide internal implementation "secrets."
*   When consuming data from other teams, be **parsimonious** (take only what is essential) to minimize coupling; when producing data, be **generous** to assist consumers.
*   If teams must work on inseparable code, they should **share a repository and deployment pipeline** rather than attempting late-stage integration.

title: Prioritizing Technical Debt as If Time & Money Matters. Goto 2022
description: Adam presents an interesting approach for classifying and tackling technical debt based on analyzing code changes over time, by authors and files, in order to identify hotspots worth investing in.
notes:
To effectively manage technical debt, organizations should prioritize **"hotspots" where high code complexity intersects with frequent developer activity**, as these areas drive the majority of maintenance costs and productivity loss. By integrating behavioral data from version control with **social factors like knowledge distribution**, teams can ensure their refactoring efforts provide the maximum impact on time and money

*   **Lehman’s Laws of Software Evolution**: Software systems must continuously change to remain useful, but this evolution inherently increases complexity unless active efforts are made to reduce it.
*   **The Ineffectiveness of Static Analysis**: Traditional tools often report massive amounts of "technical debt" (e.g., thousands of years) that are not actionable because they measure remediation time rather than the actual cost of the debt (time lost during development).
*   **Behavioral Code Analysis**: Effective prioritization requires looking beyond the code itself to analyze how developers interact with it, utilizing Version Control System (VCS) data as a "gold mine" of organizational history.
*   **Hotspot Analysis**: Prioritize debt by identifying the intersection of high code complexity and high change frequency. Code that is complex but rarely touched is a low priority, whereas complex code in high-churn areas is a critical "hotspot."
*   **Pareto Distribution in Codebases**: In almost every system, a small fraction of the code (the hotspots) accounts for the vast majority of development activity, while most code remains in a "long tail" that is rarely modified.
*   **Hotspot X-Ray**: For massive legacy files, use function-level analysis to identify specific methods with high churn and complexity, allowing for surgical, data-driven refactoring instead of risky full-file rewrites.
*   **The Human Factor and Legacy Code**: Legacy code is often defined less by technical quality and more by "unfamiliarity"—code becomes a major burden when the original authors leave and the current team does not understand the problem domain or solution.
*   **Knowledge Distribution Risk**: Loss of key contributors (off-boarding) poses a severe risk when it occurs in high-churn hotspots with low code health. 
*   **Actionable Prioritization Framework**: The highest priority for intervention is code that meets three criteria: it is a high-frequency hotspot, it has low code health (high complexity), and it is subject to high knowledge loss due to developer turnover.

title: CORECURSIVE #113 / When AI Codes, What’s Left for me?
description: A pragmatic exploration of AI coding agents that encourages developers, who identify as builders, to overcome fear and embrace these tools as collaborative partners to enhance development.
notes:
Builders should overcome the fear that AI coding agents devalue their identity and instead embrace them as collaborative partners, treating their use as a learning project to enhance problem-solving, tackle more ambitious work, and retain the fundamental satisfaction of creating. This transition, though initially awkward and frustrating like any new skill, is necessary to leverage the tools' power and maintain the drive to build.

- **Identity Shift:** Builders often resist AI coding agents because coding is deeply tied to their identity and self-worth; overcoming this fear is necessary to embrace the new tools.
- **Collaborative Partnership:** AI coding agents are not AGI; they function best when treated as collaborative partners, similar to pair programmers, requiring continuous guidance and iteration.
- **Leveraging Strengths:** The optimal approach is "human with machine." The builder provides intuition, strategy, complex problem breakdown, and quality checks, while the AI handles speed, pattern recognition, and tedious or grunt work.
- **Learning Curve:** Using these agents must be treated as a learning project or deliberate practice; initial experiences will feel frustrating, clunky, and slow, mirroring the adoption of any new, powerful technology (like switching OS or paradigms).
- **Skill Transformation:** While some low-level coding skills may become less valuable, architectural thinking, problem decomposition, and high-level guiding skills become significantly more powerful and valuable.
- **Tackling Ambition:** Collaboration with agents allows developers to tackle complex, ambitious projects they previously wouldn't have time or capacity for alone (expanded ambition).
- **Practical Application:** Start small, such as using an agent to explore an unfamiliar codebase, and rely on strong engineering practices like comprehensive linting, static typing, and duplicate code detection to stabilize the agent’s probabilistic output.
- **Refactoring Power:** AI agents excel at mass refactorings; after a developer manually completes the first complex instance, the agent can flawlessly replicate the changes across many files and call sites.

title: Multiple WIP vs One Piece Flow Example
description: Brilliant explanation of the concept of WIP and how limiting it improves delivery flow.
notes:
Focusing on finishing one task before starting the next drastically reduces time to market and eliminates the hidden productivity drain caused by context switching. Prioritizing flow over multitasking allows for faster learning, higher quality work, and a significant reduction in organizational stress.

* **One Piece Flow vs. Multiple WIP**: Focusing on completing one task at a time is significantly more efficient than starting multiple projects simultaneously.
* **"Stop Starting, Start Finishing"**: Prioritize the completion of existing work before beginning new tasks to avoid long lead times where projects stay "in progress" but remain undelivered.
* **Mathematical Reality of Delays**: Even for a "perfect" multitasker with zero context-switching time, delivering items simultaneously is 2 to 3 times slower than one-piece flow.
* **Context Switching Costs**: In complex work like software engineering, the time lost switching between tasks (resetting focus and environments) is high, often making work take 5 to 7 times longer than focused delivery.
* **Faster Learning and Feedback**: Completing items sequentially enables faster feedback cycles, allowing for quicker learning and the ability to reiterate for higher quality.
* **Employee Well-being**: Multitasking increases stress and reduces happiness; a focused work environment leads to more effective and satisfied employees.
* **Resource Optimization**: Reducing unnecessary context switching acts as a source of "free resources," increasing a team's capacity without the need for additional hiring.
