* **Scientific Mindset:** Engineering is the practical application of scientific reasoning, which begins by assuming your own ideas are likely wrong and seeking evidence to refine them.
* **Professional Responsibility:** Software engineers are engines of change and must take personal responsibility for the social and ethical context of their work.

title: Jepsen Talk
description: JOTB17 Very interesting to understand DBs and related problems/limitations.
notes:
Kyle Kingsbury (aphyr), creator of Jepsen, demonstrates how distributed databases routinely violate the safety guarantees they advertise, and argues the only honest way to know whether a system is safe is to measure it under failure rather than trust documentation. He frames the work as testing the illusions we build on top of a "pile of tires that is on fire" (EC2, the network, the runtime), and walks through years of findings plus deep-dives into Crate.IO, CockroachDB, and MongoDB.

### What Jepsen does
* **Black-box, boundary-level testing.** Jepsen wraps a wall around the entire database and only observes the boundary between clients and the system — no code analysis, just whatever binary a vendor ships, with correctness reconstructed from externally observable behavior.
* **Concurrent clients in one JVM.** All clients run in a single JVM so Jepsen knows the exact relative ordering of events. Each generates randomized operations applied via real network calls.
* **Three operation outcomes.** A call can be `okay`, a `failure` (definitely didn't happen), or a `crash` (timeout/unknown). On a crash the operation might or might not have occurred — a consequence of the Two Generals problem — so the invocation is left open indefinitely.
* **History verification under partition.** The recorded history is checked against an abstract model (do you read what you wrote?) while Jepsen cuts the network to see if safety holds.

### A catalogue of broken databases
* **Lost writes and last-write-wins everywhere.** Riak's default LWW lost writes (safe only with CRDT siblings); Cassandra and Aerospike had the same loss (Aerospike falsely claimed ACID); MongoDB (2013) lost data at every write concern.
* **Split brain and message loss.** Redis Sentinel and RabbitMQ split-brained and discarded data; Kafka 0.8 beta could shrink its in-sync replica set to zero.
* **Stale and dirty reads contrary to docs.** etcd and Consul had safe writes but stale reads; Elasticsearch lost documents in every partition class. The recurring lesson: it's acceptable to lose data or have weak consistency — but don't lie about it in the documentation.

### Crate.IO / Elasticsearch — dirty reads promoted to lost writes
* **Built on a leaky foundation.** Crate.IO is distributed SQL on Elasticsearch 1.7, which still had stale reads, dirty reads, and lost updates.
* **Dirty reads become write loss.** Elasticsearch updates use compare-and-set on a version number, but two different values can share the same version, so a dirty read of a failed transaction's value can be committed over a correct update — promoting a read anomaly into real write loss. Recommendation: never use Elasticsearch/Crate as a system of record; use a real database and replicate into them for search.

### CockroachDB — serializable but with surprises
* **Spanner-like with hybrid logical clocks.** Scale-out SQL on the Postgres wire protocol; clocks must be synchronized (EC2 hiccups break time-based leader leases).
* **Causal reverse — serializable, not linearizable across keys.** Individual keys are linearizable in their Raft group, but across keys a client doing insert A then B might legally observe just B alone.
* **Bugs found and fixed.** A timestamp-cache bug let two transactions insert duplicate values; retry of non-idempotent single-statement inserts double-committed. Root cause: failing to distinguish retriable from indeterminate errors. Cockroach now passes the suite.

### MongoDB — protocol V0 fundamentally broken, V1 still buggy
* **V0 loses acknowledged majority writes.** Because the highest optime wins leader election, an isolated primary with higher optimes can override a majority-committed write; with clock skew (libfaketime) ~93 of ~322 preserved writes were thrown away. V0 is unfixable — migrate to V1, avoid arbiters.
* **V1 still lost ~50% of inserts in some runs** due to legacy edge cases from grafting newer replication onto V0 assumptions. Use write concern majority and read concern majority or linearizable.

### How to test your own systems
* **Choosing a database is signing a contract.** Switching costs (behavior, clients, ops expertise, hiring) make it the hardest lock-in. Read the docs, then ignore them and test, because documentation is full of lies.
* **Beware weasel words.** "Strict/strong consistency," "ACID" bamboozle; insist on formally defined terms (linearizable, sequential, read committed). Pick the invariants a system actually needs.
* **Inject realistic failures, end-to-end.** Process crashes (kill -9), node failure, clock skew (libfaketime), GC/IO pauses (SIGSTOP/SIGCONT), and especially network partitions (iptables/tc). Test the whole composition, formalize high-level invariants (a username registered once can never be reused), and embrace property-based testing. Aim to do good, not be perfect.

title: RailsConf 2018: Keynote - Livable Code
description: Sarah Mei reframes software architecture as a shared living space, arguing that sustainable code bases depend more on group habits and communication than traditional engineering models.
notes:
Software development should be viewed not as a rigid engineering project, but as a **dynamic, shared living space** where the code and the team inhabiting it form an inseparable system. Success depends on shifting focus from "building" to "living," maintaining the environment through **sustainable group habits** and small, continuous improvements rather than "big bang" rewrites.

* **The engineering and manufacturing model is inadequate for software** because, unlike physical buildings, software is never "done" and the cost of change is low, requiring a focus on continuous evolution rather than a finished product.
* **Conway’s Law dictates that code structure reflects team communication**; therefore, messiness or technical debt in a code base is often a direct symptom of communication deficits or dysfunctional team structures.
* **A code base should be viewed as a "living space"** where the primary goal is sustainable "livability" for the team inhabiting it, rather than just "building" a structure and moving on.
* **Livability is context-dependent and subjective**; a code structure that is habitable for a team of five seniors may be completely unlivable for a team comprised of one senior and four juniors.
* **Code "hoarding" and decay occur through small, daily decisions** compounded by despair, rather than single acts of laziness or lack of professionalism.
* **Big-bang rewrites often fail** because they typically address the technical debt without changing the underlying group habits and norms that created the mess in the first place.
* **Livable code is maintained through group norms**, not individual craftsmanship; everyday "cleaning" (like washing dishes) must be a collective responsibility practiced by everyone on the team.
* **Rule 1: Don't make it worse.** Commit as a team to doing the "not terrible" thing when touching messy files, even if you do not have time to fix everything at once.
* **Rule 2: Value improvement over consistency.** Striving for perfect consistency often leads to an unattainable "magazine ideal" that is unlivable; incremental improvements are more valuable than waiting for a total cleanup.
* **Rule 3: Inline everything.** Perform refactoring as part of daily feature work to maintain context and business value, rather than creating isolated refactoring stories that are often deprioritized.
* **Rule 4: Talk more to everyone.** Build transparency and trust with stakeholders and team members so that cleanup work is recognized as a fundamental part of the job rather than something that requires special permission.
* **The ultimate "Silver Bullet" for software productivity** lies in viewing code and people as one inseparable, interconnected system rather than focusing solely on engineering tools and processes

title: Evolutionary Software Architectures
description: Neal Ford introduces **evolutionary architecture** as a framework for building software that supports **continual, incremental change across multiple dimensions**, enabling systems to thrive within the unpredictable "dynamic equilibrium" of the modern technology ecosystem.
notes:
Software architects should prioritize building systems that support **continual and incremental change** across multiple dimensions to effectively navigate the industry's inherent "dynamic equilibrium," where unexpected technological shifts are inevitable. By favoring **reactive flexibility over rigid predictive planning** through practices like continuous delivery, fitness functions, and domain-centric team alignment, organizations can transform their ability to evolve into a sustainable competitive advantage.

*   **Dynamic Equilibrium:** Software development exists in a state of constant shift where unexpected technologies (e.g., Docker) can render long-term plans obsolete. Architectures must be built to accommodate this change rather than trying to predict the future.
*   **Definition of Evolutionary Architecture:** An architecture that supports **continual and incremental change** as a first principle among **multiple dimensions** (technical, data, security, and domain).
*   **Dimensions of Evolvability:** While traditional layered architectures typically only allow evolution along the technical dimension, evolutionary architectures (like microservices) allow for evolution across many independent dimensions simultaneously.
*   **The Domain Perspective:** Moving toward a domain-centric view (inspired by Domain-Driven Design) allows for "bounded contexts" where implementation details are hidden, making it easier to change specific business areas without ripple effects.
*   **Incremental Change via Continuous Delivery:** Evolutionary systems separate **deployment from release**. Techniques like feature toggles and side-by-side deployments allow for testing new features in production without disrupting existing users.
*   **Decentralized Data & Governance:** Avoiding a single monolithic database reduces temporal coupling. Each service should manage its own data context, allowing teams to choose the "right-sized" technology stack for their specific problem.
*   **Proactive/Reactive vs. Predictive:** Rather than over-engineering for a predicted future, architects should use interfaces to delay decisions and remain reactive to real-world changes.
*   **Bring the Pain Forward:** Based on Extreme Programming (XP) principles, engineering friction (like integration or deployment) should be addressed early and often through automation to prevent "pain" from increasing exponentially.
*   **Inverse Conway Maneuver:** Organizations should structure their teams to reflect the architecture they want to achieve. Building cross-functional, domain-aligned teams leads to more evolutionary, domain-centric architectures.
*   **Architectural Fitness Functions:** These are automated metrics or tests applied to architectural elements to ensure the system evolves in a desired direction and maintains critical characteristics (like performance SLAs) over time.
*   **Hypothesis-Driven Development:** An evolutionary architecture acts as an experimentation enabler. It allows businesses to run experiments, conduct AB testing, and pivot based on real-time feedback rather than following a static backlog.
*   **Coupling as an Impediment:** Heavily transactional systems, "big ball of mud" designs, and coding via configuration (e.g., Enterprise Service Buses) create high coupling that prevents evolution.

title: DuckDB and Python: Ducks and Snakes living together
description: This Talk Python episode explores DuckDB, a fast, in-process analytical database, and its cloud companion MotherDuck, highlighting their capabilities and use cases in Python and data science workflows.
notes:
DuckDB is to analytics what SQLite is to transactions: an in-process, zero-dependency database you `pip install` and embed in your Python process, with a columnar, vectorized engine that runs big aggregations and joins — even larger-than-RAM, terabyte-scale on a laptop — blazingly fast.

- In-process, not in-memory: pre-compiled wheel, zero dependencies; runs anywhere Python runs (laptop, Lambda, CI, browser via Wasm, even iOS/Android). Hit 1.0 in 2024 with a stable, ~5-year-compatible file format.
- Columnar + vectorized: processes ~2,000 rows at a time to use CPU caches well while conserving memory; ~5x compression vs row stores; built for big bulk aggregations and joins, not many tiny transactions.
- Handles larger-than-memory data by streaming from disk and spilling intermediates — "solve 1TB problems on your laptop."
- Tight data-science interop: results to/from pandas, Polars, Arrow (often zero-copy), NumPy, even TensorFlow/PyTorch tensors; can run SQL directly over a DataFrame variable by reading Python locals — no explicit hand-off.
- A universal reader: queries Parquet/CSV/JSON (best-in-class CSV sniffer) and even live Postgres/MySQL/SQLite/BigQuery without importing first (data virtualization); reads and writes directly on S3-style object stores, fetching only the files/bytes a query needs.
- Friendlier SQL: e.g. GROUP BY ALL infers the grouping columns; auto-creates tables and infers schema from a query or file; full typing (unlike SQLite), nullable by default, with a union type for mixed columns.
- Concurrency trade-off: single-process for read-write (multi-threaded within it), multi-process fine read-only. For full multi-user/cloud, MotherDuck wraps DuckDB as a serverless warehouse that can split one query between laptop and cloud.
- Governance: MIT-licensed and owned by a non-profit DuckDB Foundation, so a "rug pull" is structurally impossible; an extension ecosystem lets you add capabilities.
- "Big data era is over" thesis: laptops and single big nodes are fast enough that ease of use, not scale, is what matters — great for data apps and for replacing slow Postgres/DBT analytical jobs (hours to minutes).

title: Simplify the stack
description: Good talk about simplicity and how you can use "Requests For Comments" to discuss design ideas.

title: Improving Observability and Testing In Production
description: In this episode, Dave explores what Testing in Production is, and how to Test in Production, and how this is related to working more experimentally.
notes:
Testing in production should be viewed as an essential extension of a continuous delivery strategy that uses observability and monitoring to close the vital feedback loop on a software's real-world value. By treating production data as a series of scientific experiments, teams can move beyond mere monitoring to gain actionable insights that inform better decision-making and software design

* **Testing in production is a supplementary practice** intended to be used in addition to—not as a replacement for—a comprehensive automated testing strategy.
* **The "definition of done" includes delivering value to users**, meaning a feature isn't truly complete until its real-world impact is verified in production.
* **Monitoring and observability function as tests** when they utilize defined alerts and service level objectives (SLOs) to determine if a system is operating within a "pass" range.
* **Testing thoroughness is contextual**, where higher risks (e.g., life-critical systems or massive user bases) require more care and rigor before and during release.
* **Production is the ultimate "truth" of a system**, as it reveals human mistakes and complex interactions that controlled environments may not capture.
* **Continuous smoke tests and health checks** are essential production tests that verify system availability and the ability of components to communicate.
* **Encoding system understanding into monitoring filters** helps teams identify specific problem patterns and trigger appropriate automated or manual responses.
* **Production data validates user value**, providing the only definitive way to know if features are useful or if users find ideas valuable.
* **Experimental rigor requires controlling variables**, which is best achieved by releasing changes in small, isolated steps.
* **A/B testing and cohort selection** are necessary in high-scale environments to avoid skewed results and ensure statistical significance.
* **Being data-driven means replacing uninformed guesses** with evidence-based decisions, even while acknowledging that production data is often "fuzzy".
* **Closing the feedback loop in production** allows teams to answer critical questions about the reality of their software that cannot be addressed elsewhere.

title: Keynote: Why web tech is like this
description: Interesting talk about the evolution of web technology. The talk includes interesting demos.
notes: The history of web technology demonstrates that while complexity tends to increase over time, the most significant innovations often come from individual engineers who solve immediate problems with creative, practical solutions. Understanding that modern tools frequently reinvent and refine concepts from the past empowers developers to recognize their own agency in shaping the future of the web. * **Origins of the Web:** Tim Berners-Lee invented the web at CERN in the late 1980s as a way to link and manage research notes through hypertext. * **Early Adoption Hack:** To force adoption, Berners-Lee put the CERN phone book online, making the web server a necessary tool for staff. * **Saving the Web:** Intern Nicola Pello saved the project by creating a text-mode browser, allowing the web to run on computers other than the rare "Next" machines used for the original prototype. * **Browser-Driven Standards:** Early web standards were often set by browser creators (like Netscape's Mark Andreessen) implementing features (like the `` tag) unilaterally, forcing standard bodies to follow their lead. * **Scripting Birth:** PHP was released in 1995 as a dedicated web scripting tool, while JavaScript was famously created in just 10 days in 1996 to add simple interactivity to Netscape. * **Early Accessibility:** Web accessibility became a legal requirement as early as 1992 (Australia) and 1998 (US), leading to the creation of the Web Accessibility Initiative (WAI) and the WCAG guidelines. * **ASP.NET & Desktop Patterns:** Released in 2002, ASP.NET attempted to make web development feel like desktop programming (Windows Forms) by using "ViewState" (base64 blobs) to preserve UI state across requests. * **The Rails Revolution:** Ruby on Rails (2004) introduced critical concepts like Model-View-Controller (MVC), "convention over configuration," and database migrations to mainstream web development. * **AJAX & Web 2.0:** Google Maps (2005) shifted expectations by showing that background requests (AJAX) could create smooth, no-reload user interfaces. * **Modern DevTools:** The standard design for browser developer tools (inspectors, consoles, network traces) originated with the third-party Firefox plugin "Firebug". * **The Mobile Shift:** The 2008 iPhone launch ended the era of "mobile-only" sites, forcing developers to adopt responsive and mobile-first design. * **Node.js Concurrency:** Node.js (2009) brought JavaScript to the server, using an event loop to handle many concurrent connections without the complexity of multi-threading. * **The Rise of SPAs:** Frameworks like Backbone, Knockout, and Angular (2010) introduced disciplined architecture for building complex Single Page Applications in the browser. * **TypeScript Success:** TypeScript solved the scalability issues of large JavaScript codebases by adding a proper type system, becoming one of the world's most popular languages. * **WebAssembly:** Launched in 2017, WebAssembly broke the JavaScript monopoly in the browser, allowing languages like C# (.NET/Blazor) to run natively. * **Meta-Frameworks:** Modern tools like SvelteKit unify server, client, and build systems to automate complex tasks like code splitting and hybrid rendering. * **Future Trends:** Emerging trends include browser simplification (native CSS nesting), the removal of bundling needs (HTTP/2 and 3), and "edge computing" to deliver apps globally with minimal latency.

title: Keynote: Systems Thinking
description: A great introduction to systems thinking.
notes:
Software development is as much about human interactions as it is about code, requiring a transition from blaming individuals to understanding and influencing the **complex sociotechnical systems** governed by balancing loops, reinforcing loops, and critical thresholds. By learning to **zoom out** and recognize these systemic patterns, developers can move beyond treating symptoms and begin to intentionally shape the broader environment in which they work.

* **Software development as a sociotechnical system**: Engineering is as much about human interactions and organizational forces as it is about writing code.
* **Seniority and Perspective**: A hallmark of maturing as a developer is the ability to "zoom out" to see the broad system and "zoom in" to handle specific technical details.
* **Balancing (Inhibiting) Loops**: These patterns maintain stability and keep things the same. Examples include automated tests, code reviews, and fault-tolerant mechanisms like service restarts.
* **Reinforcing Loops**: These are cycles where an effect causes more of itself. They can be virtuous (e.g., more deploys leading to smaller, safer changes) or vicious (e.g., adding people to a late project increasing coordination costs and causing further delays).
* **System Thresholds**: Small changes can lead to massive, sudden effects when a system hits a threshold, such as resource exhaustion (disk space/threads) or team size limits, often tripping a balancing loop into a destructive reinforcing loop.
* **Blaming Humans as a Red Flag**: When an individual is blamed for a failure, it usually indicates an underlying systemic issue. Solutions should focus on the forces acting on the person rather than personal discipline.
* **Influence vs. Control**: Developers do not control complex systems from the outside; they are participants within the system who can only influence its behavior by identifying and redirecting internal loops.
* **The DevOps Virtuous Cycle**: Increasing deployment frequency naturally leads to smaller change sets, which makes deployments safer and builds the confidence necessary to deploy even more frequently.
* **Vicious Cycles in Code**: Complicated code tends to attract more complicated code (preferential attachment), leading to a power-law distribution where specific modules become increasingly unmanageable.
* **Decoupling Rates of Change**: A powerful system strategy is to allow different parts of a system to move at different speeds, separating fast-moving experimental areas from stable, high-consequence core systems.
* **Contextual Responsibility**: Rigid mandates (like "document everything") fail because they ignore context. Effective systems rely on the freedom of participants to exercise responsibility based on the specific needs and audience of their work.

title: Using sagas to maintain data consistency in a microservice architecture
description: This talk explains how to maintain data consistency in microservice architectures using **Sagas**, a sequence of coordinated local transactions that replace traditional, tightly-coupled distributed transactions.
notes:
To maintain data consistency in microservice architectures where distributed transactions are no longer viable, developers should implement **sagas**—a sequence of local transactions coordinated through **asynchronous messaging** and explicit compensating logic. This model allows for reliable, eventual consistency across services while preserving the **loose coupling** and high availability essential for independent team autonomy and system scalability.

*   **Data Consistency Challenge**: In a microservice architecture, each service must have its own private database to ensure loose coupling and autonomous development. Traditional distributed transactions (2-phase commit) are not viable because they reduce availability, create tight coupling, and are unsupported by many modern NoSQL databases and message brokers.
*   **The Saga Pattern**: A saga is a sequence of local transactions. Instead of one large distributed transaction, each step of a saga is a local ACID transaction within a single service. Once a step completes, it triggers the next step in the sequence.
*   **Compensating Transactions**: Unlike ACID transactions, sagas do not have automatic rollbacks. If a step fails, the system must explicitly execute "compensating transactions" to undo the effects of all previously completed steps.
*   **Coordination Mechanisms**:
    *   **Choreography**: Participants exchange events and decide their next actions independently. This can lead to cyclic dependencies and scattered logic.
    *   **Orchestration**: A centralized "Saga Orchestrator" (often modeled as a state machine) tells participants which operations to execute. This provides better separation of concerns and easier tracking.
*   **Asynchronous Messaging**: Sagas should rely on reliable asynchronous messaging rather than synchronous protocols like REST. This ensures that the transaction eventually completes even if some services are temporarily unavailable.
*   **Transactional Outbox Pattern**: To ensure atomicity between updating a database and sending a message, services should use an "outbox" table. The message is inserted into the database as part of the local transaction, ensuring the message is only sent if the database update succeeds.
*   **Transaction Log Tailing**: A highly reliable method for moving messages from the outbox table to a message broker is monitoring the database's commit log (e.g., MySQL binlog or DynamoDB Streams). This avoids the performance issues and complexities of polling the database.
*   **User Experience Considerations**: Because sagas are asynchronous, the UI may need to handle "pending" states. This can be managed by having the client poll for status updates or using WebSockets to push notifications once the saga finishes.

title: Competing On The Basis Of Speed
description: Optimizing for speed, flow, and waste reduction. Lean Software Development
notes:
To achieve a decisive competitive advantage, organizations must prioritize speed by building quality directly into the development process to prevent defects, maintaining simplicity to allow for constant change, and strictly limiting work-in-process to ensure a rapid, reliable flow of value to the customer. This shift from traditional batch-and-test methods to lean principles effectively reduces costs, shortens time to market, and creates a sustainable leadership position in rapidly changing markets.

* **Speed as a Competitive Advantage**: Competing on speed provides a significant cost advantage—sometimes up to 50% lower than peers—and creates a formidable barrier to entry in rapidly changing markets.
* **Three Killers of Speed**: The primary obstacles to speed are waste (non-value-adding activities), inconsistency (unbalanced or irregular processes), and overload (excessive or unreasonable burdens).
* **Simplicity and Change Tolerance**: Sustaining speed requires a common underlying infrastructure and continuous refactoring to keep the code base clean. Because most software development occurs after the initial release, the process must anticipate and tolerate constant change.
* **Decision-Making Strategy**: To maintain agility, maximize the number of reversible decisions. Irreversible decisions should be deferred to the "last responsible moment" to ensure they are made with the most complete information possible.
* **Set-Based Design**: Rather than iterating on a single solution, explore a range of options simultaneously (set-based design). Preparing multiple alternatives for a decision point ensures a solution is always ready on time and reduces the risk of costly rework.
* **Build Quality In**: Quality must be built into the process, not tested in at the end. Detecting and fixing defects the moment they occur is essential for speed; a process that routinely finds defects during a separate QA phase is considered defective.
* **Automated Testing Matrix**: Testing should be categorized into unit tests, acceptance tests, usability tests, and specialized tool-based tests (like security). Unit and acceptance tests must be automated to prevent "regression deficit," where testing time grows to dominate the release cycle.
* **One-Piece Flow**: The goal of lean development is to move small feature sets through the system rapidly. This avoids the high interest rates of "technical debt" and the complexities of merging long-lived unsynchronized code branches.
* **Nested Synchronization**: Implement a structure where unit tests fire every few minutes, acceptance tests run daily, and deployment-ready code is produced every iteration.
* **Queuing Theory and WIP**: Reducing cycle time is most effectively achieved by driving down the amount of work in process (WIP) rather than simply trying to increase the completion rate.
* **The Necessity of Slack**: Driving resource utilization to 100% causes cycle times to skyrocket. Maintaining slack (aiming for roughly 80% utilization) allows the system to remain responsive and fast.
* **Honest Queues**: Limit work to actual capacity by maintaining short, "honest" queues. It is better to refuse work that cannot be handled than to maintain a long backlog of tasks that will likely never be completed.
* **System Measurements**: Success should be measured through three lenses: cycle time (process capability), financial delivery (business return), and customer satisfaction (sustainability).

title: Charity Majors on Observability, OTEL, AI ops, DevOps and Friday deploys
description: This podcast episode features Charity Majors discussing the evolution of observability from her experiences at Parse and Facebook to co-founding Honeycomb, emphasizing its event-based approach for understanding complex systems and contrasting it with traditional metrics and logging, while also covering topics like Open Telemetry, DevOps, and engineering culture.
notes:
Here are the most important points of the presentation by Charity Majors:
- **The Limitations of Traditional Metrics and Logging:** Charity emphasizes that relying solely on metrics and aggregates makes it difficult to understand complex, high-cardinality systems, especially multi-tenant platforms. She recounts how at Parse, they often struggled to pinpoint root causes, spending significant time trying to correlate metrics and logs.
- **The Power of Event-Based Observability:** The introduction of Facebook's "Scuba" tool, which allowed slicing and dicing on high-cardinality dimensions and wide events in real time, was a game-changer for understanding system behavior at Parse. This experience led to the realization that being able to treat every request as an event and analyze it with arbitrary dimensions was crucial for effective debugging and problem-solving. This was the foundational idea behind Honeycomb, which Charity co-founded.
- **Observability as "Business Intelligence for Systems":** Charity describes observability as a way for engineers to ask and answer questions about their systems that they didn't know to ask beforehand, similar to how business analysts use BI tools. This contrasts with traditional monitoring, which focuses on pre-defined metrics and known failure modes.
- **The Evolution and Broad Applicability of Observability:** Initially thought to be a niche solution for platforms with high cardinality, Honeycomb discovered that the need for observability is widespread and increasing due to the growing complexity of modern systems, including microservices, containers, and polyglot persistence.
- **Open Telemetry as a Positive Industry Standard:** Despite initial cynicism, Charity is now enthusiastic about the success of Open Telemetry (OTEL) as a vendor-neutral standard for instrumenting code and collecting telemetry data. The goal of OTEL is to allow engineers to instrument their code once and then use various backend observability providers without re-instrumentation, reducing vendor lock-in. While noting that not all vendors have been equally cooperative (specifically mentioning Datadog's reluctance to fully embrace OTEL), she sees it as a significant step forward for the industry.
- **Rethinking DevOps and Ownership:** Charity believes the traditional Dev/Ops divide was a temporary solution to increasing system complexity and advocates for engineers owning their code in production to ensure a tight feedback loop. The ability to deploy frequently, ideally as soon as code is merged, is crucial for this ownership and for quickly identifying and resolving issues. Deploying on Fridays, when done with proper engineering judgment (having time to monitor and verify), is presented as a "smell test" for the health of a team's deployment practices.
- **The Second Half of an Engineering Career:** Charity advises engineers in their 30s and 40s to focus on self-awareness and fulfillment, emphasizing that career progression beyond senior developer doesn't necessarily mean management. She encourages exploring different technologies, industries, and roles while prioritizing autonomy, mastery, and purpose in their work.
- **AI and Observability for LLMs:** Honeycomb is applying observability principles to the realm of Large Language Models (LLMs), recognizing that traditional testing methods are insufficient for these systems. Their "Query Assistant" uses LLMs to help users build Honeycomb queries. Charity highlights that developing with LLMs reinforces good software engineering practices like testing in production, frequent deployments, and the need for robust observability. She sees observability for LLMs as a potential "Trojan horse" for wider adoption of modern development practices.
- **The Importance of Engineering Culture and Teamwork:** Charity emphasizes that building a strong engineering culture that encourages learning from failures through blameless retrospectives and prioritizes psychological safety is crucial for team success. She believes in hiring good teammates who can improve together, rather than solely focusing on brilliant individual engineers.

title: Complexity is Outside the Code
description: The presentation discusses the complexity of modern software development, moving beyond simple "box arrow cylinder" architectures to a landscape of diverse services and technologies1. It emphasizes the importance of minimizing lead time to business impact rather than focusing solely on code, and suggests that teams should embrace con	tinuous learning, research and experimentation to manage uncertainty and deliver value
notes:
Dan North's Code Mesh London 2014 talk argues that the real difficulty in modern software has moved out of the code itself and into the surrounding system: the data, the third-party tools, the integrations, the deployment pipeline and the team's collective knowledge. His central thesis is that our actual job is "sustainably minimizing lead time to business impact," and that achieving it requires treating research, learning and exploration as first-class work rather than something we squeeze in around feature delivery. He uses a recurring metaphor of "bats" flying at you (problems arriving) that you must hit with the right "implement" (tool/technology), and warns that grabbing tools with a quick time-to-Hello-World only delays the pain. The talk ranges across estimation, surfacing uncertainty, double-loop learning, and building teams with distributed expertise.

* **Complexity has moved outside the code**: North's framing is that "now that the complexity is outside, it's that that we need to be able to change and rearrange to keep our line straight or even accelerating." The hard part is no longer the program but the data, tools, integrations and context around it.

* **Estimating is not a learnable skill, so give a range**: "We are the only industry on the planet who doesn't give a range when we're uncertain. If in doubt, give a range." Saying "about half a month to two months" rather than "27 developer days" communicates huge variance, deliberately imprecise units, and prompts a real conversation with stakeholders instead of a fake-precise number.

* **Surface uncertainty before banging out features**: The goal is to make stakeholders "just as nervous, just as terrified as I am," because the right first work is to understand delivery risk and narrow the range. As you surface uncertainty the range both shrinks and shifts, occasionally bumping back out when "you'll get a really nasty surprise."

* **The R in R&D is first-class work**: Research, discovery, exploration and experimentation are real work, "even in business development." He contrasts this with Scrum's PBR (product backlog refinement), where "you pretend that you can do all the analysis for a two or three-week period magically in one room, in about an hour" and then "everything after that is just typing."

* **Leverage increases risk**: Teams expecting to "leverage all these new technologies and produce features super fast" instead saw their delivery curve flatten, because "as soon as we hit scale, the problems overwhelmed our capacity to deal with them, and then we don't get to implement features anymore."

* **Wrong-tool example — Elasticsearch as a key-value store**: They decided early "we need to store a whole lot of data, we'll just put it in Elasticsearch" when they "actually just need a key-value store" and weren't even doing full-text search. While building the right tool they kept "suffering from bat bites" — getting hit by the problem they were mis-handling.

* **Most people don't have a big data problem**: His phone is "a quad-core supercomputer that also makes phone calls" with 80 GB of storage; his first VLDB in '92 ("Janus Towers") had 18 GB across two external disc bays. Most big-data work at a very large bank he advises "will actually run on my phone," yet teams reach for Hadoop because "Hadoop is big and impressive."

* **Quick time-to-Hello-World is a trap; this is research, not a spike**: Tools like Docker and Elasticsearch "on Amazon" let you grab an off-the-shelf solution, but "you don't run up against their limitations later, and you don't realize that they don't kill all the bats you thought they did, or they're different bats." Real research means constantly re-evaluating both the tech and its changing context.

* **Double-loop learning (Chris Argyris) extends Kaizen**: The inner loop improves your process; the outer, second loop asks "if you've even got the right process." Applied to teams, the question becomes "how do we change the team such that we can better face off to the work?" — via learning, teaching and cross-skilling. The aim is not for everyone to be good at everything, but "sufficient capability in all the things we care about."

* **Fixed cost, unlimited upside on research tasks**: Unlike features (fixed value, "long tail of uncertainty" on cost descending "into the rat hole of surprises"), timeboxed research has fixed cost and a "long tail of opportunity." Run five or six, most fail, one pays off hugely. IBM institutionalized this in the 1950s with technical grades mirroring management grades up to the "research fellow" (VP-equivalent) — producing Ted Codd (relational database), Benoit Mandelbrot, and Bill Joy: "Fixed cost, unlimited opportunity."

* **The Python-vs-stored-procedure story**: On a SQL Server "classic systems" team, an hours-long stored procedure (grown "one IF at a time") matched ~5–6 million trade rows in about 90 minutes. One Friday afternoon, pairing with a domain expert, North wrote a Python equivalent in two hours that produced identical results in five minutes; others got it to three minutes and into production. It became the template for killing off similar procedures, turning "a sleepy little backwater team" into "the cool kids doing stuff with Python."

* **Our real job is far more than programming**: "If you are focused on programming and that's all you do all day, you're probably doing it wrong." Delivering business impact takes network, programming, tests, monitoring and instrumentation. Specializing "works about as well as a layered architecture" — "when your job is to deliver to QA, that's what you'll do, you'll pass QA tests" rather than achieve the real outcome.

* **Distributed expertise like choosing causes in environmentalism**: You can't care about carbon, plastic bags and pandas all at once; "we just randomly pick a couple" and hope coverage emerges. Likewise a team can't master every tool, but can assign go-to people (e.g. "designated to actually study New Relic") so tools "don't start eating our face before we figure out that it was the wrong tool." Coverage is built in layers: what people are passionate about, then what they'd be willing to learn, then the leftover nobody wants (e.g. the month of deployment "paperwork, admin, meetings") which a couple of people make "hurt less" with scripts that auto-populate the forms.

* **Make learning a first-class citizen on the board**: "There should be some Post-it notes of each color in process. You should always be delivering some code, but you should always be learning, and you should always be exploring to surface uncertainty." Closing the full feedback loop ("turtles all the way down") is how a team learns to do its real job: "sustainably minimizing lead time to business impact... we can wield all the implements if we do it together."

title: Daniel Kahneman: "Thinking, Fast and Slow" | Talks at Google
description: Nobel Laureate Daniel Kahneman explores the dual-system model of the mind, distinguishing between **fast, intuitive "System 1"** thinking and **slow, deliberate "System 2"** reasoning to reveal how cognitive biases and heuristics shape our judgments and decisions.
notes:
The human mind operates through the interplay of a fast, automatic **intuitive system** and a slow, deliberate **reasoning system**. True expertise and reliable intuition only develop in **predictable environments** with immediate feedback, so it is essential to remain skeptical of your own subjective confidence in complex situations where your mind may be substituting difficult questions with easier ones.

* **System 1 (Fast/Intuitive):** Operates automatically and quickly with little or no effort and no sense of voluntary control. It generates impressions, feelings, and inclinations.
* **System 2 (Slow/Deliberate):** Allocates attention to the effortful mental activities that demand it, including complex computations. It is associated with the subjective experience of agency, choice, and concentration.
* **Intuition as Pattern Recognition:** Intuition is not a "magical" ability but rather the recognition of environmental regularities. It is essentially the same process as a child recognizing a "doggie" without knowing the specific cues used.
* **Conditions for Trusting Intuition:** Expert intuition can only be trusted if the environment is sufficiently regular to be predictable and if the individual has had extensive, reinforced practice with immediate feedback.
* **Substitution Mechanism:** When faced with a difficult or complex question, System 1 often answers a related, easier question instead, without the individual noticing the substitution.
* **Associative Coherence:** System 1 is a "machine for jumping to conclusions." It works to create the most coherent story possible from available information, often suppressing ambiguity and ignoring the quality or quantity of the evidence.
* **The Illusion of Confidence:** Subjective confidence is a feeling based on the "fluency" or coherence of the story created by the mind, rather than a diagnostic indicator of accuracy. A very coherent story can be built on very little information.
* **Limited Cognitive Resources:** Effortful mental activities (System 2) draw from a limited pool of resources. When those resources are occupied—such as by memorizing digits—self-control and deliberate reasoning are impaired.
* **Priming and Environmental Cues:** Subtle, unconscious cues in the environment (such as images of eyes or symbols of money) can significantly influence behavior, honesty, and social attitudes without the person's awareness.
* **Mental Shotgun:** The brain often computes more information than is strictly necessary for the task at hand, leading to "overflow" that can interfere with or bias the intended judgment.
* **Predictability Limits:** Experts are often no better than chance at long-term forecasting in "chaotic" or unpredictable domains (like political history or stock prices) because the environment lacks the necessary regularity for intuition to develop.
* **Educating System 2:** While System 1 is difficult to retrain, System 2 can be educated to recognize situations where intuitive errors are likely to occur, allowing for more deliberate and skeptical evaluation of one's own impressions.

title: Ops and Operability
description: Dan North explores **operability as a user experience for operations**, offering practical patterns to balance developer autonomy with the global consistency required to flow value sustainably.
notes:
Software success depends on treating **operability as a first-class user experience**, where developers take accountability for the supportability of their code by balancing local autonomy with global consistency. To achieve this, teams must build **observable and predictable systems** that prioritize the needs of operations as essential users, shifting the focus from merely "providing" infrastructure to enabling the continuous flow of value.

*   **The Shift in Bottlenecks:** While Agile and XP methods improved development speed, they shifted the systemic bottleneck to operations, which is tasked with maintaining governance, compliance, SLAs, and business continuity.
*   **Autonomy vs. Accountability:** True autonomy requires permission, skills, and resources, but it must be balanced with accountability to prevent technical fragmentation and divergence across teams.
*   **Contextual Consistency:** The goal is to achieve local autonomy and global consistency simultaneously. This means that given the same context and constraints, different teams should naturally arrive at similar technical decisions.
*   **The Smallest Amount of Advice:** Organizations should provide the minimum necessary guidance to prevent teams from making major errors without being overly prescriptive or draconian.
*   **MTTR over MTBF:** In large-scale distributed systems, failure is a statistical certainty. Focus should shift from Mean Time Between Failure (MTBF) to Mean Time To Recovery (MTTR), aiming for near-zero impact on users when a failure occurs.
*   **Designing for Failure:** Systems should be architected under the assumption that every component and stage of a process will eventually fail, using patterns like event sourcing or automated failovers.
*   **The Three Magic Questions of Incidents:** Effective incident management must quickly answer: What happened? Who is impacted? How can we reduce the impact?
*   **Logs as a User Interface:** Logs are first-class, read-only, append-only user interfaces. They should be designed for clarity, assuming the reader is a person under stress (e.g., at 4 AM) trying to diagnose a problem.
*   **Essential Log Data:** High-quality logs require unique correlation IDs (UUIDs) to track requests across distributed systems, both human-readable and machine-sortable timestamps, and a clear description of the "whole cause."
*   **Path to Production:** Automating deployment is insufficient if the manual gates, sign-offs, and organizational processes still take days. Improving the "value stream" requires understanding the entire journey from code to live feature.
*   **Component Table Stakes (Phone Home):** Every deployed component must provide a heartbeat to signal it is alive. This "phone home" mechanism should include basic status, dependencies, and configuration metadata.
*   **Operability as User Experience:** Operability is the emotional experience of the operations staff. Success is measured by reducing the frustration, resentment, and anger of those who must monitor and support the software.
*   **Ops as Enablers, Not Providers:** Operations should shift from being a "provider" of infrastructure (a bottleneck) to an "enabler" through self-service tools, "everything-as-text" configurations, and version-controlled environments.
*   **Flow vs. Resource Efficiency:** Organizations should optimize for flow efficiency (lead time and value delivery) rather than resource efficiency (keeping everyone 100% busy), as high utilization leads to infinite wait times.

title: OOP is Dead! Long Live OODD!
description: David West argues that what the industry calls OOP is dead — Java/C++ just reimplement the 1970s hierarchical program-structure-chart — and that the real value lives in Object Oriented Decomposition and Design: the thinking, not the language.
notes:
David West contends that "object-oriented programming" as practiced (Java, C++) is dead — not the ideas, but the reality: mainstream OO languages merely re-encode the 1970s hierarchical program-structure-chart (a master control module orchestrating afferent / transform / efferent modules), so an object becomes "just a program with a main and subroutines." What actually mattered — objects as domain simulation and as autonomous message-passing entities — got lost, and the real leverage is OODD: decomposition and design (the thought), since at the execution level every program is just a Turing machine.

- History lesson: Alan Kay coined "object-oriented" (and later said it should have been "message-oriented"); Smalltalk came out of Xerox PARC (Kay, Ingalls, Goldberg) and so impressed Jobs that Java was later commissioned to "kill Smalltalk."
- The classic OO features are mostly mistakes made to save machine space, not to express objects: classes (only Self lacks them), inheritance ("perhaps the absolute biggest mistake" — delegation is what objects actually need), multiple inheritance and friend declarations (the "Stroustrup rule": forgivable only once in a career), and abstract data types / typing.
- Well-designed OO systems were tiny: methods ≤ 5±2 lines, classes ≤ 7±2 methods, programs ≤ 10–15 designed classes — differences between paradigms show only at the aggregate level, never inside an individual module.
- Real OO would merge Simula's domain simulation with Self's prototypes; no commercial language supports it, so an elegant object model always pays a heavy tax mapping to relational databases or GUIs. The thought and design are what count, not the language.

title: Why you should have your own black box
description: TEDxLondonBusinessSchool
notes:
Matthew Syed argues that how we conceptualize success radically shapes the behaviors we deploy to achieve it, and that high performance is far more a psychological and cultural achievement than a matter of raw talent. Through the central metaphor of aviation's "black box," he shows that institutions which treat failure as a precious learning opportunity improve relentlessly, while those that treat mistakes as threats to status stagnate.

* **Fixed mindset vs growth mindset.** A questionnaire given to undergraduates, schoolchildren, fund managers and footballers reveals two views of success: one camp believes talent and aptitude dominate (the fixed mindset, prevalent in the West), the other emphasizes hard work, practice, systems, resilience and collaboration. Where you sit measurably changes your behavior.
* **Aviation as a growth-mindset industry.** Airlines hire talented people but realize talent isn't enough; they engage with data. Pilots voluntarily submit reports after near-misses, which are statistically analyzed to find systemic weaknesses before an accident even happens.
* **The black box as a learning device.** Every aircraft carries two near-indestructible boxes — one electronic data, one cockpit interaction — recovered from rubble and deconstructed so the same mistake never recurs.
* **The B-17 landing-gear example.** In the 1940s B-17s crashed inexplicably; a Yale psychologist found identical side-by-side switches for landing gear and flaps. Adding a wheel shape to one and a flap shape to the other gave them intuitive meaning, the crashes vanished, and ergonomics was born.
* **The safety payoff of institutional learning.** In 1912 more than half of US Army pilots died in peacetime crashes; decades of learning drove the rate to one crash per 8.3 million take-offs for major airlines in 2014.
* **Healthcare as a fixed-mindset culture.** Doctors have long expensive educations and are expected to be infallible, so a mistake is threatening, prompting defensiveness and cover-ups rather than asking how to change procedures.
* **The language of evasion.** Instead of reform, clinicians rationalize with "it was the patient's unusual symptoms," "a complication of the procedure," or "it's just one of those things" — removing any impetus to prevent future harm.
* **High blame suppresses information.** If clinicians fear being sued for honest mistakes, they won't be open about them. Fixed mindset plus high blame suppresses the very information that learning in a complex world requires.
* **The hard data of preventable error.** Per the Journal of Patient Safety, 400,000 people are killed every year in US hospitals by preventable error — two jumbo jets crashing daily. The problem isn't intelligence but a culture channeling energy toward self-justification.
* **Economists and ego.** High-reputation economists (by TV appearances) make the worst predictions, because when they err they construct ex-post rationalizations instead of revising assumptions; low-reputation ones adapt — a negative correlation between perceived talent and performance in fixed-mindset settings.

### Marginal gains: compounding improvement
* **Virginia Mason Hospital.** After a medication error they investigated and found near-identical labels, then changed labeling, color-coded wristbands, and standardized equipment dials. This openness cut insurance liability premiums by 74% and made it one of the safest hospitals in the world.
* **British cycling and Team Sky.** Sir Dave Brailsford built a growth-mindset culture, improving each component by ~1% (wind-tunnel bikes, diet, transporting mattresses for sleep, hand gel), compounding into three Tour de France wins in four years.

### Science as the ultimate example
* **The Scientific Revolution was psychological, not genetic.** Between the Greeks and the 17th century science achieved little (Bacon, 1620); the watershed came from a shift in mindset, not bigger brains.
* **Galileo and the high-blame culture.** Scholars who believed they had all the answers forced Galileo to recant — the same phenomenon seen in hospitals. Only when science treated anomalies and failures as the stage for change did progress run from Galileo to Newton to Einstein.
* **The mindset revolution in schools.** Children avoid raising hands for fear of looking ignorant; Syed wants to liberate them to ask questions and engage openly, recognizing that admitting you don't have all the answers is what orients the mind toward learning.

title: Distributed Sagas: A Protocol for Coordinating Microservices
description: JOTB17
notes:
Caitie McCaffrey, a distributed systems engineer at Twitter, presents Distributed Sagas, a protocol for coordinating microservices and making them more understandable. The core problem: moving from monolithic apps backed by one relational database (which handled all consistency, concurrency, and abstraction) to microservices and NoSQL traded strong consistency for availability and scale, smearing consistency logic across the whole architecture. Sagas restore reasonable consistency guarantees while keeping independent, composable services.

* **Feral concurrency control is the disease being treated**: borrowing Peter Bailis's term, McCaffrey describes the ad-hoc application-level logic developers hand-code to maintain integrity once they leave the relational model. Her travel-app example (booking hotels, cars, flights, then a composite "trips" feature) shows each service accreting failure-handling until it becomes an unmaintainable "Death Star" like Netflix's, Twitter's, and Uber's call graphs.
* **Why neither extreme works**: Google's Spanner gives ACID transactions globally but needs TrueTime (GPS + atomic clocks) and is still a CP system that sacrifices availability. Facebook's 2015 paper argues the opposite — users tolerate inconsistency over latency — so they don't adopt it at scale.
* **Two-phase commit exists but doesn't scale**: prepare/vote then commit/abort, but McCaffrey rejects it for hot paths — N-squared messages, coordinator is a single point of failure, throughput gated by the slowest node, locks held throughout. gRPC and Finagle deliberately don't implement it.

### The Saga heritage
* **Rooted in the 1987 Sagas paper**: a single-database paper solving long-lived transactions that held locks too long, by breaking one big transaction into a sequence of smaller ones where either all complete or compensating transactions amend a partial execution. The authors casually assumed it could be distributed, underestimating the difficulty.
* **A distributed saga = requests + compensating requests**: each pair (book hotel / cancel hotel, charge / refund) is one business-level action.

### Required properties
* **Requests must be idempotent and may abort**: asynchronous networks mean a missing response is ambiguous, so idempotency lets the coordinator safely replay until success; a normal request may abort.
* **Compensating requests must semantically undo, never abort, be idempotent, and be commutative**: "semantically" because they needn't restore exact prior state (a refund may leave line items; an erroneous email is corrected by a follow-up). They can't abort (must run to completion), idempotency allows replay, and commutativity (cancel-then-book equals book-then-cancel) makes the replay-and-undo dance safe when a delayed message finally arrives.
* **The guarantee**: once finished, either all requests completed, or a subset plus their compensating requests executed, leaving the system semantically consistent (no leaked reservations, no charge without a booking).
* **Explicit non-guarantee (no atomicity, no isolation)**: requests are visible before the saga completes, so a partial saga can block a third party until it later cancels. Acceptable for most work since we no longer live in an ACID world.

### Mechanics
* **A saga is a directed acyclic graph (DAG)**: McCaffrey's extension so causality can be expressed (book everything before charging). Car, hotel, flight run in parallel; payment runs last. Each node has a name, request, compensating request, and status, plus no-op start/end vertices.
* **The distributed saga log is the durable source of truth**: highly available (CP), since the saga can't progress without writing to it. It persists request data so requests can be reconstructed, and doubles as an audit trail.
* **The Saga Execution Coordinator (SEC) is deliberately unspecial**: a stateless process executing requests per the protocol. Unlike 2PC's coordinator it need not be the same process across a saga, isn't a true single point of failure (all state lives in the log), and recovery is just spinning up another machine.
* **Rollback recovery flips the DAG's edges**: on abort, the saga walks backward; for each node it queries the log — nothing ran (skip), start+abort (no compensation), start+end (compensate). For the ambiguous start-without-end case the only safe move is to force the request to succeed then immediately compensate it, which commutativity makes correct.

### Why it pays off
* **Complex code is isolated to one logical place**: instead of feral concurrency control scattered across services, the tricky failure-handling lives only in the SEC, returning services to true module design.
* **Services compose cheaply**: adding "trips" needs no new service code, just a new saga definition and front-end API, reusing existing services. The pattern extends to actors and is especially well-suited to serverless.
* **Practical adoption and caveats**: used on the Halo statistics service, Twitter's distributed build infra, and discussed with Uber. No production-ready generic implementation exists — only a "toy" prototype on her GitHub — because the SEC is hard to generalize. The protocol provides no atomicity, so use cases needing strong consistency (Twitter username allocation) are out of scope.

title: SE Radio Episode 221: Jez Humble on Continuous Delivery
Inside-Out TDDDD
description: Talk about joy and humanity in software development. The talk was primarily concerned with the effects of software craft on our emotional states and working relationships. Along the way, Amitai touched on Theory of Constraints, stable and unstable equilibria in work environments, a significant and deliberate omission from Scrum, my take on “legacy code”, applied empathy (never mentioned explicitly, merely woven in), and what agility looks and feels like when you’ve got it.
notes:
To achieve joyful and humane software development, professionals should recognize that **human relationships are the ultimate bottleneck** and use technical discipline—such as test-driven development and collaborative pairing—as a primary tool to strengthen those connections and build mutual trust. By focusing on technical skills to improve how people relate to both their code and one another, teams can transform "legacy" environments into adaptive systems that prioritize collective well-being and business agility.

* **Human Relationships as the Primary Bottleneck**: In any system of collaborative software development, human relationships act as the primary bottleneck constraining throughput. Easing this bottleneck requires honoring, strengthening, and growing these relationships rather than attempting to reduce reliance on them through specialization or controlled interactions.
* **Redefining Legacy Code**: Legacy code is defined as "valuable code we're afraid to change". This definition implies that "legacyness" is not a property of the code itself, but a property of the relationship between the code and the humans who interact with it.
* **Technical Excellence as a Foundation for Trust**: Technical practices like **Test-Driven Development (TDD)** and refactoring are tools to gain confidence and safety. These practices align the interests of managers and developers by providing visibility into the work and protection from production failures.
* **Scrum as an Interface Without Implementation**: Scrum sets up feedback loops but does not provide specific instructions for the software-specific parts of development. Its success depends entirely on technical implementation choices—such as how code is tested and delivered—which are often overlooked by those in charge.
* **The Definition of Agile**: Agility is the ability to change direction confidently. This is achieved by taking smaller steps, lowering the center of gravity, and focusing on balance rather than over-optimizing for a perfect first step.
* **Closing Feedback Loops to Reduce Waste**: The greatest waste in software development is the disconnect between human beings with needs. Rehabilitating broken feedback loops between developers and customers allows an organization to steer itself more effectively.
* **Team-Centric Work Assignment**: To create a more humane engineering culture, the default should be assigning work to the **entire team** rather than to individuals. Assigning work to individuals can lead to dysfunction, as it separates review and learning from the actual development process.
* **Safety for Non-Programmers**: A core mission of software improvement is to make the team environment safe for non-programmers. This is achieved by first improving the developers' relationship with their code, which ultimately allows for more reliable and frequent delivery.
* **Maintaining High Performance**: High-performing teams exist in an "unstable equilibrium". Maintaining this state against entropy requires ongoing, concerted, and shared effort from everyone involved.
* **Professional Growth Through Community**: Avoiding insularity is vital for developer growth. Organizations should prioritize community involvement, conference participation, and collaborative practices like pair or ensemble programming to remain connected to wider industry insights.

title: Sabotaging a Transformation
description: Real experiences on Agile transformations: common obstacles and strategies to overcome them. Specifically, it addresses: executive unawareness, lack of engagement, power shifts, and blame-oriented cultures.
notes:
Successful organizational transformation is fundamentally a social change that requires aligning executive leadership with ground-level developers to "sandwich" and bypass middle-management resistance while fostering co-location, full-stack competence, and a culture of continuous experimentation. To ensure these changes endure, leaders must actively mitigate sabotage by ignoring detractors and removing the change agent once the team has successfully internalized and taken ownership of the new processes.

*   **Transformation agents** often act as "tyrants" because significant social and organizational change is required to disrupt established habits.
*   **Speed as a Stressor**: Continuous delivery cycles that take 15 minutes to 2 hours create intense organizational stress because the surrounding business structure is often not ready to handle that pace.
*   **Active Saboteurs**: Opposition typically comes from individuals losing power, such as knowledge hoarders who want to remain unique experts or those who maintain status through bureaucratic sign-off chains.
*   **Process Mismatches**: Sabotage occurs when specialized departments (e.g., UX, architecture boards, or database analysts) insist on isolated, long-term workflows that create handoffs and slow down delivery.
*   **The Stalin Strategy**: Instead of arguing with a detractor, ignore them and build consensus with the rest of the group until the detractor is isolated and their opposition becomes irrelevant.
*   **The Sandwich Strategy**: Secure explicit buy-in from top-level executives and the engineers on the ground. This "squeezes" risk-averse middle management into compliance because they see the CEO's support for the change.
*   **Injecting Competence**: Place highly skilled experts into teams without giving them formal titles. They gain influence by pairing with others and demonstrating capability, effectively bypassing ineffective formal leaders.
*   **Strategic Departure**: A change agent should leave the organization once the new process is established to prevent becoming a rallying point for a "counter-culture" that wants to blame a single person for the disruption.
*   **Success Conservatism**: Beware of managers who halt innovation once "good enough" results are achieved because they become risk-averse and want to "lock in" the current success.
*   **Titles as Inhibitors**: Traditional, specialized job titles (e.g., "Back-end Developer") prevent full-stack collaboration. Organizations should move toward competence-based titles (Graduate, Developer, Senior) that reward technical breadth.
*   **Physical Proximity**: Communication effectiveness drops sharply with distance (The Allen Curve). Cross-functional teams, including UX designers and domain experts, must sit in the same immediate space to maximize productivity.
*   **Labeling as "Experiments"**: Frame new ideas or process changes as temporary experiments rather than permanent mandates. This makes it difficult for detractors to argue against them without appearing unreasonable.
*   **Killing Fear**: Identify and remove practices that generate fear, such as rigid estimations, which often lead to defensive behavior and lack of transparency.
*   **Agile Schizophrenia**: Avoid standardized, "by-the-book" agile across an entire company. Every team and problem is unique and should be allowed to morph its process to fit its specific needs.
*   **The "Pod" Model**: Use short, intensive engagements (e.g., 5 to 8 weeks) to jumpstart transformations by having external experts write actual production code alongside the client's team in their specific domain

title: Fearless Change: Patterns for Introducing New Ideas
description: Linda Rising shares proven change management patterns and strategies to help you effectively plant seeds of innovation and overcome organizational resistance to successfully introduce new ideas.
notes:
Effectively driving organizational transformation depends on applying **proven change management patterns** to overcome resistance and plant strategic seeds for new ideas. These strategies empower individuals to become **successful agents of change** by understanding the forces that block improvement and celebrating incremental successes in both professional and personal contexts.

- **Organizations often resist new ideas** even when they are discovered through reputable sources like conferences or books.
- Successful change agents use **proven patterns and strategies** to introduce innovation rather than relying solely on the merit of the idea itself.
- Transformation begins by effectively **planting "seeds of change"** to build initial interest and support for a new concept.
- Identifying and understanding the **specific internal forces that drive or block change** is critical for navigating organizational politics.
- These change management patterns are dual-purpose, applicable to both **organizational systems and personal effectiveness**.
- **Overcoming adversity and resistance** is a standard and expected requirement for anyone introducing innovation.
- **Celebrating improvement successes** is essential for solidifying new practices and maintaining momentum.
- The methodology emphasizes taking **"small safe steps"** to manage risks, build confidence, and reduce institutional friction.
- The ultimate goal is to **bridge the gap between discovering a new concept and successfully implementing it** within a real-world environment.

title: Nordstrom Innovation Lab
description: The Nordstrom Innovation Lab team uses a flash build to develop an iPad app for sunglass selection, incorporating real-time customer feedback. The team's agile process allows for rapid adaptation and feature implementation throughout the week, resulting in a functional app.
notes:
True innovation is achieved by building and refining products through rapid iterations and continuous feedback from real customers in their actual environment. This approach ensures that every development effort solves genuine problems and delivers immediate, tangible value.

* **Objective**: Build an iPad application to assist customers in choosing sunglasses by using real-time feedback in a live retail environment.
* **Flash Build Approach**: A software team set up in a flagship store to build and test an application in a surprise, high-traffic location.
* **Customer-Centric Development**: Prioritized delivering only features valued by the customer, ensuring no work was wasted on non-essential functions.
* **User Story Mapping**: Collaborative outlining of the customer journey and buying process to define required application features.
* **Paper Prototyping**: Used low-fidelity analog versions of the app to test usability and gather feedback before writing any code.
* **Rapid Feedback Loops**: Developed and delivered features in cycles as short as 10 minutes, allowing for immediate iteration based on real customer interactions.
* **Technical Troubleshooting**: Identified a hardware conflict where polarized sunglasses blacked out the iPad screen in portrait mode; resolved by locking the app in landscape orientation.
* **Iterative Feature Implementation**: Key features included side-by-side photo comparisons, zooming, photo renaming for organization, toggling between front and rear cameras, and a "new customer" reset button.
* **UX Refinement**: Added specific animations to clarify the transition between thumbnail views and the comparison screen based on user confusion.
* **Time-Boxing**: The project was strictly limited to a one-week development window to ensure focus on the most important problems.
* **Outcome**: Developed a functioning, intuitive tool ready for store implementation as either a professional selling tool or a public application.

title: The Limited Red Society
description: Joshua Kerievsky discusses the need to reduce “red” periods of time while developing software. One is in the red when he spends too much time designing, or having compilation errors or the tests do not pass. Kerievsky demonstrates a method (Parallel Change) of reducing the red while refactoring code, and discusses another approach called Narrowed Change, and answers refactoring related questions.
notes:
Based on the information presented in the provided sources, the most important points of the presentation titled "The Limited Red Society" are:
- The concept of the "limited red society" emphasizes the importance of limiting "red" in software development, which refers to failing tests, compilation errors, and the economic implications of these issues. This is presented as analogous to the well-known concept of limiting Work In Progress (WIP).
- Limiting "red" is crucial not only at the planning level but also at the coding level. The presentation argues that spending excessive time with failing tests or code that doesn't compile has negative consequences.
- Prolonged periods in the "red" (failing tests and compilation errors) are economically damaging because they prevent the delivery of working software, delay bug fixes, and lead to unhappy customers. The speaker uses the analogy of a car "up on blocks" to illustrate a system that is not functional.
- The presentation contrasts two refactoring sessions: one with a "big leap" approach that resulted in a significant amount of time spent in the "red" (64% of the session with failing tests) and another that employed techniques like "parallel change" to remain mostly "green" (passing tests) throughout the process.
- Techniques like "parallel change" (introducing new code alongside the old before switching over) and "graceful retreat" (reverting to a last known good state) are presented as strategies for limiting time in the "red" and managing risk during code changes.
- The ability to stay "green" and continuously deliver value is important even if customers cannot accept frequent releases, as it benefits the development team by ensuring the software is functional and progress is trackable.
- Frequent integration and small batches of work are encouraged as they make it easier to retreat gracefully and avoid prolonged periods of broken code.
- The presenter suggests the possibility of tracking programmer statistics, such as "time in the green," to gain insights into coding practices and the ability to limit "red".
- Maintaining a healthy codebase through refactoring, even before adding new features, is essential to avoid accumulating "technical debt" and ensuring the long-term maintainability and evolvability of the software.

title: Too busy for productivity
description: Carlin Daharsh challenges the societal glorification of busyness, advocating for a shift from constant activity toward intentional productivity and finding fulfillment in meaningful, focused contributions.
notes:
True productivity and fulfillment are achieved by rejecting the societal pressure to be constantly "busy" and instead focusing wholeheartedly on a few meaningful goals that align with one's true purpose. By prioritizing the quality of impact over a high quantity of commitments, individuals can realize their potential and drive significant change in their communities.

* **Societal addiction to "being busy"** creates a false sense of importance and impressiveness that often masks a lack of true productivity.
* **Busyness is not a badge of honor**; it is often a status symbol that leads to stress, anxiety, and unhealthy habits like skipping meals or losing sleep.
* **Busy does not equal productive**; true productivity is pushed aside when we prioritize the quantity of tasks over the value they add to our lives and communities.
* **Time is a universal equalizer**, as everyone is given the same 24 hours a day; impact is determined by how that time is prioritized rather than how much of it is filled.
* **Focus on one great achievement** rather than a million minor ones; most influential figures are known for a specific, concentrated impact rather than a long list of unrelated activities.
* **Prioritize self-fulfillment over societal expectations**; true happiness comes from identifying and pursuing what you personally value, such as being a catalyst for change, rather than following a path dictated by others.
* **Run your own race at your own pace**; the value of an achievement, such as a college degree or a fitness goal, remains the same regardless of how long it takes to reach it.
* **The world is run by those who show up**; meaningful change requires giving your wholehearted attention to the things you enjoy most.
* **Identify and overcome obstacles proactively** by seeking fellowship and managing priorities rather than succumbing to the feeling of being overwhelmed.
* **"Die with memories, not dreams"**—refuse to rush through life just to stay busy, and never settle for a version of success that doesn't align with your true purpose.

title: Virtual Machines, JavaScript and Assembler
description: Very fun talk. Also gives an interesting overview of current technology (cloud, web, etc.).
notes:
Scott Hanselman delivers a fast, comedic keynote that is really an argument about abstraction layers: how computing moved from physical hardware you fax someone to provision, up through virtual machines and platform-as-a-service in the cloud, and how the browser's JavaScript engine has quietly become the universal virtual machine we always wanted. The running device is a conversation with a veteran Intel hardware engineer who knows microcode cold but missed twenty years of the web, forcing Hanselman to re-explain the modern stack from first principles.

* **The "five computers" prediction came true.** The apocryphal quote about a world market for maybe five computers actually happened: the Google computer, the Rackspace computer, the Amazon computer, the Azure computer — giant 'refrigerators' you send batch jobs to, one per continent, became today's cloud regions.
* **Provisioning used to mean faxing a guy in Mountain View.** His Intel friend gets a VM by faxing a request and waiting weeks; Hanselman contrasts this with spinning up Linux in Azure in seconds — "that's the new web."
