* **VMs are a puppy; PaaS is trashing a hotel room.** A virtual machine must be kept alive, watered, patched — like maintaining your first house. Platform-as-a-service lets you treat the environment as disposable (trash the hotel room, come back to a clean one).
* **Scaling went from a weekend at Micro Center to dragging a slider.** Old scaling meant physically buying hardware all weekend; now the boss really does drag the slider. "It's magic — and it's all open source, any language you want."
* **The cloud lets you stop caring about the substrate.** Adrian Cockcroft (ex-Netflix), challenged that SSDs fail without TRIM, answers simply "I'm renting them, it's not my problem" — you buy performance per dollar and let the provider own the hardware risk.
* **The web started as a dumb terminal talking to a refrigerator.** All work happened in the mainframe; the screen was a VT100. Tim Berners-Lee's first web page still lives at its original URL.
* **Plugins were failed attempts to build an OS inside the browser.** Java applets, then Flash/YouTube, were 'islands of activity' embedding a VM and OS inside another universe — not what Berners-Lee wanted.
* **JavaScript quietly became the plugin-free virtual machine.** He points to an in-browser Linux emulating an x86 processor entirely in JavaScript — you can compile hello world with a real C compiler in it. Absurd nesting (an iPhone simulator running mobile Safari running a JS VM) becomes possible.
* **Atwood's Law as the thesis.** Map JavaScript onto the characteristics of an operating system and you can argue JavaScript *is* an OS — the VM we always asked for, requiring no plugins. Therefore any application that can be written in JavaScript eventually will be (demonstrated with Pac-Man in Excel cells).
* **JavaScript is the assembly language (x86) of the web.** A line he says he confirmed with Brendan Eich. The stack is HTML as structure (just divs now), CSS for style, and JavaScript doing all the real work.
* **Beware too many abstraction layers.** Don't let abstractions stack up adding complexity because you think you're slick — it doesn't quite work out, and you won't even know whose fault it is. He nudges toward 'vanilla JS' and expecting more from the tools.
* **The device in your pocket is a supercomputer.** The cloud gives scale and elasticity, but don't forget the client: every user carries a pocket supercomputer with an unused GPU — effectively one extra VM per user, on their own hardware. 'You know the cloud, get to work.'

title: Why Pull Requests Are A BAD IDEA
description: A great explanation of why working with PR can be a great idea for asynchronous and distributed teams working on open source, but a problem for product development teams. Related: <https://www.eferro.net/2021/09/code-reviews-synchronous-and.html>
notes:
High-performing software teams should prioritize **trunk-based development** and **synchronous review methods like pair programming** to ensure continuous integration, as asynchronous pull requests often create bottlenecks that delay feedback and hinder delivery speed. Statistically, integrating code changes at least once per day is essential for maintaining high quality and stability, making it vital to move away from slow, gatekeeping processes.

* Software development is inherently complex and requires a cautious, evidence-based approach rather than relying on intuition or "luck."
* **Continuous Integration (CI)** is defined by integrating code into the main trunk at least once per day to maintain a clear view of how changes interact with the whole system.
* Data from the **State of DevOps (DORA) report** demonstrates that trunk-based development predicts higher throughput, better stability, and lower rates of burnout.
* **Pull Requests (PRs)** create a tension with CI/CD because they inject slow, asynchronous human bottlenecks into the feedback cycle.
* The delay caused by waiting for PR approvals often forces developers to **batch changes** on long-lived feature branches, which fundamentally breaks the CI process.
* PRs were originally designed as a **gatekeeping mechanism** for open-source projects where trust is low; they are often inefficient for trusted, internal development teams.
* Asynchronous written feedback in PRs is **low-bandwidth** and less effective for teaching and coordination than real-time, interactive conversations.
* **Pair programming** is presented as a superior alternative, as it builds the code review process directly into the act of development.
* Pairs produce work with **significantly fewer defects** and are able to maintain a better focus than individuals working alone.
* Organizations should optimize for the **speed and accuracy of feedback** (the system's efficiency) rather than the perceived individual productivity of separate developers.

title: The Product-Led Journey
description: Interesting insights into the changes needed to become a product led company.
notes:
Becoming a product-led organization requires shifting from a delivery-focused mindset to one where design and technology are central drivers of sustainable growth through empowered, cross-functional teams that learn as fast as they ship. This transformation succeeds when leadership moves from a commanding role to an enabling one, breaking down internal silos so that product thinking and collaborative strategy permeate every level of the company.

* **Definition of Product-Led**: It is the belief that design and technology are drivers for sustainable growth and "step changes," rather than just linear or incremental delivery. It focuses on empowering the creativity and potential of cross-functional teams.
* **The Evolutionary Journey**: Organizations typically move through stages of maturity: from siloed Waterfall to Agile and DevOps, through the "Feature Factory" and "Mini CEO" models, and finally reaching truly entrepreneurial cross-functional teams where all members share responsibility for identifying opportunities.
* **The Enabling Leadership Shift**: As teams mature and improve their product skills, the role of leadership must shift from a commanding, strategy-setting "Rockstar" style to an enabling style that focuses on coaching, humility, and removing obstacles.
* **Fractal Collaboration**: Cross-functional partnership must exist at every level. A common failure occurs when top leadership and frontline teams are cross-functional, but middle management remains siloed and manages for functional incentives rather than team goals.
* **Unified View of "The Product"**: Organizations must eliminate the dichotomy between "customer-facing" teams (seen as revenue centers) and "non-customer-facing" teams (seen as cost centers). Infrastructure, internal tools, and support are all part of the product and essential for sustainable innovation.
* **Learning vs. Shipping**: A primary indicator of a product-led organization is the ability to learn as fast as you ship. Success is defined by the ability to respond to new information and spend the necessary time on a mission because it is providing value, rather than just maximizing throughput.
* **Show, Don't Tell**: To influence traditional management, avoid theoretical arguments. Instead, use small experiments, usability tests, and data insights to demonstrate the reality of user behavior and the impact of the product.
* **Business Language Integration**: Product leaders must move beyond "product speak" and learn to communicate using business metrics, balance sheets, and ROI to align with executives and the C-suite.
* **Strategy Transparency**: Utilizing frameworks like the "North Star" helps ensure that every team in the organization can explicitly map their technical or internal work to actual customer impact and the overarching company strategy.
* **Patience and Persistence**: Shifting to a product-led model is a long-term journey without a fixed endpoint. It requires methodically putting cultural and technical foundations in place so the organization can capitalize on external catalysts.

title: Blame. Language. Learning. 3 tips for learning from incidents in your organisation
description: Lindsay Holmwood explores how **shifting from a culture of blame to one of inquiry**—by mastering language, identifying cognitive biases, and fostering psychological safety—enables organizations to truly learn from failure.
notes:
Organizations should transition from a **blame-centric view of human error** to a "**just culture**" that prioritizes understanding the systemic context and the "**local rationality**" of decisions made during incidents. By replacing blame-triggering "why" questions with "**what**" and "**how**" and actively neutralizing **hindsight bias**, teams create the safety necessary to uncover genuine learning and improve future resilience.

* **Shift from Blame to Inquiry**: Organizations should move away from a "bad apple" theory of human error and instead focus on an "up and out" approach that embraces systemic complexity and multiple perspectives.
* **Three Inhibitors of Learning**: The primary factors that prevent organizational learning are blame, language, and a lack of sharing.
* **Language Hierarchy**: Use "What" and "How" instead of "Why" and "Who." "Why" often triggers defensiveness and counterfactuals (e.g., "Why didn't you...?"), while "What" helps uncover reasoning and build empathy for those involved.
* **Local Rationality**: People make what they consider to be the best decisions given the information, goals, and constraints they have at the time. Investigations should seek to understand this "foresight" rather than judging with "hindsight".
* **Confirmation Bias**: This is the tendency to seek information that reinforces existing views. To counter it, organizations should appoint someone to take a contrarian or opposing viewpoint during incident reviews.
* **Hindsight Bias**: Known as the "culture killer," this bias distorts memory by judging past actions based on the known outcome. It must be relentlessly eliminated from post-incident investigations.
* **Pre-mortems**: Hindsight bias can be used as a predictive tool by having a team imagine a future failure and then "looking back" to predict what might have caused it before the launch.
* **Psychological Safety**: For sharing to occur, the environment must be safe, ensuring there are no repercussions for individuals who divulge information that might reflect poorly on them.
* **Leadership as a "Shield"**: Managers should act as a "shit umbrella," protecting their team members from blame to foster an environment where honest learning is possible.
* **Bridge the Gap**: Learning comes from understanding the chasm between "Work as Imagined" (how managers think work happens) and "Work as Performed" (how it actually happens at the front lines).
* **Tailored Reporting**: When documenting incidents, consider creating multiple reports for different audiences (e.g., engineering vs. executives) to provide the necessary context and detail for each group.

title: Modern Agile
description: Joshua Kerievsky's Agile2016 keynote introducing Modern Agile: four principles — make people awesome, make safety a prerequisite, experiment and learn rapidly, deliver value continuously — that favor results over rituals.
notes:
Modern Agile strips agile down to four guiding principles — make people awesome, make safety a prerequisite, experiment and learn rapidly, deliver value continuously — and argues today's practitioners should bypass outmoded rituals (fixed sprints, story points, velocity) in favor of results. It's open source, applies beyond software, and is grounded in lean, the Toyota Way and real-world cases.

- Agile has been missing direction: being agile in the wrong direction solves nothing — aim at making the whole ecosystem awesome (users, coworkers, stakeholders, managers), not just shipping features (Kathy Sierra: make your users awesome).
- Old agile (timeboxed sprints, story points, velocity) is the "training wheels" — Kerievsky moved to variable-length micro-releases and effectively no estimates years ago; Ron Jeffries even apologized for story points and "velocity is killing agility."
- The definition of done is broken when it's decided inside the building: only users telling you they get value means done; focus on outcomes, not story counts.
- Safety is the #1 driver of high performance: Google's Project Aristotle (via Amy Edmondson's psychological safety) found it the top attribute of great teams — encourage everyone to contribute, listen and repeat points, don't dominate, be curious/nonjudgmental.
- Make safety concrete: automated test safety nets, Stop Work Authority cards / Toyota's Andon cord, and "tailboarding" (analyzing hazards before work); blameless culture (Etsy's three-armed-sweater award; Seth Godin: people fear blame, not failure).
- Experiment and learn rapidly: Paul MacCready won human-powered flight by iterating a plane in hours not months; IMVU shipped "teleportation" in two days instead of a months-long Sims-like feature and customers loved it. Bullet Train thinking (set an "impossible" target and figure it out) à la the Japanese railway and Jack Welch.
- Deliver value continuously: continuous deployment (IMVU 50x/day; Yahoo moving QA→QE; Airbnb deploying on day two) makes shipping safe and tightens the feedback loop.
- Modern Agile maps onto the Toyota Way's two pillars (respect for people = the vertical make-people-awesome/safety axis; continuous improvement = experiment/deliver), and reframes the 2001 Agile Manifesto for a world beyond just software.

title: Everything about distributed systems is terrible
description: Interesting advice about the advantages of using system specs to reason in complex and distributed systems.
notes:
Building reliable distributed systems requires moving beyond just writing code to formally designing and model-checking system behavior, which uncovers complex, non-deterministic bugs that are otherwise nearly impossible to find. Using tools like TLA+ allows engineers to verify their designs against an exploding state space of possible failures, ensuring fundamental correctness before a single line of implementation is written.

* **Definition of Distributed Systems:** A system where multiple agents aim to preserve a global property (like consistency) while operating with localized information and facing the reality of partial failures.
* **The Root of Complexity:** The fundamental difficulty in distributed systems stems from concurrency (the non-deterministic order in which agents act) and non-determinism (unpredictable events like crashes or errors).
* **State Space Explosion:** Even in simple systems, the number of possible states and behaviors grows exponentially. This makes it impossible to reason through every scenario manually or via standard testing.
* **Safety vs. Liveness Violations:** Systems can fail through safety violations (reaching an invalid state, like no servers being online) or liveness violations (invalid behaviors over time, such as failing to reach eventual consistency).
* **Murphy’s Law at Scale:** In high-scale systems, any behavior that is possible—no matter how statistically unlikely—will eventually occur. A "one-in-a-billion" error can manifest every few minutes in a system processing high message volumes.
* **The Limitation of Code:** Code is an implementation detail, not a design. It provides no insight into the total state space or the various transitions and behaviors possible within a system.
* **Formal Specification (TLA+):** TLA+ (Temporal Logic of Actions) is a mathematical language used to model designs. It allows engineers to describe what a system should do precisely and concisely, focusing on high-level logic rather than implementation.
* **Model Checking and Error Traces:** Model checkers exhaustively search the entire state space of a specification for violations. When a bug is found, they produce an "error trace"—a step-by-step sequence of events showing exactly how to reproduce the failure.
* **Industry Application:** Beyond academic use, formal methods like TLA+ are used by major companies (AWS, Azure) and startups to verify critical infrastructure and document legacy systems.
* **Development Efficiency:** While it requires an upfront investment of a few days, formal specification can save approximately 25% of total development time by catching deep design flaws early and preventing late-stage rework or critical production outages.
* **Complementary Tool:** Formal specification does not replace good engineering, code reviews, or testing; rather, it ensures the underlying design is sound before the implementation begins.

title: Driving a Tech-led Reimagination of eBay Through DevOps (US 2021)
description: A very interesting session about eBay's strategy to improve delivery performance. A great example of engineering leadership.
notes:
eBay's "Velocity" initiative shows how to turn engineering speed into a competitive advantage: use value stream mapping to find the constraint (software delivery), measure with the DORA/Accelerate metrics, attack bottlenecks with hand-picked pilot teams and embedded experts, and break the product/infra silo by co-leading with fully shared goals. Pilot teams doubled their productivity.

- The problem: eBay was too slow versus competitors (Amazon, Walmart, Shopify); engineering velocity drives better customer experience, business results and happier, more engaged employees.
- A 3-month survey found systemic, accumulated issues — tech debt and monolithic code, low-quality staging, oversized PRs and long-lived branches, slow code reviews, yearly framework upgrades, and team dependencies forming distributed monoliths.
- Value stream mapping (idea→value) surfaced problems at every stage, but they applied laser focus to software delivery as the Theory-of-Constraints bottleneck: unlock it and everything else (architecture, experimentation) becomes possible.
- Measure with the four key metrics: pilot teams moved from medium to high performer — ~3x deployment frequency, 2.5x faster lead time, ~3x better change-fail rate, restore time held steady — and putting those metrics on a per-app dashboard ended the debate about whether the work was paying off.
- Run ~10 platform tracks plus pilot domains (selling, search, ads) covering ~10% of apps; pursue both quick wins (5 minutes/day per engineer adds up) and bigger, riskier rearchitecture (e.g. the high-traffic View Item page).
- Concrete nuts-and-bolts wins: a 60-minute build cut to 4 minutes, PR validation from 1h45 to 10 minutes, monthly mobile releases moved to weekly (smaller batches, less rushing), plus a healthy staging environment, canary deploys and feature flags.
- Break silos by design: the two presenters co-lead from the product and the tools/infra sides with entirely shared goals and metrics, meet daily, run a weekly Scrum of Scrums, embed senior architects in teams, and apply a tight plan-do-check-act loop; the unlock is framing it as "what stops you deploying daily?" and knocking impediments down.
- Lessons and challenges: team-level wins haven't yet moved overall eBay outcomes (eyeing Mik Kersten's Flow Framework); beware gaming/over-focusing on metrics and fear of failure; the hardest group to motivate is the middle managers. Strong CEO sponsorship (Jamie Iannone) was decisive. North Star: rolling planning with small cheap experiments, single-piece flow, one-hour commit-to-deploy.

title: Serverless Architectures
notes:
Mike Roberts, an engineer/manager with nearly 20 years in the industry (former ThoughtWorks, plus product and finance companies), gave this introductory talk on serverless architectures at Emerging Technologies for the Enterprise in Philadelphia. He started a serverless-focused consulting business at the beginning of the year after a serverless article he wrote was posted on Martin Fowler's site and became popular on Hacker News, and he has a short book on the topic coming out around June. His core thesis: serverless is the next economic evolution of the cloud, continuing the path of outsourcing infrastructural responsibility, but it arrives with a fundamental architectural "jolt." The promise he frames up front: "let other people do all your repetitive work for you," "only pay for your servers when they're actually doing something useful," and "make every day a hack day."

* **Serverless as cloud evolution**: He traces the cloud from AWS launching EC2 and S3 in 2006 (infrastructure as a service, renting VMs by the hour, lead time of minutes rather than months), Azure in 2010, Google Cloud in 2011, platform as a service from 2007 championed by Heroku, and containerization via Docker (2013) building on older LXC. Serverless continues "outsourcing more infrastructural responsibility to others."
* **Two classes of technology**: Serverless consists of Backend as a Service (BaaS) and Functions as a Service (FaaS). BaaS outsources actual parts of production applications (not just processes like SaaS does). Examples: Google Firebase (popular with mobile teams, descended from Facebook-acquired-and-closed Parse), Auth0 and Amazon Cognito for user login/management, plus Amazon S3 (which he calls "the original serverless service") and DynamoDB.
* **How FaaS works**: "Functions as a service is a new way of deploying server-side software, oriented around deploying individual functions or operations." You strip away the host and long-lived application process, deploy simple code units (zip files of JavaScript/Python, or Java jars). The platform creates an ephemeral container on demand when a configured event fires, runs the function, then is free to tear the container down.
* **Event sources**: FaaS depends on event types. AWS examples he gave: message buses (event-driven systems), network file system changes (file processing and data pipelines), time (scheduled cron-like tasks), and HTTP requests (web apps and web APIs). The term "serverless" arises because teams stop thinking about server machines, processes, and operating systems "even though there are servers running somewhere" — an abstraction layer like the one Scott described in his keynote.
* **Leading platform**: AWS Lambda, launched two and a half years ago, is "without a doubt, the most mature" FaaS offering. Google's is in beta; Microsoft's and IBM's are in production; IBM's is open source, and there are independent open-source implementations.
* **Five common traits**: (1) no management of server hosts or processes; (2) auto-scaling and auto-provisioning based on load; (3) costs based on precise usage — "With Lambda, we're paying for usage by the 100 milliseconds... 36,000 times more precise than EC2," and "zero usage equals zero cost"; (4) performance defined in terms other than host size/count (for Lambda you specify RAM, and CPU/IO scale with it); (5) implicit high availability — with S3 "you don't know whether an S3 node has gone down ever," though an entire region going down still requires multi-region planning.
* **Three benefits — cheaper, better, faster**: Cheaper through efficient resource use (paying only when code does useful work) and less operations work (no deployment management or sysadmin), though operations like security and disaster recovery remain. Better/safer via fewer threat surfaces — no unpatched OSes, admin access, or open ports to worry about. Fastest of all: he quotes Adrian Cockcroft (former Netflix senior cloud architect, now at AWS): "we're starting to see applications be built in ridiculously short time periods," with complete systems deployed to production that scale in 12 hours — enabling "hack days our de facto way of working."
* **Dragons (limitations)**: He covers loss of control to vendors (countered by noting we rarely hack the Linux kernel anyway, so it's an economic tradeoff); vendor lock-in (Lambda function code itself is portable with little platform-specific code, but using the broader ecosystem — API Gateway, DynamoDB, S3, Kinesis — creates real migration cost because NoSQL, message stores, and message buses "aren't actually yet identical across the clouds"); and immature tooling, especially for deployment and distributed monitoring.
* **Testing — "test in the cloud"**: Unit testing FaaS is easy because functions are simple with few dependencies ("unit test the heck out of it"). Integration/acceptance testing is tricky since "Amazon do not give you a Amazon Lambda that I can run on my laptop" and local stubs/mocks aren't guaranteed to match production. His reluctant recommendation: embrace the cloud as a development and testing platform, reusing automated deployment scripts. Practical tip: do testing in a separate AWS account because many limits are account-wide and "it is very easy for you to DOS yourself" — treat an AWS account as an infrastructural container, not just a finance construct.
* **Latency, cold starts, and state**: Performance suffers from extra network hops and cold starts — bringing up a container takes anywhere from an extra 100 milliseconds to 3 seconds; Amazon keeps a warm container around for a few hours if hit roughly every five minutes. There is no guarantee of container reuse, so all state must be externalized — echoing the Twelve-Factor App architecture from Heroku (nearly a decade old) which said keep no state in-instance and use Redis or a NoSQL/other database. Unlike Heroku, where dynos can live for days and you "could always cheat," with FaaS "you can't cheat" and must re-architect.
* **Architectural jolt**: Serverless is an economic evolution but "comes with a jolt architecturally." Earlier IaaS and containers let you keep the same code in different boxes with long-lived processes; FaaS forces an event-driven, short-lived-instance model where pieces of your own code are outsourced to providers. "What does good architecture look like?" is still open — "if someone... says, 'Hey, I've got a book on best practices for serverless architecture,' look at them very strangely and walk away."
* **Use cases — good and bad**: Excellent for latency-tolerant asynchronous apps like data pipelines — at his former ad-tech company they processed about 300 million events a day through a serverless pipeline ("way cheaper," solved many headaches). Also good for latency-tolerant synchronous apps: Philadelphia company Bustle renders its entire website via Lambda with sub-200ms latency "four-nines of the time." Also good as "glue" for infrastructure, monitoring, and deployment (e.g., Lambda inside CloudFormation). Avoid for: sub-10ms low-latency systems (his former high-frequency trading work); large-scale stateful processing (Lambda is limited to 1.5 GB RAM and a 5-minute runtime cap); and long-running stateful systems coordinating multiple external APIs in memory.
* **Hybrid architectures**: It's "perfectly reasonable" to mix serverless and "server-full" (not hybrid cloud) — a long-lived EC2 process calling out to serverless services, or a Lambda behind API Gateway calling a traditional SQL database. The caution: beware mixing components with different scaling properties — 100 auto-scaled Lambdas each making 10 requests to a central SQL database can overwhelm it, so consider a funneling component or shifting data to DynamoDB.
* **The future**: Tooling will improve sharply (he notes Amazon's X-Ray distributed-monitoring tool just gained Lambda support, announced that afternoon); deployment practices will converge; architectural patterns will eventually be codified ("in two or three years' time... someone is going to write the Serverless Architecture Patterns book"); and organizations must change — needing true DevOps, policy/cost/security management when any team can spin up stacks, and distributed product management where "everyone can now be involved." In the Q&A he also covered open-source/on-prem FaaS options (Iron Functions/iron.io, Galactic Fog, Fission, IBM's), predicting a "Cloud Foundry of FaaS" tightly integrated with Kubernetes will emerge; pricing per function call (most value comes from intellectual effort, not infrastructure; vary cost via RAM allocation); SQL vs. NoSQL (use the right tool — NoSQL will never match SQL's full query functionality); and sensitive configuration, now solved via Lambda environment variables with KMS integration (introduced October).

title: Scalability is Quantifiable: The Universal Scalability Law
description: This talk provides a practical guide to quantifying system scalability using the **Universal Scalability Law** to predict capacity limits and identify primary performance bottlenecks like serialization and crosstalk.
notes:
System scalability is mathematically quantifiable using the Universal Scalability Law, which allows engineers to predict capacity limits and identify specific architectural bottlenecks by simply analyzing the relationship between throughput and concurrency. By understanding the penalties of serialization and crosstalk, you can move beyond intuition-based capacity planning to accurately determine when a system will hit its performance ceiling.

* **Scalability is quantifiable** using mathematical models like the Universal Scalability Law (USL), allowing for objective measurement rather than guesswork.
* **Queueing theory** shows that as system utilization increases, response times grow non-linearly, resulting in a "hockey stick" curve where performance degrades sharply as capacity is reached.
* **Waiting is caused by irregularity** in job arrival rates and service times, which leads to overlapping tasks and resource contention.
* **Queues increase availability** by allowing tasks to wait for a resource rather than failing immediately, but they also increase total response time and system overhead.
* **Concurrency is the core property** of a system’s workload and serves as the mathematical definition of system load.
* **Amdahl’s Law** defines the limit of speed-up from parallelization, identifying that the serialized (non-parallelizable) portion of a task creates a performance ceiling.
* **Serialization is a form of queuing** that occurs when access to a resource is restricted, preventing a system from achieving linear scaling.
* **The Universal Scalability Law (USL)** extends Amdahl’s Law by adding a "crosstalk" or "coherence" penalty to account for the cost of coordination between tasks.
* **Crosstalk causes "retrograde" scalability**, a state where adding more workers or concurrency actually decreases total throughput because tasks spend too much time coordinating.
* **Only two metrics are required** to model a system's scalability: throughput and concurrency.
* **Bottleneck identification** is possible through nonlinear regression of these metrics, which reveals the specific fractions of serialization (Sigma) and crosstalk (Kappa) in the system.
* **Capacity planning** using USL allows engineers to identify "headroom"—the remaining capacity before a system stops scaling or enters a "spiral of death" where performance collapses.

title: Open Source World
description: TED Radio Hour. Great episode for the general public about the influence of the open source and other related movements (open hardware, web technology, direct democracy, etc.). Very interesting. It surprised me that they didn't say anything about RMS and the free software movement :(

title: Testing Strategy for DevOps
description: Dave Farley describes his take on what a holistic test-strategy looks like, and gives us examples from the real world to describe it.
notes:
Here are the most important points from the presentation:
- **Testing in production is a crucial and often misunderstood part of a comprehensive testing strategy in DevOps.** It does not mean _only_ testing in production, which would be irresponsible for most systems. Instead, it means testing _more_, not less, and learning valuable lessons that can only be gained from a live production environment.
- A robust testing strategy involves **testing both before and after releasing software to production**.
- **Before release, there are generally two key phases of automated testing:**
    - **Commit tests** (often using TDD and unit tests) focus on technical validation and ensuring the code does what the developers intend. The goal is fast feedback (under five minutes) and a high level of confidence (around 80%) for subsequent tests.
    - **Acceptance tests** confirm everything needed to make the decision to release, including whether the software meets user needs, is configured correctly, and is deployable, scalable, secure, and resilient.
- **After release (testing in production), there are at least two different classes of tests:**
    - **Release supporting tests** ensure the software is up and running and ready for use. This can include smoke tests, health checks, and strategies like canary releasing to mitigate the risks of releasing to the entire user base at once. Automating the evaluation and promotion of canaries is also a valuable practice.
    - **Tests looking at product behavior** focus on what can be learned once the software is live. This includes monitoring performance, scalability, resource usage, and errors. It also involves verifying assumptions made during pre-release testing, such as performance and security. Advanced techniques like chaos engineering (deliberately breaking parts of the system) can be used to ensure resilience.
- **Testing in production is essential for learning about users and guiding product development**. This can involve A/B testing to see how different user groups respond to different versions of the software. It also helps determine if the product meets its intended goals, such as generating revenue or reaching a target audience.
- **Treating the release of changes as a series of small experiments allows for incremental evolution of products based on real-world feedback**.
- A significant portion of software ideas fail to deliver value. Therefore, **testing product ideas in production is crucial for identifying and discarding bad ideas quickly and cost-effectively**.

title: Many More Much Smaller Steps with GeePaw Hill
description: Good conversation about GeePaw Hill's software development approach based on taking continuous small safe steps (Many More Much Smaller Steps).
notes:
*  **The central idea is to take many more much smaller steps than you are currently taking when making changes, whether in code, in processes, or even in your personal life.** This is presented as a broadly applicable piece of "rice and garlic advice" – fundamental and widely relevant.

*  **The traditional approach often operates under the assumption that the shortest distance between two points is a straight line, leading to the belief that fewer, larger steps are more efficient.** GeePaw Hill strongly argues against this, stating that this logic only holds true in Euclidean plane geometry and not in the complex reality of software development or other domains.

*  **Taking many more much smaller steps offers significant intrinsic benefits that are often ignored.** These benefits include:
  *  **Increased interruptability:** Smaller steps mean you are more frequently in a stable, deployable state, making it easier to pause and resume work.
  *  **Enhanced steerability:** Frequent small releases allow for quicker adaptation to changing requirements, feedback, or emerging issues.
  *  **The "juice" factor (dopamine):** Completing small steps provides regular positive reinforcement and a sense of progress, which can be crucial for maintaining motivation and joy in the work.

*  **A "step" in this context means moving from an "activated" or "ready" state to another "activated" or "ready" state through a small change.** "Activated" in software development often means code that is shifted, fielded, or deployed, or at least emotionally prepped to be shipped. The key is to make these cycles very short (e.g., 15-45 minutes for code changes, less than a day and a half of team time per story shipment).

*  **The criterion for a step is not necessarily that it provides immediate positive value to the end-user, but rather that it results in a "non-negative experience".** The focus is on making a small change and ensuring the system remains stable or better, even if the value hasn't yet increased.

*  **Resistance to this idea often stems from a belief that each step must deliver significant user value or that larger, planned efforts are more efficient.** Hill argues that the intrinsic benefits of small steps and the non-linear nature of larger changes outweigh these concerns. He suggests comparing the outcome of current practices to the potential of taking smaller steps.

*  **When coaching teams to adopt smaller steps, GeePaw Hill emphasizes understanding what they want to achieve and identifying the smallest, easiest problems to solve first.** He focuses on building trust and suggesting changes as experiments rather than mandates. Patience is crucial for this process.

*  **Techniques like mob programming (or ensemble programming) can facilitate the adoption of smaller steps by providing a collaborative environment for learning and immediate feedback through micro-retrospectives.** The shared learning and mutual reinforcement within a mob can help individuals overcome the feeling that they cannot make changes in small increments.

*  **The principle of "code works for you, you don't work for the code" is closely related.** If the codebase or development environment makes it difficult to take small, frequent steps or get fast feedback, then the code and processes should be changed to make it easier. This might involve refactoring code to be more modular and testable or simplifying deployment processes. The goal is to empower developers to control their tools and workflows.

*  **Ultimately, the presentation emphasizes that the focus should be on the process of change itself, not just the specific code being changed.** The ability to make frequent, small, safe changes is crucial for adaptability and delivering value faster in the long run. Reawakening the joy in programming by making development more manageable and less frustrating is also a key aspect.

title: DevOps at Amazon with Ken Exner GM of AWS Developer Tools
description: Great talk to understand how to deliver and operate software at scale. It contains a lot of useful insights.
notes:
Achieving a successful DevOps transformation requires a holistic approach that integrates **architectural decomposition** for agility, extensive **automation through CI/CD tooling**, and a fundamental **shift in engineering culture**. Organizations must move toward a model where teams have end-to-end ownership of their services to effectively balance speed, agility, and reliability.

* **Decomposition for Agility**: Transitioning from monolithic architectures to decoupled microservices to enable independent team movement and faster software delivery cycles.
* **Tooling and Automation**: Implementing robust CI/CD pipelines and automated tools to eliminate manual bottlenecks, increase deployment frequency, and ensure system reliability.
* **Cultural Transformation**: Shifting to a "you build it, you run it" model where development teams take full ownership of the entire software lifecycle, including operations.
* **Operational Responsibility**: Moving away from centralized operations toward a model where product teams are accountable for the performance and maintenance of their own services.
* **Focus on Speed and Reliability**: Leveraging architectural and organizational changes to achieve a balance between rapid feature delivery and high operational stability.
* **Architectural Design Shifts**: Utilizing design changes to support a DevOps culture, ensuring that infrastructure and applications are built for continuous evolution and scale.

title: RailsConf 2016 - Get a Whiff of This
description: Sandi Metz explores the practical power of identifying **code smells** and applying specific **refactoring recipes** to transform complex, messy code into clean, testable, and maintainable designs.
notes:
Mastering the specific names and "recipes" of code **smells** and **refactorings** allows developers to use safe, mechanical operations to transform complex, messy code into clear and maintainable objects [1-3]. This systematic approach enables programmers to communicate without ambiguity and focus their mental energy on solving harder problems rather than struggling with technical debt [4-6].

- **Code smells** are named patterns that identify potential design flaws, providing a shared vocabulary for developers to communicate about code quality without ambiguity.
- There are **22 recognized code smells** grouped into five categories: Bloaters, Tool Abusers, Change Preventers, Dispensables, and Couplers.
- **Bloaters** (e.g., Long Method, Large Class, Data Clumps) make code larger than necessary; **Data Clumps** should be extracted into their own classes to allow behavior to coalesce around the data.
- **Tool Abusers** (e.g., Switch Statements, Refused Bequest) represent the misuse of object-oriented programming features.
- **Change Preventers** (e.g., Shotgun Surgery, Divergent Change) are smells that make the codebase difficult and expensive to modify.
- **Dispensables** (e.g., Duplicated Code, Speculative Generality) are unnecessary elements; **Speculative Generality** (coding for future needs) should be avoided as it increases complexity and indirection.
- **Couplers** (e.g., Feature Envy, Message Chains) create tight dependencies that prevent objects from being reused or tested in isolation.
- Every code smell maps to a specific, curative **refactoring recipe**, meaning developers do not need to reinvent solutions for common design problems.
- **Refactoring** is defined as rearranging code to improve its structure without changing its external behavior.
- **Message Chains** (Law of Demeter violations) should be resolved by **hiding the delegate**, ensuring an object only talks to its immediate collaborators.
- **Dependency Injection** is critical for decoupling code from specific classes or constants, allowing objects to interact based on **roles** rather than fixed identities.
- Decoupling code through injection and hiding delegates leads to **faster test suites** by removing the need to instantiate complex object networks or perform slow database queries during unit tests.
- **Cost-based refactoring**: Smelly or "ugly" code that never changes does not cost the business money and may not require immediate fixing.
- Static analysis tools, such as **Reek**, can be used to automatically identify smells within a codebase.

title: Monoliths, Migrations, and Microservices
Continuous Delivery vs. Gitflow & CD At Scale | Bryan Finster In The Engineering Room Ep. 11
description: Interesting conversation about how to introduce CD as part of the engineering strategy in large companies and how having CD as part of the vision (not as a goal in itself) can generate a healthy engineering culture that continuously improves.
notes:
**Continuous Delivery is fundamentally a quality-driven discipline** that reduces risk and improves developer morale by replacing bureaucratic, large-batch processes with rapid feedback from small, frequent changes. To succeed at scale, organizations must **treat delivery platforms as products** that combine technical tools with essential education, ensuring that a culture of engineering excellence and mission-focus is baked into the way work is done.

*   **Continuous Delivery (CD) is an evidence-based practice** that, once adopted, becomes an "invisible" commodity that high-performing organizations would never choose to abandon.
*   **Continuous Delivery is a quality initiative**, not just a speed initiative; delivering with higher quality naturally results in higher speed as a side effect.
*   Adopting CD significantly **improves developer morale** and work-life balance by replacing high-stress, multi-day fix sessions with rapid feedback and smaller, safer changes.
*   Success requires **Mission-Minded Execution**, where every employee is aligned on an inspirational goal rather than just focusing on specific technical tools or frameworks.
*   **Platforms should be treated as products**, where the primary goal is to reduce cognitive load for product teams and provide them with a "golden path" to success.
*   A platform is not just tools; it **must include education and training** to prevent teams from misusing high-powered delivery systems.
*   Scaling CD is effectively achieved through an **immersive learning "Dojo" model**, where expert engineers embed within teams to solve real-world technical hurdles.
*   **Change Approval Boards (CABs) are negatively correlated with quality** because they increase batch sizes and delay critical feedback cycles.
*   **Trunk-based development** is essential for CD; branching strategies like Gitflow are fundamentally incompatible with the requirement for continuous integration and rapid feedback.
*   **Security is impossible without Continuous Delivery**; the ability to deploy frequent, small changes is the only way to effectively patch vulnerabilities and keep software stacks updated.
*   **Automated compliance** is superior to "theatrical compliance"; checks should be baked into the pipeline to provide actual assurance rather than manual paperwork.
*   **Keep pipelines running and green** even when no code has changed; scheduled builds help identify broken dependencies and security vulnerabilities in stagnant codebases.
*   When driving organizational change, you must **find the problems leadership has** and solve them better, while ensuring you have "air cover" from the highest levels of the organization.
*   **Small, frequent steps** are the fundamental value of CD, reducing risk by minimizing the delta of change between deployments.
*   The **"Minimum CD"** concept defines a baseline set of activities required to see organizational benefits across any industry or technical context.

title: Patterns of Effective Delivery
description: This talk explores patterns of effective software delivery, emphasizing that delivery means solving problems, not just writing code, and focusing on optimizing for the right outcomes rather than just process
notes:
The presentation, "Patterns of Effective Delivery," explores strategies for improving software delivery by focusing on solving business problems, optimizing for effectiveness, and understanding the context in which patterns are applied. Here are the most important points:
- **Delivery is about solving business problems, not just writing software**. The focus should be on the outcome of the software rather than the software itself. Software is a means to an end and should provide utility and enable discovery.
- **Effective delivery requires understanding what you are optimizing for**. The presenter highlights that effectiveness can mean optimizing for different things, such as speed, time to market, user experience, or the ability to quickly try new ideas.
- **Organizations often optimize for certainty, which can come at the expense of speed and discovery**. The agile community can become overly focused on predictability, using metrics like velocity and estimation to minimize uncertainty, which can reduce effectiveness.
- **Patterns are strategies that work in a specific context**. A pattern is not a universally applicable solution, and what works in one situation might be counterproductive in another. Applying a strategy without understanding the context can lead to a cargo cult mentality.
- **The presentation introduces the concept of "Spike and Stabilize" which is a different way to approach coding and testing**. Instead of rigidly defining code as either a "spike" for experimentation or "production code" with strict testing protocols, the approach is to initially use code to explore and then stabilize it later. This means initially writing tests to explore, deferring the commitment to the quality of the code, and writing tests after to make the code TDD-shaped.
- **Another pattern introduced is "Ginger Cake," which involves copying and pasting code that is intimately known to get a new application up and running quickly**. This is not about copying code from the internet or using code generators, but rather using code that you fully understand and then modifying it to fit a new need.
- **The idea of a "short software half-life" is introduced which suggests that code should have a short lifespan**. This encourages developers to think of code as temporary and to invest more in code that has proven to be valuable. This concept challenges the DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) principle.
- **Code that is simple, understandable, readable, and obvious is important**. This means optimizing for simplicity and making code fit into the developer's head. Test-driven development (TDD) becomes a tool rather than a crutch when code is kept simple.
- **Creating urgency is a way to learn new technologies quickly**. Instead of tutorials, creating a real project with a tight deadline forces you to focus on what's essential.
- **Socratic testing involves using tests to explore code in a non-threatening way**. Rather than telling a team what to do, you work with them to write tests to understand the code, which can lead to organic discovery and improvements.
- **The agile manifesto is often misunderstood and the values have been inverted**. For example, the focus is often on process and tools rather than people and interactions. The presenter suggests we should consider what we are optimizing for and if the pursuit of certainty is hampering speed and discovery.
- **Effective delivery requires discipline, rigor, practice, and learning, and a willingness to sacrifice certainty to achieve greater effectiveness**.
The presentation challenges conventional agile practices, advocating for a more nuanced, context-driven approach to software delivery, and it emphasizes the importance of focusing on outcomes, learning, and continuous improvement.

title: From Noob to Automated Evals In A Week (as a PM) w/Teresa Torres
description: Product discovery expert Teresa Torres recounts her journey from a self-described "noob" in AI to implementing automated evaluations for an AI-powered interview coach within a week, detailing her iterative process of building and refining the tool with significant LLM assistance.
notes:
The most important lesson conveyed by this talk is the **empowering role of Large Language Models (LLMs)** as collaborative partners, enabling individuals with limited prior AI or coding experience to rapidly build and refine complex AI products through an **iterative process grounded in systematic evaluation and fast feedback loops**. This approach emphasizes that **domain expertise is crucial for designing effective evaluations**, sometimes leading to simple, code-based solutions outperforming more complex LLM-as-judge methods, ultimately ensuring the continuous improvement and reliability of the AI product.

- An AI-powered interview coach was developed to provide **detailed, automated feedback** on interview transcripts, addressing the challenge of non-expert student feedback.
- The development process started with **minimal AI/coding experience** and evolved rapidly through **iterative building and refinement**.
- **Large Language Models (LLMs)** like ChatGPT and Claude Code served as **collaborative partners** for learning, problem-solving, co-programming, and debugging.
- A shift from "vibe-checking" to **systematic, automated evaluations (evals)** was crucial for measuring quality and guiding improvements.
- Evals were designed iteratively, including **LLM-as-judge** evaluations (e.g., detecting leading questions) and **simple code assertions** (e.g., detecting general questions via keywords).
- **Domain expertise is vital for effective eval design**, sometimes leading to simple, code-based solutions outperforming complex LLM-as-judge methods.
- **Fast feedback loops** enabled rapid iteration on prompt changes, model changes, and workflow adjustments, dramatically reducing errors.
- The process involved **data investigation** (e.g., using Jupyter Notebooks as a data analysis tool) to understand failures, find bugs, and even clarify human labeling rubrics.
- **Helper functions** were used to constrain context for evals, saving money and improving efficiency (a form of "context engineering").
- Custom annotation tools, built with LLM assistance, were essential for **efficient and targeted human labeling**.
- The project scaled from a teaching tool to a **production-ready AI application**, requiring a deep dive into "real" software engineering practices like robust error handling.
- This journey highlights the **empowering potential of LLMs** for individuals with high agency to become "AI engineers" and build complex products, even without extensive prior engineering background.
- The experience reinforced the importance of **critical thinking, problem-solving, and continuous learning** in the rapidly evolving AI landscape.

title: The art of destroying software
description: Some good points about the easy evolution of a system when the services are small enough to be understandables and to be rewritten without fear.
notes:
Prioritizing the **ability to delete and replace code** is a vital technical practice that removes the fear of legacy systems and ensures long-term software health. By optimizing for **disposability**, developers can maintain more flexible architectures that are significantly easier to evolve or rebuild when necessary.

- The "ability to delete" and start over is one of the most critical quality attributes in software development.
- Systems should be intentionally optimized for disposability rather than just maintenance or extensibility.
- Prioritizing code deletability eliminates the fear and anxiety developers often feel when interacting with a complex or legacy codebase.
- Engineering efforts should focus on understanding why disposability is important and identifying specific techniques to architect software for easy replacement.

I noticed that the current source material for "The art of destroying software" only includes a brief video description. If you would like more granular details from the actual talk, I can search for a full transcript or a more in-depth summary for you. Would you like me to do that?

title: TDD Revisited - Ian Cooper - NDC Porto 2023
description: This talk re-examines TDD, emphasizing that it's about writing developer tests focused on behavior and contracts, not isolated unit tests with mocks, to guide development and enable safe refactoring
notes:
Effective test-driven development should focus on testing **observable behaviors and contracts** rather than isolating internal code units or methods through excessive mocking [1-3]. This approach allows developers to refactor implementation details freely without breaking tests, ensuring that the test suite serves as a stable, executable specification of system requirements [1, 4-7].

* **TDD is Not About Unit Testing:** TDD produces "programmer" or "developer" tests, which differ from the classical definition of unit tests. While unit testing often focuses on isolating a single class or module using mocks, TDD focuses on localizing defects to the most recent code change.
* **The Fallacy of Mock-Driven Development:** Replacing every dependency with a mock couples tests to the internal implementation details of the code. This leads to "fragile tests" that break during refactoring even when observable behavior hasn't changed, ultimately discouraging developers from improving the code.
* **Focus on Behavior, Not Methods:** The trigger for a new test should be a new behavior or requirement, not the creation of a new function or class. Tests should be written against the "contract" or the public API of a module, treating the implementation as a black box.
* **Refactoring is a First-Class Citizen:** In the "Red-Green-Refactor" cycle, the "Green" phase is for quickly reaching a solution by any means (including copy-pasting). The "Refactor" phase is where you clean the code, introduce better design, and extract classes—all without needing to write new tests, as the behavior is already covered by the original test.
* **Avoid Testing Private Methods:** Private or internal methods are implementation details. You should only test the public interface that others depend on. If a private method feels too complex, it should be refactored into a new class whose public behavior is then tested.
* **Selective Use of Mocks:** Mocks should primarily be used to handle "shared fixtures" that prevent tests from running in parallel or at high speeds, such as databases, network IO, or file systems. The goal is to keep the test suite fast (ideally under 30 seconds) to maintain developer flow.
* **The Misuse of Acceptance Testing Tools:** Tools like SpecFlow, Cucumber, or Fitness are only valuable if customers or product owners are actually writing and reading the tests. If only developers use them, the indirection of natural language adds a maintenance burden without providing benefit; developers should use standard xUnit tools instead.
* **Scope Control via Test-First:** Writing tests before code prevents "speculative development." You only write the production code necessary to make the current test pass, ensuring the design evolves only as much as required to meet the actual requirements.
* **TDD vs. Classical Automation:** TDD (test-first) is a tool for design and discovery. Classical automation (test-after) is for regression testing. Use test-after for areas where design exploration isn't needed, such as third-party SDKs, stable UI implementations, or bug fixes in legacy code.
* **Driving in Gears:** Developers should work in increments they feel comfortable with. If you are frequently using a debugger to find errors, your "jumps" (the amount of code written between tests) are too large. Smaller increments provide better defect localization.
* **The Testing Pyramid:** Prioritize fast, binary-feedback developer tests at the base. Use slower, more fragile integration and UI tests sparingly at the top for final confirmation of configuration and environment.
* **Manual Testing Still Matters:** While automation is key for deployment, manual exploratory testing remains valuable for finding "crazy" edge cases and unexpected behaviors that a developer might not think to script.

title: Being a good citizen in an event driven world
description: Ajay Nair explores how to be an effective event source provider in serverless architectures by focusing on payload design, the strategic use of event stores, and the essential role of secure, conditional routing.
notes:
Becoming a high-quality event provider requires making deliberate architectural choices regarding payload design, the use of durable event stores for reliability, and the implementation of secure, filtered routing. By balancing state transfer and notification patterns while ensuring robust failure semantics, providers can create a more scalable and effective ecosystem for decoupled, serverless applications.

* **Event-Driven vs. Serverless:** While all serverless architectures tend to be event-driven, not all event-driven architectures are necessarily serverless.
* **Core Architecture Components:** A robust system consists of an **event source** (identifies a change and emits a payload) and an **event router** (a logically separate component responsible for delivering the event to the provider).
* **Provenance Information:** Every event must contain metadata defining **who the source is, the relevance of the event, and when it happened** (timestamps).
* **Notification Pattern:** The event source only sends a notification that something happened, forcing the consumer to call back the source to retrieve data. This keeps payloads lightweight but can double traffic on the event source.
* **State Transfer Pattern:** The event payload includes the full state so the consumer never has to talk back to the source. This is essential for disconnected devices or services without public endpoints.
* **The Event Store:** Introducing a store between the source and consumer provides **durability and retention**, allowing events to be replayed or used to rehydrate production stores if a service goes down.
* **Streams vs. Queues:** Streams allow for ordered processing and multiple providers but limit concurrency to the number of shards. Queues allow high concurrency for individual events but offer no ordering guarantees.
* **Router Security:** A router must securely associate sources and destinations, ensuring only authorized entities can emit or consume specific events.
* **Filtering and Conditionals:** Routers should support event filtering (e.g., by prefix or suffix) so downstream services only trigger for relevant data, increasing system efficiency.
* **On-Failure Semantics:** The router is responsible for defining what happens when an execution fails, such as utilizing **Dead Letter Queues (DLQ)** or managing retry behaviors.
* **Stateless Execution:** In ideal event-driven models, the execution layer remains stateless and ephemeral, with a clear separation between logic, data, and state.

title: The Role Of QA in Agile Software
description: In this episode, Dave Farley explores the role of QA in modern agile teams and explores the move in some detail from gatekeepers to quality experts and ideas like continuous testing and QA as trusted advisors.
notes:
Software teams achieve high quality and speed by building error detection directly into the creation process through continuous testing and collaboration, rather than treating Quality Assurance as a final gatekeeping step. Success depends on shifting from batch testing to a model where quality is a collective responsibility, ensuring the software remains in a constantly releasable state through early feedback and shared expertise

* **Continuous Delivery** requires software to be in a **constantly releasable state**, shifting the focus from sporadic releases to a steady stream of small changes.
* The **deployment pipeline** must be the definitive measure of releasability; any manual gate or external QA sign-off at the end of the process invalidates the pipeline's effectiveness.
* **Quality must be built into the system** during creation rather than inspected after the fact. Practices like **Test-Driven Development (TDD)** and pair programming are essential for catching errors as they are introduced.
* Testing frequency is critical; **testing every change** (one-piece flow) identifies mistakes at the earliest opportunity and significantly reduces waste compared to batch testing.
* The role of **QA transitions from "gatekeeper" to "guide."** QA professionals act as quality experts who advise the team on how to think about testing before development work starts.
* **QA should be embedded within the development team** rather than functioning as a separate department, ensuring they evaluate features as they are being created.
* A **"Three Amigos" kickoff** (Product Owner, Developers, and QA) should occur before starting a feature to explore requirements and establish user-perspective examples.
* **Executable specifications** derived from acceptance criteria serve as a structured **Definition of Done**; a feature is finished only when these automated tests pass.
* All **regression testing should be automated** to maintain efficiency and provide fast feedback to developers.
* **Manual testing** is valuable only for **exploratory and subjective feedback**, such as usability and aesthetics, rather than repetitive verification of functionality.
* Teams should avoid "mini-waterfalls" by having developers and QA work concurrently on a feature, using **immediate verbal feedback** instead of formal bug reports.
* High-quality software is achieved by working in **small, fast, definitive steps** where quality is monitored continuously throughout the development lifecycle.

title: POST No AWS Bills: Cloud Cost Optimization Without APIs
description: Corey Quinn explores the limitations of automated cloud-billing tools and argues for bridging the gap between engineering and finance by aligning cloud spend with business-driven KPIs.
notes:
Effective cloud cost management requires bridging the communication gap between engineering and finance by **aligning technical spend with business-driven KPIs** instead of relying solely on automated tools. It is vital to remember that as systems become more cost-efficient and "cloudy," they naturally become harder to predict in absolute dollars, making **business-aligned metrics** the only meaningful way to measure and communicate value.

* **Cloud cost optimization tools** often fail because they focus on surface-level fixes (like Reserved Instances) rather than addressing systemic business problems.
* **Disaster Recovery sites** should be viewed as insurance policies; turning them off to save money is risky because cloud control planes often become saturated during regional outages, preventing rapid spin-up when actually needed.
* **Venture Capital incentives** prioritize "massively scalable" SaaS tools, which often lack the deep, bespoke analysis required to solve complex, company-specific billing issues.
* **Multi-cloud "single pane of glass" tools** often force engineering teams down to the "lowest common denominator" APIs, resulting in inefficient architectures.
* There is **no "API for business insight"**—current tools can track technical metrics like storage or data transfer, but they struggle to answer business questions like the specific cost of development versus production or the cost of a particular sub-service.
* A significant **communication gap** exists between Finance (focused on allocation and prediction) and Engineering (focused on feature delivery and speed).
* **Governance and cost guardrails** must be easier to follow than the alternative; if provisioning is slow or difficult, engineers will keep instances running indefinitely to avoid future delays.
* The **"Spectrum of Cloudiness"** defines a move from physical data centers to VMs, auto-scaling, containers, and finally serverless.
* As architectures move toward the serverless end of the spectrum, they become **more cost-efficient but less predictable** in absolute dollar terms.
* To bridge the gap between technical and financial teams, cloud costs should be tied to **business KPIs**, such as "cost per Monthly Active User (MAU)," rather than just raw infrastructure spend.
* There is an **inverse correlation between optimization and predictability**: the more an environment scales precisely with demand, the more the monthly bill will fluctuate.

title: Rapid Prototyping & Product Management
description: Tom Chi on rapid prototyping: escape the "guess-a-thon" by finding the fastest path to an observed, embodied experience — maximizing the rate of learning instead of debating.
notes:
Tom Chi (rapid prototyping for 15+ years, including hardware at Google X and work for the world's poorest two billion) argues that real learning comes only from observed, embodied experience — so the job is to find the fastest path to actually experiencing a prototype, not to debate what it will be like.

- The anti-pattern is the "guess-a-thon": rooms full of smart people arguing over what a product will be like, trading opinions that no one can resolve. Learnings can't come from debate; they come from an observed, embodied experience.
- So instead of arguing, build the cheapest, fastest thing that lets you actually experience the idea and observe what happens — maximize the rate of learning per unit of time and money.
- Be "hands in the medium," like a painter touching the paint: doing beats talking, and the tighter and more tactile the loop between idea and experience, the faster you discover what's true — the thread running through software, organizational/space design, and hardware alike.

title: A Thoroughly Practical Approach to Having Your Dream Job
description: J.B. Rainsberger on building a satisfying career: "find a job you love and never work" is nonsense — pursue measurability, autonomy and relevance, get comfortable with money, and negotiate the freedom you need.
notes:
J.B. Rainsberger gives a practical (not romantic) take on having a "dream job." The saying "find a job you love and you'll never work a day" is nonsense — there's a reason we call it work, and romanticizing it creates problems.

- What makes work satisfying comes down to three things (after the model he cites): measurability, autonomy and relevance — when you can't get those, that's where the misery comes from.
- Get comfortable with money: "enough" is enough for your purposes. If you're comfortable with your personal finances you're not afraid of money, which frees you to make better career choices; if you can't opt out of trading time and energy for money, at least recognize the trade you're making.
- You have more options than you think: if you can't get autonomy where you are, negotiate part-time work, or take a low-stress 9-to-5 (the "job at a library") where you leave your cares at work and go home — and pursue meaning on your own terms. The dream job is engineered through deliberate choices, not found.

title: Mastering Observability: Unlocking Customer Insights with Gojko Adzic
description: Gojko Adzic on measuring value by user behaviour change rather than effort, and extending DevOps-style observability to user behaviour — including "lizard optimization," learning from users who do inexplicable things.
notes:
Gojko Adzic argues we measure the wrong thing — effort (i.e. money burned) instead of value — and that the fix is to measure the behaviour change of users, extending the observability tooling DevOps already built for systems to watch what users actually do.

- Why orgs still don't do TDD/BDD/spec-by-example: many simply have too much money (software isn't their bottleneck), incentives reward effort, and people would rather talk to computers than to each other. Deming: "doing well that which should not be done at all is the highest form of waste."
- Measure outcomes, not output: Melissa Perri's value-exchange loop, and Ron Kohavi's data (Trustworthy Online Controlled Experiments) — only ~1/3 of Microsoft ideas improved the target metric, ~30% at Slack, ~10% at early Netflix. So 70-90% of work delivers no or negative value — depressing, but a massive opportunity (why keep testing/maintaining what should be thrown away?).
- Behaviour change is the right measure because it tracks whether you solved the problem (one behaviour change admits five solutions — a shell script vs a new app) and it's a leading indicator you can observe immediately, unlike lagging profit/market-share that tell you too late. His EU tax-ID form story: a "good idea" to streamline the form made completion worse — only the metrics revealed it.
- Extend observability to users: product analytics look for expected things (clicks, conversions); DevOps observability is built to catch the unexpected (exceptions, deviations). Log when a user hits an error screen or takes an unexpected path — crash analytics, but for user behaviour.
- "Lizard optimization" (his book): lizards are users doing things no rational person would (Scott Alexander's phantom lizardmen; "American" as gender) — e.g. paying to convert blank PowerPoints to video (they only wanted the text-to-speech audio), or uploading subtitle/APK/MP3 files. Understanding the lizard logic surfaces product opportunities; making the product better for people struggling in strange ways improves it for everyone.

title: The Next Generation of Data Products
description: A good introductory talk for newbies like me.
notes:
Hilary Mason, drawing on her work at Fast Forward Labs and her earlier role as Chief Scientist at Bitly, argues that data products are where data science, machine learning, and AI become genuinely valuable, because product is what ties together the engineering, the business implications, and the imagination of what data makes possible. Her central thesis is that the most important shift today is not just that new things become technically possible, but that computation has become so cheap that previously impossible analysis can now be applied even to frivolous, exploratory work.

* **Definition of a data product**: A product you could not build without doing some analysis in the process. Mason seeks shared vocabulary over rigid definitions, noting Big Data is fading and AI has become vague.
* **Canonical examples**: Google Maps is her favorite precisely because it is boring — anyone reads the map with no understanding of the real-time data gathering and traffic prediction underneath. Others: Google Photos semantic search, recommendation systems (profitable but unsolved), the Dark Sky weather app.
* **The cheap-computation shift matters most**: Two kinds of change — impossible-to-possible (the classic breakthrough), and nothing-to-so-cheap you can apply it to trivial exploration. Her Bitly anecdote (an 8-hour Hadoop job over 3 years of social data just to learn dogs beat cats in shares) shows the second shift is equally important.
* **Rising layers of abstraction**: Work that took weeks now takes hours; work that took teams now takes one person; in five years practitioners will have "superpowers."
* **New interfaces and newly-available data**: Growing work on unstructured/natural-language data (chatbots as one interface), plus businesses realizing their operational data exhaust is useful.
* **Pick high-value problems**: Having data is useless without the right problem. High value = currently expensive or very important to do well. Counter-example: the Disco LED dog outfit (wrong problem); positive: a 2017 deep-learning radiology paper that lowers cost and scales quality medicine to billions.

### Challenges in building data products
* **You can't buy them off the shelf**: The generic formulation of a problem is ~10x harder than the specific instance, so sellable general solutions rarely work well.
* **It's genuinely hard (a candid failure)**: Estimating calories from food photos, simplified to healthy/not-healthy, still failed because online calorie data disagreed wildly (a cheeseburger ranging 300-2500 calories).
* **Unpredictable edge cases with severe consequences**: Her Pictograph demo classified french fries by water as crabs and NYC subway photos as prisons. Because models aren't interpretable, you can't easily edit them — a serious problem when legally required not to discriminate.
* **Organizations aren't structured for data science**: Agile/sprints get shoehorned onto research, model-building, and productionization phases; she suggests naming it an experimental development process.
* **Operationalization is under-developed**: Seek the simplest algorithm that works at scale; plan for production and for data drift (quantitative drift tests, monitoring, feeding production data back into training) rather than relying on one person.
* **UX for data products is a missing discipline**: Designing interfaces for non-deterministic, probabilistic systems is hard and needs its own practice.

### Emerging capabilities
* **Fast Forward Labs' four signals**: research activity (a paper from one domain applied to another), changing economics of a technique (micro SD 128MB in 2004 → 128GB in 2014), commoditization (Hadoop turning a 12-engineer build into a button), and newly-available data (Wikipedia as the dirty secret behind many APIs).
* **Natural language generation**: turning structured data into narrative. The AP went from 300 to 3,000 quarterly-earnings stories; her real-estate-ad prototype revealed agent biases (cozy/small ≈ 400 sq ft below average). Most people want a sentence, not a graph.
* **Summarization / making text computable**: extractive summarization pulls sentences carrying the same information; the deeper capability is mapping any sentence to a vector comparable to other text.
* **Probabilistic programming**: the inverse of deep learning's data needs — domain expertise plus small/messy data, combining Bayesian inference with probabilistic languages to produce a distribution with confidence intervals.
* **Augmenting humans, not removing them**: many of the most valuable data products give people the right information at the right time rather than automating the human out.
* **Product vs services tradeoff (Q&A)**: pure ML capability without a real business problem is "an API in search of users"; winners pair data-science capability with a real problem and domain expertise.

title: Ten (Hard-Won) Lessons of the DevOps Transition
description: This talk discusses the cultural change required to adopt a devops mentality. Excellent advice and warnings derived from Randy's experience leading teams at eBay, Google, and KIXEYE.
notes:
Successful DevOps transitions depend far more on organizational and cultural shifts than technology, specifically moving toward an ownership model where autonomous, cross-functional teams have end-to-end responsibility for their services. You should remember that prioritizing quality as a core feature and fostering a truly blameless environment are essential for aligning incentives and driving continuous, long-term improvement.

* **Reorganize teams around end-to-end ownership**: Small, cross-functional teams should own a service or application through its entire lifecycle—from design and deployment to retirement.
* **"You Build It, You Run It"**: The same team that builds the software should be responsible for operating it, eliminating the divide between development and maintenance.
* **Transition from Ticket Culture to Ownership Culture**: Move away from reactive, siloed communication focused on closing tickets toward proactive collaboration focused on product success.
* **Replace Manual Approvals with Automated Code**: Eliminate centralized architecture or review boards; instead, embed specialized knowledge (security, compliance, architecture) into self-service tools, libraries, and automated tests.
* **Adopt Vendor-Customer Discipline**: Internal service teams should act as vendors, and customer teams should have the freedom to choose whether to use those services.
* **Align Economic Incentives by Charging for Internal Services**: Charge internal teams for their usage of shared services to motivate efficiency and prevent the waste of "free" resources.
* **Treat Quality, Performance, and Reliability as "Priority Zero"**: These foundational elements are as important as features. Teams should "stop the line" and address degradations immediately.
* **Integrate Testing into the Development Process**: Developers should write automated tests alongside their code.
* **Actively Manage Technical Debt**: Track technical debt in terms of engineering effort and plan for its payoff.
* **Share On-Call Responsibilities**: Rotate pager duties among all team members to build empathy.
* **Implement Truly Blameless Post-mortems**: Focus incident reviews on learning and systemic improvement rather than assigning individual blame.

title: Testing the Hard Stuff and Staying Sane
description: A good aproximation to automatic check/testing for concurrent stuff (QuickChecks). Examples with the erlang version.
notes:
John Hughes argues that hand-written tests can never keep up with the combinatorial explosion of feature interactions, so instead of writing tests you should generate them.
