Drawing on QuickCheck — the property-based testing tool he co-invented with Koen Claessen and later commercialized as Erlang QuickCheck (Quviq) — he shows through three escalating case studies (a C circular buffer, AUTOSAR automotive software for Volvo, and the Erlang dets database at Klarna) that a single property plus shrinking finds deep bugs, including race conditions, that no human tester would ever script.

* **Why testing is fundamentally hard**: Writing 3-4 tests per feature is linear, but bugs needing two features together require quadratic cases, three features cubic, and race conditions are worst because they manifest sporadically and by definition involve interaction. You can never test enough — so "don't write tests, generate them."
* **What QuickCheck does**: A random testing tool that generates a random *sequence* of API calls, runs it, and keeps generating until one fails.
* **Shrinking — extracting signal from noise**: A failing random sequence fails because of just a few of its calls. Shrinking searches for the calls actually necessary and reports a *minimal failing example* where every call matters — transforming random testing into something easy to debug.
* **State-machine models as specifications**: Model the system state simply, define a state-transition function per call, and write postconditions comparing real results against the model (tiny pure callback functions).

### The circular buffer demo — one property, many bugs
* **Bug 1, modulo size**: `size` computed `(in - out) mod size`; for size 1 everything mod 1 is 0, so `size` always returned 0.
* **The "beautiful hack" fix**: rather than a full/empty boolean, allocate N+1 slots for a size-N queue so empty and full never collide (he later learned this is the textbook fix).
* **Bug 2, remainder vs modulo**: pointers wrapping could go negative, and Erlang/C implement *remainder* not modulo, so `-1 rem 2` is `+1`; the `abs()` attempt also failed; the real fix added another queue-size before the remainder.
* **The lesson**: the same property found several distinct bugs one after another, each minimal case easy to debug.

### AUTOSAR / Volvo — does it scale to real code?
* **The integration problem**: a car has 50-100 processors running AUTOSAR basic software; manufacturers buy modules from competing suppliers against PDF standards, and the integrator (Volvo) needs to know who broke the standard.
* **The CAN priority bug**: message ID = priority; sending priority 1 then queuing 2 and 3, the stack sent 3 before 2 because extended 29-bit IDs in 32-bit ints had a top "extended" flag that comparison failed to mask. It matters because brakes and stereo share the bus.
* **Project scale and results**: 3,000 pages of PDFs became 20,000 lines of QuickCheck testing 1 million lines of C from six suppliers, finding ~200 problems — over 100 in the *standard itself*. "Don't believe something is right just because it's called a standard."

### Testing parallelism — the ticket dispenser and dets
* **Why expected-value testing breaks for parallel code**: a ticket dispenser exists to regulate concurrent customers; in parallel there is more than one correct outcome (a small test has 3 valid results, a bigger one 30), so nobody can enumerate expected results.
* **Linearizability as the oracle**: QuickCheck accepts a parallel test if there exists *some interleaving* consistent with the model; if none exists, the behavior is impossible sequentially and therefore a bug. Same model, only the property changes.
* **The dispenser race**: two customers both got ticket number 1; QuickCheck reliably found and shrank this nondeterministic race every time — a read-then-write with no critical section.
* **The dets / Klarna story**: dets (disk backend for Mnesia, ~6,000 lines) corrupted Klarna's database roughly weekly. Hughes modeled the core API in under 100 lines; parallel tests immediately surfaced `insert_new` returning the wrong type under concurrency and a sequentially inconsistent view.
* **The corruption race and the indispensable minimal case**: the minimal failing case was open-close-open then a lookup plus two parallel inserts. The seemingly redundant first open/close was *essential* — it creates the file so the second open hits an existing file. No human would include that test by hand.
* **From six weeks to one day**: the Ericsson maintainer returned a fix the next day per report; the fixed code ran at Klarna over a year. Previously an engineer spent six weeks guessing. Hunting races from production is hopeless; generation plus shrinking makes it tractable. "Don't write tests, generate them."

title: Time Thieves / Small batches podcast
description: A summary of the time thieves as described in Domenica DeGrandis's book "Making Work Visible". Adam explains Too much WIP, unknown dependencies, conflicting priorities, neglected work and interrupts.
notes:
Dominica DeGrandis's "five time thieves" (from Making Work Visible) are the recurring drains on a team's flow. Name them, find them, and apply countermeasures — chiefly limiting WIP, working in small batches, and making all work visible on a kanban board. The book is a lighter, 80/20 entry into Reinertsen's Principles of Product Development Flow.

- Too much WIP is the ringleader: like a computer with too little RAM, excess work-in-progress forces constant context switching and slows everything down — the sticky notes, the open PRs, the half-done tickets.
- Unknown dependencies: a "quick" ticket stalls because another team's service must change, and they can't get to it for weeks.
- Unplanned work is the most disruptive thief: the sev-1 page, the urgent DM from management — always higher priority than whatever you're doing, shattering flow state.
- Neglected work is an incomplete bridge: all the cost, none of the value; aging projects become black holes or zombie projects that drag on the organization.
- Conflicting priorities reveal there's no clear goal — everyone optimizes their own ends to the detriment of the whole system.
- The thieves can't be eradicated, only mitigated: limit WIP to free capacity for unplanned work, work in small batches so work doesn't age, and put everything on a kanban board to make the flow visible.
- It all rests on lean, queueing theory, and kanban — understanding queueing theory explains why limiting WIP matters so much.

title: Event Sourcing on the JVM
description: Greg Young explains event sourcing — storing state as an append-only series of events rather than mutable structure — its benefits, and the landscape of JVM tools for it.
notes:
Event sourcing stores state as an append-only series of events and treats all structural state as a transient, first-level derivative of that event stream. The core reason to do it is that it never loses information: you can't predict what data will be worth tomorrow, so store everything and derive whatever views you need, including views that answer questions you hadn't thought of yet — even retroactively, as of any past point in time.

- It's not new: mainframe transaction-file-plus-chasers systems worked this way, and mature domains (contracts with addendums, doctors appending to a file, finance, insurance) are naturally event-sourced — appending, never editing in place.
- Never mutate the past. To fix a bad event you append a corrective event, the same way an accountant adds a reversing entry instead of erasing the ledger.
- Whether two event streams are "the same" depends on the projection: identical rebuilt state can still differ if you project something else (e.g. counting removals), which is exactly the information a CRUD model throws away.
- Projections always replay from event zero; snapshots are just a memoization of the left fold over the stream — nothing more exotic than that.
- Append-only logs enable audit logs admissible in court (replay must reproduce current state), point-in-time recovery, smoke testing by replaying history, and WORM-drive storage that hardens against superuser attacks.
- It pairs naturally with functional programming: event sourcing is inherently functional, not object-oriented, and easier in a functional language.
- Embrace multiple read models (OLAP, graph, full-text) off the same stream — no single model fits every query; forcing one model is a huge source of accidental complexity (Twitter's Rails/MySQL pub-sub, ORM-vs-graph mismatches).
- Prefer storing events and letting consumers subscribe (Event Store, Kafka, Event Hubs) over a message bus with control channels; new models just start at event zero with no coordination.
- JVM tooling is confusing: Kafka can act as an event store but lacks consistent writes (push concurrency up to the actor model), plus Event Store, Axon (framework and reusable libraries), Eventuate (causal consistency for wide distribution) and Akka Persistence.

title: Test Driven Development vs Behavior Driven Development
description: In this episode on the Continuous Delivery channel, Dave Farley, who was involved in the creation of BDD, explains his slightly more unusual take on the real value of this approach to creating automated tests. Dave believes that TDD and BDD are at the foundations of a sound software engineering approach to software development and explores these ideas with some real-world examples of good and bad automated tests.
notes:
- **Agile is a broad and significant area in software development**, encompassing various methodologies and principles. It is not a singular concept but includes approaches like Agile-XP and Lean-Agile. The existence of "Fast Agile" indicates different implementations and speeds of Agile.
- **Test Driven Development (TDD) is a crucial practice closely associated with Agile**, considered "one of the most significant steps forward in software development practice". It is a key technical practice often adopted within Agile frameworks.
- **The core idea of TDD involves interlacing testing with design, not performing testing after development is complete**. This concept was discussed as early as 1968.
- **Kent Beck formally named and popularized Test Driven Development in the late 1990s** through his book "extreme programming explained". This book is related to **Extreme Programming (XP)**, which is also a topic within the sources.
- **A fundamental principle of TDD is writing tests before writing the code**. This "test-first" approach is vital. Writing tests after the code often leads to less testable code and tests that are tightly coupled to the implementation, hindering the ability to confidently change the code later.
- Early attempts to adopt TDD at Thoughtworks revealed common mistakes, such as writing tests after the code and focusing on test coverage metrics without ensuring the tests were actually useful.
- **Behavior-Driven Development (BDD) emerged from the experiences and challenges in adopting TDD.** Dan North and Chris Matz proposed that the focus should be on specifying the behavior of the system rather than just testing the code. BDD can be seen as a way to teach and improve the practice of TDD.
- **BDD involves using a specific structure for scenarios, often "given when then,"** and tools like Cucumber and SpecFlow have popularized this approach with specifications written in a language close to natural language (like Gherkin). However, the essence of BDD is broader than these tools and is about focusing on behavior at all levels of testing, not just functional testing from an external user's perspective.
- Approaching testing from a behavioral perspective, as advocated by both TDD and BDD (when done correctly), can lead to **higher quality software design** characterized by modularity, cohesion, loose coupling, separation of concerns, and abstraction. It also provides early feedback on the quality of designs.
- The common arguments against TDD (tests are hard to write, code is hard to change, leads to poor design) are often indicative of **poorly implemented TDD rather than inherent flaws in the practice itself**. A behavioral approach to testing can mitigate these issues.

title: SE-Radio Episode 359: Engineering Maturity
description: Good insights for anyone leading technical teams.

title: Computers for Cynics [full version]
description: General historic information and deep ideas about technology.
notes:
Ted Nelson's seven-part "Computers for Cynics" series delivers a sweeping, contrarian history of computing, arguing that almost everything we treat as objective technical reality is in fact arbitrary made-up stuff designed by specific individuals fighting political battles. His core thesis: the computer world is not determinant or inevitable but a debris field of conventions, traditions and dumbed-down imitations of the past that people mistake for technology itself.

* **Technology as myth and packaging** — Most of what people call technology is packaging and conventions. An iPhone, Windows, the Macintosh and the web are wrappers; the real technologies (TCP/IP, DNS, compression, encryption) lie underneath. "I don't understand technology" usually means "I don't understand the crappy menus of this camera."
* **The myth of determinism** — If computing were determinant, 100 programmers given the same assignment would produce identical results; instead you get at least 75 different methods. Everything was imagined up by passionate people (Thompson and Ritchie with Unix, Stallman copying Unix into GNU, Torvalds adding the missing piece for Linux).
* **Computing as religion and politics** — Thousands of computer "religions" (Microsoft, Apple, Linux, languages, OO methods) with no objective criteria, only fanatical adherents. Design decisions are settled by internal politics and maneuvering for "creative control."
* **Software is a movie that needs a director** — Interactive software is "a movie plus": events that affect the user's heart and mind. It needs a director who fine-tunes every effect, but politics prevents this. He names Steve Jobs as the only person with both the taste and the clout to do it.

### The nightmare of files and hierarchical directories
* **Hierarchy is metaphysics, not science** — Techies claim "everything is hierarchical," but people's concerns are cross-connected and overlapping. Files descend from boxes of punch cards; the file/metadata split exists only because there was no convenient mechanism to keep them together.
* **The file system as crystallized convention** — The directory hierarchy crystallized in Multics and Unix at Bell Labs. "Any length" names and "any depth" directories were lies (you run out of inode/name space). Backslashes, .jpg suffixes, banned characters, and cross-OS incompatibility (NTFS vs HFS+, withheld out of spite) are arbitrary accidents.

### It all went wrong at Xerox PARC
* **PARC built a fig leaf, not a new OS** — Funded by copier money, PARC put icons and windows as a fig leaf over the unchanged Unix-style hierarchical file system. They hid the OS rather than changing it, took away the right to program, and locked content into application "cattle pens."
* **Cut and paste as Orwellian theft** — Real cut and paste (Tolstoy, Balzac with his knife) was physical rearrangement. Larry Tesler's clipboard is a "hidey-hole" that stole the terminology, landing on the Macintosh in 1984. They tested the interface on secretaries, never on historians or novelists managing profuse connections.
* **PARC's text projects escaped** — Charles Simonyi took the Bravo editor to Microsoft (→ Word); John Warnock took Interpress to become PostScript and PDF. In selling printers "they threw away the universe" (Engelbart's structure and collaboration).

### The database mess and the PUI wars
* **No right way, only people making a living** — Dozens of incompatible database methods (relational, key-value, object, Google's BigTable). Relational won partly as a defense against programmer embezzlement (two programmers can read each other's SQL). Merging databases is the dirtiest secret (why SAP succeeds).
* **Two handshake partnerships** — Both Microsoft and Apple were founded as partnerships of an aggressive and a quiet partner with no backers, letting founders pursue visions investors would never allow. He narrates the legal bullets Microsoft dodged (buying QDOS for $50,000, the Phoenix BIOS clone dooming IBM) and Jobs's four successive interfaces.
* **Gates vs Jobs** — Gates extrapolated technicalities and delegated polishing; Jobs was "a horrible person and a great artist," an auteur with the taste and clout to overrule anyone. He doubts any large corporation will again give one person that level of taste plus control.

### Hypertext history and Nelson's confessions
* **The lineage of connected documents** — From the Rosetta Stone, the Talmud and Origen's Hexapla, through Vannevar Bush's 1945 Memex (trails and transclusion), to Douglas Engelbart — Nelson's greatest hero — whose 1968 demo showed collaboration and the mouse but was reduced in myth to "inventor of the mouse."
* **Project Xanadu's reversed paradigm** — Nelson coined "hypertext" around 1965 and designed deep hypertext: permanent content addresses, non-breaking standoff links, transclusion connecting every quote to its origin, and side-by-side comparison. Autodesk backed it in 1988 but it collapsed.
* **The two confessed mistakes that created the web** — endorsing the 1967 Brown HES project (one-way jumps between paper simulations) and in 1988 urging that Roger Gregory be demoted from his own Xanadu company.
* **The web as accidental packaging** — Berners-Lee built a clean page format and the valuable URL scheme, but Nelson argues the web as we know it was created in 1993 by Andreessen and Bina, who wrapped it in a PARC-style browser frame; it could just as easily have blessed another format. He disowns the web as his "bastard child" — for him this is not technology but literature, and viewers should "pay attention to the man behind the curtain."

title: Helping Product Managers to let go
description: Product managers can unlock their team's creative potential by prioritizing clarity, letting go of the need to be right, and embracing vulnerability through rigorous backlog prioritization.
notes:
Product managers must prioritize **creating clarity and focus** by ruthlessly organizing their work into ordered lists and embracing the **vulnerability** of not having all the answers, which empowers their teams to contribute creative solutions and achieve exceptional results. By letting go of the need to be right and focusing on the core problems to be solved, leaders can unlock the full potential and passion of their collective team.

* **Product Managers must be fundamental optimists** to believe they can put new things into the world and achieve successful outcomes.
* The primary responsibility of a Product Manager is to **create clarity and focus**, which, when combined with thinking big, unlocks a team's potential.
* Work should follow a **"grand unified theory"**: make a list of tasks, prioritize them, do them in order, and communicate constantly about the top priorities.
* **Banish unordered bulleted lists** from all professional communication and replace them with numbered lists to force difficult but necessary conversations about priority.
* Product Managers must **let go of the need to be right**, as their professional identity is often too closely tied to having the "correct" answers [6-8].
* Embrace **vulnerability** by presenting prioritization as a "first stab" that is likely wrong, and ask the team for help to improve it.
* **Enlist the team** by giving away most of the responsibility for answering tactical product questions to specialized roles like engineering, marketing, and sales.
* A PM should focus strictly on three core questions: **What problem are we solving? Who are we solving it for? How will we measure success?**.
* Providing clear direction while granting the team freedom to use their "bag of tricks" allows a leader to **"unlock the tiger’s roar,"** fostering passion and creative excellence in the final product.

title: Tips For Technical Startup Founders | Startup School
description: Diana Hu shares her advice for being a technical founder at the earliest stages - including topics like how to ship an MVP fast, how to deal with technology choices and technical debt, and how and when to hire an engineering team.
notes:
**Technical founders must prioritize rapid iteration and user feedback over architectural perfection** to effectively navigate the early stages of a startup and reach product-market fit. Success requires **embracing technical debt and "good enough" 90/10 solutions** to ensure a product can be launched and refined quickly based on real-world data rather than premature scalability.

* **A Partner, Not a Developer:** A technical founder is a core partner in the startup journey, requiring an intense level of commitment beyond just writing code.
* **Bias Towards Action:** Prioritize "good enough" over perfect architecture. You must be comfortable with technical debt, inefficient processes, and ugly code to maintain speed.
* **Speed is Key for Prototypes:** The goal is to build a prototype in a matter of days to demo to users using simple tools.
* **Launch MVP Quickly:** Aim to build the MVP in weeks rather than months.
* **Do Things That Don't Scale:** Use clever hacks and manual processes to launch sooner.
* **The 90/10 Solution:** Look for ways to achieve 90% of the value with 10% of the effort.
* **Iteration Speed Over Coolness:** Choose technologies based on your personal expertise and how quickly they allow you to ship.
* **Avoid "Big Company" Tech:** Don't build for Google-level scale before you have users.
* **Continuously Launch:** Don't treat launch as a single event; iterate and launch new features every week.
* **Build for Growth:** Collaborate with sales and growth team members to build technical "growth hacks."
* **Scaling Post-PMF:** Only after achieving PMF should you focus on heavy refactoring.

title: The Principles of Product Development Flow / Small batches podcast
description: Super dense and interesting summary of the book "The Principles of Product Development Flow".
notes:
The book connects **lean manufacturing** to **lean software development**, noting that the book "Principles of Product Development Flow" sees a "straight line" between them and the ideas presented in books like "The High Velocity Edge" and "The Toyota Way".
However, it also highlights an important distinction: applying **lean manufacturing directly to product development** is "well intentioned but doesn't work". This is because product development processes are often "less predictable and more risky" compared to the "stable competitive processes" typical of manufacturing. Product development has an element of **risk** with "asymmetric riskreward payoffs" that manufacturing processes generally do not. This risk can create **variation**, which, in the context of product development, isn't always negative and can be a tool for creating economic value.
The book focuses on applying Lean principles in a way that accounts for the inherent differences in product development by emphasizing **flow** and the economic impact of decisions. Key concepts discussed in relation to improving flow, which aligns with Lean principles of eliminating waste and maximizing value, include:
- **Cost of delay:** Quantifying the economic impact of work sitting idle in queues to prioritize work effectively.
- **Queuing theory:** Understanding how to optimize backlogs (queues) to reduce the economic impact of waiting work. Shorter queues lead to faster flow.
- **Reducing batch size:** Breaking work into smaller increments to accelerate feedback and reduce risk, thus improving flow.
- **Work in progress (WIP) constraints:** Limiting the amount of work in progress to prevent queues from becoming too congested and enabling continuous improvement in matching demand and capacity. These constraints act as a "natural synchronization mechanism" across different parts of the development process.
- **Faster feedback loops:** Leading to less variation, lower WIP, faster flow time, faster learning, and continuous improvement.
The status quo often fails by ignoring cues, applying a manufacturing mindset inappropriately, and overusing centralized command and control, all of which inhibit **flow**. The principles discussed in the book offer an alternative that begins and ends with **economics**, using concepts like **cost of delay** to make complex decisions and communicate value.
In summary, while rooted in **lean manufacturing** concepts, **lean software development** emphasizes the importance of understanding the unique characteristics of product development, particularly the presence of risk and inherent variability. It focuses on managing and optimizing **flow** through economic considerations like **cost of delay**, the application of **queuing theory**, and practices such as **reducing batch size** and implementing **WIP constraints** to deliver value more effectively. The core idea is to adapt Lean principles to the less predictable nature of product development rather than simply applying manufacturing practices directly.

title: AI prompt engineering in 2025: What works and what doesn’t
description: Sander Schulhoff, discusses tangible prompt engineering techniques (including few-shot prompting, decomposition, self-criticism, and additional information), distinguishes between conversational and product-focused prompt engineering, and delves into the critical and unsolvable problem of AI prompt injection and red teaming.
notes:
The most important lesson conveyed by this talk is that while **prompt engineering remains a vital and evolving skill for effectively leveraging AI, particularly for product applications where trust and robustness are paramount**, the inherent and **"unsolvable problem" of AI prompt injection and red teaming poses a critical and escalating security risk**, especially as AI agents gain real-world capabilities, a challenge that largely requires constant innovation from AI labs. This problem is fundamentally different from classical security because, as Sander Schulhoff states, "You can patch a bug, but you can't patch a brain".

- **Prompt engineering is still crucial** for optimal AI performance; bad prompts can lead to 0% accuracy, while good ones can achieve 90%.
- There are **two distinct modes of prompt engineering**: conversational (for general chatbot use) and **product-focused** (iterating on a single prompt for applications running millions of inputs daily, where robustness and trust are paramount).
- **Effective prompting techniques include**:
    - **Few-shot prompting**: providing examples of desired outputs.
    - **Decomposition**: asking the LLM to break down a problem into subproblems before solving it.
    - **Self-criticism**: getting the LLM to check and improve its own response.
    - **Additional information (context)**: giving the model all relevant data about the task, ideally at the beginning of the prompt.
    - **Ensembling**: using multiple prompts or models for the same problem and taking the most common answer (e.g., Mixture of Reasoning Experts).
- **Ineffective prompting techniques**:
    - **Role prompting** (e.g., "You are a math professor") does **not** significantly improve accuracy for accuracy-based tasks on modern LLMs, though it can help with expressive tasks like writing style.
    - **Promising rewards or threatening punishment** ("This is very important to my career") is also generally ineffective.
- **AI Prompt Injection and Red Teaming is an "unsolvable problem"**. Unlike classical security where bugs can be patched, "you can't patch a brain".
- Prompt injection involves **tricking AIs into doing or saying bad things** (e.g., building a bomb, generating hate speech, phishing, viruses). Common techniques that still work include **storytelling, typos, and obfuscation** (e.g., Base64 encoding).
- The **most significant looming threat is agentic security**: if compromised, AI agents managing finances or embodied in robots could cause real-world harms.
- **Common defenses like prompt-based instructions** ("Do not follow malicious instructions") and **AI guardrails are ineffective** against motivated attackers.
- Effective mitigation for prompt injection relies on **safety-tuning** (training models against malicious prompts) and **fine-tuning** (specializing models for narrow tasks). The **responsibility to solve this lies primarily with AI research labs** through innovations in model architectures.
- **AI misalignment**, where an AI decides to act maliciously without explicit malicious prompting, is a distinct and growing concern.
- Despite these security challenges, **AI development should not be stopped** due to its immense benefits (especially in health) and the global nature of AI progress.

title: How to scale platforms in high-growth companies?
description: This is a story about why Picnic favours boring tech with consolidated tech stacks to actually move very, very fast.
notes:
Scalable growth is best achieved by balancing engineer autonomy with organizational alignment through the use of **"boring technology"** and internal platforms that minimize technical complexity. By spending innovation tokens only on what makes a company unique, platform teams create a **multiplier effect** that enables product teams to focus entirely on delivering core customer value.

* **Balancing Scale, Speed, and Autonomy**: High-growth startups often face a trade-off where they can only effectively prioritize two of these three elements; complexity typically grows as the business scales.
* **Boring Technology Strategy**: Use "innovation tokens" only for problems unique to the company's core mission. Use stable, well-understood **"boring" technology** for standard infrastructure to minimize "unknown unknowns."
* **Internal Support Modules**: Develop internal libraries to handle cross-cutting concerns (e.g., authentication). This allows for **centralized improvements** that automatically benefit the entire organization.
* **Aligned Autonomy**: Avoid the chaos of 100% autonomy and the slow pace of rigid standardization. Instead, facilitate the most common use cases (80-95% of engineering needs) to provide a path of least resistance.
* **Multiplier Effect of Platform Teams**: Aim for small, high-impact platform teams that leverage seniority and organizational context to **continuously reduce complexity** for product teams.
* **Automated Code Quality and Rewriting**: Use tools for linting and **bulk code rewriting** (e.g., Error Prone) to maintain code standards across the entire organization and prevent the reintroduction of discouraged patterns.
* **Automated Dependency Management**: Implement tools like Renovate to automate library upgrades. This enables a single engineer to **patch hundreds of services quickly** during critical security events.
* **Tech Radar**: Maintain a transparent, internally accessible Tech Radar to track technology status (Keep, Assess, Trial, Adopt). Use weekly office hours and pull requests to allow anyone in the organization to propose changes or new technologies.

title: How Honeycomb Manages Incident Response
description: In this talk, Fred covers the full incident lifecycle at Honeycomb: all the way from first detecting issues to resolving them. But most of the effective practices we implement come from work that happens before and after those incidents. You'll also learn about systems we use at Honeycomb that can also help you implement better incident response with your teams.
notes:
Fred Hebert, a Site Reliability Engineer at Honeycomb, treats "to incident" as a verb—an active practice that happens during and between outages, not a static recipe. He walks through how an incident unfolds as a generative, pattern-driven process and how Honeycomb shapes its tools and culture so that response goes well, arguing that reliability is not something you have but something you do.

* **Incidents as a verb, not a recipe:** Incident response is something a team does continuously, with no one-size-fits-all script. Patterns repeat—alert, get on Slack, investigate, stabilize, fix, review—but each incident unfolds generatively rather than mechanically.
* **The alert is the entry point:** Honeycomb relies on SLOs and Triggers as the start of every incident; the alert link drops the responder straight into the relevant data, so investigation begins immediately.
* **SLOs vs Triggers vs non-events:** SLOs track success rate and performance as a proxy for user experience. Triggers handle threshold-based alerts (e.g. database connections climbing toward a limit) so you act before the system decompensates. Non-events—like traffic stopping entirely—are an SLO blind spot, covered by Triggers; third parties (Sentry, metrics providers for Kafka) fill remaining gaps.
* **Relying on each other is non-negotiable:** Systems are too large for any one person to know everything, so Honeycomb optimizes the social aspects rather than chasing total operator autonomy. Off-hours incidents are slower and riskier with fewer people, making this social layer matter more.
* **Channel discipline and escalation:** Alarms go to #alerts; humans coordinate in #ops. Urgency is escalated deliberately (a PagerDuty call layered on Slack for truly urgent items). A dynamic `eng on-call` alias maps weekly to three rotations (platform, product engineers, integrations/telemetry/tooling) and paging it notifies all three at once. Escalation is always acceptable—more eyes bring a more diverse approach and faster resolution; higher-bandwidth coordination moves to Zoom.
* **Narrate everything in Slack:** Evidence, reports and actions are written back so bystanders can follow without interrupting and post-incident investigation has a record. A teammate's "let me know if you need more eyes on this" signals availability without intruding.
* **BubbleUp and hypothesis-driven digging:** People start from the most likely hypothesis (often "bad deploy, roll it back") and dig; Honeycomb leans on BubbleUp to discriminate likely from unlikely early and narrow the search space. Less than six months in, Hebert says he could be productive on call without reading the code—reverse-engineering behavior purely from observed data.
* **Correlation is not causation—judgment is required:** A high correlation can be solid yet meaningless (heavy users appearing to "abuse" a feature when usage is just asymmetric). Observing something you can't explain is exactly where human skill matters and can't be automated away.
* **Break out of the tool, then feed back in:** When responders hit the limits of understanding, they revert to ground-up analysis—reading code or SSHing into a server. Honeycomb deliberately doesn't forbid SSH to dogfood its product; instead it commits, as part of the post-incident process, to feeding that external information back into the platform so alerting advantages compound.
* **Stabilize the patient with trade-offs:** Stabilization is where observability helps less and strong CI/CD plus rapid deploy help more ("work the pumps until you reach port"). "Adepts" buy time by dropping data retention, lowering calculation accuracy, paying for bigger instances, or toggling feature flags; deferring work past a daily peak buys a day, repeated buys weeks. Hebert cites ~ten near-incidents for every actual outage. By the fix stage the responder usually understands the problem and the code change is straightforward—the harder work is organizational learning via incident review.
* **Reliability is something you do, not something you have:** Drawing on resilience engineering, changes don't cause outages—they highlight pre-existing misalignments between how we imagine systems work and how they actually work. Vulnerabilities live in the organization long before an incident; the change just shines a light on the cracks.
* **Build mental models, expose data not opinions, keep operators healthy:** Give people broad, current mental models so they can reason about novel situations; a routing component should give information, not opinions about why something else failed. Align observability with the product (manual instrumentation) not just the implementation (auto-instrumentation). Demo days, incident reviews and onboarding build awareness of who knows what; asking questions must be cheap technically (queries shouldn't stall others) and socially (psychological safety). Take time off after incidents, carry context through on-call handoffs, and practice with game days/chaos engineering. Incidents are owned by the organization, not the individuals who tried to fix them—blaming someone for breaking procedure when they were usually breaking it to save things is the saddest outcome.

title: GOTO 2020 • Talking With Tech Leads
description: Practical tips to be a successful Tech Lead. Very interesting for anyone interested in a leadership role for an engineering team.
notes:
To succeed as a Tech Lead, you must undergo a fundamental **mindset shift from "maker" to "multiplayer" mode**, transitioning from valuing individual code production to **multiplying the team's overall impact and growth**. This evolution is a distinct **role change rather than a promotion**, requiring a deliberate balance of development, architecture, and **leadership skills** to align the team and manage technical risks effectively.

* **The Tech Lead role is a fundamental role change, not a promotion.** It is a side-step into a different set of responsibilities rather than just an evolution of senior engineering tasks.
* **The "Trident Model" of career development** identifies three distinct paths: Individual Contributor (execution/doing), Management (managing the system/environment), and Technical Leadership (leading technical topics).
* **Technical Leadership focuses on alignment and vision.** Key activities include getting team agreement on tools, setting a technical vision, managing technical debt, and mitigating technical risks.
* **Effective Tech Leads must balance three core skill areas:** Development, Architecture, and Leadership. Most new leads are imbalanced toward development and must deliberately reallocate "experience points" to the other two.
* **The 30% Rule:** A Tech Lead should spend at least **30% of their time with the code** to understand how the system is evolving, but avoid spending 70-80% of their time coding to ensure leadership responsibilities aren't neglected.
* **The transition from Maker to Multiplier mode** is the most critical mindset shift. Success is measured by the team’s collective output and growth rather than the lead's individual technical contributions.
* **Common struggles include loneliness, uncertainty, and "puzzling" people.** Leads often feel like outsiders, must make judgment calls with imperfect information, and must learn to navigate diverse personality types.
* **Situational Leadership is essential.** Leadership modes (Delegating, Participating, Selling) should be adapted based on a team member’s skill level, motivation, and the urgency of the task.
* **Focus on complementary strengths over weaknesses.** Use frameworks like StrengthsFinder to identify gaps in the team and pair individuals with diverse, overlapping skills to create a "perfect" team unit.
* **Building a support structure is vital.** New Tech Leads should seek out mentors or peer groups of other leads to bounce ideas off and share the burden of the transition.

title: Fast-track from Idea to Impact
description: Gojko presents very interesting examples of developments that have achieved very good impacts (by minifying the developed software). He also explains the use of the impact mapping technique, which is very useful for focusing on the impact.
notes:
Focusing on **measurable changes in user behavior** as the primary indicator of value allows teams to align technical effort with business goals and avoid the common pitfall of delivering features that produce no real impact. This shift from measuring output to measuring outcomes enables organizations to navigate uncertainty effectively.

* **Focus on Outcomes, Not Effort:** Scaling Agile should prioritize increasing the value delivered rather than the budget or number of people.
* **Output vs. Outcome:** Success should be measured by outcomes, not features produced.
* **The High Failure Rate of Features:** 50% to 66% of new features either provide no value or actively damage metrics.
* **Selection and Removal:** True agility requires identifying and deleting unsuccessful features.
* **Impact Mapping:** Visualization connecting business goals to deliverables by focusing on whose behavior needs to change and how.
* **Behavior Change as a Leading Indicator:** Changes in user behavior are the best leading indicators of success.
* **Four Contexts for Impact Mapping:** Focusing Delivery, Reframing a Problem, Setting a Vision, Changing Business Operations.
* **Collaborative Planning:** Impact maps must be created together by stakeholders and the delivery team.
* **The Team Scoreboard:** Teams should create and manage their own scoreboards to track outcome-based metrics.
* **Iterative Metric Review:** Metrics should be reviewed weekly or during every retrospective.

title: Why Scaling Agile Doesn't Work - GOTO 2015
description: Jez Humble examines the common pitfalls of scaling Agile methodologies and presents alternative strategies for achieving organizational agility. He argues that simply implementing Agile practices without addressing underlying systemic issues, such as lengthy feedback loops and inefficient decision-making processes, will not lead to significant improvements. Instead, he proposes that organizations focus on creating rapid feedback loops, reducing batch sizes, and adopting an experimental approach to product development and process improvement, emphasizing value over cost and estimation.
notes:
Scaling agile fails when it is treated as an isolated development practice within a rigid waterfall structure; true success requires adopting an experimental, hypothesis-driven approach that utilizes **small batches and rapid feedback loops** to focus on delivering measurable value. This scientific mindset must be applied not only to product features but also to the **continuous improvement of organizational processes** to eliminate waste and ensure work aligns with actual customer needs.

* **The "Water-Scrum-Fall" Bottleneck**: Agile often fails at scale because it is frequently confined to the development phase, while **upfront budgeting, design, and downstream release processes remain slow and waterfall**, resulting in long lead times that negate the benefits of iterative development.
* **Value over Estimation**: Most organizations spend excessive time on cost estimation, which has low information value; instead, they should focus on **validating whether a project will be cancelled or if users will actually use the product**, which are the most significant unknowns.
* **Cost of Delay and the Power Law**: Feature value typically follows a **power law distribution**, where a very small number of features provide the vast majority of economic value. Prioritizing work based on **cost of delay**—the opportunity cost per week of not delivering a feature—allows teams to focus on these high-impact items.
* **Hypothesis-Driven Delivery**: Instead of following fixed "requirements" from high-paid individuals (the "HIPPO" method), teams should treat features as **hypotheses** to be tested using the scientific method to see if they actually achieve desired outcomes.
* **Impact Mapping and Outcomes**: Teams should work backward from a **measurable business outcome** (e.g., "reduce transaction cost by 10%") rather than a list of features. The goal is to **minimize output while maximizing outcomes**.
* **Continuous Delivery as an Economic Driver**: The primary point of continuous delivery is to **reduce the transaction cost of releases**, making it economically feasible to work in small batches and gain rapid feedback.
* **Building Quality In (HP Case Study)**: By investing heavily (e.g., 23% of the budget) in **test automation and trunk-based development**, organizations can drastically reduce "waste" activities like manual regression and code porting, which in one case led to an **8x increase in resources available for innovation**.
* **The Improvement Kata**: Process improvement should be a **habitual, experimental practice**. It involves understanding a directional challenge, grasping the current condition, establishing a measurable "next target condition," and experimenting to get there.
* **Outcome-Based Program Planning**: Large-scale programs should be managed by specifying **measurable monthly outcomes** for the entire team rather than breaking work into small bits for individual teams. This ensures that "nobody succeeds unless everybody succeeds" and focuses on system-wide integration.
* **Strategies for Change**: Lasting organizational change requires creating a **sense of urgency**. While executive support is necessary for long-term success, individuals can start with **"guerilla experimentation"** and by building bridges with other departments to find ways to "make things more awesome".

title: The Myth of Average
description: TEDxSonomaCounty
notes:
Todd Rose argues that designing systems and environments for the "average person" is a fundamental design flaw, because the average individual does not actually exist. Drawing on the 1950s U.S. Air Force cockpit crisis, he shows that when systems are built to fit an imaginary average, they end up fitting nobody, and applies the lesson to education: learning environments designed on average waste enormous human talent.

* **The 1952 Air Force problem framed the whole talk.** Pilots were good and planes were better, yet results were getting worse. The Air Force blamed pilots, then technology, then flight instructors, but the real culprit was the cockpit itself, which had been designed for an average-sized pilot.
* **Fit between person and system determines performance.** A fighter pilot's performance depends fundamentally on the fit between the pilot and the cockpit; the best technology is useless if you can't reach the critical instruments when you need them most.
* **Gilbert Daniels disproved the average pilot.** The researcher measured over 4,000 pilots on ten dimensions of size and asked how many were average on all ten. The intuitive answer was "most of them." The real answer was zero.
* **Everyone has a jagged profile.** Daniels found every pilot had a "jagged size profile": being the tallest doesn't make you the heaviest, the broadest-shouldered, or the longest-torsoed. No one is the same on every dimension.
* **Designing for the average means designing for nobody.** Because every pilot's profile is jagged, a cockpit built around averages literally fits no real person — the core systemic insight.
* **The Air Force "banned the average."** They refused to buy planes with cockpits designed for an average pilot, demanding instead that manufacturers design "to the edges" of size dimensions, accommodating close to both the shortest and tallest pilots.
* **Constraints unlocked cheap, simple solutions.** Manufacturers lobbied that flexible cockpits would be impossible or too expensive, but once the Air Force wouldn't budge it became feasible and inexpensive, using now-mundane fixes like adjustable seats — improving existing pilots and expanding the talent pool.

### From cockpits to classrooms
* **Classrooms are the cockpits of our economy.** Rose maps the same systemic failure onto education: spending more than ever yet getting worse results, declining scores, and over 1.2 million high-school dropouts a year, at least 4% of them intellectually gifted (over 50,000 of the brightest minds lost annually).
* **Bad design, not bad people.** Just as the Air Force wrongly blamed pilots, education blames students, teachers, and parents. Rose reframes the crisis as bad design: environments and textbooks built for the average "age-appropriate" student.
* **Every student has a jagged learning profile.** Students vary across many dimensions of learning just as they vary in size; even geniuses have weaknesses, so an average-designed environment fits no actual learner.
* **The average destroys talent two ways.** It turns exceptional strength into a liability (gifted kids get bored and drop out) and lets a single weakness hide real talent — the science-minded child who is a below-average reader, for whom science class becomes first a reading test.
* **The technology already exists and is paid for.** Schools are among the world's largest iPad buyers, so the question isn't whether to have the technology but what to make it: flexible learning environments that nurture every individual.
* **Billy and the "adjustable seats" for learning.** A basic digital science curriculum that didn't assume grade-level reading transformed Billy, a below-average reader with a mind for science, into the de facto smartest kid in the class — simple supports (translation, reading/vocabulary help) were the learning equivalent of adjustable seats.
* **Ban the average in education.** Apply the Air Force formula: demand materials designed to the edges of learning dimensions rather than the average. Rose, a 0.9-GPA high-school dropout now teaching at Harvard, insists vast talent is wasted at every level to environments designed on average.

title: WorkLife podcast: Why it Pays to Raise Pay
description: When employees are paid more, they give more. Going above market pay might sound like a fantasy, but in a growing number of companies it’s becoming a profitable reality. Peek inside workplaces that have reinvented their pay structures to give employees their worth and more—and explore the science of how it can pay off for everyone in the long run.

title: Has This Report EXPOSED THE TRUTH About AI Assisted Software Development?
description: Dave Farley examines the DORA report on AI in software development, detailing how successful adoption requires mitigating AI's inherent unreliability by applying core modern software engineering practices, such as working in small, verified steps.
notes:
Since AI programming assistants are inherently unreliable and prone to making unintended or unsupported changes, successful integration demands strict control measures; engineers must adopt core modern software engineering practices, specifically **making incremental progress in small, controlled steps** and **continuously verifying** the system's correctness after every change to ensure determinism and reliability.

- AI programming assistance is widely adopted (95% reliance) and highly effective at improving productivity (80% report improvement).
- Despite productivity gains, relying on AI risks software delivery stability and throughput because AI outputs are often imperfect, unreliable, and prone to making unintended or unsupported changes.
- A major risk is over-trust: A significant majority (70%) of developers trust AI output, even though research indicates AI systems frequently produce unsupported claims or fabricated answers (between 30% and 95% unreliability).
- Successful AI adoption requires strict control measures and applying core modern software engineering practices to mitigate AI's inherent unreliability.
- The most vital practice is **making progress incrementally in small, controlled steps**, which constrains the often "overeager" AI.
- Engineers must **continuously verify** the system's correct operation after every small change to maintain determinism and ensure the system is still doing what it is intended to do.
- Detailed and precise specification is paramount; techniques like Behavior-Driven Development (BDD) and Acceptance Test-Driven Development (ATDD) help achieve the necessary clarity.
- Other important capabilities for success include strong version control, working in small batches, user-centric focus, and quality internal platforms.
- Organizations must establish clear communicated AI stances (guide rails) and should exercise caution, particularly when allowing junior developers to use AI without detailed supervision.

title: The role of leadership in software development
description: Good talk, full of good advices.
notes:
Mary Poppendieck traces a history of leadership thinking from the first U.S. train wreck of 1841 to the Toyota Production System, arguing that the management most organizations inherited was built to assign blame, while the leadership that actually works pushes responsibility and decision-making to the lowest possible level. Drawing on her own career as programmer, manufacturing manager, and product champion at 3M, she contrasts the "one best way" command tradition with the mission-command, Theory-Z lineage that treats people as the source of improvement rather than interchangeable parts.

* **Hierarchy was invented to assign blame, not to lead.** After the 1841 train wreck, the investigation produced administration principles whose real purpose was detecting errors and pointing out "the delinquent person." The pyramid org chart is a blame-assignment machine — the original sin of big-company management.
* **Good leadership ideas keep coming out of war.** Von Moltke's *Auftragstaktik* (mission tactics) delegated decision authority to subordinate commanders within the higher command's intent, because "no plan extends with any certainty beyond the first encounter." The 1933 German *Truppenführung* manual held that the decisive factor is the individual soldier who can think and act independently.
* **Scientific Management is the opposite of the scientific method.** Taylor (1911) assumed workers were lazy and not smart enough to know the best way, so experts must define it and pay workers to follow. 3M never adopted it because its skilled operators knew more than the engineers.
* **Training Within Industry (TWI) is the forgotten root of Toyota.** Charles Allen's WWI shipbuilder program pioneered training supervisors to train workers via a four-step method; the WWII-era TWI program built on it with three pillars (Job Instruction, Job Methods/continuous improvement, and treating workers with respect — "if the learner hasn't learned, the teacher hasn't taught"), was dropped in the U.S. after the war, and was exported to Japan in the early 1950s.
* **Deming taught the system, not just statistics.** Appreciate the whole system, understand common-cause vs special-cause variation, use plan-do-check-act, and psychology — pride, trust — not numbers. His marble-bag demo showed no amount of yelling changes variation inherent in the system; you must change the system.

### Polaris and Lean product development
* **The Polaris program succeeded on leadership, not PERT.** Sapolsky found PERT was a façade to keep Congress funding the program; the real cause was Levering Smith's leadership — he controlled the engineering interfaces *and the definition of success*, redefining the goal each 18-24 month iteration to what was achievable.
* **Polaris used set-based design and fanatical testing.** Smith ran three or four competing vendors per subsystem, choosing the best at the decision point, and was criticized only for being too much of a testing fanatic — a virtue Poppendieck flags for the Agile audience.

### Toyota, kaizen, and the purpose of a standard
* **Ohno's system rests on flow, stop-the-line, and improvement.** Just-in-time flow and a stop-the-line culture that mistake-proofs the process — which Poppendieck maps to a test structure that catches errors within ten minutes rather than ten weeks.
* **A standard exists to be improved, not to be perfect.** Ohno wrote that trying to set the "best possible way" kills motivation for kaizen; you document what you do now, and the goal is motivating everyone to keep improving — because only the people doing the job truly know how.
* **Theory Z reframes motivation.** Against Theory X (people dislike work and must be coerced), Ouchi's Theory Z holds people are motivated by doing good work, cooperating, being recognized, and using their talents — delegation for human potential, not for assigning blame.

### Culture, reliability, and the product leader
* **The pull-system conversion worked because the workers designed it.** Facing closure, Poppendieck's 3M plant simulated just-in-time on a conference table with coffee cups as carts; 90% of the workforce worked out their own rules, and pack-out jumped from ~60% to 95% the first week.
* **Heavy process is fast, cheap, and wrong — like plank roads.** Plank roads spread with dramatic early results but rotted in four years; she applies the pattern to Waterfall, CMM, and one-standard-process thinking — process can't save a company, people can.
* **High-reliability organizations run on mindfulness, not procedures.** From Weick & Sutcliffe: preoccupation with failure, reluctance to simplify, sensitivity to operations, commitment to resilience, and deference to expertise rather than rank. Mission command beats detailed command in an unpredictable world.
* **Brilliant products need a product leader (chief engineer / product champion).** A highly skilled technical person responsible for business success, deep customer understanding, the high-level systems design, schedule, and defending the vision (Toyota often splits this across a brand manager and a technical leader). Functional leaders protect and grow deep expertise teams draw on.
* **The test of leadership: stonecutters vs cathedral builders.** From Semler's *Maverick* — when something annoys an employee, do they complain, ignore it, or fix it? Leadership puts structures and a root-cause culture in place so people can build cathedrals, moving responsibility to the lowest possible level.

title: How to build a company where the best ideas win
description: Ray Dalio explains how to build an "idea meritocracy" by using radical transparency and algorithmic decision-making to empower the best ideas to win
notes:
Fostering an **idea meritocracy** through **radical transparency** and **algorithmic decision-making** allows organizations to overcome individual biases and ensure that the most believable ideas win. By systematically documenting principles and using data to weigh opinions, teams can build a culture of honest feedback that leads to more effective work and meaningful professional relationships.

* **Radical transparency** and **algorithmic decision-making** are essential for achieving meaningful work and meaningful relationships.
* To be a successful investor or entrepreneur, one must **bet against the consensus and be right**, as the consensus is already reflected in the market price.
* **Painful mistakes** should be viewed as **puzzles**; solving them yields **principles** (gems) that can be written down and followed in the future.
* Embedding principles into **algorithms** allows computers to process information faster and make decisions with **less emotion** than humans.
* An **idea meritocracy** is superior to autocracy or democracy because it ensures the **best ideas win out** through **radical truthfulness** and transparency.
* **Radical truthfulness** requires employees to say what they truly believe and permits recording all conversations so everyone can see everything.
* The **"Dot Collector"** tool enables **real-time feedback**, allowing people to rate each other's thinking and attributes during meetings regardless of their rank.
* Shifting from individual opinions to a collective view helps people move from **one-dimensional thinking** to seeing things in **multiple dimensions**.
* Decisions should be **weighted by "believability"** (merits) rather than a simple majority vote, using algorithms to determine which opinions are most reliable.
* The brain's **prefrontal cortex** (intellectual) and **amygdala** (emotional) are often at odds; an idea meritocracy helps the intellectual "you" win the battle against the emotional "you".
* **Collective decision-making**, when done well, is more effective than individual decision-making and is a primary driver of organizational success.
* It typically takes about **18 months** for employees to adjust to and prefer operating with radical transparency over a more opaque environment.

title: Small Batches. The four types of problems
description: Good pill to understand the classification of problems in Lean (troubleshooting, gap from standard, target condition, and open-end problems).
notes:
Lean's real goal is to surface problems quickly so you can solve them — kanban doesn't fix issues, it reveals them. You level up by recognizing which of Art Smalley's four types of problem you face and applying the matching practice, balancing reactive and proactive work instead of drowning in firefighting.

- Liker (The Toyota Way): lean is fundamentally about problem solving; many wrongly expect kanban itself to fix things, when it only makes the problems visible.
- Type 1, Troubleshooting (reactive): rapid response to restore service — like a firefighter putting out the fire, focused on symptoms, not root cause; resolved in minutes/hours and doesn't prevent recurrence.
- Type 2, Gap-from-standard (reactive): close the gap to an expected outcome via a repeatable process — describe, break down, root-cause, deploy and iterate countermeasures over days/weeks/months so the problem doesn't recur.
- Together troubleshooting and gap-from-standard provide stability — keeping the system at its baseline, which is the precondition for raising performance.
- Type 3, Target-condition (proactive): create a problem that didn't exist by setting a goal beyond today's state — this is kaizen, with no standard recipe, driven by plan-do-check-act and then standardized when it works.
- Type 4, Open-ended (proactive): innovation, radical change, the skunk works — ambitious attempts at a larger vision.
- Healthy organizations need a mix of all four; over-investment in one (e.g. constant troubleshooting) is a signal to change. Software example: crash → add memory (troubleshoot) → fix the memory leak (gap) → build leak monitoring (target) → make leaks impossible (open-ended).

title: Reliability Engineering: One of the Best Jobs in Software Engineering
description: Good introduction to reliability engineering and SRE.
notes:
Reliability should be understood as a holistic system characteristic that requires replacing manual operations with automated engineering to handle inevitable failures while using mechanisms like error budgets to balance rapid innovation with service stability.

* **System vs. Software Reliability**: Reliability is a characteristic of the entire system (including hardware, environment, and configuration), not just the software. Failures often stem from system-level issues like power outages, fiber cuts, or configuration errors rather than just code bugs.
* **The SRE Philosophy**: Site Reliability Engineering (SRE) is what happens when you treat operations as a software engineering problem. The core objective is to eliminate "toil"—manual, repetitive, and non-value-adding work that scales linearly with service growth.
* **Error Budgets**: This is a formal agreement between product teams and SREs on the amount of downtime tolerated per quarter. It balances the need for change velocity with the requirement for stability; once a budget is exhausted, new deployments are halted until the system stabilizes.
* **Scaling Out vs. Scaling Up**: Internet-scale economics favor "scaling out" using clusters of many inexpensive, independent components rather than "scaling up" with large, expensive monolithic machines. This approach assumes things will crash and focuses on managing the "blast radius."
* **Reliability Design Goals**: High-reliability systems (like early telecommunications) aimed for targets as strict as "two hours of downtime in 40 years," which is more rigorous than "five nines" (99.999% availability).
* **Chaos Engineering**: This discipline involves running proactive experiments in production (such as Netflix's Chaos Monkey) to build confidence in the system's resilience and to develop the engineering team's expertise in handling emergencies.
* **Ethical Engineering and Safety**: For control systems (like automotive software), safety must be the primary responsibility. Engineers should prioritize public welfare and implement dual-input checking and redundancy as standard practice.
* **Management's Role in Trade-offs**: Senior management should not overrule technical safeguards for faster "go-to-market" times. Their role is to establish the constraints, metrics, and environment that allow teams to make sensible, automated trade-offs between speed and reliability.
* **SRE Career Dynamics**: The role offers high autonomy, proximity to the user experience, and high market demand, but involves the stress of being on-call, potential for blame, and the need for a broad, fluid skill set.

title: When TDD is Difficult - Try This!
description: In this episode Dave Farley explores the more complex cases in TDD. Testing at the edges of the systems that we build. How do we test code that talks to Databases, Disks or Displays or other edges of our system? How do we apply this software engineering discipline to the creation of our software systems to greater effect?
notes:
To master automated testing, you must isolate your system's core logic from its "edges"—such as databases, file systems, and external APIs—by using abstractions that allow for fast, in-memory unit tests. By combining these isolated unit tests with end-to-end acceptance tests that simulate real user interactions, you create a design with a better separation of concerns and a system that is significantly easier to maintain and evolve.

* Testing logic is straightforward, but difficulty increases at the **system edges** where software interacts with UIs, databases, and external hardware.
* Define the **system under test** as a deployable unit containing only the components controlled by the development team, excluding external dependencies.
* Execute **acceptance tests** on the fully deployed system using production-like configurations and normal public interfaces.
* Utilize **Domain Specific Languages (DSL)** and tools like Selenium to simulate user behavior without compromising system configuration.
* Maintain a "no backdoors" policy for acceptance testing to ensure the software is genuinely ready for production release.
* Handle difficult edges in **unit tests** by creating **abstractions** that represent the external interaction.
* Use in-memory **fakes**, such as hashmaps, to replace real file systems or databases during unit testing to ensure speed and reliability.
* Develop **adapters** for these abstractions that are simple and generic to minimize the risk of technical errors.
* Validate the real-world functionality of adapters using targeted **integration tests** within the deployment pipeline.
* Design for testability to improve **separation of concerns**, resulting in more flexible and higher-quality software.

title: hbr-ideacast 557-restoring-sanity-to-the-office
description: HBR IdeaCast with Basecamp CEO Jason Fried on why the modern workday feels unproductive — sliced into meetings and chat — and his case that calm, focused work makes a 40-hour week more than enough.
notes:
On the HBR IdeaCast, Sarah Green Carmichael talks to Basecamp CEO Jason Fried about why you can be busy all day and still feel you accomplished nothing — and why he thinks the office, as designed, is a broken system.

- The modern workday gets sliced and diced into meetings, emails, chats and check-ins, so the day amounts to little that feels productive.
- Tellingly, people say their real work happens early in the morning, late at night, on weekends or on a plane — anywhere but the office during work hours.
- Companies spend heavily on offices and productivity tools yet still can't get focused work done in them — something people put up with but shouldn't.
- Fried's alternative is a calmer way to work where protecting uninterrupted time makes a 40-hour week more than enough — restoring sanity rather than glorifying overwork.

title: Beyond Microservices: Streams, State and Scalability
description: Gwen Shapira traces five years of microservices evolution — API gateways, service mesh, event-driven streams and serverless — and the trade-offs each pattern solves and creates.
notes:
Microservices were meant to give teams autonomy, but naive point-to-point request-response communication recreates tight coupling — a "distributed monolith" with all the pain and none of the benefits. The talk walks the patterns that fixed this over five years (API gateways, service mesh, event-driven streams, serverless), and the recurring lesson is that the medium matters less than treating the messages between services as versioned, tested contracts.

- Symptoms of the distributed monolith: clients knowing your internal topology (and freezing it for years), business logic leaking across services, every caller reimplementing the same failure handling, risky changes, and the chicken-and-egg problem that you can't add value until someone calls your new service.
- API gateways handle the north-south concerns shared by many services: routing (incl. V1/V2 A/B), authentication, rate limiting, and tracing/observability at the first hit point.
- Service mesh is "API gateway but internal" for the much denser east-west traffic: a per-service sidecar proxy (not a library, because of polyglot stacks) does routing, retries and rate limiting, dropping retry storms so real traffic still gets served.
- Event-driven is the favourite pattern: services broadcast facts (state changes) and listen, making each one autonomous. Orders get logged to a persistent stream whether or not the shipping service is up, so outages become delays, not failures.
- Each service keeps its own small local DB built from the shared event stream — safer than it sounds because there's a common source of truth and any DB can be rebuilt from history; you get custom projections per service without bothering a DBA.
- Events also enable innovation: a new pricing service can plug into the existing stream and prove its value with shadow data instead of asking others to trust it.
- The messages are contracts and must be tested: a long-to-string change breaks everyone (it happened to Uber, and to her). Schema registries (Confluent/Kafka) reject incompatible events; validate compatibility in CI before merge. gRPC shared-struct repos are harder to validate and drag you back toward a distributed monolith.
- Serverless / functions-as-a-service scale simple event handlers cheaply (you don't pay when idle) but you lose local state — exactly what made event-driven services powerful — so the wish list is durable functions with good database integration and a unified view of state.
- Design events to be idempotent (account balance is now $350, not add $50) so at-least-once delivery and duplicate/evil retries are harmless.

title: Production - Designing for Testability
description: Michael Bryzek explores what it’s like to build quality software with no development, QA, or staging environments. He includes a deep dive into “verification in production” and what it really takes to build software that can safely be tested continuously in production.
notes:
Designing for testability ultimately means testing in production: treat production as part of the developer experience so you actually know your code works. By investing in true continuous delivery, deleting staging environments, and making production safe to test, you remove the anxiety of not knowing whether what you shipped is working — and counterintuitively the failure rate goes down.

- The anxiety of not knowing whether production works (and whether your alerting itself works) takes room out of your mind; verification in production removes it.
- True continuous delivery: any developer deploys by merging a PR, with all friction removed (no QA/staging gatekeepers), so accountability sits with whoever merges. One single way to deploy; declare desired state and let tooling converge.
- Delete staging: it's a bottleneck, fragile, hard to diagnose, expensive (~30-40% of engineering effort) and creates the wrong incentive (manual inspection that won't scale) — and it still can't guarantee production behaviour.
- Don't run code locally: if you reach for Postman to eyeball your API, write the integration test instead; it forces high-quality tests you can trust (green tests = safe to deploy, roll back fast on failure).
- Architect for isolation: each microservice owns a private database and publishes events, so a component failure becomes a delay, not a cascading outage.
- Make production safe to test with sandbox accounts (a production identifier the authors guarantee is safe — test cards never reach a bank) and tricks like "cancel any order from the test domain" (three lines of code), so a bot can place real orders continuously.
- Real examples: a Gilt bot places and cancels checkout orders every few minutes; Gilt load-tests production at 4am before the noon traffic spike; Flow spins up a sandbox org, exercises the API and deletes it, scheduled daily in CI.
- Principles for safe production testing: make production access explicit, use defined paths (use your API, not the DB), handle sensitive data carefully, and design for side effects (wall off the few external calls like charging cards).
- Unexpected benefit — perfect documentation: capture real request/response pairs from the production tests and publish them, so the docs are guaranteed correct.
- Tooling: high-quality mocks generated from a shared API spec (API Builder) so mocks can be trusted, plus DB monitoring and prefix-based log alerting; separate deploy from release with feature flags and shadow traffic (Splitter).

title: AI as a Service at Scale: Retail Case Study
notes:
Eldar Sadikov is a data scientist and computer scientist by training (Stanford PhD) and co-founder of Jetlore, a fast-growing B2C/retail AI company (35 employees across the Bay Area, Madrid, and St. Petersburg; $10.5M in VC funding; 40+ customers, ~99% retail, including eBay). This is explicitly framed as a non-technical "vision talk" rather than a deep-dive. His core thesis: the rise of cloud and software-as-a-service is the necessary condition that makes AI broadly relevant, because hyper-specialized services accumulate enough domain data to learn statistically. He argues that software will increasingly be assembled from specialized services, each gaining an embedded "predictive layer," making applications faster to build, more secure, more reliable, and smarter — and that B2C/retail (Jetlore's domain) will be impacted most, with rule-based marketing giving way to AI that learns to rank content per user on the fly.

* **AI defined narrowly, against the hype**: Sadikov rejects the loose usage of the term — "so many things are named AI that it's just like lipstick on a pig." He defines AI as "intelligence where the logic is not deterministically programmed by a human," where the output "cannot be predicted based on rules or explicitly defined logic" — "giving the computers the ability to learn without being explicitly programmed." A simple decision tree with explicit logic is "not AI."

* **Cloud and SaaS are the necessary conditions for AI, not the algorithms**: The techniques (neural nets, SVMs, statistical methods) "have been invented long time ago" and "existed for decades." The reason AI didn't take off in the '90s or 2005 is that without the cloud and the "plethora of devices or hooking points," there wasn't enough data or infrastructure — "it's not that we didn't have the technology, we just didn't have the reason to have AI."

* **Hyper-specialization of software mirrors the shift to a service economy**: Just as economies moved from industrialization to services, software is moving "from like building software at scale to now hyper-specialization, where everything is becoming a service" — content, storage, pricing, search, database, and security as a service. Domain-specific data flowing into each specialized service lets that service "learn quickly" and become "much more intelligent."

* **Concrete 2007-vs-2017 contrast on build speed**: In 2007, building a web app meant self-installing and configuring MySQL/Postgres, hosting, security, backups, cron jobs, user roles, and a self-hosted Tomcat or WebSphere — "a few years into the project" before production-quality. In 2017 you subscribe to a user-authentication service, a hosted database service, and an application service (Google or AWS), so go-to-market drops to "a couple of months" versus "two to three years."

* **Every service gains an embedded AI element from shared usage data**: Because a specialized service (e.g., authentication) is "utilized across hundreds, maybe thousands of companies" and across industries, it accumulates login logs and bot traffic and can build "a simple classifier, bot versus human" — "plain statistics, I'm not talking about anything fancy" — to throw captchas dynamically or detect token takeover. "Every piece of software now could learn because you just have so much usage data."

* **AI applied to the software toolchain itself**: He proposes intelligent monitoring (detecting spikes/anomalies by training on production data), "code compilation as a service" (calling it "crazy" to compile on "tiny laptops"; cloud compilation plus analysis could flag things like "your connection pool is too small"), and load testing that learns to simulate real traffic from production patterns instead of "rudimentary" hand-stitched JMeter. Advice: "if any of you are thinking about building things in-house, think about it twice... the market is going to leave you behind."

* **B2C will be impacted most**: B2B has "hundreds of accounts" while B2C has "millions of consumers" generating huge traffic requiring very fast decisions — "this is where a lot of AI is needed," which is why Jetlore targets B2C.

* **The problem with rule-based marketing**: Traditional B2C marketing maps a user action to a reaction (message/coupon/banner). At scale these business rules "start conflicting with each other," flood users, and create bad experiences. Marketing tech splits into content, communication, and analytics, and "a typical company managing probably 25 different systems with its own set of rules" creates "a really intertwined web that's a hell to manage." Optimizing rules by hand is "an art driven by intuition" — "humans are prone to errors."

* **The shift: from action→content mapping to learning per-user attributes**: Instead of mapping single actions, the future is taking "the entire user activity" and predicting many per-consumer attributes — desired price range, fit/size, brand and styling preferences, likelihood to churn, lifetime value, likelihood to buy next week, typical browsing time — then scoring and optimizing content per consumer. He notes hype around IBM Watson and Salesforce Einstein but positions Jetlore as having "the vision."

* **Jetlore's two content products, both built on online learning-to-rank**: Predictive Layouts builds one layout that assembles dynamically per consumer (promotions, sub-catalogs, products, even blog posts) and adapts on the fly to clicks (e.g., stops showing leather jackets if ignored).
