Predictive Ranking re-sorts product listing pages — which "drive probably 50% of all traffic" and are normally sorted statically by new arrivals/best sellers — using product attributes (fit, styling, price, color, sleeve length) and live user reactions, retraining the model on the fly.

* **Measurable results and the eBay case**: Jetlore drives "anywhere from 30 to 70% uplift in sales" because it trains across many retailers rather than one. For eBay, the win was traditional newsletters (where most customers aren't in buying intent), dynamically varying content one-to-one so "no two customers will ever see the same content back-to-back," measured via an A/B test against an internal best-sellers-in-last-browsed-category approach. He dismisses "quasi-metrics" like email-trigger uplift since those users were "already low in the funnel."

* **Technical Q&A — real-time scoring, not collaborative filtering**: Unlike collaborative filtering / matrix factorization ("precomputed recipe... putting a user into a segment"), Jetlore does real-time scoring with an online feedback loop, scoring "anything below a million items within sub-second range" via "native code optimization." Each action breaks into hundreds of features, yielding "thousands of attributes per user." Non-repetitiveness and diversity are stated optimization criteria. On the future, he favors adding a "predictive layer" to standard software functions (login, networking, security, compilation, load testing) and "verticalized" AI solutions (pharma, hospitality, retail, banking) — "I don't believe in general-purpose AI, I don't believe in Watson." He likens the coming productivity boost to "the industrial revolution, but... on a software scale," and declined to reveal the model/training details ("if I tell you, I have to kill you").

title: Implementing Programmer Anarchy
description: GOTO Chicago 2014. Very good points about hiring, transformation and real power for the developers.
notes:
Fred George recounts how he and the team at a London startup called Forward built what he provocatively named "Programmer Anarchy": an organizational model that strips out managers, business analysts, QA, and iteration managers, leaving only two roles — people who understand the domain and developers who build it. He frames it as a direct descendant of Kent Beck's Extreme Programming (renamed, as XP became the blander "agile"), born from a simple desire to go faster, and proven across the Forward startup, the 120-year-old Daily Mail / Mail Online, and the Silicon Valley startup Outpace.

* **Anarchy is a name, not an invention.** George admits he didn't invent the practices; his contribution was naming it. "Anarchy" was deliberately controversial, chosen partly as a recruiting magnet in London while competing against Google and banks paying double for C++ programmers.
* **Agile is fundamentally a social change.** C-level executives grasp that a social shift is coming, but risk-averse middle managers don't, which is why agile efforts end up half-baked: a team does something spectacular and the organization quietly kills it. The change has to be driven from the top.
* **Story tyranny is the problem anarchy solves.** In mature agile shops driven entirely by small stories, programmers feel like drones subject to customer whims. George wants to move interaction up the requirements hierarchy from the story level to the feature level: tell developers what you want to accomplish, then get out of their way.
* **Kill QA — same skills as developers.** Modern QA must write code (Selenium, Cucumber) and understand architecture, so the distinction evaporates; one-time acceptance testing before deploy is obsolete for live systems, so QA folds into developers' constant production monitoring.
* **Kill the BA — a game of telephone.** The BA listens to the customer who really knows, repeats it, and runs back for answers. George wants to talk to the real domain experts directly, with no intermediary — developers can learn accounting as easily as a BA could learn Clojure.
* **Kill the iteration manager — a fabricated role.** George traces it to a ThoughtWorks consultant who, told a client already had project managers, rebranded the same job as "iteration manager."
* **Decompose the manager into distributable functions.** Kent Beck's XP book has no manager, only a "clerk" who tracks stories. George teases apart clerk, leadership (often a developer is the natural leader), coaching, plus two roles teams actually want.
* **Ambassador and concierge are the keeper roles.** The ambassador represents the team's interests to outsiders but cannot commit to schedules/dates on its behalf — anything outside agreed parameters goes back to the team. The concierge serves the team, procuring whatever it needs (a management inversion).

### Letting programmers run loose
* **Forward shipped to production every 3-5 minutes.** Across a 40-hour week, achieved by killing roles and processes while propping things up with DevOps to move fast safely.
* **Programmers rewrote freely, and it paid off.** Rewriting an energy-switching system, developers chose Clojure, Ruby, C++, Node.js, R, MySQL, and MongoDB at once. When energy prices spiked and the old .NET system fell over, the new Clojure calculator absorbed 100% of traffic on a single VM and made money. Click-tracking rewritten from Ruby (32 servers) to Node.js (22 servers, lower latency). George admits he'd have stopped both rewrites had he had the power — not having it was a good thing.
* **Mistakes are affordable.** Having replaced non-developer roles with programmers, you can afford for half your programmers to make mistakes and still come out ahead.
* **Most agile best practices disappeared.** Co-location killed standups, narratives, retrospectives, and estimates; microservices killed the big balls of mud that pairing/testing/refactoring exist to manage; continuous integration gave way to continuous deployment.

### A 120-year-old company and beyond
* **Mail Online needed software as fast as its journalism.** The publisher could put an article online in 20 minutes yet software shipped on a 3-6 month cycle with siloed specialists. George targeted a one-month feature cadence with poly-skilled workers, tearing out desks for legless tables and unassigned seating.
* **Master/journeyman/apprentice productivity.** Apprentices ~1x, journeymen ~2x, masters 5-10x. A new HR ladder added the "systems developer" (poly-skilled across 5-7 key technologies, paid like a senior developer to incentivize breadth); key technologies capped at 10-12, so self-learning was motivated.
* **Programmers self-allocate.** People roll off a project when they judge they're no longer needed; programmers know who the best database person is better than managers do.
* **The result: 130,000 lines of Java replaced by 4,000 lines of Clojure.** Absorbing 20 new hires in two months, the team replaced the entire Mail Online front end (3.2 PB/month) running faster than the old code — a rewrite two prior CTOs had failed at and been fired over.
* **Flat, anarchic organizations are powerful recruiting tools.** At Outpace everyone is hired simply as a developer; people left banks, took pay cuts, and commuted 90 minutes for the quality of the work.

title: How to gain control of your free time
notes:
Laura Vanderkam, who writes about time management, argues that the popular approach of shaving minutes off everyday tasks gets it exactly backward. We don't build the lives we want by saving time; we build the lives we want, and then time saves itself. Drawing on time diaries from extremely busy people, she reframes time management as a question of priorities rather than efficiency tricks.

* **The flawed premise of time-saving tips**: Magazines ask how to "find an extra hour" by shaving minutes—doing only right-hand-turn errands, gaming microwave times, DVRing shows to fast-forward through commercials to save 32 minutes for exercise. Her retort: another way to find 32 minutes is to not watch two hours of TV a day.
* **Time saves itself, not the reverse**: After studying successful people's schedules hour by hour, she concludes we don't save bits of time and add them up to finally do what we want—we put what matters first and the time follows.
* **The broken water heater story**: One woman in her study of 1,001 days came home to a broken water heater flooding her basement; dealing with plumbers and cleaning crews consumed seven hours of her week. Asked at the week's start to find seven hours to train for a triathlon, she'd have said "can't you see how busy I am?"—yet the emergency made seven hours appear.
* **Time is highly elastic**: We cannot make more time, but time stretches to accommodate whatever we choose to put into it. The key is treating our priorities as the equivalent of that broken water heater.
* **"I don't have time" means "it's not a priority"**: A woman running a business with 12 employees and six children was out on a spring-morning hike; every minute she spends is her choice, so instead of "I don't have time for x," she'd say "I don't do x because it's not a priority"—more accurate language.
* **The $100,000 dust test**: Vanderkam could say she has no time to dust her blinds, but if offered $100,000 she'd do it fast. It's not a lack of time; it's that she doesn't want to—time is a choice, even if some choices carry horrible consequences.
* **Write next year's performance review now**: Pretend it's the end of an amazing year and identify the three-to-five things you did that made it so great—planning forward rather than only reviewing backward.
* **Write next year's family holiday letter now**: Imagine the year's end and write the (unsent) holiday letter describing what mattered. Between the review and the letter you get six to ten goals for the year.
* **Break goals into doable steps**: A family history starts with reading others for style, drafting questions, and scheduling interviews with relatives. A 5K starts with finding a race, signing up, building a training plan, and digging the shoes out of the closet.
* **The Friday-afternoon planning ritual**: Schedule priorities first by thinking through weeks before you're in them. Friday afternoon is a "low opportunity cost" time to think about what those priorities should be.
* **The three-category priority list**: A short list—career, relationships, self—with two to three items each, then look across the coming week to plan them in. The three categories remind us all three deserve attention.
* **There are 168 hours in a week**: A 40-hour job plus 56 hours of sleep still leaves 72 hours; even 60-hour weeks leave 52. A study found people claiming 75-plus-hour weeks overestimated by about 25 hours. Use bits of time for bits of joy—read something wonderful on the bus, swap an impossible family dinner for a family breakfast—because even when busy, we have time for what matters.

title: Gojko Adzic On How Agile Failed at the BBC and the FBI | The Engineering Room Ep. 3
description: Dave and Gojko chat about a wide-ranging series of topics on product development, steering development organisations to success, Palchinsky principles and how agile development failed for the FBI and the BBC.
notes:
**Focus on driving and measuring meaningful changes in user behavior rather than just delivering features, as technical excellence provides no value if it fails to solve real problems.** You should remember to evaluate outcomes within your sphere of influence instead of simply tracking tasks within your direct zone of control.

* **Value through Behavior Change**: The most effective way to measure and design value in software is through observable changes in user behavior rather than high-level business goals like profit or market share, which are often too detached from weekly deliverables.
* **Impact Mapping**: A strategic tool used to visualize the big picture and align technical deliverables with the specific behavioral changes required to reach business objectives.
* **Zones of Control vs. Spheres of Influence**: Measuring metrics within a team's direct "zone of control" (e.g., story points, code coverage) is often misleading. Teams should focus on measuring their "sphere of influence"—whether their assumptions about user behavior and value are playing out correctly.
* **The "Agile" Failure Mode**: Large-scale projects (like those at the BBC and FBI) often fail despite using Agile because the methodology is used to justify changing requirements without ever validating if the features delivered are useful or needed.
* **Polchinski Principles for Planning**: Effective planning in uncertain environments requires three things: variation (having more ideas/options than you intend to implement), survivability (ensuring one failed idea doesn't kill the whole project), and selection (the brutal removal of features that do not deserve to be maintained).
* **Feedback and Sensor Poisoning**: It is common for organizations to measure what is easy to measure rather than what is important. Measuring things proportional to effort (like velocity) provides a false sense of progress and can lead to "sensor poisoning," where the feedback loop becomes misleading.
* **The Evolution of Selection**: While the industry has improved at building small things (survivability), it remains poor at "selection"—the process of identifying and removing code or features that no longer deliver value.
* **Serverless as an Equalizer**: Modern cloud technology has drastically lowered the barrier to entry, allowing very small teams to handle massive scale, implement canary releases, and use advanced monitoring that previously required thousands of employees.
* **Importance of Protocols over Syntax**: In distributed and serverless systems, defining the communication protocol between components is the most critical architectural decision. A correct protocol allows components to be fixed or replaced independently.
* **Distributed Consistency**: Abstractions like serverless do not absolve engineers of the need to design for eventual consistency. In high-volume systems, "one-in-a-million" edge cases become daily occurrences that must be handled by design.
* **Systems Thinking and Human Factors**: Engineering problems are often actually human or system problems. Improving productivity may require addressing non-technical factors like worker welfare or environmental conditions rather than just optimizing code.

title: Applying the Serverless Mindset to Any Tech Stack
description: Good talk about how serverless enables a new way of thinking.
notes:
**Serverless is a mindset focused on maximizing business value by treating technology as a liability and offloading non-core tasks to managed services.** It is a continuous journey of reducing operational distractions to focus entirely on the unique differentiators that provide actual value to your customers.

* **Serverless is a mindset and a direction**, not a specific technology (like Lambda) or a final destination.
* The primary goal of any technical choice is **delivering business value**; technology itself is never the point.
* **Focus** is the ultimate "why" of serverless—it is designed to give you time back to work on the specific problems your business cares about.
* Most of any product consists of **"table stakes"** (features required just to compete); focusing effort on making these 20% better than competitors is a waste of time.
* Organizations should identify and prioritize their **differentiators**—the unique value that actually makes customers choose them.
* **Code is a liability**, not an asset. It inherently contains bugs and requires ongoing maintenance; the less code you own, the better.
* **Configuration is your friend**. While developers may prefer the feeling of coding, configuration often delivers the necessary value faster and with less long-term burden.
* Follow a **hierarchy for technology selection**: 
    1. If the platform has it, use it.
    2. If the platform doesn't have it, buy it.
    3. If you can't buy exactly what you need, rethink your requirements to fit what is available.
    4. Only build it yourself if it is a core differentiator.
* **Accept a lack of control** when using managed services. Specialized providers have economies of scale and expertise that allow them to run services more reliably than an internal team, even if you cannot personally "fix" their outages.
* Serverless is a **ladder of progression**. Moving from on-prem to cloud, or VMs to containers, are all valid rungs on the ladder toward reducing overhead and increasing focus.
* Practice **minimalism** by removing components, options, and complexities that do not directly contribute to providing business value.
* **Automate yourself out of a job** to ensure you are always moving toward higher-value tasks and solving more complex business problems.

title: The Continuous Culture
description: #LeadDevNewYork 2018
notes:
Kim van Wilgen argues that continuous delivery is only the first baby step toward what really matters: a continuous culture built around experimentation and fast learning. Drawing on the State of DevOps report and the Cynefin model, she frames software development as a complex domain where cause and effect can't be predicted in advance, so the only way to find value is to probe, experiment, and ruthlessly eliminate what doesn't work. She walks through nine lessons spanning delivery, product, compliance, HR, teams, testing, and architecture.

* **Large projects fail, small ones learn**: Per the State of DevOps report, large projects have only a 10% success rate versus 74% for small ones, and carry 64% unused features versus 14% — not because small teams guess better, but because they learn faster which experiments failed and eliminate them. The cheapest code to maintain is code that isn't there.
* **Software lives in the complex domain**: Using Cynefin, treating software as merely "complicated" (solvable by analysts writing 1200-page reports) is the worst mistake. In the complex quadrant cause and effect can't be understood upfront, so you must probe and experiment — which is why A/B testing works.
* **Lesson 1 — Safely and sustainably deliver software**: CD is the baseline that makes incremental change possible. If deployments cause panic or overtime, you're not ready. The cited record is 7,448 deploys/day (one every 11.2 seconds).
* **Lesson 2 — Product development that ships value small**: Value only appears when users start using it, so she kills year-long roadmaps and sprint estimation, rewarding the number of issues pushed to production to incentivize the smallest possible slices.
* **Effort/value triage**: A weekly one-hour portfolio board sorts ideas; "maybe" ideas go to a parking lot for two months — you minimize effort discussing code you aren't building.
* **Four key metrics**: Citing Humble — lead time and release frequency (tempo), plus time to restore service and change fail rate (quality); better teams improve both at once.
* **Lesson 3 — Bring compliance and security along**: Auditing accepted hourly releases once everything was in version control with peer review, only-green-to-production, and conformity by design. Monthly internal hacking sessions, automated vulnerability scanners, and canary releasing replace fallback plans and release comms.
* **Lesson 4 — Reinvent HR and management for autonomy**: One director left his office to sit among teams, then let go; the management team replaced four-hour weekly meetings with a transparent daily standup and a "Blue Room" wall showing vision. Research shows employees value autonomy, respect, and responsibility, not ladders.
* **Flip feedback to compliments**: Annual reviews built on "you do great, but improve here" induce fear; instant, frequent compliment apps give endorphins that reinforce the right behavior.
* **Teams and learning**: Endless DevOps-vs-DevSecOps naming debates are the opposite of collaboration. Learning (conferences, new languages, an internal Academy, hackathons) is made visible on Jira boards and treated as real work.
* **Microservices and an inverted test pyramid**: ~400 microservices let them replace a service rather than fear production. She inverts the test pyramid by risk, not speed: test early what changes most, and push too-expensive tests toward monitoring.
* **Branching is your mother-in-law**: Branching delays when you learn your code breaks things. Prefer co-creation, hidden back-end code, feature flags, and only then branching. Release strategy layers acceptance, rehearsal, a canary production environment (~1% of clients), and feature flags.
* **Lesson 8 — Customers don't want continuous delivery**: They care about broken interest (bugs per hour across ~10M lines of COBOL and 3M of PHP). Take customers on the journey — improve first, then accelerate; pull rather than push.
* **Lesson 9 — Be ready for a surprise**: The path of success is not a straight line up; it will drain your energy, but hang in there because it's worth it.

title: From Kubernetes to PaaS to Developer Control Planes
description: Interesting talk on trends to remove cognitive load from developers in the era of cloud native services. Interesting ideas on how to develop a platform as a product (UX, focus on developer workflows and tool interoperability).
notes:
To overcome the cognitive load of modern cloud-native development, organizations must treat their internal platforms as products by prioritizing user experience and integrated workflows that provide a seamless developer journey. This approach requires shifting focus from managing a sprawl of tools to building opinionated, self-service interfaces that emphasize interoperability and reduced friction for engineers.

* **Treat Platforms as a Product**: Platforms should be staffed with dedicated roles—including Product Managers and UX designers—and managed as internal products with clear goals and iteration based on user feedback.
* **DevEx Requires Good UX**: High-quality Developer Experience (DevEx) is impossible without proper User Experience (UX) principles. This applies to both graphical dashboards and command-line interfaces (CLIs).
* **Manage Developer Cognitive Load**: The complexity of modern cloud-native ecosystems has caused developer cognitive load to spike. Platforms should aim to provide a PaaS-like (Platform as a Service) experience on top of Kubernetes to simplify the developer journey.
* **The Developer Control Plane**: This concept provides a unified layer—often a thin UI or service catalog—that integrates the "code, ship, and run" phases, reducing the friction caused by tool sprawl.
* **Full Lifecycle Ownership**: The industry is shifting toward "full lifecycle developers" who are responsible for the entire process of coding, shipping, and running their applications, rather than relying on traditional hand-offs to operations teams.
* **Focus on Workflows and Interoperability**: Success depends on consistent end-to-end workflows and tooling interoperability. Using standards like the Kubernetes Resource Model (KRM) and Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) allows different tools to work together with minimal custom configuration.
* **GitOps as the Standard Protocol**: Git is increasingly accepted as the single source of truth for both application code and infrastructure configuration. GitOps—using a reconciliation loop to sync state—is becoming the best-practice workflow for deployment.
* **Apply Team Topologies**: Utilizing the Team Topologies framework helps organizations structure platform teams to provide self-service capabilities, effectively reducing the burden on stream-aligned (application) teams.
* **User Research and Observation**: Platform teams must conduct user research and actively observe developers as they use tools to identify points of friction and ensure the platform actually meets user needs.
* **Standardization and Opinionated Paths**: To reduce friction, platforms should offer centralized, opinionated ways of doing things while still allowing for flexibility when developers need to "break out" for specific use cases.

title: Scaling Organizations and Design with James Shore
description: Very interesting conversation with James Shores. It covers many topics, including fast-agile, mobbing, testing without mocks, technical practices, etc. Highly recommended.
notes:
To achieve sustainable growth and high-quality software, organizations should embrace **fluid team structures** that self-organize around immediate needs while practicing **evolutionary design** to keep the cost of change low.

* **Horizontal vs. Vertical Scaling**: FAST involves a large group acting as a single team that self-organizes into sub-teams on a very short cadence (every two days).
* **FAST Mechanics**: Teams meet at the start of the cadence to decide on tasks and volunteer for sub-teams based on interest and skill.
* **Reflective Design**: Evaluate if the current design is effective for today's problems. "Make it easy to change, then make the easy change."
* **Design for Today**: Focus on making the design perfect for the problems being solved right now.
* **Long-term Health**: A 10-year-old codebase maintained this way can be higher quality than it was at age five.
* **Sociable State-Based Testing**: Tests allow the real code of dependencies to run, validated by checking return values or state changes.
* **Nullable Infrastructure**: Production code is designed with "nullable" versions that perform no external I/O but still execute real production logic.
* **Refactor/Red/Green/Refactor**: A broader "outer loop" involves refactoring the design first to enable the next red-to-green cycle.

title: Oredev 2011: Sleeping with the enemy
description: Gojko Adzic describes why independent testing should be a thing of the past. He explains how testers engaging with developers and business users create opportunities to accomplish things they cannot do otherwise.
notes:
Effective software delivery requires replacing traditional silos of distrust with a collaborative model where **testers move to the beginning of the development cycle** to define quality and drive meaningful outcomes. By shifting the focus from effort-based metrics to **shared ownership of quality**, developers can take responsibility for test automation while testers provide strategic value through exploratory testing and process guidance.

* Traditional software delivery models are often rooted in systemic distrust between business, developers, and testers, leading to inefficiencies despite a shared goal of customer satisfaction.
* Independent testing as a siloed, end-of-process activity acts as a bottleneck and should be replaced by early QA involvement.
* The role of "tester" should shift from being the person who does all the testing to the person who ensures the right testing gets done.
* Quality ownership should be a shared responsibility: programmers should automate tests while testers define the right test cases to automate.
* Moving QA from the end to the beginning of the development process allows them to guide development rather than just validate it after the fact.
* Automating routine "checks" allows testers to focus on high-value, critical activities such as exploratory testing.
* Story points and other effort-based metrics are ineffective and easy to manipulate; productivity must be measured by outcomes and actual value delivered.
* Successful software delivery relies on facilitating strong communication and trust among business users, developers, and testers.

title: Benefits Of Going Beyond The Make-It-Work Phase
description: Interesting talk about the benefits of using TDD in embedded systems. The talk is full of useful tips, patterns, and strategies very useful in both embedded and non-embedded environments. Special mention to how Francisco uses mutation testing in the development process.
notes:
Embedded developers should transcend the "make-it-work" phase by using Test-Driven Development and architectural abstractions to decouple business logic from specific hardware and vendor dependencies. This approach treats hardware as a replaceable low-level detail, effectively removing development bottlenecks and ensuring that software remains portable, testable, and sustainable over the long term.

* **Embedded systems** are composed of hardware, firmware, and software, where **firmware** is specifically defined by its dependencies on hardware and its tendency to become obsolete as that hardware evolves.
* Software development should ideally follow three distinct phases: **"Make it work"** (understanding and solving the problem), **"Make it right"** (engineering the code for maintenance and evolution), and **"Make it fast"** (meeting performance requirements).
* The **"Main God"** anti-pattern—where a single module handles everything including hardware interaction—creates code that is tightly coupled to specific microcontrollers and cross-compilers, making it non-portable.
* A **"Hardware Bottleneck"** occurs when developers are forced to debug directly on hardware, making it difficult to isolate whether errors are coming from software, firmware, or the hardware itself.
* The **"Vendor's Trap"** is the mistake of treating vendor-provided tools and libraries as partners; these should instead be treated as low-level details hidden from the application logic.
* **Internal quality** is more important than external quality because a well-engineered codebase naturally results in fewer defects and allows for a sustainable pace of delivering value.
* **100% code coverage** is a necessary metric for identifying untested code, but it is merely a **precondition** for a comprehensive suite and does not guarantee the quality of the tests.
* **Mutation testing** is a vital technique that injects automatic faults into code to verify that the test suite is semantically stable and capable of catching errors.
* The **"Embedded Dual Target TDD"** workflow allows for fast feedback by running tests on a host machine during development while periodically verifying the same specifications on the target hardware.
* A **Processor Abstraction Layer (PAL)** should be implemented to hide low-level microcontroller details, ensuring that the majority of the code remains portable and reusable.
* Adopting a **plugin architecture** allows the business logic to remain independent of specific hardware components, which are treated as swappable "plug-ins".
* **Performance optimization** should be based on objective measurements rather than speculation to avoid "useless optimizations" that do not fulfill a specific timing requirement.
* **TDD (Test-Driven Development)** is primarily a **design and development technique** that removes the fear of refactoring, rather than just a testing method.
* **Debugging** is viewed as a non-value-added activity that indicates a failure in the development process; the goal is to replace it with **self-checking specifications**.

title: InfoQ podcast: Courageous Leadership and Evolutionary Architecture
description: Rebecca Parsons (Thoughtworks CTO) on the traits of courageous leaders who transform organizations, and on evolutionary architecture — fitness functions, continuous delivery, and database refactoring.
notes:
Rebecca Parsons (Thoughtworks CTO) covers two threads: the "courageous leaders" who successfully transform organizations, and evolutionary architecture (her book with Neal Ford and Pat Kua) — applying agile principles to the parts of a system long assumed fixed.

- Four traits of courageous leaders: (1) understanding how transformative technology is — you're a technology company that happens to be in retail/finance/etc.; (2) supporting experimentation and risk (if experiments never fail they aren't experiments; "how many succeeded?" as a metric means you're not taking enough risk; protect your people); (3) a central, respectful role for the customer (engage them where they are); (4) work is collaborative — fewer lone-wolf heroes, diversity of thought, good ideas from everywhere. It's culture: paint a picture of the future and let the team help plan the route. (Examples: Domino's "a tech company that makes pizzas," IAG in Australia.)
- Evolutionary vs adaptable architecture: adaptable implies you pre-decided the axes of change (switches/parameters you build and maintain but may never flip). Change is now too unpredictable for that — instead make the architecture as easy to change as possible on whatever dimension (integration, persistence, app architecture, topology, scaling).
- Fitness functions: decide up front the critical architectural characteristic (performance, security, MTTR — you can't optimize all) and test your decisions against how well they move toward it. Coupling/cohesion still matter (looser is easier to change, but stringent performance needs tighter coupling — balance them).
- What makes it practical: continuous delivery (push-button deploys remove the risk that made changes too scary), environment standardization, automation and small increments — so you can tell which change broke things. Microservices are an early instantiation (more degrees of freedom than a layered architecture, where a vertical-slice change touches every layer).
- Database refactoring: data is a huge asset but ages poorly; migration is conceptually easy and practically brutal. Make big changes as a series of small DB refactorings so you find the "magic column that meant something different in 1984" in isolation, instead of thousands of unexplained migration errors.

title: Succeeding with TDD: Pragmatic Techniques for effective mocking
description: Venkat Subramaniam's live-coded TDD session building a currency-exchange service in C#, showing pragmatic mocking: eliminate dependencies where you can, invert the rest behind interfaces, and hand-roll test doubles to stay focused on your own logic.
notes:
In a live-coding session (C# / Visual Studio), Venkat Subramaniam builds a currency-exchange service test-first to demonstrate pragmatic mocking. The throughline: TDD lets you solve one unknown at a time, moving from unknowns to knowns, and the key to testability is how you handle dependencies — untestable code is always a design problem, not a tooling one.

- Start with a canary test (canary-in-a-coal-mine) to confirm the environment works, then drive behaviour incrementally: a markdown (2% off → 98), then a rate with no vendors, then with one vendor — gradually adding complexity.
- First principle for dependencies: eliminate them wherever possible (a more important design decision than inverting); only for the dependencies you can't remove do you invert them by introducing an interface.
- To avoid hitting the real web service, hand-roll a tiny test double that implements the vendor interface and returns a known price — keeping focus on your own logic rather than on how you talk to the service.
- For unavoidable framework dependencies (e.g. DateTime.Now), shims/fakes can stand in, but use them sparingly — powerful yet slow. Closing message: TDD is extremely approachable; he's never met genuinely untestable code, only design issues — tools help, but it's the combination of approach and tools that makes the difference.

title: Frozen DevOps? Team Topologies Comes to the Rescue! - DevOpsDays Poznań 2021
description: In this talk Manuel covers the self-imposed limitations of blindly following some “myths” around DevOps. Most organizations are stuck in the "frozen middle" of DevOps evolution due to a lack of organizational sensemaking. They must think beyond technical capabilities to unleash the potential of their teams to deliver with greater autonomy and a sense of purpose.
notes:
To overcome the **"frozen middle"** of DevOps, organizations must prioritize intentional team structures and interaction modes that minimize **cognitive load** and eliminate blocking dependencies. By treating internal platforms as **curated products**, businesses can empower stream-aligned teams to achieve a faster, more sustainable flow of value.

* **The Frozen Middle:** Approximately 80% of organizations remain stuck in the "middle" tier of DevOps evolution, failing to reach high performance despite adopting cloud and automation tools.
* **Structural vs. Technical Barriers:** High performance is hindered more by organizational structure and team dynamics than by technical implementation.
* **Blocking Dependencies:** A primary cause of low performance is blocking dependencies (e.g., waiting for an operations or security team). High-performing teams focus on non-blocking, self-service models to achieve lead times of under one hour.
* **Managing Cognitive Load:** Excessive cognitive load on software teams—often dealing with hundreds of different tools—prevents high performance. Organizations must manage and shift "extraneous" cognitive load (infrastructure, tooling) away from stream-aligned teams.
* **Four Fundamental Team Types:** Organizations should structure around four specific team types: **Stream-aligned** (delivering value end-to-end), **Platform** (providing internal services), **Enabling** (expert mentors), and **Complicated Subsystem** (niche specialists).
* **Intentional Interaction Modes:** Teams should use three defined interaction modes—**Collaboration** (temporary discovery), **X-as-a-Service** (consumption without direct help), and **Facilitation** (mentoring)—to reduce friction and maintain flow.
* **Platform as a Product:** Internal platforms should be treated as compelling products that are **optional** for teams to use, rather than mandated. They must be curated based on internal customer needs and managed by dedicated product managers.
* **Focus on the "Team of Teams":** Success at scale requires optimizing the entire organizational ecosystem for fast flow and continuous learning, rather than focusing on individual or single-team efficiency.

title: Continuous Delivery simply explained
description: This talk provides a comprehensive overview of Continuous Delivery as a holistic engineering mindset that applies scientific principles, rapid feedback loops, and rigorous automation to ensure software is always releasable and of high quality.
notes:
Continuous delivery is a holistic engineering mindset that applies scientific principles and automated feedback loops to transform software development into a reliable process of continuous learning and discovery. By working in small, experimental steps and automating the path to production, teams can manage complexity and ensure their software is always in a high-quality, releasable state.

- Continuous Delivery (CD) is a holistic, engineering-based approach to software development focused on learning, discovery, and managing complexity.
- The primary goal is to ensure software is always in a releasable state, shifting the focus from thinking harder to running small, controlled experiments.
- Cycle Time is the core metric, measuring the duration from an initial idea to working software in the hands of users; the objective is to reduce this to under a day or ideally under an hour.
- Every code commit is treated as a Release Candidate. The deployment process acts as a system to falsify these candidates; if any test fails, the candidate is discarded because it is proven unfit for production.
- Automation is mandatory for nearly everything, including testing, deployment, and configuration management, to ensure a repeatable and reliable process.
- A Deployment Pipeline serves as the central mechanism that controls variables and manages the transit of a change from commit to a releasable outcome.
- CD relies on multiple feedback loops, ranging from seconds-long Test-Driven Development (TDD) cycles to broader executable specifications and direct user feedback.
- The approach requires small, autonomous, and empowered teams to drive out waste and avoid the bottlenecks of cross-team coordination.
- Working in small, incremental steps encourages a more modular and cohesive system design, which helps in managing the inherent complexity of software.
- Seven Essential Techniques: Reduce cycle time, automate nearly everything, control variables, work in small steps, make evidence-based decisions, use small empowered teams, and apply lean/agile principles.
- CD is a mindset that applies scientific reasoning to solve practical development problems, resulting in higher quality, better efficiency, and improved financial outcomes.

title: Mind the Product 2012
description: Marty Cagan outlines the essential principles for building disruptive products, emphasizing **continuous discovery, cross-functional collaboration**, and a culture of **data-driven iteration** over traditional project thinking.
notes:
Effective product development requires shifting from a project-based mindset to one of continuous discovery, where cross-functional teams use rapid iteration and data to validate that they are building something truly worth the effort. It is vital to remember that innovation stems from the deep collaboration of product, design, and engineering working side-by-side to solve significant problems rather than simply executing a fixed roadmap.

* **Vision and Passion**: Success starts with a deep belief that the current way of doing things can be improved, focusing on a **2-3 year vision** where money is a consequence of solving a problem rather than the primary purpose [1-3].
* **Uncertainty of Business Plans**: Traditional business plans and ROI analyses are often inaccurate because it is impossible to know an idea's value or its development cost until the solution is actually explored.
* **Two-Step Decision Process**: Teams should first decide if an opportunity is **worth working on** (market discovery) before determining if they can build a **viable solution** (product discovery).
* **Customer Limitations**: Customers often do not know what is technologically possible and typically cannot identify what they actually want until they see a functional representation.
* **Product Discovery Competency**: The goal of discovery is to find **Minimum Viable Products (MVPs)** using the fastest and cheapest methods possible, rather than building full-scale software to test an idea [9-11].
* **The Two-Thirds Rule**: Assume at least **two-thirds of ideas will fail**; the remaining third will likely require three to four iterations to achieve success.
* **High-Speed Iteration**: Effective discovery requires a rapid rhythm, ideally aiming for **one iteration per day** to stay ahead of "management patience" and funding limits.
* **Prototyping Tiers**: Use **low-fidelity prototypes** for quick simulations, high-fidelity prototypes for deeper testing, and **live data prototypes** (minimal, non-production code) to gather empirical evidence.
* **User Testing Focus**: Testing must determine not just if a customer *can* use a product, but fundamentally if they **would use it** and why.
* **Dedicated Product Teams**: Organizations should move from project-based work to **durable, cross-functional, and co-located teams** that own a specific domain or user type.
* **Outcome-Based Management**: Instead of feature roadmaps, give teams **KPIs and objectives** (outcomes), allowing them to discover the best way to achieve the desired results.
* **Holistic Design**: Design is not "look and feel" added at the end; it is the **entire user experience** and "how it all works," and must be included from day one.
* **Engineers as Innovators**: Engineers are the single biggest source of innovation because they understand what is possible; they should be involved in **discovery**, not just coding.
* **Data Over Opinion**: A strong product culture uses **validated learning** and data to settle disagreements rather than relying on the opinions of executives or managers.
* **Vision vs. Details**: Be **stubborn on vision but flexible on details**, allowing for pivots—changing the customer, business model, or problem—when discovery shows the original path is not viable [27-29].

title: Microservices Retrospective – What We Learned (and Didn’t Learn) from Netflix
description: This presentation offers a retrospective analysis of the speaker's experience implementing microservices at Netflix from 2007-2013, examining both the successes and the lessons learned along the way. The speaker discusses key aspects of Netflix's innovative approach, including their "extreme and agile" culture, early adoption of cloud technologies like AWS and Cassandra, and focus on developer freedom and responsibility. The presentation also highlights specific technical patterns and practices developed at Netflix, such as the use of service access libraries, lightweight serializable objects, and chaos engineering.
notes:
True organizational agility and resilience are achieved through a holistic **systems thinking** approach that aligns a high-talent culture of **freedom and responsibility** with technical patterns like loosely coupled microservices. Success depends on prioritizing **flexibility and the removal of friction** over rigid efficiency, ensuring the entire organization is structured to minimize the time between a new idea and its successful execution in production.

*   **Culture of Freedom and Responsibility:** Netflix operates as a high-performance team (not a family), where managers provide "context, not control," and employees are treated as fully formed adults responsible for their own development.
*   **Highly Aligned, Loosely Coupled:** Teams share a single goal but work individually with clear APIs, allowing for rapid evolution without the friction of centralized processes or handoffs.
*   **Systems Thinking and Agility:** The organization optimizes for agility over efficiency, recognizing that squeezing a system for maximum efficiency often removes the flexibility required for innovation.
*   **Engineers on Call:** To improve code quality and learning, developers are responsible for the code they ship and are placed on call to fix it if it breaks.
*   **From Monolith to Microservices:** The architecture transitioned from a central SQL database and stateful sessions to a stateless, distributed NoSQL (Cassandra) system with layered, decoupled service interfaces.
*   **Bounded Contexts:** Microservices are defined as loosely coupled services with bounded contexts; if a service is not loosely coupled, it cannot be independently deployed.
*   **Immutable Infrastructure:** Code is never updated in place. Instead, new versions are launched as new service groups alongside the old ones, enabling safe "testing in production" before routing traffic.
*   **Chaos Engineering:** Tools like Chaos Monkey are used as a design control to prove system resilience, ensuring that the architecture can handle instance failures and aggressive autoscaling.
*   **Preventing Retry Storms:** Advanced reliability requires cascading timeout budgets and intelligent retry logic (e.g., retrying a different instance rather than the same one) to prevent work amplification during failures.
*   **The Innovation Loop:** Success is driven by the OODA loop (Observe, Orient, Decide, Act), minimizing the "mean time between idea and making stuff happen" through incremental features and AB testing.
*   **Strategic Open Source:** Netflix open-sourced its internal tools to establish best practices, build a strong technology brand, and benefit from a shared ecosystem of external contributors.
*   **Model-Driven Architecture:** Unlike traditional data centers, the cloud provides a dependably complete model where the state of the infrastructure can be queried and audited in real-time.
*   **Deep Autoscaling:** High cost-efficiency is achieved by autoscaling deeply enough to reach 40-50% average utilization, rather than the single-digit averages common in many cloud environments.
*   **Inverted Conway’s Law:** Organizations should set up their team structures to mirror the architecture they want to build, ensuring the system evolves in the desired direction.

title: Mastering the Problem Space to Achieve Product-Market Fit
description: Good explanations about modern Product Management concepts (Product market fit, Problem Space vs Solutions Space, Lean product management, etc).
notes:
Achieving product-market fit requires a disciplined focus on defining the **problem space** and identifying **underserved customer needs** before jumping into specific features or solutions. By resisting "solution pollution" and using frameworks like the **Kano model**, product managers can articulate a unique value proposition that effectively differentiates their product from the competition.

* **PM Motto:** "With great responsibility comes no power".
* **Product-Market Fit Pyramid:** A framework consisting of five layers of hypotheses: Target Customer, Underserved Needs, Value Proposition, Feature Set, and User Experience.
* **Market vs. Product:** The "market" is defined by the target customer and their underserved needs, while the "product" is defined by the value proposition, feature set, and UX.
* **Problem Space vs. Solution Space:** Problem space focuses on customer needs and benefits (the "why"); solution space focuses on specific implementations or designs (the "how").
* **Solution Pollution:** The mistake of including specific solutions (e.g., "add a dropdown") within requirement definitions, which obscures the core customer problem.
* **The "Why" Technique:** Use the question "Why?" to strip away solution pollution and reach the fundamental customer motivation or benefit.
* **Benefit Ladders:** A method of grouping detailed product features into clusters that "ladder up" to high-level macro benefits like saving time, saving money, or increasing confidence.
* **Kano Model Categories:** Customer needs are classified as Must-Haves (basic expectations), Performance benefits (more is better), or Delighters (unexpected "wow" factors).
* **Value Proposition Grid:** A strategic tool used to compare your product against competitors to identify unique differentiators and decide where to say "no" or "not now".
* **Winning Formula for PMF:** To lead a market, a product should meet all must-haves, be the best in at least one performance benefit, and offer a unique delighter.
* **Instagram Case Study:** Instagram succeeded by focusing on a clear value prop: "Fast" (Performance), "Beautiful" (Delighter), and "Photo sharing" (Must-have).
* **The PM Role:** While designers design and developers build, the primary job of the product manager is to define the product by mastering the problem space.

title: TEDxRainier - Joe Justice - WikiSpeed
description: Great example of agile mindset. Very inspirational.
notes:
Applying modern software development frameworks like **Agile, Lean, and Scrum** to complex physical engineering allows for unprecedented speed, modularity, and efficiency by drastically reducing the cost and time of making changes. This iterative approach enables teams to solve major global challenges rapidly, proving that flexible, collaborative processes can transform traditional industries and drive significant social impact.

*   **Rapid Iteration**: Development cycles are reduced from the industry standard of 10–25 years to just 7 days, allowing for continuous and fast improvements.
*   **Software Methodologies in Hardware**: Application of Agile, Lean, and Scrum techniques to physical manufacturing to increase speed and flexibility.
*   **Extreme Modularity**: The car is designed with interchangeable modules; for example, the engine can be switched from gasoline to electric in the time it takes to change a tire.
*   **Test-Driven Development (TDD)**: Safety tests and functional requirements are designed before the parts are built to ensure reliability and speed up the integration process.
*   **Reducing the Cost of Change**: Using low-cost tooling and innovative materials (like $800 carbon fiber bodies) to avoid being locked into expensive, long-term manufacturing molds.
*   **Pairing and Knowledge Sharing**: Team members work in pairs to eliminate the need for formal training and extensive documentation, facilitating immediate knowledge transfer.
*   **Distributed Collaboration**: Utilizing a global, volunteer-based team structure supported by free digital tools and social networking.
*   **Velocity and Morale**: Recognition that team morale serves as a multiplier for velocity, and visualizing workflow is essential to remove non-creative bottlenecks.
*   **Universal Applicability**: The "WikiSpeed" process is seen as a framework for solving complex global problems beyond automotive engineering, such as medical center development or disease eradication.

title: The Elegant Solution: Toyota's Formula for Mastering Innovation
description: Interesting presentation on how Toyota incorporates continuous learning and innovation into the company's daily operations.
notes:
Sustainable innovation is rooted in a culture that empowers **every worker to be a daily problem-solver**, focusing on small, incremental improvements rather than just major technological breakthroughs. By shifting the responsibility for perfection to those closest to the work, an organization can transform its operations and consistently deliver **elegant solutions** that align with both customer and societal needs.

**Innovation Definition**: Innovation is simply finding a better way to solve a problem. It is not limited to technology but applies to any domain where a process or solution can be improved.

**The Scale of Ideas**: True innovation cultures, like Toyota’s, implement ideas at a massive scale—approximately one million per year. These are not just suggestions but documented improvements using a specific discipline (often the A3 process) involving investigation, design, experimentation, and reflection.

**Core Principles of Innovation**:
*   **Ingenuity and Craft**: Personal, applied creativity and resourcefulness to do more with less.
*   **Pursuit of Perfection**: Viewing the gap between current reality and an ideal state as a problem to be solved. Perfection is a "guiding star" for the journey, even if unattainable.
*   **Fit with Society**: Ensuring solutions meet a pressing need and provide a delivery system that makes the innovation useful to the world.

**Elegant Solutions**: An elegant solution is defined as achieving the optimal outcome using the minimum number of steps or resources.

**Systems Thinking over Culture**: Culture is rooted in system and structure. To change an organization’s culture to be more innovative, you must change its systems and structures first.

**Standardized Work**: Standards should not be rigid, long-term scripts. Instead, they are snapshots of the current best practice—a template meant to be followed, challenged, and improved constantly.

**Kaizen (Continuous Improvement)**: This involves a cycle of creating a standard, following it, and then finding a better way. It emphasizes small, incremental ideas implemented as close to the front line as possible.

**Genji Gambutsu ("Go Look, Go See")**: True innovation requires connecting deeply with customers through direct observation to identify unarticulated needs before the customer even realizes them.

**Hansei (Reflection)**: Mandatory reflection at project milestones to understand the "delta" between expected and actual results. The goal is learning and process improvement rather than placing blame.

**Worker Empowerment**: The individual closest to the work is the expert. They are not just responsible for operating a process but for perfecting it.

**Reframing Failure**: Every new idea should be treated as a pilot or experiment. If the primary goal is to learn, a project cannot fail because even a negative result provides valuable knowledge.

**The "Clamshell" Implementation Strategy**: To start an innovation movement, begin with one team and one manager. The team must tackle a specific problem within 30–45 days using zero additional budget or headcount, proving that ingenuity can solve problems without needing extra resources.

title: Parallelizing computation with dask
description: Matthew Rocklin on Dask: scaling the existing PyData stack (NumPy, pandas, scikit-learn) to many cores and clusters without rewriting it.
notes:
Dask scales the existing PyData ecosystem (NumPy, pandas, scikit-learn) to multiple cores and clusters without rewriting it — by mirroring those APIs and adding a flexible task scheduler — so you keep your familiar code but escape the single-core, fits-in-RAM limits.

- The problem: Python's analytical stack is powerful but mostly single-core and limited to data that fits in RAM; rewriting the ecosystem (as Spark did in Scala) isn't feasible — too much trusted, interdependent code.
- Dask "scales PyData": Dask Array ~ NumPy and Dask DataFrame ~ pandas (close, not identical), running many small NumPy/pandas operations across cores or a cluster; everything is lazy until .compute().
- Two separate parts: the data-structure libraries and a generic low-level task scheduler (like threading/multiprocessing but with task scheduling, load balancing, resilience and node failover).
- It shines on a single machine too: streaming through larger-than-RAM data on a laptop is the most common use, not just clusters.
- Workers can be added and removed dynamically mid-computation; the real win for IT is removing idle workers, since data-science workloads are bursty, giving good cluster utilization.
- A built-in live Bokeh dashboard exposes scheduler telemetry (tasks, memory, network, per-line profiling) — it's so good people sometimes run Dask just for the profiler.
- dask.delayed exposes the low-level engine for custom, non-array/dataframe parallelism with normal Python for-loops — people "wanted the engine, not the car."
- Built in pure Python (Tornado for concurrency/TCP, plain dicts for state); fast enough that it's "a factor of 2-5 slower, not 100x," with pluggable comms (TLS, UCX/InfiniBand).
- Sub-libraries: dask-kubernetes/yarn/jobqueue (deployment), dask-ml (scikit-learn-style scaling), dask-cuda (GPUs). Practical ceiling ~1000 machines; most problems are far smaller.
- The future is social, not technical: the core is "done"; growth is spreading Dask into domains (e.g. Pangeo for climate/earth science) that didn't fit Spark/Hadoop/TensorFlow.

title: #12 e-Meetup | Sam Newman - Monolith Decomposition patterns
description: Great presentation on how to approach the division of a monolith and the patterns to evolve an architecture towards microservices. In addition to the presentation, the subsequent questions are also very interesting.
notes:
Microservices should be treated as a strategic means to solve specific organizational or technical problems rather than a default architectural goal, requiring an **incremental and evolutionary approach** to decomposition that emphasizes early production feedback over high-risk, "big bang" rewrites. Focus on the "why" behind the migration and prioritize the **strangler fig pattern** or similar methods to safely transition functionality while coexisting with the existing monolith.

* **Monoliths as the Default:** Monolithic architecture is not inherently bad and should be the default approach for most systems until specific needs, like scale or team autonomy, require a change.
* **Microservices as a Means to an End:** Microservices are a tool to solve specific problems (e.g., scaling bottlenecks or independent deployability) rather than a default architecture choice. If you don't know what problem you are trying to solve, you shouldn't be using them.
* **Incremental Migration:** Avoid "big bang" rewrites. Migration should be an evolutionary process where functionality is extracted one piece at a time, allowing for learning and adjustment based on production behavior.
* **Deployment vs. Release:** These are distinct activities. You should deploy new services to production early and often to test infrastructure and monitoring, even if no traffic is being diverted to them yet (the "release").
* **Strangler Fig Pattern:** This involves wrapping a new system around the old monolith and using an interceptor (like a proxy) to divert calls to the new microservice. This allows for migration without necessarily changing the underlying monolithic code.
* **Branch by Abstraction:** Use this pattern for internal code-level decomposition where you create an abstraction for a component and gradually swap out the implementation from the monolith to a service.
* **Data Decomposition Complexity:** Breaking apart a shared database is much harder than splitting code. It requires breaking foreign key relationships and moving join logic from the database to the service tier.
* **Latency Costs:** Moving joins to the application tier increases latency. This often necessitates strategies like caching or data duplication (creating "memories" of data at a point in time) to maintain performance.
* **Managing Referential Integrity:** When foreign keys are lost during decomposition, you must choose between accepting inconsistency, using soft deletes, or utilizing events to trigger cascading actions across services.
* **Avoiding the Distributed Monolith:** A distributed monolith occurs when services must be deployed in lockstep. This is a "nightmare" scenario that combines the complexity of distributed systems with the lack of independent deployability found in monoliths.
* **Inverted Data Ownership for Reporting:** For OLAP and data warehousing, the responsibility should be on the microservice owner to publish their data (e.g., via Change Data Capture) rather than a central team reaching into the service's private database.
* **Circular Dependencies:** While generally avoided, circular dependencies between a monolith and a new service can be tolerated as a temporary interim state during the migration process.
* **Service Boundaries:** If you find yourself constantly making changes across multiple services for a single feature, your service boundaries are likely in the wrong place, potentially leading to a distributed monolith.

title: Rebuilding the Inbox: The Corporate and Cultural Adventures of Modernizing Yahoo Mail
A Philosophical Look at System Dynamics
description: Donella Meadows’ lecture on causal loop diagrams (The most interesting part starts at the 18th minute). Via @RuthMalan@mastodon.social.
notes:
Understanding complex systems requires shifting focus from isolated events to the underlying structure of **feedback loops, levels, and rates** that dictate how a system behaves over time. By identifying whether these internal causal relationships are self-reinforcing or goal-seeking, you can better comprehend and influence the dynamic patterns that emerge within the world's most difficult problems.

* **Definition of System Dynamics:** A philosophical and mathematical method for learning about complex systems, focusing on how they change over time rather than their state at a single point in time.
* **Holistic Perspective:** Prioritizes understanding the behavior of the system as a whole over the characteristics of individual elements.
* **Causal Linkages:** Models are built on direct causal mechanisms where one element physically or logically causes a change in another, rather than relying on simple statistical correlations.
* **Nonlinearity:** Most real-world relationships are nonlinear; for example, increasing a resource may have a high impact initially but eventually reach a point of saturation or diminishing returns.
* **System Delays:** Time lags between an action and its effect (such as aging or infrastructure development) are critical because they fundamentally alter the dynamics and behavior of the system.
* **Feedback Loops:** The world is viewed as a collection of closed causal chains.
    * **Positive Feedback:** Self-reinforcing loops that lead to exponential growth or exponential decline.
    * **Negative Feedback:** Goal-seeking loops that counteract change to move the system toward a specific state or equilibrium.
* **Levels and Rates:** The two fundamental building blocks of a system:
    * **Levels (State Variables):** Represent the measurable state or condition of the system at any given time.
    * **Rates (Decisions/Actions):** Represent the flows that change the levels based on the current state of the system and specific goals.
* **State-Determined Systems:** The behavior of a system is primarily determined by its internal structure and state variables rather than external events or "open-loop" inputs.
* **Structure-Behavior Relationship:** Specific causal structures generate predictable patterns of behavior (e.g., a positive loop generates exponential growth; a negative loop generates goal-seeking behavior).
* **Human Decision-Making:** Asserted that all human decisions are made within the context of a feedback loop, where information about the state of the system is compared against a goal to formulate an action.

title: Let It Go: Building a Global Social Enterprise by Empowering Your Employees
description: Rupert Scofield, CEO of FINCA, on building a 12,000-person global microfinance organization on trust — empowering people and giving them permission to fail, balanced with controls.
notes:
Running a global organization (FINCA: 12,000 employees, 23 countries, 2M microfinance customers) comes down to trust: you have to empower people and give them permission to make mistakes, but balanced between blind trust and no trust, with the systems and controls to weed out the bad actors.

- Empowerment works but requires training: decentralizing the fertilizer warehouses to 35 villages succeeded, but one undertrained person came up short and resented it — empowering people is right, but you must train them well enough.
- Trust is how the organization scales: FINCA grew by trusting others to run pilots (training CARE and Save the Children in village banking) and four social entrepreneurs with $10k each — risky bets that paid off.
- Hire people smarter than you, especially the CFO; founders who didn't trust or delegate to competent staff hit a ~10,000-client ceiling and failed.
- Don't over-correct: after frauds in the 1990s FINCA swung to "trust no one," played defense for a decade, lost its strategy and let competitors catch up — learning the controls lesson too well was itself a costly mistake.
- Land in the middle: no-trust drives away good employees and customers; the goal is controls that catch bad actors without creating an atmosphere of distrust.
- Give permission to fail by example and by real latitude: give people "a pile of chips, not the whole casino" — small-stakes risks they can learn from; someone who can't admit a mistake can't learn.
- Leadership is destination (where you're taking the org), credibility (a toolbox of solved problems) and toughness (getting back up after reverses) — plus not taking yourself too seriously, and sacrifice (put yourself last).
- Hire for values, not just skills: ask "why do you want this job?"; betrayal hurts, but most people are trustworthy, so stay tough and keep trusting.

title: Agile data science
description: ThoughtWorks podcast. Interesting to understand how to have a great collaboration between data scientists and developers when creating a product.

title: 275-rr-the-evolution-of-agile-and-evolutionary-design-with-james-shore
description: James Shore explores the technical roots of **Agile**, highlighting **evolutionary design** as a key practice for managing technical debt and scaling organizations through the alignment of team structures and software architecture.
notes:
To achieve genuine agility, **technical practices like evolutionary design must be prioritized alongside management processes** to ensure the codebase remains flexible and sustainable. Success depends on building only what is needed today while **aligning organizational structures with system architecture** to minimize complexity and communication overhead as teams scale.

* **The Evolution of Agile:** Agile began as a grassroots movement by programmers to solve the "software crisis" of failed projects. Over time, it was co-opted by a project management mentality, which often led to the exclusion of technical excellence and programmers from the conversation.
* **Agile at its Best:** True Agile is about bringing all necessary perspectives together to ship valuable software. It prioritizes people and interactions over bureaucracy and emphasizes continuous learning through retrospectives and experimentation.
* **Technical Debt vs. Sloppy Programming:** Strategic technical debt is what is deliberately left out of a system to ship faster. In contrast, "sloppy programming" is simply poor-quality code that should be avoided.
* **YAGNI (You Aren't Gonna Need It):** Developers should only build code for the problems they have today. Anticipating future needs often leads to over-engineering, bugs, and "tendrils" that make the system harder to change later.
* **Evolutionary Design:** This approach involves starting with the simplest possible implementation and using gradual refactoring to improve the design as new features are added. It allows for shipping a Minimum Viable Product (MVP) faster while keeping the codebase flexible.
* **The Role of Experience in Design:** While evolutionary design is powerful, it requires experience or a solid "mental model" to avoid painting oneself into a corner or getting stuck in "local maxima" (traps where simple iterations can't solve a complex architectural problem).
* **Agile Architecture:** Instead of upfront design, "Agile Architecture" involves focusing refactoring efforts on areas where future changes would be most painful, ensuring the system is ready for those changes without actually implementing them yet.
* **Scaling Agile and Conway’s Law:** Organizational structure eventually reflects the code's structure. To scale effectively, teams should be designed to minimize inter-team communication and maximize "full ownership" of specific segments of the problem.
* **Communication Complexity:** Communication overhead increases exponentially (N^2) as more people are added. Small, cross-functional teams (typically 6-10 people) are the most efficient units for software development.
* **Phase Transitions in Team Size:** Organizations face "phase transitions" at different sizes (e.g., 30 people or 150 people). Beyond these points, the natural social limits of human relationships (Dunbar’s Number) require different organizational strategies.
* **Meta-Evolution of Models:** Successful organizational models (like the Spotify model) are context-dependent. Companies should not copy the specific structure of another organization but rather the *evolutionary process* and learning culture that created that structure.
* **Jelling Teams:** Developing the skill to "jell" teams quickly—integrating new members into a shared culture and mental model—is more valuable than trying to keep a static team together forever.

title: Working Effectively with Legacy Code • Michael Feathers & Christian Clausen • GOTO 2023
description: This interview with Michael Feathers, author of "Working Effectively with Legacy Code," explores practical strategies for managing and improving large, untested codebases, including techniques for testing, refactoring, and understanding software change mechanics. Feathers and interviewer Christian Clausen also discuss the impact of AI on code quality, the challenges of advocating for testing in organizations, and the importance of prioritizing efforts based on code value and criticality
notes:
In this interview with Christian Clausen, Michael Feathers revisits the core ideas of *Working Effectively with Legacy Code* nearly two decades after publication, arguing they remain timeless because legacy code keeps being written. He reframes legacy code as fundamentally a problem of understanding and confidence, where tests serve as a safety net and a tool for interrogating behavior, and ties almost every testing difficulty back to an underlying design problem.

* **Legacy code is a subjective judgment, not a hard line**: Feathers reiterates his classic definition — code without tests — but says what he keeps returning to is *the degree to which you actually understand what the code does*. Code whose behavior surprises you is where you're really in trouble.
* **Surprise as a quality signal**: A system should be largely unsurprising. If you find something counterintuitive, it raises the question of what else is hidden.
* **Tests vs. typing as routes to confidence**: Confidence can come from naming, hierarchy, and a strong type system, not only from reading code (humans read code slowly). Compiled languages give more *affordances* — different ways to interrogate the codebase.
* **Leaning on the compiler and deliberately introducing errors**: Compiler errors — and deliberately breaking code to learn about it — are another form of testing. A red bar can also mean a compile error, which Clausen's TDD formulation accounts for.
* **Deep synergy of testability and good design**: Almost everything painful about testing indicates a design problem. Fix the design and you get both a better design and better testing affordances — you learn to *listen to the system*.
* **Don't over-valorize tests during refactoring**: Some teams move away from testing because structurally-dependent tests hinder refactoring. Feathers will put tests "in the parking lot," do a structural refactoring with confidence gained another way, then rewrite tests for the new structure.
* **Stubbing is the hard skill**: The painful, undertaught part of testing is learning to stub correctly; most teaching over-focuses on red-green-refactor process.
* **Tests should grow outward, not map one-to-one to classes**: Echoing Beck and Ian Cooper, grow tests from a point and refactor outward to cover a larger space — stay on the public interface, don't test private methods.
* **Published vs. public**: Echoing Fowler, languages lack a way to mark methods as *published* (a contract for the outside world) vs. merely *public*; teams must make this distinction themselves.
* **Breaking encapsulation selectively**: He does it reluctantly, at the edge of a new boundary worth preserving — "cracking the eggs to make the omelet," and *best is the enemy of good*.
* **Limiting non-local effects**: Building isolation before writing a test (e.g. injecting a singleton through the constructor) erects firewalls against effect propagation; tests then act as tripwires.
* **Value is not uniform (Pareto)**: ~20% of code carries higher value, and commits cluster in a few hotspot files. Concentrate effort on high-value, high-change, high-criticality areas; stabilize messy-but-stable areas only to the point of understanding. He references hotspot tooling (CodeScene, SonarQube), DDD, Team Topologies, and building a culture of refactoring.
* **Scratch refactoring and meaningful hands-on work**: Copy code into a plain text file and rename/move freely without fear since you'll never commit it. When understanding stalls, do something mechanical and meaningful; writing a single test is *asking a question of the codebase*.
* **AI's impact**: It will change things but we're "safe for a little while"; AI turns work into generate-and-test, so understanding code and judging correctness still matters, and design fundamentals (cohesion, coupling) stay important.

title: Large-Scale Deep Learning for Intelligent Computer Systems
description: Kindly introduction about what is deep learning.
notes:
Jeff Dean reviews roughly five years of Google's work applying deep neural networks across research, computer systems, and product groups, arguing that the same fundamental building blocks now power perception (vision, speech), language understanding, and translation. His central thesis is that progress comes from scaling computation and data together, learning representations directly from raw inputs, and replacing brittle hand-engineered subsystems with end-to-end learned models.

* **Understanding information as the core challenge**: Google's mission to organize the world's information fundamentally requires *understanding* it — reading text in street scenes, recognizing photo contents, grasping that a query like "car parts for sale" needs deep meaning, not word matching.
* **What a deep neural net actually is**: A stack of layers of simple artificial neurons; each computes a weighted sum passed through a non-linearity (today the rectified linear unit, max(0,x)). "Deep" just refers to the number of layers; the system learns a complicated function mapping inputs to outputs.
* **Training by gradient descent and the chain rule**: Loop over labeled examples, run forward, and use the chain rule to back-propagate small weight adjustments — across hundreds of millions of parameters.
* **Why neural nets revived around 2011**: Invented in the 60s-70s, they faded for lack of compute and data; Dean's project, started in 2011, pushed large-scale systems, and adoption spread organically as product teams saw each other's wins.
* **Speech recognition**: Replacing the hidden Markov acoustic model with a neural net cut word error rates by more than 30% — the biggest improvement veteran researchers had seen.
* **ImageNet and computer vision**: Top-5 error fell from ~25-26% to ~3% in five years; Andrej Karpathy trained himself for 100 hours and scored 5.1%. Models can beat humans on fine-grained distinctions like dog breeds.
* **Vision in products**: Google Photos search without manual tagging; Street View text detection trained to predict a per-pixel heat map of where text appears.
* **Search ranking**: A neural net deployed as the third most important ranking signal — only after building debugging/understandability tools to overcome the search team's hesitancy about opaque models.
* **Sequence-to-sequence**: Sutskever/Vinyals/Le framed translation as mapping a sequence of words to another, reading word by word then emitting the target — state-of-the-art as a single end-to-end model. The same architecture powers Gmail Smart Reply and image captioning (swapping the encoder for an image model).
* **Systems and scaling**: Model parallelism partitions neurons across machines; data parallelism keeps parameters in a central server while ~500 replicas compute gradients (async stale gradients still work up to ~50-100 replicas). DistBelief scaled well but was inflexible; TensorFlow (open-sourced under Apache, most-forked GitHub project that year) keeps the strengths while being easier, using a dataflow graph of tensors auto-partitioned across CPUs/GPUs/phones.
* **Q&A takeaways**: Computers remain far from human intelligence (whose strength is transferring knowledge to new situations); small teams should use transfer learning and cloud compute; interpretability is an open problem (check train/test distribution mismatch, keep models fresh, build visualization tools); Dean's cited career mistake was omitting distributed transactions from Bigtable, fixed later in Spanner.

title: Tidy First?
description: Great talk about the human relationships generated during software development. Kent explains these relations and uses them to analyze the development flow, the need for small safe steps, and the tension generated between the people involved. For me, this talk is a must.
notes:
Software design is fundamentally an exercise in human relationships that requires balancing structural improvements with behavioral changes to maintain trust and collaboration between those who request software and those who build it.
