*   **Map milestones rather than long-term plans:** Create impact maps for specific product milestones or the next most important value rather than trying to map out a multi-year strategic plan, which becomes too unwieldy.
*   **Utilize "Christmas Prioritization":** Move away from "Must/Should/Could" categories and instead have stakeholders choose between "Yes for now" and "Yes for later" (sequential Christmases) to better prioritize which goals to deliver first.

title: Backstage: Restoring Order To Your Chaos
description: good introduction to Backstage, the environment, the main plugins, etc.
notes:
Building developer portals with Backstage involves consolidating all the tooling, services, documentation and ownership metadata a developer needs into a single front end, so autonomous teams stay fast as the software ecosystem grows. By taming the fragmentation that comes from scale and autonomy, Spotify turned a worsening onboarding metric around and aligned thousands of engineers across tens of thousands of components.

* **The autonomy paradox that triggered Backstage.** Spotify believed speed and innovation depended on giving teams autonomy, but as the company hit hyper-growth in 2016 that autonomy made the ecosystem fragmented and complex, slowing everything down again; the trigger was the "time to 10th PR" metric (a new hire's tenth pull request to internal GitHub) hitting ~60 days, which prompted them to build Backstage.
* **A developer portal is a product category, not a wiki or a service catalog.** Zolotusky defines it as one front end for the entire platform: CI/CD, monitoring, security, testing, plus services, data, ML models, and documentation (component docs, API docs, tutorials, org charts, strategy docs) all managed in one place regardless of who owns the underlying tools.
* **Three core capabilities: create, manage, explore.** Create means making new things that adhere to org best practices; manage is the day-to-day operation and health of what's running; explore is the collaboration piece — finding which service exposes an API you need (the recurring "playlist system" example: you don't know the service name, the API, or the docs).
* **Backstage is an aggregator, not a replacement.** The portal unifies tools but doesn't duplicate them; the canonical example is CI/CD, where a developer gets a one-glance view (are builds succeeding, what commit is in production) but clicks through to the actual CI/CD system to debug deeply rather than recreating all its detail inside Backstage.
* **Extensible plugin architecture is the source of its power.** Plugins add functionality customized to a company; they range from frontend-only (the Tech Radar plugin), to plugins with their own database and backend (the service catalog), to thin proxies (the CircleCI plugin proxies out to CircleCI rather than replicating it).
* **A distributed ownership model around the plugins.** A core team maintains Backstage itself, a separate team owns and operates the Spotify instance, domain experts own individual plugins end-to-end without central involvement, and feature teams own product features — connected by tight feedback loops. At Spotify ~150 (heading toward ~200) plugins are produced by ~100 teams outside the Backstage org.
* **Ownership is enforced as a first principle.** Every piece of software should have a single owner — meaning a single team, not a single human point of failure — so anyone can find and contact the owning team. That team also owns all service metadata (lifecycle states like production-ready/experimental, tiering, dependencies).
* **Software Templates (internally "the scaffolder") bake best practices into creation.** Instead of policing teams with sticks and punishment for non-compliance, templates make the compliant path the path of least resistance — a one-click button and a short form generates boilerplate (Kubernetes YAML, CI/CD config), auto-registers the new component in the catalog, and stays continuously updated so each new thing reflects the latest best practices.
* **Templates are also the easiest on-ramp to Backstage.** Manage and explore require components and good metadata to already exist (often stuck in heads, spreadsheets, or docs), whereas giving developers a one-click way to create services, data pipelines, or ML models seeds the catalog and gets an org started.
* **Custom template actions extend creation to any system.** Demonstrated with a TypeScript action making REST calls to Grafana to provision a dashboard; the same mechanism can register a new service in ServiceNow, add employees to Workday, or call any API needed during onboarding/creation.
* **The software model: components, systems, domains, plus relations.** A component is anything with code (service, library, data pipeline); components belong to systems (e.g. the playlist system spans services, caches, databases) which belong to domains (podcasts, music playback). Components expose APIs and depend on resources, with users and groups for ownership. Typed entities (service/website/library, gRPC/GraphQL, Cassandra/Dynamo) plus relations build a graph of software knowledge, with annotations for extra non-critical data; at Spotify all of this lives as YAML in each service's repo (though adopters can use other methods).
* **Proven results and community standing.** The time-to-10th-PR metric dropped from ~60 days to consistently under 20 (averaging 10-14), Backstage was open-sourced in March 2020, donated to CNCF, and moved from sandbox to incubation, with over 100 public adopters across industries and maturity levels.

title: CQRS/DDD
description: Very interesting talk about CQRS and Event Source with a very good introduction about Bounded Contexts, Ubiquitous Language and DDD economics.
notes:
Greg Young argues that the most misunderstood and most important idea in Domain-Driven Design is the bounded context, and uses it as the foundation for explaining CQRS and event sourcing. His central thesis is that complexity comes from forcing a single model to serve unrelated purposes; the cure is to recognize linguistic boundaries, model behavior rather than data, and specialize reads, writes and integration separately. CQRS and event sourcing, he notes, came out of an algorithmic trading system doing 20,000-40,000 transactions per second.

### Bounded Contexts and DDD
* **Bounded context is the core of DDD, not entities and value objects** — Young says Evans' chapters 11 to the end should have come first; without context, nothing else in DDD makes sense.
* **Bounded contexts are a linguistic, not technical, concept** — the same word ("policy" at an insurer) means different things to sales, claims and actuaries. You find contexts by listening for words people use differently, which reveal real organizational partitions.
* **The ubiquitous language is not ubiquitous** — it lives inside a single context; an organization has multiple ubiquitous languages, and IT must never impose one top-down.
* **Apply DDD only where you get competitive advantage** — DDD is expensive. Young ranks each context by competitive advantage and technical complexity (t-shirt sizing) to compute rough ROI; low-advantage contexts (accounting, intranet) can be bought or built fast and thrown away.
* **Domains are behavioral, not data** — model from use cases and verbs, not nouns. A database-backed object model of getters/setters with transaction script on top is an anemic data model, not DDD.

### Event Sourcing
* **Make state transitions explicit via events** — instead of letting an ORM implicitly derive what to persist, model every state change as a past-tense event (e.g. InventoryItemDeactivated) carrying the relevant data; events map naturally to use cases.
* **Current state is a left fold of previous behaviors** — like a bank balance summed from transactions, state is rebuilt by replaying the append-only event stream; you never edit or delete, only add compensating events, so no information is ever lost.
* **Not losing information is the real business value** — you can't predict what questions the business will ask in five years, so an update/delete silently discards information you may later need.
* **Projections let you take new ideas back in time** — write event handlers that build a structural projection and replay the whole log, so a brand-new report appears as if data had always been collected.
* **Event log vs event sourcing are distinct** — the time-travel value comes from merely having an event log; event sourcing (rebuilding domain objects) is an additional step.
* **Replay enables debugging, temporal models, and smoke tests** — saved commands re-run in the debugger; a temporal model is replaying events up to a date; they re-ran every command before release and diffed produced events to catch unexpected consequences. Don't replay behavior (re-charging a card), only state transitions, since events carry already-computed results.
* **Rolling snapshots are a heuristic, not the default** — stream backwards to the most recent snapshot then replay forward, but snapshots couple storage to the domain object, so use them only for aggregates with hundreds of events.

### CQRS
* **CQRS is simply separating reads from writes** — built on Meyer's CQS, it puts commands on one object and queries on another, allowing different decisions on each side.
* **A command captures intent that CRUD throws away** — CRUD's four verbs lose *why* state changed; an imperative command preserves the operation the client intended.
* **Reads and writes have different needs** — the write/domain model is behavioral and models invariants; the read model is screen-centric (one DTO per screen). With 10:1 (or 10,000:1) read-to-write ratios, a thin read layer can project straight from the database (even raw SQL), removing the need for getters on domain objects.
* **Reads are eventually consistent and scale independently** — almost all queries already operate on relaxed consistency, so you can multiplex events into many denormalized read models and scale reads near-linearly while the consistent write side stays single.
* **Behavioral, event-based code is far easier to test** — with events you just assert exactly one event was produced; tests read as English (given past-tense events, when a command, expect past-tense events). Setters in a domain model are evil and getters a smell.

### Integration and pitfalls
* **Integrate between contexts through events, never synchronously** — push events and let other systems build their own projections without polluting your context; synchronous transactional integration means one system going down takes the whole organization down.
* **The biggest failures are misapplication** — using CQRS/event sourcing everywhere (a web diary for your sister), skipping the analysis behaviors require, or adding it to a working CRUD system that derives no business value. Young typically introduces an event log feeding a third-normal-form model first and frames the value in money, not as "experimental."

title: Platform Orchestrators: The Missing Middle of Internal Developer Platforms?
description: Good introduction to the different approaches to platform creation for development, with an emphasis on Platform Orchestrators, which are not as well-known but can play an important role in the success of these platforms.
notes:
Daniel Bryant argues that internal developer platforms suffer from a "missing middle": teams build either top-down portals (Backstage) or bottom-up infrastructure tooling (Terraform), but neglect the platform orchestration layer that sits between them. He pitches a "middle-out" approach centered on delivering everything-as-a-service, encapsulating process automation, and providing fleet management at scale.

* **Three approaches to building a platform.** Bryant identifies the top-down application-developer-focused approach (built around Backstage), the bottom-up operations/infrastructure-driven approach (the Terraform workflow), and a third middle-out platform-engineering-focused approach that he advocates as the most powerful if done right.
* **The missing middle.** The central thesis is that orchestration is the overlooked layer: encapsulating and abstracting process automation, providing a clean platform API, and enabling fleet management, sitting between the developer portal and the infrastructure.
* **Origins in the "developer control plane."** He traces the idea to his PlatformCon 2022 talk and his time at Ambassador Labs, where he sketched code/ship/run layers (leveraging CNCF tech), a custom UI (Backstage or Clutch), and a fuzzy middle layer of integrated workflows, CRDs, APIs and webhooks that orchestrate the platform.
* **Gartner validation.** He references Gartner placing platform engineering at the peak of inflated expectations (Nov last year) and their "What is Platform Engineering" article, which depicts three levels (product, digital platform, infrastructure complexity) and calls out developer experience, self-service, and automated infrastructure operations.
* **The why of platforms.** General goals: go faster (everything-as-a-service so developers reliably and sustainably ship value), decrease risk (automate manual processes into reusable components, citing the Log4j case where network rules could mitigate while software was fixed), and manage/scale resources as a fleet.
* **Top-down portal limitations.** Backstage is praised for its UX, service catalog, and plugin extension mechanism, but Bryant warns it is a portal, not a platform; as a TypeScript framework often used as a facade calling infrastructure APIs, it serves Day Zero and Day One well but struggles with Day Two operations.
* **Bottom-up infrastructure limitations.** Terraform/CDK/CloudFormation "infrastructure-as-my-platform" with GitOps pipelines is highly automatable, but infrastructure abstractions leak toward developers, increasing cognitive load; at large financial organizations the diversity of tech (Terraform, CloudFormation, bash, mainframe) needs another layer on top to orchestrate.
* **Industry exemplars of the middle.** He highlights Crossplane (claims, composite resources and functions as the orchestration part), the KubeVela/canoe-style frameworks splitting application and operation, Humanitec's reference architecture (based on McKinsey work) explicitly naming platform orchestration, and Kratix with its Promise API encapsulating platform components.
* **The platform orchestration layer defined.** Aimed at the platform engineer; customers are developers, suppliers are operators/infrastructure folks. Focus is on the platform API (Kratix Promises, Humanitec resource definitions, Crossplane compositions, Argo/Flux CD rollouts) callable via UI, CLI or API, rather than the raw infrastructure API.
* **Tooling spectrum and trade-offs.** Options range from opinionated commercial tools (Humanitec, Massdriver, Qovery, Port) and frameworks (Backstack, KubeVela/OAM, Kratix) down to building your own on CNCF tech; opinionated tools give high Day One bang-for-buck but cause friction if you don't share their opinions, while less opinionated tools give freedom but you own the pain. He seeks the "Goldilocks" balance.
* **Closing guidance.** Build your platform intentionally rather than defaulting to portal or infrastructure-as-code; watch for the missing middle (called out in Thoughtworks' Tech Radar alongside Humanitec and Kratix); treat scaling Day Two ops or leaking infrastructure abstractions as signals the middle isn't right; and adopt platform-as-a-product thinking focused on customers' jobs-to-be-done across Day Zero, One and Two.

title: Surviving Your Inevitable Agile Transition
description: J.B. Rainsberger explores how to navigate corporate Agile transitions by prioritizing feedback-driven mindsets, early earned value, and technical practices like TDD and refactoring to manage system bottlenecks.
notes:
True agility is a mindset centered on **gathering feedback** rather than "thinking harder" to prioritize early earned value and predictable feature delivery. Success lies in treating agile practices as a **toolbox** to resolve systemic bottlenecks through measurable experiments instead of following rigid, fashionable checklists.

*   **Core Philosophy:** The fundamental difference between Waterfall and Agile is the approach to uncertainty: **Waterfall means when in doubt, think harder; Agile means when in doubt, gather feedback.**
*   **Economic Goal:** Agile's primary financial objective is **Early Earned Value (cash flow)**. It prioritizes getting "cash in pocket" sooner to become self-funding, rather than maximizing long-term Return on Investment (ROI) through unrealized appreciation.
*   **Refactoring as Risk Management:** The only proper justification for refactoring is money—specifically, **reducing volatility in the marginal cost of a feature**. It prevents "project heat death," where the cost of adding a feature becomes higher than the cost of starting over.
*   **The "Go Well to Go Fast" Principle:** While skipping design and refactoring might allow for a faster start, there is a crossover point where technical debt makes progress nearly impossible. **The only way to go fast over the long term is to go well.**
*   **Refactoring Styles:** Distinguish between **"cleaning the kitchen"** (refactoring as you write code) and **"cleaning the garage"** (scheduled maintenance tasks). Cleaning as you go is significantly more efficient than deferred maintenance.
*   **Theory of Constraints (TOC):** Every system has a **bottleneck** that constrains total throughput. To improve a system, you must identify the bottleneck and apply the appropriate Agile tool to increase its capacity; improvements elsewhere provide no system-wide benefit.
*   **Agile as a Toolbox, Not a Checklist:** Agile is not a maturity model or a set of levels to conquer. It is a collection of **etudes (exercises)** designed to help teams master techniques so they can eventually design their own unique process.
*   **Adoption via Experiments:** To transition successfully, frame the adoption of practices as **three-month experiments** with clear, binary success criteria (e.g., "reduce defects by 90%"). This manages both skeptics and over-enthusiastic "cheerleaders" by letting data determine the outcome.
*   **Personal Wins:** Technical practices are often adopted not because of rational business arguments, but because of **personal wins**, such as reducing work-related anxiety or the "pain in the chest" caused by constant bug reports.
*   **Combating "Fashionable Agile":** Avoid treating Agile as a fashion accessory or a "defined process" from a book. If a transition consists only of renaming Waterfall phases with Agile words (e.g., "stabilization sprints" instead of testing), it is likely just **chaos with different words**.

title: Building a Responsive Organization
description: Sami Honkonen lays out the building blocks of a responsive organization: complexity theory, systems thinking, small experiments, data-driven decisions, and empowered execution.
notes:
A responsive organization — one that adapts quickly when the world changes — rests on a few foundations: understanding complexity (most digitalizing businesses have moved from the complicated to the complex domain, where cause and effect are only visible in retrospect), so you operate by running small safe-to-fail experiments; systems thinking (performance is governed by the system, not individuals); being genuinely data-driven while avoiding its pitfalls; and empowering people to execute.

- Cynefin (Snowden): obvious (bicycle — best practices), complicated (Ferrari — expertise/strategy), complex (forest — only retrospective cause/effect, so experiment), chaotic, and disorder. Digitalization moves businesses into the complex domain while they keep using complicated-domain methods.
- In the complex domain, default to small, inexpensive, safe-to-fail experiments: scale what works, dampen what doesn't. Start with experiments measured in minutes, then hours, then days.
- Systems thinking (Deming, Seddon): performance is governed by the system (process, policy, org chart), not the individuals — put great people in a bad system and the system wins; swapping "bad apples" never fixes a structural problem (sales vs product blaming).
- Value flows horizontally to the customer, but management structures are vertical, which drives local optimization. Clarify purpose from the customer's perspective, design the whole org around it, and measure achievement of purpose, not activity.
- Incentives tied to targets are "the business equivalent of homeopathy" — contingent do-this-to-get-that relationships destroy intrinsic value; budgeting is largely a theatre of guesses aggregated into a semi-random number used for command-and-control.
- Be data-driven, but mind the pitfalls: regression to the mean (not causation), stagnation (waiting forever for data — ~3-7 samples is enough), rear-view-mirror lagging indicators, proxy/vanity metrics (closed tickets ≠ customer value; a wrong measure is worse than none), and the curse of metrics (Goodhart: a measure that becomes a target stops being good).
- Empowered execution (McChrystal, Team of Teams): "if it's not illegal or immoral, you have the authority to do it" — removing limitations removes excuses; if a general managing lives can do this, we can empower a $100 experiment.
- Design questions: what is the least structure your org can have without slipping into chaos, and when structure is needed, how do you make it as lightweight as possible? All structures eventually become wrong and are hard to change.

title: Attitude Determines Altitude- Engineering Yourself
description: Randy Shoup argues that attitude — growth mindset, trust and confidence — determines what you achieve more than skills or opportunities, told through his own personal story.
notes:
Your attitude, more than your skills or opportunities, determines what you achieve. Three attitudes to cultivate: a growth mindset (you can improve with effort), trust (the basis of teams), and confidence (come across as confident, ideally actually be it) — and for each, aim for the healthy middle of the spectrum.

- Growth mindset (Carol Dweck): believing talent can be developed leads you to embrace challenges, persist through obstacles, see effort as the path to mastery, learn from criticism, and be inspired (not threatened) by others' success — the most effective way to improve is to know that you can.
- Mastery comes from effort, not birth: Gladwell's ~10,000 hours of deliberate practice, sustained by focus (Goleman) — moderating the deliberative neocortex and the impulsive amygdala; attention is a trainable muscle, which is what mindfulness practice builds.
- Aim for the middle of each spectrum: a growth mindset without "if I don't improve I'm terrible"; trust as "trust but verify" (neither naive nor distrustful); confidence between fear and arrogance.
- Trust: McGregor's Theory X (people are lazy, micromanage them) versus Theory Y (intrinsically motivated, get out of their way); the DevOps Handbook's generative organization (high trust and sharing) predicts better, faster, more stable outcomes than bureaucratic or pathological ones.
- Psychological safety (Google's research) — is the team safe for interpersonal risk-taking? — was the number-one predictor of team success, above the skills of its members.
- Confidence: the imposter phenomenon affects ~70% of people at some point (and disproportionately drives underrepresented groups out of tech); Dunning-Kruger — novices overestimate ("Mount Stupid"), experts underestimate.
- Building confidence: Amy Cuddy's power poses shift hormones in 2 minutes; reframe "fake it till you make it" as "do it until you become it"; find a core strength to build the rest of your life on. Happiness = reality minus expectations.
- Shoup tells this personally — he abandoned this same talk in 2015 to a panic attack and imposter feelings — modeling the vulnerability the talk advocates. "Whether you think you can or you can't, you're usually right" (Ford).

title: Polyglot Data
description: Good tips related to persistence, entities, events... Good explanation about the information lost when we only store the actual state of an entitiy instead the stream of events of the entity. There is no "best" database, only "best database for" some use cases. The benefits of separate bounded contexts and decide wich databases are the bests for this bounded context... Separation of reads from writes (CQRS). Lot of good stuff.
notes:
Greg Young argues that most accidental complexity in software comes from forcing a single data model to do everything, when the right approach is to store an immutable series of events as the book of record and derive whatever specialized models you need from it. Starting from event sourcing, he shows how storing facts (not perceptions of facts) lets you spin up new read models at any time, and how matching each problem to the database that actually fits it — while consciously weighing operational complexity — eliminates enormous amounts of accidental complexity.

* **Events as the source of truth, not current state**: Mature industries (finance, accounting, insurance) have no concept of current state — they store facts that occur at points in time. Your bank balance isn't a column; it's a summation of all transactions. Any table value is just a cache, never authoritative.
* **Structural models lose information by design**: A purchase order can be stored as structure or as a series of events (cart created, items added, shipping added). Storing structure throws away information; storing events lets you replay to reconstruct any structure while keeping everything. Young's one rule as CTO was never to lose data.
* **Append-only, immutable model**: No update and no delete — like an accountant who never erases the middle of a ledger. A side effect: an HTTP cache on an immutable event can be set to infinite.
* **The same end-state proves data loss**: 'created, 3 added, 1 removed, shipping' versus 'created, 2 added, shipping' land at the same structural endpoint; if two different use cases leave your structural model identical, you've proven you're losing data — the only question is what it's worth.
* **Projections derive read models from events**: A projection can target in-memory, SQL, neo4j, MongoDB. His Amazon-cart example (items removed shortly before checkout are likelier bought later) shows the power: a structural model's report only works from deployment forward, but a projection replays the entire history, so the business sees it as if it had always existed.
* **New perceptions on old history**: Because projections replay from the beginning, you can apply today's understanding to past facts, even reconstruct what a report would have said at a past moment.
* **Every database sucks (in its own way)**: No best storage exists; object databases were great at navigation but horrific at OLAP reporting. A relationship-querying problem in SQL took ~10 engineers about a year and a half-million-dollar SQL Server; reimplemented in neo4j (a real graph problem) it was fixed in three days and outperformed on a laptop. Twitter on Rails+MySQL is another model mismatch.
* **CQRS — reads and writes are different**: Most systems are 90-99% reads, yet people build a third-normal-form model optimized for writing and then struggle to scale reads. Writes are simple (appending to a log — which neo4j, Cassandra, Oracle, SQL Server all do internally); the interesting, scale-sensitive side is reads.
* **Time as a dimension of separation**: A monthly batch MapReduce across 80 nodes can be paired with a small complex-event-processing layer that re-runs thin slices for near real-time answers without running the huge cluster constantly.
* **Producer- vs consumer-driven subscriptions**: The 'bus in the middle' forces distributed transactions and two drifting sources of truth. Instead store all events and expose them over a pull protocol like Atom/RSS; each subscriber remembers how far it has read and rebuilds by asking for events after -1. Writing to a single event store needs no distributed transaction — save and publish are the same operation, one source of truth.
* **The trade-off: accidental vs operational complexity**: The wrong model buys accidental complexity; many specialized models buy operational complexity (each must be configured, monitored, backed up). The right answer sits in the middle via conscious trade-offs. Typical real systems have at least three models — a key/value or document store for fast reads, a search index, and an OLAP store for reporting — so learn what each database is genuinely good and bad at.

title: Moving Fast at Scale
description: Randy Shoup explores how high-performing organizations achieve both speed and stability through autonomous business-aligned teams, disciplined problem-solving, and incremental value delivery.
notes:
High-performing engineering organizations achieve both speed and stability by empowering small, autonomous teams to focus on solving specific business problems through incremental delivery and disciplined prioritization. Success stems from minimizing work-in-progress to finish higher-value features faster while maintaining a rigorous definition of "done" that ensures actual customer value is delivered.

* **Speed and Stability are Correlated**: High-performing organizations do not trade speed for stability; they excel at both. Elite performers deploy 46 times more frequently and recover from failures 2,600 times faster than low performers.
* **Business Impact of Engineering Excellence**: High-performing engineering organizations are 2.5 times more likely to exceed business goals, including profitability, market share, and productivity.
* **Small, Mission-Aligned Teams**: Organize into cross-functional "two-pizza teams" that include product, development, QA, and operations. Teams should be aligned with business domains (e.g., Checkout, Payments) rather than technical layers (e.g., Database team).
* **Cellular Mitosis for Scaling**: As teams grow too large, split them into smaller "daughter" teams with new, well-defined boundaries rather than reorganizing the entire company.
* **Autonomy with Accountability**: Provide teams with clear business problems and metrics but allow them the autonomy to choose their own technical solutions. Teams must be held accountable for the results and sustainability of those choices.
* **Problem-Solving over "Typing"**: The primary job of an engineer is disciplined problem-solving, not just writing code. Always ask: "What problem are you trying to solve?" 
* **The Curse of Knowledge**: Engineers must bridge the gap with business partners by explaining technical trade-offs and options that non-engineers may not realize exist.
* **Eliminate the Biggest Waste**: Building the wrong thing is the greatest waste in software development because it represents 100% wasted effort.
* **Fewer Things, More Done**: Limit Work in Progress (WIP) to increase throughput. Assigning multiple people to a single high-priority feature delivers value faster than having everyone work on separate, lower-priority tasks.
* **Incremental Value and Option Value**: Break features into small, releasable chunks. This delivers value to customers earlier and provides the "option value" to change direction or stop development based on real feedback.
* **Minimal Viable Feature (MVF)**: Focus on the minimum implementation required to solve the problem reliably. Constantly evaluate the marginal cost of more "polish" versus the marginal benefit.
* **Scope vs. Quality**: When time or resources are constrained, always reduce the scope of the feature rather than compromising on quality.
* **Definition of Done**: A feature is not "done" until it is tested, released to production, turned on for the user, and monitored. Anything short of this produces zero customer value.

title: Software Engineering F&*K Up Behind The Passport E-gate Failure"
description: In this episode, Dave Farley talks about the issue, how poor software engineering led to this, how distributed systems come into it all, how to avoid something like this happening again and at the end of the video asks for answers on some concerning issues around the whole story.
notes:
Dave Farley dissects the May 2024 failure of the UK's electronic passport e-gate system, which simultaneously took down all airport e-gates and left tens of thousands of travellers queuing for hours. His core thesis is that this was not bad luck or a tooling problem but a failure of design: distributed systems will always fail somewhere, so the engineer's job is to design for resilience rather than assume perfection everywhere.

* **The actual failure was incidental coupling, not a cyber attack.** Early reports speculated about a cyber attack, but Farley argues it was a technical failure. A Home Office software update on an unrelated system exceeded the data limits in the contract, so BT shut down the Wi-Fi that supplied that update. Unfortunately that same Wi-Fi was also the route between all the e-gates and the Home Office database of terror suspects and barred persons.

* **A single point of failure made simultaneous nationwide failure inevitable.** With only about 275 e-gates across UK airports, the only way they could all fail in unison was a shared common point of contact. Because looking up who is barred relies on shared data, that lookup was the obvious candidate, and building it with no mitigation or contingency was an extremely risky choice.

* **Distributed systems fail in far more ways than other systems.** Once you distribute processing across a network, you add many new failure modes: bugs in either app, inability to establish a connection, the network failing mid-route, the receiver being too busy or unable to understand the message, and every one of those failures can happen again in reverse on the response. Most of these are outside the team's direct control.

* **He directly disputes the "it's too complex, buy more observability" framing.** Farley quotes John Atkinson of Riverbed Technology, who argued that complex IT estates are too difficult to keep running without additional observability tooling. Farley disagrees three times: the answer is not better tools or perfect foresight, it is better design.

### Designing for resilience

* **Resilience means assuming everything can fail at any time, not preventing all failure.** He recounts a workshop with an Australian bank where the room fell into stunned silence when he said the key to resilient systems is to assume everything will fail. They had believed the job was to stop anything from failing; resilient systems instead let each part make progress even when other parts are failing or missing.

* **Three mitigation strategies.** First, add redundancy: alternative communication paths, clustered databases, failover mechanisms. Second, cache copies of the data at strategic points. Third, reduce the number of failure points by shortening the distance between the data and its point of use, since a wide area network is riskier than a LAN, which is riskier than collocated data.

* **His preferred fix: a local "bad person cache" at each e-gate.** Combining caching and proximity, each e-gate holds its own copy of the barred-persons list. During normal operation (letting people in) no network connection is needed at all, so the system keeps working even when an unpredictable failure happens elsewhere.

* **The trade-off is eventual consistency, which is acceptable here.** Each gate's copy can't be guaranteed identical, but the system is designed to become consistent later, with differences measured in milliseconds. He notes the existing system can't guarantee all gates are in step either, due to race conditions and communication latency.

* **Implement with a distributed log of events.** A central server issues an event whenever someone is added to or removed from the barred list, and each e-gate (or each airport) consumes those events to maintain its local copy. Farley calls this a well-understood distributed log strategy and cites doing the equivalent for a distributed point-of-sales system across over 10,000 stores.

* **The manual fallback exposes the design flaw.** During the outage, passport officers used laptops to verify people. Farley asks whether those laptops used an alternative route to the database or a local copy. If the former, why wasn't the e-gate system designed to auto-failover to that route; if the latter, that's essentially his proposed solution but done manually and slowly.

* **Root cause is a single-source-of-truth mindset.** He suspects the system was designed by someone with a traditional database focus on one central source of truth rather than a genuinely robust distributed design. "Give someone a hammer and all problems look like nails."

* **The general lesson: limit the blast radius and use chaos engineering.** No matter the distributed system, assuming everything must be working for it to be useful is doing it wrong. Engineers should assume things will break, limit the blast radius, and use techniques like chaos engineering to discover failure modes they haven't yet imagined. It's never enough to imagine the happy path; the job is always to ask "yes, but what happens if?"

title: The 10 COMMANDMENTS Of Continuous Integration (CI)
description: This YouTube video outlines the 10 essential rules for continuous integration (CI), emphasizing its role as the foundation of a modern deployment pipeline and continuous delivery
notes:
The most important points of the presentation "The 10 COMMANDMENTS Of Continuous Integration (CI)" are:
- **Continuous integration is the first stage of a modern deployment pipeline and is central to continuous delivery**.
- Continuous integration is a discipline focused on making changes visible and integrating frequently to avoid "integration hell".
- **When done right, continuous integration leads to faster, safer, and more collaborative software development**.
- The foundational idea of continuous integration is that **there is only one version of the truth**, which is the current version of the software.

The 10 commandments of continuous integration are:
1. **Always run commit tests locally before committing**. This aligns with test-driven development.
2. **Wait for the results of the commit build**. If something breaks, the committer should be the first to know and fix it. Aim to keep continuous integration build times under five minutes.
3. **Fix or revert failures within 10 minutes**. If a fix isn't possible in that time, revert the change to avoid blocking the pipeline.
4. **Revert changes when necessary**. If a teammate breaks the build and is unavailable, revert their change.
5. **Monitor your changes through the pipeline**. Ensure changes pass acceptance, performance, and security tests.
6. **Treat a broken build as a build scene**. The person who caused the break is responsible for fixing it.
7. **Commit small, commit often**. Ideally, commit multiple times a day. Small changes are easier to test, debug, and integrate.
8. **Maintain a fast commit feedback loop**. Aim for feedback on commits within 5 minutes and full pipeline transit within an hour to catch problems early.
9. **Automate everything**. Automate testing, configuration management, deployment, and build creation to remove manual errors and ensure consistency.
10. **Take ownership of failures**. The person who introduces a failure is responsible for fixing it, even if it breaks something unexpected.

**Continuous integration requires discipline and accountability**.

title: Rails Conf 2013 The Magic Tricks of Testing
description: This talk strips away the veil and offers simple, practical guidelines for choosing what to test and how to test it. Finding the right testing balance isn't magic, it's a magic trick; come and learn the secret of writing stable tests that protect your application at the lowest possible cost.
notes:
The presentation focuses on strategies for writing effective unit tests, emphasizing that good tests are not magic, but rather a set of learnable "magic tricks". The core idea is to write tests that are **thorough, stable, fast, and few**. Here are the most important points:
- **Unit tests focus on a single object**: Unit tests should focus on testing one object in isolation, not the interactions between multiple objects, which is the focus of integration tests. The object should be treated like a "space capsule" with a clear separation between its inside and outside.
- **Messages are Key**: The approach to testing is centered around the messages that the object under test receives, sends, or sends to itself. These messages come in two flavors, **queries and commands**. Queries return a value and have no side effects, whereas commands have side effects and do not return a value.
- **Test incoming query messages by making assertions about what they return**. Focus on the interface not the implementation, so the implementation can be changed without breaking the tests.
- **Test incoming command messages by making assertions about direct public side effects**. Direct public side effects are the responsibility of the last Ruby class involved.
- **Do not test messages sent to self (private methods)**. These are implementation details and should not be tested directly. If they need to be tested for development purposes, segregate them and be prepared to delete them.
- **Do not test outgoing query messages**. These messages have no side effects and are therefore invisible to the rest of the app. Testing them is redundant.
- **Test outgoing command messages by setting expectations using mocks**. This ensures that the message is sent without depending on all the objects and messages between the object and the distant side effect.
- **Mocks are essential but must be kept in sync with the real objects**. Mocks are test doubles that play the role of some object in your real app. It is the programmer's job to ensure that the doubles keep the promise that they're going to implement a common API.
- **Minimalism in Testing**: Write the fewest tests possible to achieve thorough coverage. Every unnecessary test adds cost, and the goal is to find the simplicity that lives at the heart of complexity.
The presenter also notes that while these rules are useful, they can be broken when it will save money. The important thing is to understand the rules and then break them consciously, not through ignorance.

title: Digital Transformation: Thriving Through the Transition
notes:
Jeffrey Snover, Microsoft Technical Fellow and inventor of PowerShell, argues that careers advance not through steady effort but through correctly riding industry transitions, where there are always new winners and new losers. Using his own 13-year fight to ship PowerShell and his framing of digital transformation, he urges technologists to position themselves as people "moving the business forward" so they end up on the winning side of the current software-eats-the-world shift.

* **Stair jobs vs. elevator jobs.** In normal periods you climb your career one slow step at a time behind whoever is ahead of you. Periods of disruption are "elevator jobs" where things line up and you can jump several levels quickly — but the same elevator can also go down. Transitions are the wind in your sail, or they sink you.
* **Reward follows whoever moves the business forward.** As a Technical Fellow, Snover sits in reward reviews: leaders first identify who is moving the business forward, then how to compensate them. Budgets are fixed, so money handed to those people comes directly from those who aren't.
* **"Do you want to be relevant?"** The Tivoli CTO told him he was working on a product earning $15,000 a sale instead of one earning 2-20 million, and asked if he wanted to be relevant. It was a "moment of clarity": impact mattered more than the technology he was personally passionate about.
* **Being excellent at something that no longer matters is fatal.** Citing Bill Gates on Digital's decline: DEC died because it was excellent at something that stopped mattering. Betting on the right transition (NT, large datacenters) repeatedly carried Snover's career.
* **Conviction through a five-year demotion.** Coming into Microsoft as a Bill-Gates-sponsored industry hire betting on automation over the GUI, Snover was demoted for five years while the company chased GUIs and the .NET-everywhere Longhorn disaster. He persisted because he was convinced PowerShell was right.
* **The Coalition of the Willing.** Persecuted by executives who insisted "admins don't want command-line interfaces," Snover survived by partnering with the Exchange team, who were betting a multi-billion-dollar business on his technology; their backing made attacks back off.
* **Clear the bar by a wide margin.** Facing seven (then a surprise eighth) draconian rules for getting .NET into Windows, he aimed not for merely "green" but "greener than green," solving each requirement by an unambiguous margin so it couldn't be argued away.
* **The cost was real and unspoken support is useless.** Five years as a "pariah" cost him a lot of unpaid rewards. An executive later admitted "we were rooting for you" silently; his lesson: if you root for someone, tell them, because it would actually have helped.

### Software is eating the world
* **The biggest transition of our careers.** Drawing on Marc Andreessen, software both replaces traditional businesses and captures a growing share of value even in physical products like cars (10-100 million lines of code). Over half the world's top companies by market cap are now software-driven, and more developers are hired outside tech than inside it.

### Core vs context (Geoffrey Moore)
* **The other Moore's law matters more.** Geoffrey Moore's core/context distinction will matter more for the next 20 years than transistor-doubling did for the last 20. Core delivers differentiated value you can charge a premium for; context is everything you need but never staff with your best people.
* **Mission-critical context is the killing field.** Work that is no longer differentiating but still mission-critical is where companies over-invest, accumulate technical debt, and die.
* **Create bandwidth, then invest in innovation.** Two distinct moves: free up capacity by moving context to the cloud (SaaS, lift-and-shift, lift-and-modernize into containers), then invest that freed energy into cloud-native innovation (serverless/functions) focused on the customer-insight-to-feature loop. Build what differentiates you, buy what doesn't.
* **The starts and stops for new heroes.** Stop clicking next (automate), stop crafting no-value-add solutions, stop building snowflake servers (embrace DevOps), stop using low-leverage architectures (leverage the cloud), and stop dialing it in — invest in your career.
* **Uncomfortable is the new normal.** Snover admits he still walks in afraid his badge will turn red because he doesn't know what he's doing; the pace guarantees permanent discomfort, so the skill is learning to push through it rather than waiting to feel settled.

title: Organize for Complexity
notes:
Niels Pflaeging argues that management as a social technology died about 40 years ago and survives only as an "undead" zombie still embedded in organizations. His central thesis is that markets shifted from complicated to complex, and the centralized, top-down model that worked in the Industrial Age is now counterproductive; the only viable response is radical decentralization of decision-making toward the parts of the organization that touch the market.

* **Management is a dead social technology**: It still exists in companies, hospitals, public administration and NGOs, but there is no life in it. It was a brilliant invention for its era, yet the Industrial Age that justified it is over, making management a zombie that doesn't know it is dead.
* **Culture is a shadow, not a lever**: From a Systems Theory standpoint culture cannot be changed or "hacked" directly. An organization has exactly the culture it deserves because it produces culture all the time. You can only change your position toward the light — work the system — and culture changes as a consequence.
* **Taylor liberated the doing from the thinking**: Frederick Winslow Taylor's *Scientific Management* (~104 years ago) separated thinking from doing, placing thinking at least one hierarchy level above the workers and steering the organization top-down like a machine, producing an efficiency explosion.
* **The org chart is the addiction**: The org chart is a fundamental tool of Taylorism that defines who thinks and who merely follows. Information flows up, decisions are taken at the top, commands flow down, and steering happens from above through standardized processes, rules, planning, targets, incentives and time control.
* **Complicated vs complex is the key distinction**: A complicated system (a machine, software, accounting) is dead, externally controllable, and is either working or broken. A complex system (the weather, people, teams, markets) is alive and produces surprises all the time. Solving complex problems with complicated means is wasteful or destructive.
* **The Taylor bathtub**: Before the Industrial Age, craft work was predominantly complex and locally customized. Management drove complexity down to build machine-like organizations for explosive, standardized mass markets. From the 1970s onward complexity grew back into value creation, but most organizations never shifted the paradigm.
* **Toyota and the pioneers**: Companies like Toyota (transformed in the 1950s-60s), Google, Southwest Airlines and W.L. Gore mastered highly dynamic, customized value creation. Most others remain stuck in command-and-control — "alpha"/management versus the decentralized "beta" model.

### Pyramid to Peach
* **Organizations have inside and outside, not top and bottom**: The pyramid is a beautiful illusion. Systems Theory says organizations only know an inside and an outside. The "peach" model has a thin outer skin — the **periphery**, which touches the market — and a **center**, which does not.
* **Decentralize decision-making to the periphery**: Centralized steering works only when markets are complicated and unsurprising. Once markets become complex, the center can never steer against the market, so steering must be outsourced to the market itself via a network of highly autonomous, mini-company-like teams.
* **Three structures, front stage vs backstage**: The formal structure (the pyramid, front stage) and the value-creation structure (the peach, backstage where real work happens) fight each other in most organizations instead of being aligned.
* **The center serves the periphery**: If the periphery demands nothing, the center should do nothing. Only the periphery earns money from the external market, so central functions (HR, marketing) should behave like mini-enterprises paid only when they deliver something valuable.

### Theory X is a self-fulfilling prejudice
* **Theory X people don't exist**: Via his post-it experiment, almost everyone rates themselves Theory Y yet estimates many others as Theory X. McGregor's claim is that Theory X people have never existed — it is a dangerous prejudice and a self-fulfilling prophecy: stupid systems produce "X" behavior, which is an intelligent human response to bad systems.
* **Fix systems, not people**: Practices like project management, HR as a function, planning, salary ranges and incentives are tools built for nonexistent Theory X people. Management means fixing and steering people top-down, while **leadership means working the system together**; planning fails in complexity, where agile iteration is needed instead.

title: Avoid These Common Mistakes Junior Developers Make
description: A must-see talk. Dave Farley describes 8 common mistakes that junior developers often make and offers his advice on how to avoid them. Whatever your approach to software engineering and software development, whether you are practicing Continuous Delivery, DevOps, or something else, we think that you may find some helpful ideas in this video.
notes:
Effective software development is fundamentally a **problem-solving and learning discipline** rather than a simple coding exercise.

* **Software development is about solving problems, not just writing code.**
* **Requirements are a shared discovery process.** Don't treat development as mere translation of business requirements.
* **Speed comes from learning, not typing fast.**
* **Understand the problem domain deeply.** The best developers gain enough insight to spot opportunities even domain experts might miss.
* **Don't be afraid to ask for help.** Asking questions is a sign of intelligence and self-confidence.
* **Architecture is everyone's responsibility.**
* **Testing is part of the developer's job.** Automated testing is the best way to ensure a problem is actually solved.
* **Take professional responsibility for quality.** Writing clean code is a professional responsibility, not a feature to be negotiated.
* **Focus on what you can control.** Demonstrate better ways of working through your own actions.

title: Why Becoming an Effective Leader Involves Changing the Language You Use
A Brief History Of Spotify: Gustav Söderström
description: Explore the evolution of Spotify as Chief R&D Officer Gustav Söderström shares the **strategic "magic tricks," product pivots, and data-driven innovations** that transformed the startup into a global audio leader.
notes:
Building a world-class product requires delivering a **"magic" user experience** and proactively adapting to major platform shifts before they manifest in data, while recognizing that **exponential growth is ultimately the cumulative result of continuous, linear improvements.**

* **Founding Vision:** Spotify was designed to combine the **unlimited access model** of piracy (like Napster) with the **legality and user experience** of a paid service (like iTunes).
* **Technical Innovation:** The platform’s initial success was driven by a "magic trick" of **extreme speed**, achieving playback latency of approximately **250 milliseconds** by hacking the TCP protocol and using custom peer-to-peer technology.
* **Mobile Strategy:** To successfully replace the iPod, Spotify focused on **offline synchronization**, allowing users to store up to 10,000 tracks locally to ensure the service worked in planes, subways, and areas with poor data.
* **The Freemium Model:** The company pioneered a business model where a high-quality free product served as a **proprietary engine for paid conversions**, eventually achieving industry-leading conversion rates of over 30%.
* **Navigating Negative Growth:** The company survived two major crises of negative growth: one caused by **label-imposed usage caps** on desktop and another caused by the **global shift from desktop to mobile-only** usage.
* **The "Shuffle" Pivot:** Spotify maintained its mobile growth by identifying that users naturally shuffle their music 50% of the time, allowing them to offer a **free mobile tier limited to shuffle play** without cannibalizing the premium on-demand experience.
* **Machine Learning Transition:** Spotify evolved from a manual "music programming language" (playlists) to an **AI-driven "self-driving" experience**, using machine learning to power discovery features like **Discover Weekly**.
* **Data as a Labeling System:** The platform utilizes over **three billion user-generated classifications** (playlists) as a massive data advantage to train algorithms on musical sentiment and niche tastes.
* **Hardware Ubiquity:** In 2013, the company developed a **cloud-centric media streaming protocol** (Spotify Connect) to ensure the service integrated seamlessly with third-party speakers and home audio systems.
* **Direct Listing:** Spotify innovated its path to the public market by choosing a **direct listing** over a traditional IPO, which avoided dilution, bypassed high investor discounts, and allowed for **immediate employee liquidity**.
* **Compound Growth Philosophy:** The leadership views success not as a single exponential event, but as the **sum of many linear improvements** that eventually compound into exponential results.

title: Highlighting Silicon Valley Strategies for Improving Engineering Velocity, Efficiency, and Quality
description: David Mercurio gives an engineer's view of cultural strategies at Facebook, Snapchat and Stripe that improve engineering velocity, efficiency and quality.
notes:
Culture is more than perks — it's how a company approaches problems. The recurring Silicon Valley pattern (Facebook, Snapchat, Stripe) is to solve a recurring problem once, with a bottom-up foundational solution everyone builds on, rather than asking each team to be careful — reinforced by people practices (onboarding, rotations) and blameless incident handling.

- Facebook's repeated privacy bugs were fixed not by "everyone check your code" but by making the social graph privacy-aware: every node has a policy of ordered allow/deny rules, and data is always loaded with the viewer so unauthorized data never comes back — easy to unit-test, centrally optimizable, and it served a personalized-video product built 5 years later.
- Snapchat abstracted storage behind a "dumb client": the server tells the client which (signed) URL and headers to use, so they could split across cloud providers for cost leverage, location and even higher uptime than any single provider — without changing clients.
- Stripe isolates PCI compliance behind a thin layer (Apiori, written in Go) that tokenizes card data, so the rest of the (Ruby) codebase works with tokens and moves fast without PCI process overhead — solve problems bottom-up because every layer above benefits.
- People practices: Facebook Bootcamp (commit on day one, work across teams → shared ownership and "move fast") and Hackamonth (a month on a new team → bidirectional knowledge sharing, lower bus factor, better retention); Stripe's DevStart and engineer/leadership rotations transfer knowledge and culture, including to new offices.
- Blameless incidents: a "big red button" form auto-generates a memorable name, Slack channel and timeline; an incident-PM rotation coordinates; the review is blameless (if a human can take down the API, the system is at fault) and tracks incidents over time to spot cross-team patterns and decide when to slow down and pay tech debt.

title: Real Example of a Deployment Pipeline in the Fintech Industry
description: Dave Farley shows a real deployment pipeline in-use in a Fintech company called TransFICC based in London. TransFICC adopted Continuous Delivery from the start and practice it at an advanced level. Delivering frequent, changes into production in a complex system, working in a regulated industry.
notes:
Dave Farley walks through a real, actively-used deployment pipeline at TransFICC, a fast-growing London fintech startup that acts as a translation/routing broker between trading organizations and external trading venues. The talk's thesis is that a deployment pipeline is not just a build-and-test mechanism but a central tool that organizes the entire development process, and that the specific technologies matter far less than how the team works around the pipeline.

* **The problem TransFICC solves**: Trading institutions need to integrate with many partners, and each new partner adds another distinct communication channel handling organizational and technical differences. TransFICC implements a single integration so a trading organization only integrates once, while TransFICC absorbs all the partner integrations — effectively a "device driver for trading."
* **Technology stack (deliberately downplayed)**: Software built in Java with Gradle, Jenkins for continuous integration, Ansible for configuration management, and custom deployment tools built on top of SSH that provide both the transport mechanism to move bytes and the authorization to secure transfers — critical in a highly regulated industry. Farley stresses you don't need any of these specific tools to do continuous delivery.
* **"Big feedback" display**: The pipeline's UI is intentionally basic — simple HTML and CSS. Green boxes mean releasable, red means a failure and not releasable, grey means a stage is disabled (being created or retired). Underneath sit thousands of test cases across many automated test environments.
* **Commit stage**: Shows the current change, commit message, who made it, and when it last ran (eight minutes ago in the example). The most important signal is simply whether the build is green. A progress bar keeps the picture dynamic so team members can gauge how long a change will take to come through.
* **Integration tests as a tactical addition**: TransFICC runs integration tests for cases too slow for the commit stage but that catch common errors faster than a full acceptance run. Farley advises running them in parallel with acceptance tests, still treating the commit stage as the gate, to get fast early failure.
* **Acceptance stage**: Runs a wide collection of trading-scenario tests evaluating the whole system. Farley argues a big red block on a shared display — ideally permanent in the team room — is a far stronger signal than an easily-ignored email or Slack notification.
* **Stable libraries as independent pipelines**: Common stable libraries change slowly and are treated like external dependencies with their own deployment pipelines; when changed, the binary output is recommitted into the main pipeline and re-evaluated — the same approach used for third-party libraries.
* **Parallel adapter testing**: TransFICC's large collection of translators/adapters to external trading venues are tested independently of and in parallel with one another, reflecting their business model.
* **Judd's happy-path demo (TDD)**: A developer starts a new feature by writing a test first (true test-driven development), writes code to pass it, then builds, tests, and deploys the full distributed system locally via a Gradle target called "drs" using the same mechanisms that deploy to production. The whole flow from reading the requirement to committing took 11 minutes; the commit stage then ran in about seven minutes (they target under five).
* **Trunk-based development**: TransFICC work nearly all the time on origin master, practicing true continuous integration — every commit triggers the pipeline, which rejects any change not deemed releasable.
* **Michael's failure-path demo**: He runs nearly 500 tests locally in about 15 seconds (a benefit of fast TDD tests), commits, and the commit stage rejects the change after eight test failures. He clicks through the big-feedback UI to the underlying Jenkins report to diagnose.
* **The embargo system**: TransFICC built an embargo mechanism that temporarily blocks others' route to production during big changes, keeping teammates informed since CI is a publication process on a shared resource. Farley warns it's a blunt-force tool that blocks everyone, so use it with care.
* **Commit stage as primary catcher, acceptance as backstop**: The failing commit test gave fast feedback in minutes; had it been missing, an acceptance test would have caught the problem much later and more expensively. A well-optimized pipeline catches most errors at the cheap commit stage, with acceptance acting as a backstop and confidence builder.

title: Successful Go Program Design, 6 Years On
description: Peter Bourgon distills idioms and anti-patterns for designing production Go programs, with explicit dependencies as the central principle.
notes:
A tour of patterns and anti-patterns for well-designed production Go, six years into the language. The single most important rule, repeated throughout: make dependencies explicit — pass them in rather than reaching for package-global state — because that one discipline is what makes Go programs readable, testable and composable.

- Embrace Go's conventions instead of fighting them: always gofmt (un-formatted code is a red flag), follow the code-review-comments and naming guidance, and name packages for what they provide, not what they contain (avoid "common"/"util"); never use dot imports.
- Repo structure optimizes for the consumer: play nice with `go get`; for a binary-with-library put library code in a `lib` subpackage, for a library-with-binary put the command under `cmd/foo`.
- Make configuration explicit: define and scope flags in func main (not globals/init), and pair every environment variable with a flag so the binary is self-documenting.
- Construct cleanly: struct-literal initialization over piecemeal field assignment, avoid nil checks by supplying no-op defaults, and make the zero value useful (even for config objects).
- Resolve cross-referencing components by combining them, splitting out shared subparts as injected dependencies, or modeling the relationship as a channel owned by the calling context.
- Channels orchestrate, mutexes serialize: channels suit semaphores, scatter-gather and the actor pattern (consider a single channel of funcs), but plain shared state and iterators are better as mutexes/callbacks — channels carry a mutex inside and aren't free.
- Loggers, metrics, HTTP clients and DB handles are all dependencies, not globals; treat `init` as a bad smell since it only mutates global state.
- Testing is just programming — be wary of cargo-culting heavy TDD/BDD DSLs from other languages; define small client-side interfaces for consumer contracts and take dependencies as interfaces so fakes substitute easily.
- Logging is expensive (log only actionable events); instrumentation is cheaper than you think (instrument utilization/saturation/errors or rate/errors/duration).
- Operational tips: vendor dependencies for binaries but not libraries, prefer `go install`, and remember an elegant monolith is very productive — don't reach for microservices reflexively.

title: Less - The Path to Better Design
description: This talk strips away the well-known design principles and exposes the hidden, underlying goals of design. It reveals programming techniques that allow you to write less code while creating beautiful, flexible applications.
notes:
The presentation emphasizes the importance of **designing code that is easy to change** and reduces the cost of change. The core idea is that code should work today but be adaptable to future requirements. The presenter introduces key concepts and techniques related to object-oriented design, focusing on managing dependencies and reducing coupling between objects. Here's a breakdown of the most important points:
- **Definition of Design:** Design is not about following rigid rules or what someone else dictates, but is the art of arranging code with the goal of reducing the cost of change. The fundamental tension in design is between present needs and future changes.
- **Diagnostics for Good Code:** Instead of focusing on bad code traits (rigid, fragile, immobile, viscous), the presentation offers four diagnostics for good code:
    - **Transparent:** It should be clear what will happen if the code changes.
    - **Reasonable:** The cost of adding a new feature should be proportional to its value.
    - **Usable:** Code should be reusable in new contexts without duplication.
    - **Exemplary:** The code should encourage future changes that are beneficial for the app.
    - **Knowledge and Dependencies:**Objects should know what they do, but not necessarily how they do it.
    - **Knowledge creates dependencies, which create risk**.
    - Managing dependencies is at the center of design.
    - **Depend on things that are more stable than you**.
    - **Decoupling:**When uncertain about the stability of objects, **decouple them by making them know as little about each other as possible**.
    - **Loosening coupling makes code more flexible** in the face of change.
    - Decoupling can be achieved by having objects tell each other what they want rather than directing how to behave.
- **Abstractions and Duck Typing:**
- Focus on **abstractions** rather than concrete classes to reduce coupling.
- Use **duck typing** (where the type of an object is less important than the methods it implements) to create more flexible code.
- Design objects as if they exist in isolation, where they cannot and do not know what other classes exist, instead focusing on the message and less on the class.
- **The Role of Uncertainty:** Uncertainty is not a reason to guess, but a directive to decouple. Design should help preserve the right to make decisions later.
- **Feeling for Design:** Good designers have a feeling for what's right that guides them. This feeling comes from the space between objects, with more space leading to more flexibility.
- **Loose Coupling:** Loose coupling is a key technique, but it can become another concretion. It's essential to find the abstraction, but it is important not to abstract too soon.
The presentation uses a refactoring example of a bicycle touring company to illustrate these concepts. The examples show how to reduce dependencies between the `Trip` and `Mechanic` classes by driving details into the objects that should know them and depending on their public interfaces. The core message is that focusing on stable abstractions and minimizing dependencies is essential for writing code that is both functional and adaptable.

title: Episode 066: From OO to FP & All the Things in Between w/ Sandi Metz
description: Podcast:   The Elixir Fountain Podcast

title: Making developer experience a reality
description: There’s often lots of talk about how companies can make their developers more productive. But it may be more useful to think about developer effectiveness: how to ensure they’re building the most useful products for their customers. This is about providing an environment that allows developers to be effective. Our special guests from Spotify and Etsy give us their unique perspectives.
notes:
Stop optimizing for developer "productivity" (easily gamed vanity metrics like lines of code) and optimize for developer effectiveness instead: give engineers an environment of fast, friction-free feedback loops so they spend their energy building the right things. The biggest wins come from removing the many small frictions, not from one big bandwagon migration.

- Productivity questions quickly degenerate into gameable, harmful measures (commits, lines of code); "effectiveness" reframes it as working smarter and providing an environment where developers can be effective.
- Software development is a series of feedback loops, micro (write code → does it compile/pass tests, done 10-100x/day) and macro (stand up a new service, join a team, find documentation/API info); high-performing companies obsess over making the micro loops fast.
- The compounding cost of small frictions: a step that should take 15-30s but takes 2 minutes bleeds context, cognitive load and focus (you go check email / take a walk); these hidden frictions rarely get optimized because they're hard to measure.
- Beware the big-bang trap: teams adopt microservices/cloud/a platform expecting to be "perfect in two years" and often end up slower, having added complexity (a distributed system) without creating an effective environment.
- Etsy (Keyur Govande): a decade-old PHP/MySQL monolith they deliberately keep, investing in deployment velocity and a human-presses-the-button push queue (ownership/accountability) while removing toil; lead time stayed flat through 10x growth.
- Spotify (Pia Nilsson): hyper-growth with falling productivity showed up in the onboarding metric (time to 10th PR rose past 60 days); root causes were context-switching from a fragmented ecosystem and not being able to find things — which led to building Backstage, golden paths, and "fleet management" (automatic PRs) under an autonomous culture.
- Governance done right is a paved road / golden path: guide people to the right default while letting them go off-road if they own the consequences; treat internal platforms as products (talk to users, prioritize, A/B test, build incrementally).
- Measure to justify investment: Accelerate's four key metrics plus higher-level signals (onboarding time, lead time, quarterly engineer satisfaction/NPS surveys) make the case and expose hidden rework; Spotify cut onboarding from 60+ to ~20 days.

title: MMMSS – The Intrinsic Benefit of Steps
description: Why we should work in small safe steps (3s).
notes:
Based on the provided podcast excerpts, the most important points of the presentation are the concept of **MMSS (many more much smaller steps)** and its intrinsic benefits compared to taking larger, less frequent steps. The presentation argues that **experienced practitioners will often prefer taking a greater number of smaller steps to reach a goal, even if the total effort appears equivalent to fewer larger steps**.
Here are the key takeaways:
- The core idea is that **taking many more much smaller steps provides intrinsic value that improves performance with each step because they are "ready to ready" steps**.
- The presentation contrasts MMSS with traditional approaches:
    - **Old school theory** typically favors a single large step.
    - **Rework avoidance theory** is indifferent to step size based on a linear relationship between effort and step size.
- The intrinsic benefits of taking more smaller steps are divided into two main categories:
- **Responsiveness benefits (related to the producing system)**: These arise from the increased frequency with which the system returns to a state of readiness. They include:
        - **Interruptability**: Interruptions are cheaper and easier when the system is frequently ready.
        - **Steerability**: It is cheaper and easier to change direction when the system is in a ready state, which occurs more often with smaller steps.
        - **Reversibility**: Smaller steps are easier to take back or correct with minimal cost.
    - **Target parallelism**: More frequent readiness allows for a more granular approach to satisfying multiple stakeholders with differing priorities.
- **Human benefits (related to the individuals doing the work)**: These benefits stem from the impact of smaller steps on the people involved in value production. They include:
        - **Scope**: Smaller steps inherently involve fewer entities and actions, reducing complexity and the combinatorial increase in difficulty associated with larger steps.
        - **Rhythm**: Completing steps releases brain chemicals that create a sense of reward. More frequent smaller steps lead to a more continuous release of these chemicals, boosting energy and productivity.
        - **Safety**: Smaller steps are less risky due to their greater ease and lower stakes. Errors are smaller and less costly to correct.
    - **Autonomy**: Smaller, "ready to ready" steps in live systems, like trunk-based development in programming, allow for greater individual and team autonomy, which is a key motivator.
- While not all benefits are realized with every step, the human benefits are often consistently present.
- The principles of MMSS are not limited to programming but are applicable to **generalized change towards any horizon goal**.
- The presentation acknowledges that there is a counter-argument from the "old school rework avoidance approach" which will be addressed in a later discussion.
In essence, the core message is that by embracing the seemingly counter-intuitive approach of taking "many more much smaller steps," individuals and teams can achieve goals more effectively due to a range of intrinsic benefits that enhance responsiveness and empower the human element in the process.

title: The New Code — Sean Grove, OpenAI
description: OpenAI's Sean Grove introduces "The New Code," arguing that specifications are the universal artifact of intent, crucial for aligning both humans and advanced AI models.
notes:
Specifications, not the resulting code, are the universal artifact of intent and value, serving as the essential tool for aligning humans across different disciplines (like engineering, product, and legal) and for effectively training and evaluating advanced AI models. Therefore, the ability to write robust specifications that clearly capture intent is quickly becoming the most valuable and scarce skill for programmers in the emerging era of "The New Code".

- The value programmers bring is largely in **structured communication** (80-90%), not just the resulting code (10-20%).
- The **source specification**, not the generated code or binary, is the valuable artifact of intent.
- **Specifications** are the universal artifact of intent and value, essential for aligning humans (engineering, product, legal, policy, safety) on shared goals.
- The more advanced AI models become, the more severely the bottleneck of knowing _what_, _how_, and _why_ to build will be felt; **effective communication** becomes the most valuable programming skill.
- **Code is a lossy projection** from the specification; specifications embody all necessary intentions and values to generate the code.
- A robust specification contains enough information to translate into multiple targets (e.g., code, clients, documentation, tutorials).
- The new scarce skill is **writing specifications that fully capture intent and values**.
- The **OpenAI model spec**, a collection of versioned, human-readable markdown files, serves as an example of a specification that aligns all humans within the company and can be used for model alignment.
- Specifications can encode **success criteria** and unit tests to verify model compliance (e.g., using IDs to link clauses to challenging prompts).
- Specs serve as a **trust anchor** and a reference point for determining expected behavior and identifying bugs (as seen in the 40 sycophancy issue).
- Specifications can be used as both **training material and evaluation material** for automatically aligning AI models through techniques like deliberative alignment.
- Specs are analogous to code: they compose, are executable, testable, have interfaces, and can be shipped as modules.
- The concept of specification is **universal**: Programmers align silicon (via code specs), Product Managers align teams (via product specs), and Lawmakers align humans (via legal specs).
- Every prompt used to align an AI model is a **proto-specification**; users are spec authors.
- **Engineering is not about code**, but the precise exploration by humans of software solutions to human problems, moving towards a unified human encoding (specification) of how to solve these problems.
- The future IDE may be an **Integrated Thought Clarifier** that pulls out ambiguity in specifications to improve communication between humans and models.

title: Software engineering daily Gitlab with Pablo Carranza
description: Pablo Carranza on scaling GitLab and the January 2017 production-database deletion — a model of blameless, radically transparent incident response.
notes:
How GitLab (a Rails monolith shipped as the very same package that runs GitLab.com) scales, and the story of its January 2017 production-database deletion — a model of blameless, radically transparent incident response. The deeper lesson: drive engineering with data, not assumptions, and treat run books and postmortems as the path to continuous improvement.
